text: *The Burial of Woes* is a collection of ten volumes, with a sequel of two volumes, written by Zhu Yiqing of the Qing dynasty. **Table of Contents** Preface Volume One - Cloud-piercing Zither - Madame Xiong - A Man from Jiaxing - The Story of Pan - Zhou Kui - The Grave of the Loyal Dog - Qi Ziyi - A Teacher Worth Learning From - Carrying Rice - The Old Man of Wuxi - Capturing a Thief with a Corpse - Master Zhong - The Snake's Remains - Gambling for Food - Snow Maiden - Wu's Chaste Lady - Cheng Guangkui - Zhu Tianji - Thunderstrike - Crickets - Living Buddha - Universal Script - Sea Eel - The Great Man - Capturing Ghosts - Mr. Guo - Zhang Chidi - The Delicate Zither Volume Two - Enlightened Monk of Zhaoqing - Double Wedding - Zhou the Spoiled - Dog Stew - Shao Shimei - Shen Boyan - Lady Chen San - The Great Man - Cloud and Rain - Prince of the Spring River - Frost - Epidemic Oddities - Flood - The Immortal of Guli - White Sparrow - King of Turtles - Xue Jianyang - Examining the Match - Strange Snake - Weighing Snake - Famous Doctor - Hand Skills - Frog Teacher - Teaching Iron Boy - Golden Butterfly - The Failure of Persimmon Garden - Hui Nü - Jia Quan - Zhi Shi - Abortion Volume Three - Locked Yin - The Fireworks Bureau - Flattery and Disaster - Delivering Poetic Rhymes - Tortoise Mirror - Secret State - Bamboo Packaged Boat - Golden Mirror - Herbal Remnants - Rice Cake Ah Liu - Qin Hui as a Pig - Jia Sidao - Ghost Ship Volume Four - Two Servants' Story - Duan Zhu - Mr. Jin San - Reading the Law - Selling Poems - Poetic Prophecy - Autumn Swallow Poem - Fan Chi Temple - Shi Shi - Empty Man - Ghost Lantern - Sacrificial Text for Crocodiles - Shooting Rabbits - Ma Hongmo - Taoist of Mount Mao - Leaf the Great Poet's Prophecy

Ventriloquism** **Liu Zizhuang** **Xiong Bolong** **Paintings in the Treasury** **Medium Writings** **Jade Person** **Catholicism** **Brave** **Xiang Wang's Racecourse** **Continuation Volume Two** **Wu Zhiqi** **Person with Facial Sores** **Chen Jushan** **Burial of Silkworms** **Dog that Repays Debts** **Flaying** **Immortal Formulas** **Geng Tong** **Praise of the Unusual Creature in the Biography of Lu Zhongyi** **Imperial Examination Papers** **Tui Bei Tu** **Li Zicheng** **Xu Zhuyuan** **Analysis of the Biography of Mao Wénlóng** **Self-Introduction** **Since the year Xinchou to Gui Si, my two elders passed away one after another, and I then abandoned all thoughts of advancement. The bird has grown weary of flight, and the horse is content to remain hidden. My lifelong friends have mostly passed away, and I also fear to see those who have achieved success. Looking up and down, I am surrounded by four walls, and every day I only rely on my young daughter for company. It seems that all the joys of life have come to an end. Then I sighed and said: "I am indeed poor!" However, the body may be poor, but the mind cannot be poor! I truly am a discarded talent, unworthy to be compared with the many heroes of the world. Even cicadas and earthworms do not know about rain and snow, and the cicada does not know about the changing seasons, yet they can still make their voices heard through their tiny openings. How could I, who am but a useless remnant, be less capable than even these cicadas and earthworms? Thus, whether it be by the light of a lamp while drinking wine, or in the cold rain at a secluded window, when old friends do not come, and I have some insight, I will write a few lines to relieve my inner turmoil and express the pain of my wasted years. When my thoughts suddenly become blocked, and a new realm opens up, I feel as if a solitary phoenix is soaring thousands of feet high, looking down upon the mortal world, how could I possibly know about the petty matters of the world! And so, I often raise a cup of wine and say: "Alas! If I had this talent, I would not have been able to serve in the history bureau and be a slave to Ma and Ban, but it is enough to amuse myself with this."

This spring, returning to my hometown, I brought this manuscript with me in my chest. Fellow scholars saw it and all agreed that it could be published, planning to raise funds for its printing. Recently, Mr. Jiang, a friend from this area, took on the task of engraving. Grateful for their kind intentions, I was able to avoid the feeling of being forgotten after death, so how could I possibly refuse? I am now old, and recalling the past fifty years, I have used my time to engage in aimless singing and crying. My life and status can be imagined from this. Therefore, I wrote these few words to record my regrets. On the eighth day of the eighth month, in the twenty-fifth year of the Daoguang era, Guian's Zhu Yiqing, known as Meishu, inscribed this in his temporary residence by the Xunxi River.

The Cloud-Piercing Guqin During the reign of Emperor Kangxi, there was a Taoist priest named Wangquan from the Gouqu region. Originally from a prominent family in Wuchang, he was orphaned in his youth and sought refuge in Taoism on Laoshan Mountain. He was bold and free-spirited, fond of reading and drinking, and skilled in painting bamboo. He was particularly adept at playing the guqin and would always purchase high-quality materials at great expense, even going as far as mortgaging his possessions. Later, he heard of a merchant named Wu Wei Long from Xianan who had a large collection of ancient guqins. Wangquan packed food and traveled to visit him. When the merchant saw that he was carrying an ancient guqin, he asked, "Are you also skilled in playing the guqin, Master?" Wangquan replied, "Indeed, it is my lifelong passion, but I regret not having encountered famous materials." He then pointed to the guqin he was carrying and said, "This was once owned by the Song dynasty official Jia Xiang, and I purchased it for 500 taels of silver, but it is not the highest quality. I heard that you have many ancient guqins, so I did not hesitate to travel such a long distance. May I be granted the opportunity to see one of them?" The merchant then engaged in a discussion about the theory of guqin playing. Wangquan provided a detailed explanation of the techniques of playing the guqin, and his explanations were profound and insightful. The merchant did not fully understand all of the explanations at first and asked Wangquan to demonstrate his skill. Wangquan took out his guqin and played the piece "Shuixian Cao." The merchant sat upright and listened attentively, as if the distant mountains, forests, and the sound of waves were evoking images in the room. He was deeply impressed by the performance.

Quan paused his zither and said, "This tune was passed down from Boya to Jikang, named 'Guangling San,' which refers to watching waves at Guangling. After Jikang's death, this tune was lost, and I have composed it anew based on my understanding." Shang then took out over ten of his cherished zithers, but all were not worth seeing. The last zither had gold cat's eye inlays, dragon liver stone pegs, and two characters inscribed on the back: "Chuanyun." Its texture was dense and lustrous, with an ancient dark hue, making it a rare treasure. Quan loved it so much he couldn't put it down and asked to exchange his own zither for it. Shang refused, and even when Quan offered 500 gold, Shang still refused. He then called a servant to take it inside. Quan then got up, left in disappointment, and sought advice from the gatekeeper. The gatekeeper replied, "The master only pretends to appreciate the refined arts; he has no true appreciation. Now that you see the master appreciates it so much, how could he be moved by profit?" Quan then left, rented a room in a temple, vowed not to return without the zither, but ultimately had no plan, and just drank every day.

Not long after, one evening, he drank alone under the moon, reflecting on his dwindling resources and the fact that he would never obtain the precious qin, and he wept sadly. Suddenly, he heard the sound of footsteps in the shadow of the wall, and a woman with a graceful and charming appearance approached, smiling. She said, "On such a fine night, please allow me to sing and play the music to accompany your wine, to break the silence, is that acceptable?" Quan, surprised, asked the beautiful woman where she had come from. The woman replied, "Do not trouble to question further; I am not someone who will bring you misfortune." She then took a yellow-toothed clapper from her bosom and sang a segment of the "Qin Heart," her melody being melodious and mournful, and her gaze and glances creating charm. Quan drank several cups in a row and finally fell asleep on the bed. When he woke up, the slanted moon was shining through the window, and the woman was still sitting by the lamp. He quickly got up and urged her to return to sleep, and the woman said, "I am not a runaway; since you have favored me, I feel there are still people like Zhong Rang in the world."

Having known the depth of your feelings, I therefore left my master to come here, intending to entrust this body to you. Regarding matters in your heart, perhaps one could still serve as a substitute, but it seems you reject deeply," she said, wiping tears with her sleeves. Quan saw that she was lonely and cold, yet turned even more graceful, and embraced her into his arms, explaining the reason for lingering. She said, "This is an easy matter." Upon hearing this, Quan was extremely happy and said, "Then tonight I wish to die for love." They embraced and entwined together. Afterward, when the birds were singing and the stars were scattered, the woman quickly stood up and said, "How can we stay here any longer?" Quan explained that the qin had not been obtained, and she reassured him with a smile, saying, "Just proceed without worry." She then went to the corner and took a small box, took out a set of water field clothes and skirts, and hats and shoes, changed into Daoist attire, and together hurriedly prepared. They opened the back door and left.

Halfway into a village inn for wine, there was already a Taoist seated. Quan bowed and conversed with him, their discussion profound and distant. The Taoist then invited him to drink together. The woman avoided and left. The Taoist whispered, saying, "You followed the young nun, who is not a person. Tonight, when you sleep together, I will perform a ritual outside. You should tightly hold and not let go." Following his advice, Quan indeed obtained a lute, which was the one Shang treasures. Greatly delighted, he held it up to show the Taoist. The Taoist said, "This is the lute left by Yang Guifei, passed down to Emperor Lizong of the Southern Song Dynasty, once used for burial, and later unearthed by Yang Lianzhenjia. Only you are worthy of this object. It also shows that divine objects do not end up in the hands of ordinary people, but you cannot return to Lao Mountain again." Quan suddenly heard this, as if waking from a dream, then rose and bowed twice, carrying the lute into Zhongnan Mountain and did not return.

The Historian Said:** With the merchant from Wu, who possessed a vast collection of lutes, yet only managed to acquire a single "Piercing Cloud" lute, we see that divine treasures are not easily obtained. Alas, he did not know its true value and lost it due to careless handling. It was named "Fearsome Dragon," a name that aptly reflected its nature. The Taoist from Mount Lao, desiring a fine lute, wept bitterly while offering all his wealth. Suppose he ultimately failed to obtain the lute; would he not return home in despair? How foolish was the Taoist's obsession with the lute! Yet, without such foolishness, how could he have achieved such a remarkable feat? Those in the world who are overly relaxed and self-satisfied, and who ultimately fail to master any art or skill, all consider themselves unfoolish. Thus, the Taoist's foolishness is beyond reach. **Madame Xiong** During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong, the father of Qin Zhongyue from Shenmu, while serving in the military, passed through Wulong Mountain. On a hunting trip, he became lost and saw five peaks rising abruptly, with cliffs standing tall on all sides. The dense forest was filled with the roars of tigers and the howls of foxes. Beneath a shadowy rock, the snow had not yet melted, revealing a cave in the ravine. The entrance to the cave was smooth like a mirror, indicating the presence of something within. Growing more anxious, he climbed vines in search of a path but found none. Suddenly, he heard a foul wind passing, and a bear suddenly appeared, snatching him and dragging him into the cave. The cave was about an acre wide, with sunlight filtering through a crack. The interior was half-covered with feathers, piled up to a thickness of more than an inch. The bear carried him to this place and then exited, picking up a large stone from the entrance to block the cave and leave.

A Man and a Bear Qin believed that the bear had found something rare and exotic, and would likely bring its kind to share in the feast and test their strength. As they were wandering, the bear suddenly lifted a stone with its hand and entered, carrying a deer in its left hand and throwing it before Qin. It caressed Qin and showed a playful expression, then took the deer meat and ate it, also feeding Qin. Qin observed that the bear's intentions were not harmful, so he took out the fire-making tools he had brought and started a fire, gathering fallen leaves from outside the cave to roast the food. The bear discarded the remaining meat and joined Qin in eating. As soon as the child tried a word, he would nod and jump with joy. At night, the bear would hug Qin and sleep. After several months, the bear gave birth to a boy, whose appearance below the waist was covered in bristly hair like a hedgehog. Qin initially did not have a child and was quite content. The bear nursed the child as a loving mother, and over time, the child gradually learned to speak and became very familiar with humans. The cave door was always open, indicating a sense of trust and openness. Qin considered escaping and returning home, but he could not leave the child behind. After four years, the child grew strong and robust, resembling an eight or nine-year-old, and walked as if flying. One day, when the bear went out, Qin took the child and fled, running wildly for dozens of miles. They encountered several hunters and followed them, taking a path back to civilization.

At first, Qin went hunting and did not return, and everyone thought he had been eaten by beasts. When he finally came back, the villagers asked about the reason and saw that the boy was tall and strong, resembling a bear or tiger, and they were even more surprised and delighted, as if they had gained a rare treasure. However, the boy often thought of his bear mother, and he repeatedly wanted to go look for her. His parents tried to stop him, but he would always cry and refuse to eat. Later, the boy grew stronger and enjoyed riding and shooting arrows, with the strength to pull back a thousand catties, displaying god-like courage and invincibility. One day, he took his bow and arrows, mounted his horse, and rode off, not returning until dusk. They searched everywhere but found no trace of him, suspecting that he had gone to join his bear mother, but no one dared to follow him. Qin, thinking the boy was still young, believed he must be dead and cried bitterly. Soon after, the boy returned with the bear mother. He said that when he first left, he asked people where the Five Dragons were, and following their directions, he rode ahead without getting lost. Whenever he could not find food along the way, he would shoot birds and animals to eat. Finally, he reached the southeastern part of Yulin, where he met a woodcutter who claimed to know where the bear mother was. The woodcutter led him to the cave entrance, and suddenly disappeared. The boy entered the cave, and the bear mother suddenly emerged from outside, intending to grab and eat him. The boy held onto her and cried, explaining everything, and showed her the hair from his lower body as proof. She then stopped. The boy then asked the bear mother to come out of the mountain, but she refused. The boy pleaded with her for several days, and she finally agreed, but she would not come unless the boy carried her back. Before he finished speaking, the bear rushed toward Qin. Qin knelt to apologize, and the boy also wept and begged for forgiveness, and the bear then glared at them and stopped. Qin stood up and called his wife out, and the bear bowed in response. At that time, Zhong Yue was only twelve years old.

During the second year of the Tian Shun era, Bo Lae attacked Shenmu. Zhong Yue gathered local militia to defend against him at Dingbian Camp, and his forces were invincible, pursuing Bo Lae to the Hetao region and capturing him. A high-ranking military commander reported his achievements, and Zhong Yue was appointed as the commander of Yulin. During the Hongzhi era, Huo Shuai attacked the frontier, and Zhong Yue decisively defeated him, slaying Huo Shuai. He was then promoted to Left Grand Marshal, a hereditary position. When a general pardon was granted, Zhong Yue also requested an imperial decree for Xiong Mu, the mother of Xiong Tai Tai. The emperor, recognizing her noble birth, issued the decree. When the decree arrived, Xiong Mu was brought out, dressed in official attire, and thanked the emperor according to protocol, behaving like any other person except that she could not kneel or speak. Later, the empress dowager heard of the matter and visited Xiong Mu, bestowing upon her the title of Xiong Tai Jun. From then on, she was known as Xiong Tai Tai. The external historian remarked: "Xiong Tai Tai," I once heard about her from a friend, finding it a novel story, so I have recorded and shared it. Some say this story has already appeared in *Zi Bu Yu* (Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio). If this version does not differ significantly, it can be omitted. *Eight Horizons Translation History* also mentions: The country of orangutans is located in the vast ocean. During the Jiajing era, a merchant named Fu Yu from Wuling encountered a storm at sea, and his ship sank. He and his companions drifted to a desolate shore, starving, and ate wild fruits. Soon, creatures resembling humans with hair and covered in leaves approached, all greeting the humans with joy. They were taken to a cave, and later, one female became the wife of Fu Yu and bore him a son. Eventually, Fu Yu returned home. As the son grew up, he often sold tea in the market and was nicknamed "Xingxing Ba Lang" (Orangutan Eight). This story is worth noting, so I have included it here.

In the first examination of the Zhejiang provincial examination in the Ximao year of Daoguang, the candidate from Jiaxing, Li Rong, with the number 17 in the Tao room, had already completed his three essays by the time the lamps were lit. As he picked up the lamp to recite his work, he was in the midst of a creative fervor. Suddenly, a cold wind blew in, dimming the light to the faint glow of a bean. A young woman, lightly dressed and with her sleeves hanging down, unhooked the curtain and entered. She looked intently at Li Rong and said, "I have been looking for you for a hundred years!" Li Rong, startled, let out a loud cry, "Li Qing, spare me!" Then he began to dance and toss dust in the air, speaking incoherently but with a gentle and tender tone, which upon close listening seemed to be in the accent of the Central Plains. By dawn, the military supervisor went to inform the examiner. When the examiner arrived, he saw Li Rong making gestures of fighting, with his fingertips red as if smeared with cinnabar. Li Rong then burst into laughter and said, "What can you do to me?" The examiner took Li Rong's scroll and looked at it, sighing with regret. He then called two attendants to escort Li Rong out. As soon as Li Rong came out of the main gate, he went straight to the crowd, grabbed a pair of glasses, tore them apart, and threw them away, clapping and laughing loudly, "All right, all right!" The people asked and found out that Li Rong was mad, so they did not argue with him. However, Li Rong seemed quite lucid, greeting those who were taking the exam in the same room as usual.

Returning to his lodging, the crowd gathered around him to inquire about what had happened. The man said, "At first, I saw the woman enter, but I was completely unfamiliar with her. Then I no longer paid attention to my thoughts, but only saw the woman teaching me to raise my dagger and stab myself, and also teaching me to untie my belt and hang myself. Both these actions were taken from me by my ancestor, who told me, 'This case is far from settled. Remember that tomorrow at the hour of the tiger, Guan Sheng will be offering incense here. At that time, you must go out and cry for help, and there may yet be salvation.'" The next day, as dawn broke, I suddenly heard delicate and bright music from the air, accompanied by the sounds of a temple. Looking far off, I indeed saw clouds of incense surrounding the chariot of the Emperor God, which slowly approached. I immediately went out, prostrated myself on the ground, and mournfully prayed. The Emperor God turned his head to the left and ordered the examination of the old case. A clerk holding a tablet, resembling the judge officials carved in temples, took a yellow register from a chest, and after examining it at length, knelt and reported, "This matter dates back three lifetimes ago." The Emperor God took the register and read it thoroughly, then ordered the retrieval of the two books of virtue and vice. He said to the man, "This matter will be handled justly by the other party, and the deep injustice is indeed pitiable. I myself have no further grounds for inquiry. However, since the matter occurred in a previous life, and you have committed no sins in this life, whenever you encounter the spring and autumn sacrifices, you must be sincere and reverent. This single thought alone can serve as an example to encourage filial piety, and it is enough to preserve your life." He then took a red brush and had me hold out my hand, painting my fingertips with it. He instructed, "When you return to your lodging, you may use this to wave and send them away. However, their anger has not yet been vented, so you must remember, when you leave the hall, to reach the main gate and immediately take a mirror from someone, tear it open, and throw it away, and you may thus avoid misfortune." After giving these instructions, he ordered his chariot to depart.

I returned home, and upon seeing her, she appeared disheveled and panicked, frantically searching for something while calling out. She caught sight of me, her eyebrows raised in anger, and rushed forward to grapple with me. I blocked her with my hands, and she hesitated, stepping back, and gnashed her teeth, saying, "Faithless man! You still rely on this supernatural power, would I leave you because of this?" She left with deep resentment. I was so happy that I jumped for joy, running to the main gate, where I saw dozens of people with green faces, fierce expressions, long hair, and holding weapons, arranged in two rows, lifting swords and stabbing wildly. I quickly took out my glasses and threw them in all directions, and with a thunderous sound, all the demons disappeared. I then went to buy sacrificial animals and fabrics, performed rituals at the altar, and returned home. From then on, I did not go to the examination hall again.

Pan's Biography Pan Yu Yu, from the Lake District, was known as Mei An. He was orphaned at a young age and grew up in poverty. At twenty, he entered the local school but had not yet married. Despite his humble beginnings, he was diligent in his studies and possessed a handsome appearance. Women who saw him in the inner chambers all vied to like him. He served as a tutor for the Liu family in Suzhou, and behind his study was an old garden. One day, after a spring rain had just cleared, Pan grew tired of reading and called a servant to open the back door. He stepped into the garden, where the waters and mountains were layered, and the buildings were deep and secluded. After turning several times, he saw a stretch to the northeast with red railings curving and intertwining, beyond which apricot blossoms were in full bloom, their petals stretching far and wide like snow. Below was a pond, and on a bridge stood a pavilion that looked like a bird's wing. Pan intended to rest there but suddenly heard the sound of raindrops on the eaves. Looking up, he saw a tower emerging among the treetops, its structure majestic and overlooking everything. As he wandered, he heard a voice reciting poetry from inside the tower. Listening closely, the words were, "Even if we meet again in our next life, I fear we may not recognize each other." The tone was filled with sorrow, almost unbearable to listen to. Without thinking, Pan let out a long sigh. Soon, the wind blew, and the curtain opened. A woman leaned against the railing, looking intently, her beauty unparalleled, yet her brows were furrowed like distant mountains, and her eyes glistened with tears. She looked just like a pear blossom soaked in rain. Pan was instantly captivated, then suddenly realized, "Is this not Su's Lady Lan? How did she come here?" The woman nodded and said, "Oh, yes." She then descended and invited Pan inside.

After the greetings, she recounted her wandering life. She originally lived in Su Family Lane of the prefecture city, being the younger sister of the protagonist's sister-in-law, named Jinglan. After marriage, she followed her husband as a guest official in the eastern mountains. Last year, her husband died of illness, and she then brought his coffin back home. Since childhood, she had been quite close to the protagonist, and they had been separated for six years. The protagonist asked if her elder sister's family still had anyone, and the woman replied, "There is an uncle, who last year took his family to the capital city, and I recently heard that he has also passed away. At home, there is only an elderly aunt from Changzhou Wei family, and the family name is also small, so we have rented a place to live here." After saying this, tears and snot mingled on her face. The protagonist then moved closer and wiped away her tears. The woman angrily said, "Just after meeting, why don't you show any compassion with a word, and treat me so lightly!" The protagonist stood up to apologize, and the woman began to laugh happily, asking slowly if her elder sister was well and when her brother would come here. The protagonist narrated in detail his recent situation, and said, "Your lord still does not have a wife, where will your elder sister put this?" The woman remained silent for a long time. The woman's servant brought a cup of tea, and after sipping it, the setting sun was already on the curtain hook. The protagonist stood up, and the woman saw him off at the door, whispering, "After this, in your free time from studies, do not hesitate to visit." The protagonist agreed, and they parted ways dispiritedly.

That evening, the girl lay down to bed, tossing and turning without sleep. When the crescent moon had risen, she fell into a drowsy slumber. In her dream, the man came, approached the bed with tenderness, and the girl no longer restrained herself; they became intimately entwined. Upon waking, she found her embroidered trousers still damp. After dawn, she reluctantly rose to tidy her appearance, sat alone with her chin resting in her hand, and found it almost unbearable to bear. Suddenly, the maid reported that the man had arrived. The woman went out to greet him, smiling and saying, "Brother, you may truly be said to possess the faithfulness of Wei Sheng." He replied, "To behold your lovely form, I would not hesitate to die; what I regret is that Wenjun has not yet agreed to be with me." The girl could not help blushing on her cheeks, her face flushed like a red peach. Overcome with emotion, the man seized her hand, but she withdrew it, saying, "Sir, do not be so bold! What if the servants come upon us? What then?" He persisted in his teasing, and she finally asked him to wait until nightfall. Only then did he release her hand and return.

The water clock had stopped, and the student secretly opened the back door and left. He arrived at the place where the girl was, and found that the courtyard gate was half open. Inside, the golden lamp glowed brightly, and he saw the girl sitting lost in thought with books spread out before her. He entered, and she glanced up, surprised but pleased, and stood up. The student walked forward and embraced her, but she frowned and said, "A person with a short life or bad fortune, like a willow flower that follows the water, drifts aimlessly without support. When I saw your appearance and demeanor, I knew you were not someone who would stay in a humble place for long. If you truly care for me, I wish to promise to grow old together, but I must wait until my mother passes away before we can discuss it. If you start by deceiving me and end by abandoning me, then I will not be like a willow flower that follows the water and is easily discarded." The student heard this and took her to the moonlit courtyard, where they swore their pledge of eternal love. After the oath, the girl urged him to get up, but he remained kneeling and said, "Since you have agreed to me, these few days of separation feel like years. If we must wait until your mother passes away, I fear I will die of thirst first!" She pulled him up and said, "Crazy boy, why are you so eager?" They went to bed together, and their intimacy was even more intense than before. As soon as the rooster crowed, she got up and said, "From now on, I belong to you. Do not abandon me like an autumn fan!" The student said, "How could there be a heartless man like Pan An?" They parted with solemn promises. From then on, they met frequently, and their meetings were never in vain.

However, the protagonist was often worried about poverty, and this year he failed the imperial examinations again. His wife tried every means to comfort him, even going so far as to pull out her hairpins and search through her boxes, never showing any sign of fatigue. Later, when he was about to take the examination again, he was once again concerned about having no funds for the journey. His wife, knowing this, made great efforts to search for money to support his trip. As he was about to depart, the protagonist went to bid farewell. In the middle of the night, the wife got up first, took the protagonist's clothes, and added more cotton to them. The protagonist lay watching her, murmuring softly, saying, "With the intention of adding more thread, with affection, adding more cotton." The wife looked at him for a long time, her eyes filled with sadness as she wept. (During the reign of Emperor Xizong of Tang, palace women were often tasked with making battle robes to be bestowed upon the soldiers. One border general received a robe, and inside it was a poem that read, "With the intention of adding more thread, with affection, adding more cotton. This life is over, I wish to form a bond in future lives." The border general immediately presented it to the emperor, who asked, "Who in the palace wrote this poem?" A palace girl fell to the ground, asking to die. The emperor laughed and said, "I will settle this life's fate for you." He then gave this girl to the border general.) The protagonist regretted his slip of the tongue and quickly got up, embracing his wife, trying his best to comfort her, and then stopped. The next day, he departed.

Upon the announcement of the examination results, the successful candidates were listed, and at that time, the woman's mother-in-law had already passed away. Hearing the news of success, she secretly rejoiced, hoping for a happy reunion, and eagerly awaited his arrival. After a long while, she learned that he had already married a woman from a certain family in the prefecture, and she did not believe it. The following spring, he traveled to Suzhou with his fellow examinees, bid farewell to his host, but his footsteps remained elusive. From that point on, she lost all hope, and after half a year, she fell into depression and fell ill, eventually passing away. On her deathbed, she cried out twice, "This grudge must be avenged!" She was only twenty-three years old. Later, he passed the imperial examinations and was selected as a junior official. The following year, he sent someone to Lake to fetch his family. Upon inquiring of his servant, he learned that "红兰" had long since decayed, and he sighed in response. However, from then on, he was often distracted and unhappy. One night, while he was drunk and sleeping soundly, he suddenly saw the woman with disheveled hair, holding a knife, her expression grim. She rushed in from the courtyard, raised the blade, and stabbed straight at his chest. He was in extreme pain, cried out, and woke up. His family members were all startled awake and looked at him. He held his chest with his hand, turned restlessly, and groaned without ceasing. The family members were about to summon a doctor, but he refused, explaining the cause of his nightmare. He said, "My illness cannot be cured." He ordered them to prepare for his afterlife affairs. The next day, as he was on the verge of death, he composed a quatrain orally: "Only did I seek the truth of a good dream, who would have thought it would turn into the cause of a nightmare. To realize it's too late to turn back, it's best to leave the mirror of sin for others."

A Relative of Mine Told Me This Story After Leaving the Capital. He Also Said that When the Student Had Not Yet Passed the Imperial Examinations, His Family Was Extremely Poor. Every Night, When Reading, the Lamp Oil Ran Out, and He Often Sat Alone in the Room, Silently Reciting Various Scriptures Until Midnight Without Stopping. One Night, as Severe Cold Set In and Nearing Midnight, He Heard a Rustling Sound Outside the Window. At That Time, the Moonlight Was Faintly Bright, and He Secretly Got Up to Peek. He Saw a Person with Long Hair, Curly Beard, and a Dark Complexion, Like the Portrayal of Xiang Yu (楚霸王) in the Play *Qian Jin Ji* (The Play of a Thousand Gold Pieces). The Student Held His Breath and Stood Silently, Waiting to See What He Would Do. The Person Then Took Out an Object from His Waist, Sharp and Long Like an Awl, and Inserted It into the Window Frame, Pry

Zhou Kui In the 17th year of the Chongzhen era, Li Zicheng forced his way to the capital city. The emperor sent the eunuch Xu Cheng secretly to instruct Zhou Kui, the father-in-law of the empress, to encourage the nobles and relatives to contribute to the military fund. Kui stubbornly refused to contribute anything. Xu Cheng sighed and said, "With a father-in-law like this, the state of affairs can be known!" Kui was forced to contribute only 10,000 gold pieces and begged the empress to help. When Li Zicheng entered, Kui surrendered the crown prince to him. He plundered Kui's household and obtained 520,000 gold pieces. Later, when Li Zicheng retreated from Shanhaiguan after being defeated, the Qing army pursued him, and Kui surrendered again to the Qing. Li Zicheng fled with his baggage, and the capital fell into chaos. Kui's family took advantage of the situation to plunder his household, almost completely stripping it of its wealth. Later, they asked, saying, "You are a noble relative. We have always been well-treated by you. Now that we have acted in this manner, how can we face you again?" They cut off his head and left. (According to *Jishi Benmo*: When the rebels broke into the capital, they plundered Kui's household and obtained 520,000 gold pieces, as well as tens of thousands of other treasures.) 义犬冢 In Jianshun Village in Wujiang, there was a peasant woman named Zhao. Her home was in Dongcun, which was only about three miles from Jianshun Village. The woman was filial to her mother. One early winter, she accidentally caught several sparrows, which tasted delicious, so she kept the rest. The next morning, she washed and combed her hair, changed into a dress, and took the sparrows to visit her mother. Her mother happened to be ill, and she took out the sparrows to feed her. Her mother ate more as a result. After lunch, she called her mother and talked with her in detail. Before they knew it, it was dusk. The woman, thinking that it was time for the harvest, returned home.

Upon reaching the outskirts of the village, the sky gradually grew dark. Looking far into the woods, a faint light shone out. Hurriedly approaching, several beggars were seen sitting around the temple gate, cooking their evening meal. The woman told them she was lost and asked for fire. One beggar said, "It's dusk, and you're here, not to meet a little monk secretly?" Another beggar laughed and said, "Fire is easy to get, but we need your water in exchange." Then he stood up, intending to force her into sexual acts. The woman angrily slapped his cheek and shouted, "Murder!" The beggar, enraged, took a clod of earth and stuffed it in her mouth, called the other beggars, stripped her of her clothes, and strangled her with a cloth from her waist. They carried her into an empty grave, removed the cloth, covered her with tiles and stones, and scattered away. Midnight came, and the woman suddenly awoke, opening her eyes and looking around. She saw the slanted moon not yet set, the fields covered in thick frost, and the cold wind cutting through her bones. Shivering with the cold, she could not immediately rise but called out, "Help!" Just then, a butcher from her neighborhood passed by the village entrance, heard her cries, and ran at the sound, intending to pull her out. The woman refused, saying, "I am naked, and no matter if I am frozen stiff, how can I face people?" The butcher understood and quickly ran to tell her husband, who fetched her clothes, led her back to the place, and then left. By then, it was dawn, and the woman emerged, crying and telling her story, and they went home together. The villagers reported the incident to the county magistrate, who ordered a strict search but found no leads. A few days later, they suddenly heard that the previous butcher had been bound with stones and drowned in the river, and the villagers rushed to see, and indeed, it was true.

Before this, the butcher would go early in the morning to the village market to slaughter sheep, and there was always a black dog following him. On that day, the shop owner got up early to wait for him, but the butcher did not come. Suddenly, the dog ran wildly into the shop, bit his clothes, and made a crying sound. The shop owner scolded it but it would not leave, and he was moved by this. He followed the dog out of the shop. After walking a short distance, they reached the riverbank behind a temple, where the dog stopped. The dog had already jumped into the water, and soon pulled out a body, which it dragged onto the shore. Upon closer inspection, it was the butcher, with his hands tied and stones attached to his face. The shop owner was utterly shocked and ran to tell his son, who came with him to the river. The dog was also curled up dead beside the body. The son then wept and asked the shop owner to return with him. They went to report the incident to the authorities, who caught and executed the beggar. They ordered the dog to be buried beside the tomb and erected a stone tablet to commemorate it, inscribed with "义大冢." Qi Ziye, also known as Jianzhao, was a scholar from Guian. His family lived on the banks of a canal in the county, and he entered the county school at the age of fourteen. He had a handsome appearance but a frivolous nature. His family was originally wealthy, but he would turn away as if he did not recognize his relatives when they were in need. He would only spend lavishly in places frequented by women, and as a result, his family's wealth gradually declined. By the time he was in his thirties, he had no children. Later, he contracted a wasting disease, and his wife advised him to change his ways. He thought she was jealous and abandoned her.

Afterward, he arrived at the front of the guard's residence in Ma Jun Alley, where two people were fighting on the road, and he stood back to wait. Glancing, he saw a young woman inside the door, whose grace was unparalleled, and she occasionally showed half her face to look at him. Gradually, they made eye contact and fell in love. He saw that only an old woman followed her, and he hurriedly stepped forward and bowed, asking the old woman to deliver a message from his widowed aunt and asking when she would return home. The woman blushed and bowed, pretended to agree, and invited him in and offered him a seat. She asked if his aunt was well. Then, she sent the old woman to fetch some drink. When the old woman left, he suddenly stood up and pulled her clothes to seduce her. She struggled to free herself, and she took him into a room on the left side of the hall. There was a bed in the middle, covered with a green silk canopy. The bedding was fragrant and soft. The beauty and luxury were beyond what he had ever seen. Afterward, she helped him remove his robe. As they embraced, her skin fragrance wafted out. She was very aroused, moving restlessly, and they were extremely satisfied, more so than usual. He was exhausted. She then put her arm under his head and told him to rest briefly, but they still embraced. After a while, she suddenly woke up and said, "The old woman is coming, please rest. I will come back soon." She then left. He felt his lower abdomen swelling, almost unable to support it. He also stood up and found a pair of slippers by the pillow. They were small and delicate, almost like water chestnuts. He put them in his sleeve and left, asking the woman to say goodbye. She tried to hold him back but couldn't, and tears welled up in her eyes. He promised to come back that night and left sadly. After he returned, his semen continued to flow. The next day, he fell seriously ill and soon died.

Previously, when he was alive, he always carried a single box with him, which was locked very tightly, and even his wife and concubines could not secretly peek inside. After his death, there was nothing to use for his funeral, so his family members entered his room to check if the box contained any valuables. They thought it must have some savings inside and hurried to open it. They found that the box was full of women's shoes. Some were so small they barely covered a finger, others were large like lotus boats, and they came in various colors—red, green, black, and white—yet there were no matching pairs. The only other item in the box was a single pair of embroidered slippers. The family members were greatly disappointed and buried him in a makeshift grave outside the southern gate before dispersing. Some said there was a beautiful ghost in the palace. In the past, a woman had once agreed to marry someone, but the arrangement did not come to fruition, and she hanged herself. The experiences of the protagonist were likely influenced by this ghost.

In the western part of our county, there is a temple called Fengdeng. The monk there is named Keshi, who identifies himself with monastic rules. A woman from a neighboring village had been having an affair with the monk. During the Spring Festival, the woman, dressed elaborately, went to the temple to burn incense. The monk led her into his room and engaged in intimate behavior, and they fell asleep together. Just then, the village head came to inquire about matters at the temple. A young novice monk entered and found the monk nowhere to be seen, only a pair of embroidered shoes on the bed and the monk's sandals on the ground. The novice monk approached, lifted the curtain, and called out to the monk. The monk woke up suddenly, saw the novice monk, and became furious. He then got up and seized the novice monk. The novice monk wept and pleaded his innocence, explaining that he had mistakenly entered the room. The monk became increasingly surprised and turned to the woman, saying, "Guard him well, do not let him leave." He then left. After a short while, he returned, tied up the novice monk, stuffed his mouth with cotton, and beat him to death. That night, the monk kept the woman at the temple. After everyone had gone to sleep, they carried the body out, opened the back door, and threw it into an abandoned well, covering it with broken tiles and stones. The next day, the monk accused the novice monk of being lured away by his mother's family and stealing something, and reported the case to the authorities. The official accepted bribes from the monk, arrested the novice monk's father, subjected him to torture, and ordered him to hand over the novice monk. However, the father was truly unable to find him. The case remained unresolved for over two months.

When the plum rain had just cleared, several children were playing the game of collecting herbs behind the temple. Suddenly, they saw a small snake winding its way up the well. They all chased it. The snake entered the well, and one child hurried to look inside, but his cap fell into the well. The child immediately took a young bamboo pole to retrieve it, but the cap had already sunk. He tried again, and then a foot stuck out of the water. Soon, the corpse floated to the surface. Terrified, he threw the bamboo pole and ran to tell his father. The father then called the neighbors and went together to pull the body out. The body was still not decomposed, and the wounds were clearly visible, like carvings, while the face was clearly recognizable. They reported the incident to the authorities. The county magistrate arrived and examined the body, which was determined to have died from being beaten with a stick. He asked the monk if there was a previous case filed at the Supreme Police Office. The magistrate then ordered the officials to fetch the case file, and after much deliberation, he called two officials to search the monk's room, but found nothing. Eventually, they searched a room behind the Buddha altar, where the bed, quilt, and curtains were extremely luxurious, but there were no other objects. Only a braid was found in a drawer, and the magistrate asked the monk why this place was set up and what the braid was used for. The monk did not know. The magistrate said, "Then do you not know who killed the person?" The monk again said he did not know. The magistrate laughed dryly and said, "You may not know, but the murderer is here." He then used torture on the monk, who died and then revived, but still refused to confess. The magistrate became angry and ordered another round of torture. Suddenly, he noticed a young woman in the crowd, weeping silently.

Hastily summoned to the table, the judge interrogated her, saying, "What kind of place is this, and yet you come here to shed tears!" She replied, "I am originally from my teacher's neighbor's house, and seeing him unable to withstand torture, I couldn't help but feel pitifully." The judge asked, "Then how do you view the monk's torture of his disciple? At that time, how could you stand by and watch him die?" The woman exclaimed in shock, "This matter has nothing to do with me." The judge flew into a rage and ordered her to be tied up. The monk, seeing her twisting and crying pitifully, was heartbroken as if cut, and then confessed the reason for killing his disciple, saying, "The matter was due to adultery, but when he was about to die, this woman was actually not present. It is enough for me to bear the suffering of the knife mountain and sword trees alone." The judge laughed and said, "Today, you can be said to have shown great mercy." Consequently, he arrested the woman along with the monk, and the case was settled. The monk was beheaded in the marketplace, and the woman was sentenced to await execution by hanging, dying of illness in prison after more than a year. It is said that during the execution, it took seven strokes of the axe for the monk's head to fall.

The Historian says: When I was young, I saw this monk who abstained from meat and alcohol, and his demeanor was modest and respectful. Therefore, he was known throughout the village as a monk of high virtue. I also heard that he had affairs with more than one woman. When he saw the burial of a novice, he planned to leave immediately. He had already reached Tonghe Bridge when he suddenly saw the bridge suspended in the air, towering dozens of feet high. He went back and forth several times, and it was always the same. He wandered aimlessly until dawn, realizing he could not escape, so he decided to accuse his father. This bridge was only a short distance from his temple and was the only way in and out. Alas! Does the monk know that one can be deceived with trickery, officials can be bribed, but ghosts and spirits are difficult to deceive? What about the one with long hair who has concealed something? Yet isn't this also fate? As for the two young men from Songjiang, one was the grandson of a high-ranking official who was poor and unable to support himself, while the other was his former servant who was wealthy. The grandson went to beg for mercy. Just as they were threshing rice, he ordered the servant to carry five dou of rice with him. The servant could not manage it and stopped to rest in the street. The grandson asked him, "Why are you so weak?" The servant sighed and said, "I am not just a laborer; my ancestors were scholars." The grandson was surprised and said, "If so, then we are relatives!" But both men could not carry the rice, so they embraced each other and wept, saying, "Alas, our parents, who bore us with such toil!" The crowd gathered to watch. An elderly man gave them a bamboo pole, and together they lifted the rice and returned home. Both men's ancestors were high officials during the reign of the Chongzhen Emperor. At the time, people said, "Five dou of rice, two young men, unable to carry it, having read the *Book of Poetry* in vain and lamenting the toil of our parents, could our ancestors have foreseen this?"

Wuxi Elder During the New Year's Eve, an elderly man from Wuxi was burgled when thieves broke through the wall into his room. The elder got up and caught one of the thieves, who turned out to be the son of an old friend. The elder made no fuss and whispered to him, saying, "Why have you come to this, nephew? Your father and I were quite close. I guess you are in dire straits and had no choice but to do this." He gave him a hundred coins to help him get through the year and several hundred more as capital. The man was ashamed and could not return to his hometown. The elder moved him to another place, where he became quite successful. Several years later, the man bought a boat to visit the elder, arriving at the elder's door at midnight. He saw a man hanging on the door and called the boatmen to lift him onto the boat, then threw him into the river and returned. A year later, he visited the elder again and told him about the previous incident. The elder said, "I owe you a lot for your help. The one who died earlier had been causing trouble with the children during the day. If it weren't for you, I might not have been able to see you now." This elder's intention was on par with the intentions of ancient sages who treated thieves kindly, and he deserves such a generous and kind reward.

The following are two stories that I have found in historical records. Wealthy and privileged young people should read them, as they are sufficient to serve as a warning. The old man's deep intention is especially worthy of emulation, and it is not necessary to discuss his rewards. In my hometown, there was a man surnamed Dai who lost all his wealth through gambling. His home was always empty of valuable possessions. One evening, as he was about to return home, he had no oil to light the lamp. He checked his pocket and found only three coins left. He decided to stop and went to bed wearing his clothes, thinking that he still had no way to provide for tomorrow's breakfast. Unable to sleep, he tossed and turned. Suddenly, he heard a rustling sound. A thief dug a hole in the wall and entered. Dai secretly watched what the thief was doing. The thief took out some fire paper from his pocket, blew on it slightly, and the firelight spread around. He searched the entire room but found nothing to take. After a long time, he sighed slightly and left. Dai quickly took out the three coins from his pocket and gave them to the thief, saying, "I regret that my poverty has reached this point, causing you to leave disappointed. This kind of situation, only you and I know. With this small gesture of respect, I only ask that after you return, you kindly overlook this and do not reveal my shame." Compared to the old man, one is serious and the other humorous, both worth passing down. (This story can also be told alongside Xu Wenchang's story of calling a thief and giving him a silver cup.)

From the *Book of Sui: Biographies of Hermits*, it is recorded: Li Shiqian of Zhao Commandery was known for his filial piety in serving his mother. Once, when a thief stole from his fields, Li Shiqian looked at him and avoided confrontation. A servant once caught a thief stealing grain, and Li Shiqian explained to him, saying, "This was caused by poverty and hardship." He quickly ordered the thief to be released. From the *Tanzuan of the Duke*, it is recorded: Yu Zhongliang, a judge, once returned home from outside to find a thief stealing a tin lampstand in front of his house. Yu Zhongliang avoided him, waiting until the thief took it out and then entered. Later, when his family reported the loss of an item, Yu Zhongliang said, "This item has been unusable for a long time, and I have already exchanged it with a tin worker." On another day, when he was entertaining guests, one of the guests, who was poor, secretly took a silver cup. His wife, seeing this from behind a screen, told Yu Zhongliang, who laughed and said, "Last night I already discarded one of the wine vessels; how could you have mistaken it?"

In the *Book of Sui*, it is recounted that Shi Qian's acts of kindness and generosity were too numerous to count. Some believed he possessed hidden virtues, but Shi Qian said, "Hidden virtues are like ear ringing—only the individual can hear them, and others are unaware. As for what I have done, my children all know about it. How can there be any hidden virtues?" This is the depth of virtue in the ancients, who did not consider themselves as having hidden virtues. *Ghost Captures Thief* For several years, in the area northeast of the town, stretching for several dozen miles, many coffins were stolen. Some people reported this to the authorities, but often they suppressed the information and did not report it, which allowed the thieves to become more brazen. I was in the village of Zhu, and in a neighboring village, two men broke into a coffin together. The body was that of a young woman, and her family was usually wealthy. There were hundreds of gold pieces attached to her body, and the two men were overjoyed. One thief inserted his right foot into the coffin, stepped between the thighs of the body, and took almost everything. Then, he tried to remove her inner clothing, but suddenly felt his right foot trapped like a bundle, and he could not pull it out. The other thief tried to save him but failed, so he grabbed the clothes and fled, while this thief remained stuck in the coffin. By morning, the villagers who saw it quickly caught and tied him up, and his foot also came loose. They then handed him over to the authorities. The magistrate came to inspect the scene, interrogated them, and captured one of his accomplices. Both were sentenced to death. Some say that hearing of this will serve as a warning. However, the spirit of ghosts is temporary, while the lack of spirit in people is common. Is there any end to their misfortune?

A man named Jia, who had always been fond of gambling, had already squandered his entire family's wealth. After his wife fell ill and was near death, she told Jia, "My illness has reached this point, and if something unexpected happens, I will have nothing to cover myself. What shall I do?" Jia replied, "Now that our food supply is often running out, and there is no way to borrow money, can you not see that my clothes are as tattered as a magpie with many patches? How can I take care of you?" Without a second thought, he left. His wife died after a great cry. Her family heard about it and arrived with a few items of women's clothing, buying a coffin and preparing her body for burial. Jia returned home and was pleased to see the preparations. Together, they carried the coffin to the burial place. The next day, he went out to gamble again and lost all his money. Ashamed and angry, he returned home, unable to come up with a plan. As he approached the village, the moon was barely up, and he did not dare to enter his house. Instead, he went directly to the burial place and secretly opened the coffin. His wife suddenly sat up, shocking him so much that he ran back. Soon, he heard the sound of wooden shoes growing closer. He turned around and saw his wife walking slowly towards him, only about ten or so steps away. He ran home as fast as he could and quickly locked the door. Then he heard a loud knocking on the door. Peering out, he saw his wife standing outside, her hair disheveled and eyes filled with anger. Jia shivered in fear and dared not stop. Soon, the roosters crowed, and the east gradually became light. He heard a sound outside, like a wall of earth collapsing. Peering out again, he saw that his wife had disappeared without a trace. He opened the door and went out, seeing her body lying face up on the ground, stiff and motionless. He laughed at the body and said, "Even today, you can still chase your husband?" He then dragged her body back to the burial place, completely stripped her of everything she was wearing, placed her body in the coffin, and fled.

During the Jiaqing era, there was a wealthy man named Jin in a town. He had a son named [unnamed]. After the old man died, his son became a gambler and a good-for-nothing. He first mortgaged his land and house, then sold his wife and daughter, but still could not make ends meet. One day, he destroyed his ancestral tomb, took the bricks and tiles, and sold them. Later, he opened the seven coffins, discarded the bodies of his parents, and sold their coffins. The whole family was furious and bound him and sent him to the county. The magistrate came to inspect the situation and interrogated him. Mr. Jin's son said, "The coffins in the tomb are all worth more than several dozen gold pieces. Grandfather and father had this wealth, but they did not leave it to their descendants, instead burying it with them. Isn't this cruel? Thanks to this, they have remained undecayed until now. Selling them can bring in a lot of money." The magistrate was very angry and ordered them to bury him in lime to dissolve him.

外史氏曰：Animals know their mothers but not their fathers, while the xiao and jing devour their parents from birth. That Jin family member is not even shamefacedly human? Yet he can be so cruel to his own kin, how much more so to others? Even if one were to cut his flesh to feed dogs and pigs, they would still refuse to eat him, so how could such a crime be concealed? Yet, instead of punishing him publicly as an example, they merely kill him to cover up the deed—surely there are those who will secretly observe and see through the deception? Consider the case of someone who, unable to bury his own wife after her death, profits from the situation, to the point that his anger reaches the spirits in the afterlife, his heart and courage shattered, yet he remains recklessly unaware of the warning. What need is there to deeply analyze such a person? Even his relatives are content to remain silent, allowing him to escape justice, let alone those in authority above? Yet from this, I fear that acts of sudden violence during the day may arise. The *Book of Changes* says, "If you see frost, you know that ice is coming; all things come gradually." The reason I have recorded these three matters is not merely out of concern for the underworld.

The following events are certainly enough to shock the senses. Since then, the area around Houzhucun has seen countless cases of desecration. Last winter, on the left side of Beishen, twenty-four coffins were robbed in one night, all at the intersection of Xiutong, a place unheard of in other counties. At that time, there were still occasional complaints to the authorities. Last spring, the coffin of Zhitang's sister-in-law was also desecrated. Zhitang complained to the county, but the officials could not catch the thieves, so he paid the litigation fees to settle the matter. Others might perform Buddhist rituals and bury the remains, somewhat to alleviate the ridicule. Now Zhitang has passed away, and half a month ago, the coffin of his sister-in-law was desecrated again. Twenty or more coffins were desecrated at the same time, and no one complained to the authorities anymore. Because it has become a habit, and they know it is of no use. Thus, it encourages theft; is there any reason for this? Recorded on the first day of the first month of the autumn season in the 22nd year of the Daoguang era.

Mr. Zhong, the Jinshi Qian Xielian from Pinghu was the son of a certain Zhongcheng Gong Zhen. He went to the capital for the selection process and arrived at Tongzhou. It was already evening, and all the lodgings were full. There were only three rooms in the back of the house, which were said to have been haunted by fox spirits, and no one dared to stay there. Qian wanted to take a look, but everyone thought it was not advisable. Qian smiled and said, "What harm could it do? I have always read 'The Story of Qingfeng,' and I often lamented not being able to meet such a person. If there really are fox spirits here, I will invite them to share my bed to pass the time of loneliness." He immediately ordered the rooms to be opened. The tables and beds were covered with dust, and two servants spent over an hour cleaning them, setting up the bedding. After lying down, he could not fall asleep. It was nearly midnight, and everything was silent. A slanted moon illuminated half the window, and he was lost in thought. Suddenly, he heard the sound of footsteps, light and delicate. Two women walked out from the northwest corner, hand in hand. One of the women said, "Last night, I went to see the moon at Lugou Bridge and played chess with my sister Yun. I lost two games in a row. We had agreed to play again tonight, but just now a little maid came and said, 'There is someone here, a refined and cultured young man. We cannot miss this opportunity.' So I invited my sister to come and take a look." As she spoke, she pointed to the bed, then approached and pulled back the curtain, laughing and scolding, "What kind of bookworm are you, daring to occupy someone's private quarters!" Qian looked up and saw that they were all beautiful women in their twenties. He sat up and said, "I admire your divine beauty and wish to briefly enjoy your fragrance, to spend a night of intimate and tender moments."

At the crow of the rooster, warning the dawn, it was a stranger, a wayfarer, what could be predicted? The older one, with a handkerchief, brushed her and said, "My sisters will come to bewitch you." The younger one said, "Sister stays here, younger sister goes away." The woman then laid herself in his embrace. Qian felt his heart move, quickly changing his mind, thinking that she who had the appearance of flowers and snow-white skin was an evil spirit. He quickly drew his dagger to stab her, but already empty with no one in it, he decided to move out, yet ashamed to be laughed at by everyone, he returned to the pillow. Exhausted, he fell into a drowsy sleep, suddenly feeling his entire body cold, he woke up in shock, the bedding and mattress were all soaked with water, the two women stood laughing outside the curtain. Qian, naked and jumped out, shouted loudly, "Evil fox, stop running!" The two servants also got up, but the two women had already fled, the bathtub remained there. Soon, everyone in the inn got up, the bathtub was one that the innkeeper had prepared to marry off a daughter, looking into the back room, one of them was already missing.

The sky gradually brightened, and Qian bundled his clothes and set off. Along the way, he met a martial arts candidate named Yang from the same county, who was going to take the exam in the capital. They spoke about their journey. Yang laughed and said, "This is due to the gentleman's timidity. If I were to go, I fear he will not be able to keep up with the pace." Riding a horse, Yang arrived and asked to stay in the building. The host said, "Have you not heard of what happened to a guest last night?" Yang replied, "I am here because I heard about what I heard." Knowing it could not be disputed, the host allowed it. After Yang lay down and leaned on his pillow waiting, after a while, he saw an old servant girl with messy hair and deformed ears, stumbling forward. Yang leaped up and asked what she was doing. The girl said, "My mistress Lotus heard that the gentleman is here, and accompanied by Seventh Sister, she fled to Cloud Sister's place to play chess. Just now, she hurriedly forgot to put on her sleeve, so she has sent me to retrieve it." Yang asked why they had fled, and the girl said, "I do not know. Mistress Lotus only said, 'By looking at your face, she suspects you are the reincarnation of Scholar Zhong, so she dares not approach you.'"

Yang was overjoyed and, the next day, boasted to the crowd, believing he would surely pass the imperial examinations. The crowd looked at his appearance: his eyes were like those of a tiger, his nose was raised, his twisted beard circled his cheeks, and his face was dark like a ghost, resembling the image of Zhong Kui depicted in the world. They hid their laughter and withdrew. But from then on, the fox in the tower never came again. My younger brother Wu Shoutuo's family once had a fox haunting them. Cabinets would open on their own, beds would move without cause, and drawers would suddenly catch fire. There was a jar containing dozens of packages of honey candy; after opening the jar to eat, the packaging remained intact, but the contents were all empty. Such things happened for half a year, and all attempts to drive it away were unsuccessful. So, they decided to perform a religious ritual, involving fasting, inviting monks from the Yun Chao monastery, and performing a three-day repentance ceremony. As soon as the monks left, the evil spirit became silent and had no further impact. The idea of repentance is indeed effective. However, it is not as direct and easy as Yang's method of driving away the fox.

Snake Residual My father once told me that when he was in Fuyang, he met a man who was tall and robust, but had lost all his facial hair, and his skin was cracked and resembled snake skin. He suspected that the man was insane, but the man himself said, "About half a month ago, I went to a friend's house for a night of drinking and returned home drunk. Stumbling along a mountain path, after a while, the slanting moon gradually set, and the village paths became indistinguishable. Suddenly, I lost my footing and felt as if I had fallen into a pit. Touching it, my hand felt hot, soft, and greasy, with a strong, foul odor that was unbearable. I thought I had been buried in a fish's belly, so I quickly drew my dagger and slashed. With just one swing, I was thrown and the world spun around me, dizzy and disoriented. Fortunately, after several slashes, the situation was resolved. I rushed out and stumbled back home. In the morning, I went to check, and found a giant python about ten zhang long, dead by the stream. There was a hole in its belly with visible knife marks. It was because I was too drunk and full that I was not poisoned, but the experience was as if I had been reborn." The man's surname was Qiu, his given name was Pin San, and he had passed the military examination in the Wuwu year. From then on, people called him "Snake Residual."

Gamble on Eating During the Qianlong period, Wu Baihua, a junior minister, was known for his exceptional appetite. There was also a general from the imperial clan who was equally renowned for his eating prowess. One day, Wu Baihua said to the general, "I have long admired the size of your stomach, which can hold more than one person. As for me, though I may not be well-read, I am quite eager to eat. But I wonder, who is better between us? I would like to compete with you to see who can eat more. What do you think?" The general laughed and agreed. Wu Baihua then instructed his attendants to hold tally sticks nearby, and for every bowl of rice he ate, he would give one tally stick. After finishing, they counted the tally sticks, and the general had eaten 32 bowls, while Wu Baihua had only eaten 24. Wu Baihua was不服 and suggested they compete again the next day. The general laughed and said, "A defeated general, dare I challenge again?" The next day, they set up a meal, but it consisted only of rice and no side dishes. Wu Baihua said to the general, "This is what is called 'white rice.' Yesterday, I considered meat eating as coarse, so I slightly conceded one tally. Today, we will compete with plain rice. If I lose again, I will willingly submit to your command." They then began eating and counting tally sticks. The general stopped after eating 20 bowls, while Wu Baihua ended up with 36 tally sticks. It turned out that Wu Baihua had eaten more because he had already eaten meat and was easily satisfied, while the general could not finish his meal without side dishes.

*The Records of the Grand Historian* records that when Lian Po met an envoy from the State of Zhao, he ate a full meal consisting of one *dou* of millet and ten *jin* of meat. Upon returning, the envoy reported that General Lian still had an excellent appetite. Indeed, how truly remarkable was his appetite! During the reign of Fu Jian of the Former Qin, there was Xia Mo, who served as Left Inspector, and Hu Mo Na, who served as Right Inspector, as well as the eunuch Shen Xiangjian, who served as Attendant-in-Charge. All three were eight *chi* tall, and all were strong and skilled in archery. At each meal, they consumed one *dan* of rice and thirty *jin* of meat. Compared to Lian Po, they were no less impressive than Zhuo Shuo compared to a dwarf. Prince鸾 of Nanyan was from Jinan; he was nine *chi* tall, with a waist as wide as ten *wei*. He could ride a horse without needing to grip the saddle or stirrups. Murong De, upon seeing him, was amazed by his towering figure and strength, and offered him food. Prince鸾 ate more than one *hu* of it. Murong De was astonished and said, "To consume so much food cannot be sustained by farming alone, and his talent and appearance are also extraordinary." He was then appointed as the head of Fengling. Upon taking office, Prince鸾 governed with clarity and order, earning great renown, and was later summoned to serve as the governor of Donglai. If those three men had met Prince鸾, they would have been like a minor sorcerer meeting a great one. According to *The Records of the Former Yan*, the three men were each thirteen *chi* tall, and the rest of the statements are the same as above. One *dan* of rice and thirty *jin* of meat—this was the total amount consumed by the three men together.

Zhu Xieyuan, courtesy name Maohe, was from Shan Yin, Zhejiang. In the twentieth year of the Wanli era, he passed the imperial examinations as a Jinshi. He served in various positions, eventually reaching the rank of Left Censor of Sichuan. In the early years of the Tianqi era, he was appointed as Minister of War for defeating Shu Chongming and An Bangyan, and also supervised the armies of Guizhou, Yunnan, and Guangxi. In the early years of the Chongzhen era, he served as the Governor of Guizhou, was bestowed the imperial sword, promoted to Grand Tutor, and received a hereditary appointment as Commander of the Jingwei Guard. In the fourth year, he was promoted to Grand Preceptor and Left Column General for his victory at Taohongba. In the sixth year, he was given an additional hereditary appointment as Deputy Commander of the Jingwei Guard. In the eleventh year of spring, he died in office. Zhu Xieyuan was eight chi tall, with a belly as large as ten times the circumference of an adult's arm, and his drinking and eating were equivalent to that of twenty people. When he first served in Shaanxi, he met an elderly man who taught him the arts of divination involving wind and weather, and geomancy, and before parting, told him, "Take good care of yourself; in the future, if there is trouble in the southwest, you will be responsible for it." Jiang Ping Kangmin, a remarkable man, predicted before the war began, "There will be trouble in Shu, and it will be Zhu Gong who will pacify it!" And indeed, that came to pass. Zhu Xieyuan was not ashamed of being compared to General Lian Po for his excellent appetite. However, when compared to three others, he had not reached half their strength, yet he achieved so much. The three others, who became attendants only because of their strength, could not feel ashamed compared to the general who eats well?

Near the governor of Zhejiang and Fujian provinces, Mr. Sun, whose given name was Erzun and courtesy name Pingshu, had been suffering from edema for many years. Recommended by Mr. Meng Bai, officials were dispatched to invite Doctor Meng to diagnose him. When Doctor Meng arrived, the governor's illness was beyond treatment. After the diagnosis and examination, Doctor Meng asked how much the governor had eaten recently. The attendant replied from the side, "At this time, his stomach qi is greatly weakened, and he can only eat seven or eight bowls per meal." Doctor Meng was surprised upon hearing this and said, "If he eats so much, how can it be said that his stomach qi has declined?" The attendant replied, "The father doesn't know, compared to what he usually eats, it is less than one-third of what he usually eats." He then mentioned that when the governor was not ill, he usually ate ten pig trotters, and other things similarly.

Snow Maiden In the late Ming dynasty, my village was often plagued by bandits. A villager named Snow Maiden, whose wife was a woman of unblemished virtue and quiet demeanor, was especially filial to her mother-in-law. One day, when her husband went out, a bandit broke into the mother-in-law's room. The mother-in-law, realizing she could not escape, took a knife and stabbed herself, splattering blood on the bandit's clothes. The bandit seized the knife, preventing her from dying, and ultimately raped her. Seeing the knife nearby, the mother-in-law suddenly grabbed it and struck the bandit, hitting him in the thigh. Enraged, the bandit stripped her naked and used the knife to stab her lower body, causing a fatal wound. Some imitated the example of the Li family, who submitted a surrender letter, and inscribed her door with "Li Family with a Hole in the Buttocks." Her family all considered it a shame and no longer mentioned it. Alas! How could they not think about it deeply! When a wife is robbed, she may later become like a flower that follows the water, defiled and dishonored. Snow Maiden, having already been defiled, this shame could not be washed away even with the water of the West River. If she had not cultivated noble virtues in her daily life, how could she have fought to the death without hesitation? She was unfortunately raped by the bandit. Yet, her resolve is pitiable. Those who wish to reform morals should still acknowledge and praise her, to be mentioned alongside the story of Fei Gong'e who killed a tiger. The authorities ignored her, the villagers laughed at her, and even her own family tried to hide it. The lack of understanding among people has led to such an inversion of right and wrong! Therefore, I acknowledge and praise her, to shame those who are like the women of Hejian.

The Virtuous Woman of Wu The Virtuous Woman of Wu was the child bride of the Li family from Lujiadou in Wucheng. Her husband, due to poverty, sought livelihood in Chu. The woman lived alone with her widowed mother-in-law. She endured bitter hardships, serving her mother-in-law as if she were her own mother, and was praised by the village. There was a rascal, a distant relative of her husband, who, seeing her mother-in-law leave, knew the woman was bathing and pushed open the door to enter, crouching and grabbing her foot. The woman, in a hurry and not knowing what to do, quickly covered herself in the water and cried out loudly. The rascal immediately put his hand over her mouth and held up white gold to show her. The woman suddenly opened her mouth and bit his arm, causing blood to flow into the basin. He again tried to bite him, but the rascal's wound was severe, and he had already fled. Soon after, the mother-in-law returned, and neighbors also gathered. The woman was very angry and frustrated, her heart agitated and unable to speak. After a long while, she tearfully described her situation and said, "As a woman, I have been so humiliated; even though the act was not completed, how can I live on!" She then threw herself into the river. Someone managed to save her. The community then gathered to encourage her, and she politely thanked them, her expression becoming more calm. The mother-in-law's shame and anger gradually subsided, and her vigilance relaxed slightly. Just past the second watch of the night, the woman ran out and drowned herself. By the time people knew, it was too late.

Alas! If the chaste woman had not died, it could not be said that she was unchaste, and yet she did die. How much more weight or honor would it have added to the moral teachings? Yet at that time, those in power were as deaf as if they had plugs in their ears. How could there be a desire for fewer lawsuits and clearer laws, and consider that more affairs are not better than fewer? A ruffian named Bao San, because of his strong and imposing physique, was called "Big Cannon." Recently, as a bandit leader, he was captured and imprisoned in the Suzhou prefectural jail. In Anji Mountain, there was a peasant woman from a village, in her early twenties. In early summer, she carried a basket into the mountains to pick tea. She had just given birth a month ago, and because the red skirt she wore was not suitable for kneeling, she took off her skirt and tied it to a tree. Suddenly, she looked up and saw her neighbor's son Shi Si carrying a basket coming too. The woman blushed and turned to kneel, not daring to look back. Shi Si suddenly grabbed her wrist and glanced at her, saying with a smile, "You are so weak, and yet you endure such suffering, truly I feel compassion for you. If you can follow me, please let me take on the labor for you." The woman screamed loudly, and Shi Si immediately covered her mouth with one hand, tied her to the tree, and raped her. She struggled in vain and allowed him to act shamelessly. Afterward, Shi Si took off her red skirt and left with it. The woman, filled with shame and anger, did not return home. She struggled to free herself and hanged herself with a belt.
