By evening, the husband arrived and found his wife hanged on a tree. Her red skirt had disappeared, and he knew she had been defiled. He hurried back home to file a complaint at the county office. On the way, a sudden strong wind blew up with sand, and dark clouds swirled chaotically, so he returned home. Just as he reached the door, there was a thunderclap, and in the flash of lightning, it seemed as if an eagle's talons had grabbed a person and thrown them from the sky onto the courtyard, with a red skirt covering the top, kneeling in the mud. When he looked closely, the person had a burnt head and face, with fine holes on the top of the head as if pricked by needles, and blood was still spurting out. On the person's waist, there was red writing that read "the criminal Shi Si," neither seal script nor clerical script. Thus, he knew the one who caused his wife's death was this person. Cheng Guangkui During the reign of Emperor Kangxi, the governor of Jiangsu Province, Zhang Berxing, submitted a report: after the list of successful candidates was posted this year in the Jiangnan literary examination, there was much public criticism. Hundreds of people carried a statue of the God of Wealth directly into the school, alleging that the examination was unfair. Subsequently, the chief examiner Zuo Bifen reported that the county magistrate Wu Rixin and Fang Ming, whom they had recommended, were not proficient in literature and reasoning. The emperor ordered the Grand Secretary Zhang Penghe to go to Yangzhou to investigate with the governor-general Gali and Zhang Berxing. It was then discovered that the deputy examiner Zhao Jin and Cheng Guangkui had colluded in the examination. The Ministry of Justice deliberated that Cheng Guangkui had concealed written materials in the examination hall and proposed to execute him. The county magistrate Fang Ming and Wu Qin, whom he had recommended, were to be executed by beheading or strangulation, depending on the case.

Previously, Cheng Guangkui's father, Cheng Weng, was a former wealthy merchant from ShanYang. He was greedy and mean. At forty years old, without a son, he went to Ganlu Temple to make offerings to the monks and pray for one. There was a scholar named Zhao Wenhui from Zhaoqing who was living in the temple and was skilled in accounting. Cheng Weng conversed with him and found him agreeable, so he brought him back home and employed him to manage the salt affairs for several years. Zhao Wenhui asked to deposit his thousand gold in Cheng Weng's custody, which Cheng Weng agreed to, intending to use it for his own benefit. Later, Cheng Weng had an affair with Zhao Wenhui's wife, and the servant reported this to Cheng Weng. Cheng Weng and the servant hatched a secret plan to send Zhao Wenhui on a mission to Shouzhou to transport salt. They waited until night and secretly returned, planning to kill him when he arrived. The servant agreed. The next day, after dusk, the servant arrived, took a knife and climbed over the wall, sneaking up to the house. He heard Zhao Wenhui's wife whispering, "Madman, you once said that our brief encounter was unsatisfying, but now we can enjoy a long night together." Shortly after, the sounds of lovemaking continued. The servant's anger flared, and he broke the door, entering the room with his knife raised. Zhao Wenhui woke up in shock, and the knife struck his wife's head. His wife was in extreme pain and held the knife with both hands, allowing Zhao Wenhui to escape. Soon, his wife died. After reporting to the authorities, Zhao Wenhui could not be found, and the servant was sentenced to strangulation.

At that time, the old man's concubine was pregnant with Guang Kui, but the pregnancy was overdue, and no birth occurred. One night, as they were just about to sleep, the concubine had a dream of a man who claimed his surname was Zhao, spoke with a Cantonese accent, and climbed onto the bed to sit on her abdomen. She woke up in shock, cried out in pain, and then went into labor, giving birth to a boy. The old man was greatly delighted. The concubine then told him about her dream, and he became even more delighted, considering it an extraordinary sign, and named the child Guang Kui. However, whenever the child saw the old man from the cradle, he would cry incessantly. As the child grew older and began his studies, he was extremely dull and slow-witted, yet the old man's expectations for him were very high. At the age of fifteen, the old man took him to Ganlu Temple to fulfill a vow. There, they encountered a fortune-teller, who was asked if the child could read and study to rise in society. After looking carefully for a long time, the fortune-teller said, "Your son's appearance is plump, but his eyebrows and eyes only show the aura of gold and silver, the color of wine and meat, and not a hint of scholarly aura. I fear he will not even be able to get a small piece of land. Moreover, there is a hidden sign of death in his destiny. Even if he manages to succeed through connections, it may not be a blessing for your family." The old man shouted angrily, "You dirty beggar from the rivers and lakes, dare you look down on my father-in-law! When my son becomes famous, I will come to gouge out your eyes!" He left in a huff. From then on, the old man became even more determined. Later, during the Ximao provincial examination, the old man had always been close to the chief examiner. He then offered him a hundred pounds of gold as a gift and asked him to intercede, arranging for Guang Kui to enter the examination hall under an exception and he eventually succeeded. The matter was exposed, and the old man was also sentenced to death for bribery, and his family's property was confiscated by the government.

**Zhu Tianji** Zhu Tianji, styled Zikai, was a scholar of the local academy in the Hu region. From a young age, he was alert and quick-witted; by seven he could compose poetry. As he grew older, he read extensively, with no bounds to his learning. He was handsome, and whenever women in the inner quarters saw him, they vied to throw fruits at him. Yet he remained upright and self-disciplined, paying no heed to their admiration. His parents, delighted by this, believed he would surely become a great talent, and so they gave him the style name “Daji” (Great Vessel). At fourteen, he entered the county academy, and the following year received the stipend of a successful examinee, his name becoming widely known. Yet his nature was naturally upright and forthright, and he was domineering in the literary world. His writing flowed like a spring, often outshining his peers. His boldness often invited jealousy, and he was on good terms with only Longmian Fang Gongquan. However, he frequently failed in the examination halls, and by now he had passed his prime. His second wife, Wu, was beautiful and virtuous, and they had one daughter. His livelihood grew increasingly strained, and he supported himself by teaching in private academies, though his efforts were often met with obstacles and conflict. His father often admonished him, saying: “With your actions, how can you resemble someone seeking official success? Do you not know of the weeping at Jingshan Mountain? Throughout history, there have been many people like Bian He. Why not break your principles a little to save yourself from poverty?” Zhu wept in reply: “The affairs of the world are easy to understand. Yet jade may be shattered, but its whiteness cannot be destroyed. If you wish to compromise and seek conformity, even if I were to starve to death, I would not do it. It is also something your father would not wish to see.” From then on, even though the smoke from the stove was often absent—signifying frequent lack of food—he remained calm and composed.

Shortly after, both his parents passed away. As his father lay dying, he called his son and said, "Though I was poor, I always believed that you could easily achieve high office, and I took comfort in that thought. Now I cannot wait to see it happen, but if you can pass the imperial examination one day, I will still find some comfort in the afterlife. Otherwise, there are still ghosts and spirits, and I will not be fed by them." The son wept bitterly and accepted his father's words. After the burial was completed, his wife died soon after. His daughter, who was fifteen years old, was then married to the son of a friend. He bid farewell to his relatives, prepared his belongings, and set out as a baigong student to take the Jingzhao examination, vowing not to return if he did not succeed. When the results were announced, he failed again. He went out for a walk and visited Tao Ran Pavilion alone. One day, after observing him for a long time, the person said, "I have seen many people, but your nose has a column-like bone, and your abdomen has the six壬, indicating that you can live to a very old age. From the hairline down, you have only noble qualities and no common ones. Your back alone suggests that you will have a remarkable encounter in the future. However, it will surely be far overseas, as this place may not be your destiny."

The person returned to his residence angrily, reflecting on the fact that there were very few close friends in the capital city, and it was not easy to stay for a long time. Since Mr. Fang was garrisoned in Ningguta, he decided to visit him. When he arrived, Mr. Fang had already been granted a token of release from exile. The customs of Ningguta were originally simple and honest, and people treated him as if he were an old host, even when he traveled back and forth over a hundred miles. The person then prepared a letter of introduction and paid respects to a certain official. Just as the letter of introduction was presented, the official became very angry and drew a knife, about to kill him. Because the local custom revered white, and red was used as funeral items, the person just touched what was forbidden. He ran back to Tokyo, and his breathing became a little calmer. Looking around at the palace and city foundations, the setting sun flickered. He hesitated while holding the reins, finding himself in a difficult situation where one cannot advance or retreat. Suddenly, a rider galloped to from the east, and the person thought he was being caught up with, and ran again. He heard a loud shout from the horse: "Why don't you wait a moment, Zikai?" The person looked back and recognized it as a distant relative, Mr. Wu. He then stopped the horse and asked why he came. Mr. Wu said: "Just now returned from trading ginseng in Ningguta. The lodging is not far, please go and rest temporarily." They therefore went together to Stone Buddha Temple and stayed there. The person complained about his desperate situation and suffering. Mr. Wu said: "Tomorrow I will go to trade in Cambodia. That country calls scholars 'Ban Jie,' and those who enter officialdom through this path are considered clear and upright. With your high talent, why worry about wealth and honor there?" The person secretly thought that even if he were to drift across the sea, he could still make it. At dawn, they got up, went to the harbor, and boarded a merchant ship together. With favorable winds and sails set, they reached Chenla (i.e., Cambodia) in two days and two nights.

As soon as he stepped ashore, all who saw him were startled and fled, some standing back and looking from afar. The protagonist was surprised and asked why. Someone said, "This place is near Dan'er, where people consider dark skin beautiful. Your brother has ice-like skin and jade-like bones, so it's no wonder you're treated like a stranger barking at a dog." The protagonist was filled with regret and almost died of shame. The companion said, "There's no need to worry." He then undressed, took out a brush, and ground light ink, applying it evenly to his face. When it came to the protagonist, he said, "Why make a face like a ghost to flatter people?" Despite being persistently urged, the protagonist had no choice but to let the companion do as he wished. From then on, he roamed the cities, and wherever he went, he was loved by everyone. The companion also praised him, and after some time, the king heard of his talent and specially summoned him for a test. The protagonist was delighted and carried his brush inside. The king sat on a bed adorned with seven treasures, and his attendants led the protagonist down to the steps. The king looked at his prime minister and said, "Even if judged by appearance, he is still worthy of adding luster to our ranks." He then granted the protagonist a writing material made from a mixture of deer hides and white chalk, instructing him to write an essay on the *An Luo* tree. The protagonist referenced the *Sui Shu* (Book of Sui) and *Bencao* (Herbal), elaborating and enriching his composition. In an instant, he finished and quickly wrote it out, presenting it to the king. The king read it several times but could not understand it. The prime minister explained that the style of writing in China was like that. The king was angry and said, "Since you wished to be tested, how dare you not follow the established rules?" He tore the writing material and threw it down, ordering the protagonist to be helped out. The protagonist returned to the boat in shame and sweat, thinking that enduring shame and disfiguring himself had only brought him disgrace. Unconsciously, he burst into tears.

At that time, the goods on the same boat had all been sold. Those who heard of his misfortune also sympathized with him, and they then carried him back together. Halfway, they encountered a typhoon, the boat capsized, and his relatives and companions all perished. The survivor luckily clung to the mast, drifted to an island, and crawled onto the shore. He asked and found out he was in Japan. He walked forward, and several miles away, he gradually saw signs of human habitation. He looked far into the south of the city, where many peaks pierced the sky, and below them, a strip of red wall was hidden among the bamboo groves. He thought it was the garden of a noble family. As he approached, he saw the garden gate wide open, with several maids in elaborate makeup standing outside. They saw him and chased after him. One maid, exceptionally beautiful, spoke in Wu dialect. Seeing his condition, she asked where he came from. He wept and recounted his story. The maid sympathetically said, "Sir, you have just arrived here, and the language is not understood. Moreover, it is already late in the day, where will you find lodging? Will you not die of cold and hunger? Fortunately, you are a refined scholar, and the lord has not yet arrived, so it is not inappropriate to stay temporarily." She then consulted with the other maids and led him through winding paths and mountains, making it difficult to discern the way. After several turns, they entered a side building, where bamboo mats and paper windows were clean and elegant, pleasing to the eye.

All the maidservants had dispersed, leaving him alone in sorrowful thought. Suddenly, a maid brought a lamp, offering him food and snacks. He took and ate, finding the taste exceptionally delicious. Seeing him shivering all over, the maid immediately returned to fetch blankets and a warming stove, smiling and saying, "I couldn't find a man's clothes just now, but after you sleep, you can dry your wet clothes yourself." Unable to hold back his emotions, he said, "You have brought me back to life, and I swear to repay you one day." The maid smiled again and said, "A true man cannot be so weak as to end up here. I, as a fellow villager, risk my life to help you. Tomorrow the king will arrive, and I cannot stay. How can you repay me?" He then realized that this was the king's secondary palace. That night, although he lay down, he could not sleep. Early in the morning, he went to the garden, thinking of planning further with the maidservants. But all he saw beyond the layered mountains, with their blue-green hues, were magnificent palaces with heavy pearl curtains. Gradually, the dawn light filtered through the forest, and the green birds chirped, with no human trace. Recalling the distant mountains of his homeland, sorrow arose within him. He then took a brush, dipped it in dew from a camphor tree, and wrote a poem on the wall: "Though wandering far and wide, my spirit remains bold. My stomach is full of words, and my robes are still green. I wish to rest on weary straw, but it cannot grow horns. My name is but a piece of paper, with long hair to show. The waves of the island nation pierce through thorny bamboo, and the spring colors of my homeland recognize the pink peach blossoms. Dare I compare my poem to the *Xiangshan Collection*? Gazing wistfully at the distant homeland, I scratch my head repeatedly."

The man had just finished writing when he heard a call from outside the wall. Soon after, the maid rushed in, saw the poem, and was startled, saying, "The king is about to arrive. If he asks about this poem, what words can I use to reply?" The man was greatly afraid and intended to leave, but the king had already called and embraced him. The maid quickly led the man to hide behind a hill. The king's carriage happened to pass by under the wall, and he caught a glimpse of the poem. He stopped his carriage to read it and asked who had written it and where the person was. The maid truthfully replied. The king did not get angry but just called the maid in and secretly instructed her, "Last night, I once dreamed of wandering here, reading this poem. A person beside, who seemed to be a Bodhisattva image, told me, 'The two of you have a fate in a future life, and you will reunite. Tomorrow, that person will come. Carefully remember the poem and lyrics, do not forget them.' Now this poem is unchanged word for word. You try to go and ask; if it is a student from Huzhou, then guide them to come."

The maid replied in a voice and went away. After a while, she returned and reported that the woman was afraid of punishment and dared not come out. Wang pondered for a moment and then rushed up, leading the maid to the back of the mountain. He saw Sheng, his face weathered and no longer the jade-like beauty of the past, and could not bear the sorrow. He held Sheng's sleeves and wept, saying, "I came slightly late due to state affairs, causing you to be frightened. Now I am fortunate to be unharmed. Do you still recognize me, your second-life Yuxiao?" Sheng looked at the woman, who was about twenty years old, tall and graceful, with a beauty that seemed divine. Her skin, eyebrows, and eyes were all a perfect match for his former wife. At once, he was filled with doubt and disbelief, wiping his eyes and asking, "Is it not a dream?" Wang shook her head and said, "It is not a dream. When I was alive, I often remembered past lives. After parting from you, I went to the underworld, where they said that I, before marriage, had once fallen ill and did not take water or food into my mouth for three days. Although your illness eventually healed, mine was truly caused by this. This situation is indeed pitiable, so they allowed me to find you again, to mend the broken mirror and fill the heaven of separation and hatred." She continued, "That is why I have not yet married, waiting for you."

The protagonist, upon hearing the news, felt as if he had just awakened from a dream. He then asked the maid, "Is this your king's princess?" The maid covered her mouth and laughed, saying, "This is indeed the king. Our country has traditionally been ruled by women. Now, the king was born as a divine maiden, capable of commanding a hundred spirits, so the nation reveres her as their ruler. Do you not see that there are no men serving on the left or right?" (According to the *Records of the Three Kingdoms*: In Japan, there has been a practice of having a male relative assist in governance since the reign of Emperor Humei. Few have seen such a ruler, who is guarded by a thousand maids and served by one man for food and communication. Her residence is always guarded by armed personnel.) The protagonist was then overcome with joy and sorrow, recalling the past, and tears streamed down his face. The princess wiped his tears with an embroidered handkerchief and took him back, instructing that a palace be prepared for him. The next day, she ordered a carriage to be prepared and had him returned in a palanquin. She informed his parents and appointed him as a prince consort, and they were wed. After entering the bridal chamber, it was truly no different from the time when they first fell in love. In the morning, the protagonist watched the princess prepare her makeup by the curtain. She looked into the mirror, and he regretted his own aging, but the princess's affection for him grew even deeper.

After several days, the woman and the man composed poetry together by the lamp, with a maid standing by. The man pointed to the maid and said, "Although destiny is predetermined, without this person, how could there be tonight?" The woman laughed lightly and said, "Then how can I repay your kindness?" The man remained silent, but he looked at the maid and smiled. The woman immediately stopped composing and ordered another maid to bring a lamp and bedding, leading the man to the maid's bed. The maid was too shy to look up, and the woman hurriedly pulled the maids along. After entering the room and lying down, the maid whispered, "This night's encounter, could it be something one could dream of? But I'm so excited I can hardly bear it." The man smiled and said, "I'm old, but this is how I repay you. How can I avoid being bold?" And then, the act of consummating the relationship took place, and then it stopped. From then on, the man had numerous lovers and no longer chased after fleeting desires.

The concubine gave birth to one son and one daughter. The daughter was named Liu Ti, and the son was named Long Jian. The boy was exceptionally intelligent and taught himself to read and study, understanding all classics and histories at a glance. The father often pointed to the boy and said to his daughter, "This is a remarkable child. You should remember this. One day when you return to China, you will surely earn honors to glorify the afterlife. Thus you will cover past sins, and even if I die, my eyes will be closed in peace." He passed away at the age of seventy-nine. At the time of his death, he ordered to be buried in a simple coffin and plain clothes, without returning to be buried in the ancestral tomb, to mark the unfulfilled wish. The daughter could not go against his wishes and buried him as instructed. She then sent envoys with a petition to enter the imperial court. The court agreed. The daughter then passed the throne to Liu Ti and took along Long Jian and the two sons from the maid into court. They stayed in the capital to seek a trial for the son. The emperor allowed him to take the provincial examination as a student. He succeeded in the palace examination as the second-place winner and was appointed to the position of editor at the Hanlin Academy. He served up to the position of Left Censor-in-Chief at the Censorate, being clear and upright with notable achievements. After the birth of the imperial son, the emperor bestowed honors on his ancestors. At over fifty years old, his mother died. After completing the mourning period, he submitted a memorial, requesting to go and bring back his father's coffin. The emperor praised his action and granted six months of leave to bring back and bury together.

外史氏曰：投书湘水，愁寄芙蓉；抱璞荆山，泪满怀袖。况乎烟墨无言，文章憎命，古今之以红为白，以白为黑，而颠倒是非者，岂独夷俗然哉？以余所闻，诸生神清叔宝，才艳安仁，其天姿磊落，不可一世，而儒雅恂恂，不敢失声于仆隶，亦何至所向辄穷乃尔哉？嗟乎！怀刺生毛，一生作客；卖文以活，四海无家。至于水尽山穷，而窜迹龙沙，投珠海国，亦谓琵琶别抱，庶几雪恨九泉也。而乃遭按剑于柬埔，泣冤禽于碧海，岂吾相不当侯耶？抑此中亦无汝文字缘耶？设延津不复再舍，东野终已无儿，则此恨绵绵，一腔血更洒何处？盖至前路更无知己，而欲以识曲子期望诸巾帼也，则天下之衔冤入地，而聚哭于青枫黑塞间者，当不少矣。噫！ The World History of Chinese Civilization: A Sourcebook 外史氏曰：投书湘水，愁寄芙蓉；抱璞荆山，泪满怀袖。况乎烟墨无言，文章憎命，古今之以红为白，以白为黑，而颠倒是非者，岂独夷俗然哉？以余所闻，诸生神清叔宝，才艳安仁，其天姿磊落，不可一世，而儒雅恂恂，不敢失声于仆隶，亦何至所向辄穷乃尔哉？嗟乎！怀刺生毛，一生作客；卖文以活，四海无家。至于水尽山穷，而窜迹龙沙，投珠海国，亦谓琵琶别抱，庶几雪恨九泉也。而乃遭按剑于柬埔，泣冤禽于碧海，岂吾相不当侯耶？抑此中亦无汝文字缘耶？设延津不复再舍，东野终已无儿，则此恨绵绵，一腔血更洒何处？盖至前路更无知己，而欲以识曲子期望诸巾帼也，则天下之衔冤入地，而聚哭于青枫黑塞间者，当不少矣。噫！ The World History of Chinese Civilization: A Sourcebook 外史氏曰：投书湘水，愁寄芙蓉；抱璞荆山，泪满怀袖。况乎烟墨无言，文章憎命，古今之以红为白，以白为黑，而颠倒是非者，岂独夷俗然哉？以余所闻，诸生神清叔宝，才艳安仁，其天姿磊落，不可一世，而儒雅恂恂，不敢失声于仆隶，亦何至所向辄穷乃尔哉？嗟乎！怀刺生毛，一生作客；卖文以活，四海无家。至于水尽山穷，而窜迹龙沙，投珠海国，亦谓琵琶别抱，庶几雪恨九泉也。而乃遭按剑于柬埔，泣冤禽于碧海，岂吾相不当侯耶？抑此中亦无汝文字缘耶？设延

The Sky is Composed of Accumulated Qi, and Lightning Strikes People The sky is composed of accumulated qi, and thus the Master of the Garden (Yuan Mei) says that the sky bestows blessings and misfortunes upon people, just as humans treat ants: when one throws a bone, the entire clan gathers to enjoy it, thinking it a blessing for humans; but soon after, when one pours boiling water over them, the entire colony is annihilated, and it is thought that the ants have brought a curse upon humans, though humans remain unaware. His words are so profound that they could make even a stone nod in agreement. Yet when it comes to lightning striking people, from kings and nobles down to scholars and commoners, these events are clearly recorded in history. Other examples, such as Zhang Dun becoming a prostitute and Qin Hui turning into a pig, are found in various folk tales. There are also instances that I have witnessed firsthand, where the reasons cannot be understood.

During the reign of Emperor Jiaqing, I heard in the county that a centipede was struck dead by lightning within the Feiying Pagoda. Its length was over two chi, and its width slightly more than two cun. Its entire body was intact, except for a single hole at the top, no larger than a needle, from which black water continued to flow. Several years later, I visited the home of my cousin Jiang Shifang in Chenzhuang, where I saw a chisel mark on the inner wall of the passage, running from the end of the beam straight down to the ground, about the width of a finger and half an inch deep. Entering the courtyard, I saw a rotten branch on the southeast side of the ginkgo tree in front of the eaves, its leaves all scorched black. A chisel mark ran from the tip to the root of the branch, as deep and wide as the one on the wall. My elder sister told me that last summer, this tree was burned by lightning fire, and the chisel marks were also caused by lightning striking. At the moment the thunderclap erupted, the whole room was as dark as night. I saw only flames circling the house, smelled sulfur in the air, and felt the ground hot as fire. At that time, the children were all wearing straw sandals. After the rain, they looked down and found purple blisters on their feet. Some said that the thunder god was pursuing a demon, hence the chisel marks in both places. To this day, the small branches on the tree remain leafless.

In the autumn of the seventh year of the Daoguang era (1827), a column in the family's tower was struck by lightning. The column was hollow inside, and wood chips were inserted into it almost everywhere, all about an inch long and sharp and fine like needles. They were arranged around like nails driven in. It was especially strange. According to *The Laughter Between Flowers*, after the early years of the dynasty, when the southern city was ravaged by war, the school grounds were the most desolate. The two annual sacrifices held at the Grand Success Hall were never opened. In the summer of the Bingxu year (1836), lightning and thunder surrounded the hall for three days without descending. The school officials found it strange and opened the doors to inspect. They saw something on the wooden board with the inscription of Confucius, arranged in clusters, with a brilliant light radiating outward. Upon closer inspection, it was a large centipede encircling the board. The white, clustered parts were its feet. One of the school officials, who was clever, knew that the lightning was rotating in the sky and must be this evil creature. If it struck down, the board would surely be destroyed. The monster had a spiritual nature and knew that the thunder god must fear Confucius. It would not dare to damage the board, so it used this to avoid being struck by lightning. They then used a fire-resistant hook to pull the board down. The centipede wriggled and tried to escape, but the thunder from above struck, and the centipede was then reduced to a pulp. The officials were greatly pleased and circled around to look, seeing the words "逆阉魏忠贤" (Rebellious Eunuch Wei Zhongxian) on its belly. Is the centipede at Feiying Tower also of this kind?

Crickets The game of crickets began with the palace people of the Tianbao era. Today, this game is only found in Zhejiang. However, the crickets die when frozen, even less able to withstand the cold than flies and mosquitoes. Occasionally, some can be kept until the next year, approximately, these insects must be strong, and must be carefully protected. I once kept a cricket, until the next year's Lantern Festival, on which day it was extremely cold. Upon returning and seeing that the water was already cold, the cricket had frozen stiff. Also when my father was still alive, he kept a cricket, until the next year's Qingming Festival, it died. These two crickets were both brave and strong, they did not meet any opponents. Recently I read the *Tui Shi Jizhu* by Jin Ao, which states that in the palace, crickets are kept during autumn. Until the Lantern Festival in the first month, then they are placed inside the lamps of the Ao Mountain. After the music has ended, suddenly, the sound of crickets is heard coming from the Ao Mountain. Then it is known that the palace also has such creatures.

**The Living Buddha** Emperor Taizong of the Tang Dynasty once sent the monk Xuanzang to Tibet to seek Buddhist scriptures, for the Buddha originally came from the Western Regions. In the 27th year of Emperor Kangxi’s reign, because Russia requested peace, the Emperor specially dispatched Zhang Penghe and Chen Anshi to go there. Upon reaching Russian territory, they encountered several foreign monks whose faces resembled those of Arhats, yet their bodies and bones were all soft, allowing them to place their feet on their heads and pass their heads through their armpits. One of the monks could speak Chinese and said, “It is said that people from the Great Western Heaven (India) searched for a living Buddha in China, traveling throughout the famous mountains of Wutai, Potala, and Emei, but found no Buddha. They heard that the Dalai Lama possessed one, so they went to see him, but realized it was not so. They also heard that there was a 'Jin Dan Lama' in foreign lands who resembled a Buddha, so they journeyed to the remote wilderness to see him, but it was not so either. At the time of the Erdi conflict, their belongings were taken from them, and they barely survived.” Zhang Penghe said to him, “You abandoned life and death, traveling throughout the world in search of a living Buddha, yet you still could not find one. In the end, do you still believe that the Buddha’s fruit (enlightenment) exists in the world at all?” The monk smiled and said, “Only today have I come to know that it does not exist at all.” If this foreign monk had met Xuanzang, both would have smiled and returned, without having to endure such a long and arduous journey.

通字 Marquis Shen Wubiao stayed at the home of Jin in the prefectural town. One of his young disciples was reading the *Zuo Zhuan* when he came across the phrase "Gongsun Meng had intercourse with Ai Jiang." He asked Wubiao what "通" meant. Wubiao explained it as "illicit sexual relations," but the disciple still did not understand. Wubiao then said, "Imagine two men and women sleeping together on the same bed; that is what '通' means." The disciple nodded and smiled. At the time, Jin had a female servant who was slightly attractive, and she had an affair with the servant Zhu. It was summer, and whenever the master was asleep, the servant would go out to meet Zhu. Her bed happened to be behind Wubiao's sleeping chamber. One day, Wubiao left, and the disciple saw the servant go upstairs. She did not come out for a long time, so the disciple secretly went to the back of the curtains to peek. He saw the two of them in the midst of their intimate encounter, completely unaware. The disciple hurried down and ran to the central hall, clapping his hands and shouting, "Ah! Xiao Mo is having an affair with Zhu!" Those who heard him could not help but fall over in laughter. There was once a private tutor who was teaching the character "淫" and said, "淫 means a great illness for women." A young student overheard this and remembered it. Later, when his mother was ill, he did not come to school for several days. The teacher asked him why, and the student replied that his mother was "淫." The teacher was shocked and asked him to describe the situation. The student then explained that his mother was actually ill. The teacher became furious and said, "Then how can it be called '淫'?" He was about to punish the student, but the child wept and explained what he had heard the previous day. The teacher laughed and let it go. The disciple of Wubiao was just as quick to understand.

I once lived as a guest in Hexi, and there was a relative of the Zhao family named Zhao, who was naturally simple-minded. He was close friends with Xu Huanfeng in Ji, but Huanfeng treated him indifferently. Later, when Zhao's neighbor came to Hexi, Zhao met him on the road and asked about Huanfeng's recent condition, treating him with utmost hospitality. The neighbor was surprised and said, "I once heard Huanfeng tell someone, 'I have a casual acquaintance with Zhao's son.' Now seeing how you treat him, this statement should not have come from Huanfeng's mouth." It was because the neighbor had always been at odds with Huanfeng. However, Zhao had never known what a "casual acquaintance" meant and mistakenly thought it was a "food acquaintance." He then asked someone, "What is a food acquaintance?" The person did not realize that Zhao had misunderstood and casually replied, "It probably just means meat friends or drinking buddies." Zhao became extremely angry and shouted, "How could Zhao be friends with you just for food!" He rushed to Huanfeng's door and started cursing. When Huanfeng came out to ask what was happening, Zhao rushed forward and punched him, breaking his teeth and bruising his forehead, and then went to the courthouse. During the reign of Emperor Qianlong, the sea tide at Zhapu did not recede, and the seawater overflowed the embankments, flooding and destroying countless houses and livestock. The stone lions in front of the Tianfei Temple on Tang Mountain rolled straight to the general's office and stopped. After the tide receded, a sea eel was stranded in the embankment and did not move. It was dozens of feet long. People rushed to cut off its flesh and boiled the oil to substitute for lamp fuel. As more and more people started cutting, the eel could not bear the pain and suddenly jumped and flipped over, crushing and killing hundreds of people.

**The Giant** Long ago, there was a sea vessel that had set out to trade in the Kingdom of Johor, but was carried by a typhoon to an island. The place was surrounded on all sides by towering mountains, and the surrounding area was desolate with no human paths. Ten or more men on the ship, bored and restless, went ashore together and climbed up by grasping vines and ropes around their waists. After half a day, they reached halfway up the mountain, where there was a huge stone like a millstone, looking down upon the coast. Upon ascending it, they felt the mighty sky wind, so cold and fierce that one could not stay, while the cries of owls and howls of apes shook their very souls. They quickly searched for the way back and returned. Not even a few steps away, they glimpsed a giant in the deep forest, over ten zhang in height, coming slowly with disheveled hair. Upon seeing the men, he was greatly pleased, and leaped to reach them. The birds chattered, and he touched and smelled them all around. He immediately broke off a vine from the rock wall and passed each of the several men through their jaws, like threading fish. After threading them, he bent the ends and tied them to the tree and left. The men, on the tree top, saw the giant had already gone far away. They quickly drew their swords and cut the vine, grabbed branches to descend, and ran wildly to the shore. By then, the wind had changed direction. They boarded the ship just as the sails were raised, but the giant had arrived. At that moment, the boat had already left the shore, and the giant reached out with his hand to pull it. One of the men drew his sword and cut off the giant’s hand. The giant retracted, and two fingers fell into the cabin — each only a single joint. They weighed it, and it was eight jin in weight, over two chi in length. Lu Ciyun’s *Ba Hong Yi Shi* records: During the Chenghua period, soldiers from Suzhou and Wu went to Chongming Island, and encountered a tall person similar to this one. The finger that was cut off from that giant was a full shi and four cun long, being only a single segment of a finger. It is said that it is still kept in the Jiading treasury.

In the *Borderland Observations* by Chen Zengqi, it is recorded that in the 26th year of the Kangxi era, a seafarer from southern Yunnan, while sailing, saw afar a pagoda-like structure towering above the clouds. Upon approaching, he realized it was a person. The creature yawned and stretched to get up, seized seven people to eat, and then returned to its pagoda-like form. The others secretly fled to their ship. The creature lifted its foot and immediately reached them, dragging the ship. The sailors attacked it with axes, severing a finger, which was two feet long. They returned to present the severed finger to Governor Fan. Some said, "This must be a land of a single person." Could this be what the maritime merchant encountered? According to the *Book of Strange Things*, people in the northwest sea are three thousand miles tall. The *Strange Things of Liangzhou* also mentions that there is a giant in Lingding who is ten thousand miles tall. This aligns with the *Chu Ci*’s description of a "long man" who is a thousand fathoms tall, all of which are excessively long. In the southwest of the sea, there is a country called Wanda, located south of Gela, bordering the southern sea. In the sea, there is a mountain that rises abruptly and jaggedly, often emitting flames that are carried by the wind, especially in summer. It is commonly called "Flame Mountain," as it is situated at the extreme south of the sea. Western foreigners said that ships from their country often came to the foot of this mountain. The sailors climbed the mountain to look around and saw from afar that the people in the cave were eating raw fish. Realizing they were being watched, the people shouted and chased after them. The sailors fled in panic, and those who lagged behind were seized and eaten. When they returned to their ship, only sixteen remained, and they quickly hoisted their sails and fled. Since then, no one dared to return.

My father also recounted that when he was fifteen, he once suffered from typhoid fever. After more than a month, he could finally get up, but he still dared not leave the room. One day in the morning, he happened to be tired and leaned obliquely on the bed. He saw an old woman, around seventy years old, with a broad face and dark skin, and a plump figure. She was wearing a brown single-layer shirt and a green scarf and skirt, and held an oiled paper fan as she approached the front door. My father shouted and asked, "What are you doing here?" The old woman replied, "I am looking for your old lady." My father said, "The old lady is not here." The old woman responded, "Oh," and immediately left. At that time, there were several tailors outside the room making clothes, and downstairs was the kitchen. My father suspected that there had never been such a person visiting the house, so he forced himself to get up and went out to ask if the tailors had seen this person. They all said they had not seen her. Then he went downstairs, and my great-grandmother and grandmother were cooking under the stove. He asked them, and they also said they had not seen her. They all sighed in amazement. Not long after, my great-grandmother fell ill and passed away after more than ten days. Only then did he realize that the words "looking for your old lady" were true, and it was undoubtedly a ghost.

Ghost Hunting During the reign of Emperor Ling, Shen Xueqiao from Hongdun once moved his lodging to the main room on a hot summer night. Because of the heat, the windows were left open. One night, after waking up, the moonlight was slightly slanted, clear and bright like daytime. Shen Xueqiao saw a person wearing a cool hat, a blue cotton shirt, and straw sandals, with a pale and thin face, sitting alone in the northwest corner. Shen Xueqiao suspected him of being a thief, so he jumped up to seize him. The person had already come out to the front of the eaves. As Shen Xueqiao was about to catch him, the person jumped onto the table. Shen Xueqiao quickly grasped his feet with both hands, but they were empty, and the person was nowhere to be seen. Only then did he realize that the person was a ghost. Shen Xueqiao's nephew, Yuqing, said: Once, he was reading books on the upper floor. Behind the wooden partition, during the silkworm-raising month, leaves were often stored inside. One night, after lighting the lamp, he heard a rustling sound behind the wall, as if someone was picking up leaves and putting them into a basket. Soon, he heard the fine and delicate sound of drawers opening and closing, gradually reaching the door. A young woman, about twenty years old, wearing a simple single-layered silk robe in watercolor, a black silk half-sleeve, and a light red skirt, with bright eyes and a high hairdo, leaned forward to look intently. She stayed for a long time before leaving. Yuqing was surprised and hurried to the front door. He could not find her, so he went downstairs to ask his mother, "Did anyone just come upstairs to pick up leaves?" His mother replied, "No, there wasn't anyone." Yuqing told her what he had heard and seen. His grandmother, standing beside them, sighed and said, "This is your former mother, Lady Lu. She used to come here to pick up leaves when she was alive. Her ghost soul probably still loves this place and wants to see you once. However, she was gracious and virtuous. If she comes again in the future, my child, do not be afraid." Therefore, Yuqing could see his ghost mother, how fortunate it is!

Yu Qing also said: His ancestor Han Wang, before his death, would every night lock the doors and personally carry a lamp to thoroughly check everything. After his death, every night after dusk, there would always be a faint light appearing from the back alley, moving until it reached the first gate, but no one was seen. This continued for almost three years, and then it stopped. Later, there was a man named Guo from Zhu Village who once returned from selling cloth in Xincheng. On the way, he encountered a person in black clothes, who seemed like a servant of a wealthy family. The person invited him to a place that looked grand and official. Upon entering, a gatekeeper led him in, where he saw the host, dressed in formal attire, sitting majestically on the main hall. On either side, there were dozens of attendants, some dressed formally and solemnly, others in simple clothing. The host called out to ask about Guo's hometown and surname. Guo knelt and said he had done no wrong, begging to be allowed to return home. The host said, "Do not speak much, this is fate." He then ordered the attendants to set up a feast and commanded Guo to sit among the many people. Soon, the music started, and all kinds of dishes were served. After several rounds of drinking, Guo stood up to say he wanted to return home, but was refused. Guo continued to plead persistently, and the host became displeased, saying, "If so, you must remember to meet me on a certain day in June next year at the western side of Tingzi Bridge. Do not break the appointment." Guo agreed and left. When he reached the door, the moon was already slanting, and looking back, there was no house, only an ancient tomb. He hurriedly ran back home and told the family what had happened, but they did not understand why.

Not long after, it was the following June. After the planting was completed, I went with several villagers to the East Mountain Temple to watch a play and returned in the late afternoon. We walked past the western side of the Pavilion Bridge, not even a mile away, when my companions suddenly disappeared. The others were surprised and searched until we found De sitting dangerously on the water's edge, suspecting he was about to bathe. We called out to him, but he did not respond. Upon closer inspection, he was dead. Counting on my fingers, the date of his death exactly matched the predicted date of June some day. O, the Foolish One On the tenth day of the second month of spring in the year of the Goat (1645), as dusk approached, I was about to close my door when I saw my neighbor, O the Foolish One, carrying a basket and holding an umbrella, going out in the rain to buy things. The next morning, I heard that he had died in the small river near the Golden Drum Bridge. After a meal, I happened to visit my second sister's house and talked about it. She told me that after the second drum strike last night, the rain and wind started, and several people were heard shouting and cursing intensely, as if fighting, and eventually it became quiet. Soon after, she heard voices from the other side of the river, got up, and looked through the window, seeing several neighbors standing on the bank with lights and poles, pointing at the water and saying, "It looks like two people are hugging, luckily they haven't sunk yet." They then pulled him out, but he was already dead. O had originally gone to the street to buy things, but for some unknown reason, he ended up here. O had epilepsy, and half a year ago, he broke open the coffin of his second son and took out the body, saying, "This is a golden Buddha." From then on, his face turned blue-black like indigo. I thought he would not eat new food anymore, but I did not expect him to die in the water, and at that time, the disease had not occurred.

Before this, Zhang had a daughter-in-law named Xi, who, due to Zhang blaming her for stealing food, drowned herself out of resentment in a stream outside the gate and died. By that time, Zhang Wu, his young son living with him, was gathering firewood and came to the west of the stream, passing by the coffin of Mrs. Xi. Upon returning home, he began to have alternating chills and fever, muttering softly, "Auntie (meaning Mrs. Zhang, the mother of Zhang Wu), do not think that I came yesterday to demand my father's life. All drowned ghosts must wait three years before they can come ashore, and another three years before they can find a replacement. I was not yet there. My father's death was due to another ghost taking over there." When asked if he was happy in the underworld, he replied, "I have no one to control me now, but I suffer from hunger and cold. Mother, you only need to send me several hundred paper money and a bowl of porridge, and that will be enough." Following his words, she recovered. Perhaps, Zhang was foolish towards his son and daughter-in-law, being extremely unkind, so he did not wait for the daughter-in-law to become vengeful, but instead made another ghost hasten her death, to punish her, I suppose. It is said that any person who drowns, if their mouth and nose are clogged with mud, it is a sign that a drowned ghost is claiming their soul, and they cannot be saved. This year in May, while I was at home, I saw a young boy from the East Village village who drowned in a river. When they pulled him out, mud was blocking his mouth and nose. Despite trying to save him, he did not revive.

绮琴 绮琴，出身于丽水的沈氏家族，初名湘碧。她从小失去父母，性格极其聪慧，容貌艳丽妩媚，姿态优美如同美玉。她擅长填词，精通音律。母亲对她非常疼爱，像珍视拱璧一样，为她选择夫婿非常困难，因此到十七岁还待字闺中。有一天，邻居的老妇人宋媪来到她家，见到绮琴后感叹道：“这位小姐如此苗条，如果我是男人，岂能不为情所动而死？”母亲笑着让她去寻找好的姻缘。宋媪拍手说道：“像颇牧这样的佳婿就在宫中，何必远求？”母亲说：“您说的是韩生吗？我也久闻他的才华，只是他虽然有才却贫穷，这该怎么办呢？”宋媪回答：“哪里有像他这样年轻却长期贫困的人？”当时，韩生泰瞻是镇上的名士，住在绮琴家，正好他的婚事已经断了弦超过一年。母亲于是与儿子商量此事。儿子说：“如果能找到像韩生这样的女婿，岂止是参军那么简单？但是他的家庭需要亲自操持家务，恐怕妹妹不习惯过贫苦的生活。”母亲也有些犹豫。绮琴正好回家，详细解释了宋媪的话。从此以后，她每天早早起床，晚上很晚才休息，凡是烹饪、缝补等家务事，总是亲自操劳而不感到疲倦。哥哥和嫂子悄悄观察到她的想法，告诉了母亲，母亲于是下定决心。她召来宋媪，示意韩生。韩生本是个深情之人，一听到这个消息，不禁感激涕零，但又担心事情会有变化。

Previously, the girl entrusted the old woman with her jade arch璧, asking her to find a craftsman to carve two phoenixes on it. Upon hearing these words, she immediately returned home to retrieve her pendant, disguising herself as the old woman to give it as a gift, saying, "This thing is to be remembered." She then went back to report. Soon after, she arrived at the girl's place and told her about the gift. The girl was shocked and blamed, saying, "If the matter does not go well, what then?" She immediately ordered her maid Xiu Chun to go and ask for the return of the pendant. Xiu Chun, the maid the girl loved, knelt down and said, "This matter has been planned by the maid for a long time. Considering his character, how could he be someone who breaks his word? If we go to ask for it now, it will not chill his heart and hurt the old mother's feelings?" The girl wept, restrained herself, and stopped. But from then on, if he needed anything, he would always send the maid.

The woman was skilled at playing the flute. Once, by lamplight, she composed a ci poem titled *“Recalling the Flute Playing on the Phoenix Terrace,”* but at the final line, she paused, uncertain. She then sealed the draft and handed it to her maid, saying: “This piece still has one character that is not yet stable. You must go and ask Han郎 on my behalf so that it may be completed.” She also ordered the maid to accompany the scholar to the furnace. The maid went to the study and delivered the woman’s message, handing the poem to the scholar. He unrolled it, read it, and could not stop praising it. He then took up his brush and revised the character. Soon after, he fixed his greedy gaze upon the maid, who scolded him, saying, “Do you not recognize me?” He replied, “Your divine skin reflects snow, your cloud-like hair is piled like crows. Tonight, looking upon you, I find you even more beautiful than by day. If one day heaven grants our wishes, would you be willing to follow me, carrying only a quilt?” The maid’s cheeks flushed red as she lowered her head and toyed with her sash, unable to utter a word. The scholar, unaware of how his spirit had been stirred, suddenly rose and pulled the maid onto his lap. The maid had long considered herself a small star, but she had not expected such sudden familiarity. She resisted, saying, “If you insist upon this, I would rather die!” The scholar could not bring himself to press further, and immediately released her. The maid departed, and after that, she never came again.

The son had already politely expressed his wishes to his parents, and the marriage was about to be arranged. Coincidentally, twenty miles from the city, there was a wealthy girl from the Gu family, who was also gentle and charming. The son's father, seduced by the matchmaker, admired her dowry and firmly decided to proceed with the betrothal. The son was unwilling, but his father rebuked him with the duty of filial piety, and the son dared not speak again. The wedding was scheduled, and the girl first learned of it. She rejected makeup and adornment, refused food and water, and lay curled up all day. The mother came to comfort her, saying, "How could you be so disheartened? The husband, though cold in his promise, is there not another good match elsewhere?" The girl wept and said, "Mother's teachings I dare not disobey, but the jade pendant has already been given away and cannot be returned." The mother then knew all the previous events. Knowing that she could not be changed suddenly, she temporarily instructed the maid to take good care and not be negligent. She then left. After several days, the girl suddenly forced herself to get up and dress, calling the maid to bring tea. When the maid brought the tea, she did not see the girl. A little maid said, "Just saw the music teacher go into the back garden." The maid followed and entered, only to find the girl already in the pond. The maid also jumped in, and the little maid cried out loudly on the side. The family rushed to save her but it was too late. The mother cried day and night, and soon after, she also died.

At the time, the protagonist had just married, and his marriage with Mrs. Gu was harmonious, as described by the metaphor "the zither and lute are very harmonious." However, he often sat alone, muttering to himself, and would take out a jade pendant to play with it, shedding tears. One day, Mrs. Gu noticed this and inquired about the reason, taking the pendant from his hand and intending to hide it. The pendant fell to the ground and broke into two pieces. The protagonist became angry and left the house in a state of anger, suddenly encountering Madam Song, who glanced at him and said, "I've heard that your new wife is quite content. Do you also want to know about your old friend's news?" The protagonist urgently asked about her situation, and Madam Song narrated recent events in detail. Before she finished speaking, the protagonist cried loudly and said, "I have betrayed Miss Qin, but what do I have left in this world?" He then left. He visited his friend in Qingtian, intending to learn swordsmanship from him.

Walking to the middle of Kuo-ch'ang Mountain, he saw two women graceful in the distance ahead, surprised that they were walking alone without companions, and riding a slow horse to catch up. One of them was Xiang Bi, and the other was Xiu Chun. Shocked, he asked, "How did the two of you get here?" The women raised their heads to see him, and seemed to have a resentful look. Xiu Chun's star-like eyes slightly turned, and she seemed even more angry. Whispering, she said, "Go away, Qinq'!" They entered the forest together, and finally did not look back. The protagonist followed them, walking several miles until the forest ended, and there was a sheer cliff rising to the sky, with no path in sight. The two women walked up together, reaching the waist of the mountain, where the wall suddenly opened, and they entered, followed by Xiu Chun. The protagonist climbed up the vines to follow, looking at the wall and shouting, but found no seams. A faint moon rose, tigers roared, and wolves howled; looking down, he saw a thousand-foot deep ravine, clear like a mirror, and it seemed as if the two women were there. The protagonist was no longer afraid. Turning around to enter, he found himself already on flat ground. Wandering until dawn, he no longer returned to the city, and instead decided to cross the river, intending to reach Lingyin to become a monk.

Upon arriving at Cold Spring Pavilion, the protagonist encountered a monk with epilepsy. The monk greeted with a smile and said, "Do you also wish to attain enlightenment? But there are no immortals from the formless realm here, and there is still one unresolved case on your register of love, how can you foolishly dream of ascending to heaven?" The protagonist prostrated and said, "I only seek to repent my sins and karmic debts." The monk replied, "This alone proves that your attachment has not been severed." The protagonist further said, "I also seek liberation." The monk taught him to return and follow, but the protagonist showed reluctance. The monk became angry, bent down, picked up a brick, and threw it at him, saying, "Go, go! Hold this and knock, and the door will open." The protagonist knew he was no ordinary monk, accepted it, and returned.

Crossing the river, he returned to that place, climbed the wall, and knocked a few times. Suddenly, he heard someone in the wall sigh and say, "Faithless lover, since you have come to this, why did you do it in the first place!" The voice was extremely similar to Xiu Chun. The man was listening attentively when the stone door suddenly opened wide. Overjoyed, he leaped inside. Inside, there were jade flowers and jade grass, mist curtains and cloud windows, as if he had entered the fairy cave of the Moon Palace. After turning a few corners, he saw a woman in elaborate makeup leaning on a stone railing, holding a red plum branch to pin her hair up. She glanced at him and wanted to avoid him. The man pulled her skirt and first apologized for breaking the promise, then recounted the hardships of searching for each other. He then took out his dagger and was about to stab himself, but the woman quickly took it away and said, "I died, and the underworld, due to my anger, caused me to die, allowing me to return to life. I and Xiu Chun have both lost interest in the world. I also know that the fault of breaking the promise is not entirely on the man, but it cannot help but make one feel uneasy! Now making the man abandon his family and endure hardships, how can my heart be at peace? But since I experienced a small calamity, recalling earthly ties, it has already become as cold as ice and snow. Now I will be a world-outside friend with you, to resolve the karma of this life. If it comes to matters of love between men and women, please go back and find your old sword." The man then asked to be a close friend. After a while, they became more intimate, and the matters of the inner chamber were almost more than just plucking eyebrows. The woman could not bear the disturbance and asked to be replaced by Xiu Chun. From then on, they cooked stones for food and picked flowers to make wine. After a year, Xiu Chun gave birth to a son.

Not long after, a sudden autumn wind arose, and leaves gently drifted in the courtyard, stirring in the protagonist an unbidden feeling of homesickness. The woman urged him to return home to visit his family, but he could not bear to say goodbye. She presented him with a feathered robe, saying, "This was given to me by a night-flying fairy. If you remember, put it on, and within half a day, you can fly back." The protagonist donned the robe and, to his surprise, found himself transformed into a bird. He flapped his wings, and gracefully soared into the sky. In a moment, he arrived home, but everything looked unfamiliar. He asked about his wife, who had also passed away before, and found only his father, who was on the brink of death. Upon entering, he saw that his father could no longer speak, and he wept bitterly, causing his father to die. The protagonist grieved so deeply that he wore mourning clothes. After the burial, he put on the feathered robe and flew away, never to return.

The Monk from Zhaogqing Temple In the year of Ding Mao (1767), during the provincial examination, Mr. Dong from Wucheng rented a lodging in the area near the Yongjin Gate. The dwelling was low and narrow, and the owner moved his wife and children out to make room for him. At the time, the oppressive summer heat had just subsided, and the first crescent moon was beginning to rise. Outside his window was a low wall that could be easily climbed. Mr. Dong, sleeping alone without company, tossed and turned unable to sleep. By midnight, he heard someone enter the courtyard. Soon after, he saw a shadow at the paper window, a bald-headed monk. Realizing that the intruder was not a thief, Mr. Dong pretended to sleep in order to observe. The monk, feeling around the window, caused it to slide open with a grinding sound. He entered, placing his handkerchief and fan on the table, then undressed and approached the bed, calling out softly, "Dear sister, I have come!" Mr. Dong could not help but laugh out loud, "The monk has made a mistake. I am here as a tenant, not your former lover." The monk was greatly shocked and fled naked through the window. Mr. Dong got up and took the fan from the table, noticing a poem titled "From Xiao Cang Shan Fang to Mr. Liang Shanzhou" on it, inscribed with "For the great monk's enlightenment." It was written by Mr. Shanzhou. He felt secretly pleased.

Early the next morning, he dressed up and carried his fan out of the Qiantang Gate. Walking around the lake, he inquired about people and learned that a certain monk was the chief abbot of the Zhaoguang Temple. He presented a letter of introduction and paid his respects, briefly speaking a few words, and took out the fan from his sleeve to give it to the monk, saying, "I have long admired your moral conduct. I regret my worldly impurity and lack the chance to be your tutor. Now, fortunately, I can approach the lotus seat. I dare to present this as a token of my devotion." The monk received the fan, recognizing it as the one he had lost the previous day, and remained silent for a long time. He joined hands and thanked him, asking, "Where do you reside?" The protagonist answered one by one, then left to return to his lodging. After sitting down and resting, the monk suddenly appeared, wearing a robe and red shoes, entering while fanning. Upon seeing the protagonist, he bowed to the ground. The protagonist helped him up, and the monk, seeing that there was no one around, took out a wrapped item from his sleeve and said to the protagonist, "Your great kindness, I am deeply grateful but unable to repay. This small token is to prepare as a token of gratitude, do not laugh at this humble offering." The protagonist declined and thanked him, and the monk placed it on the table and left. The protagonist opened the package and weighed it, discovering one hundred taels of silver. He was very happy and locked it in a chest. Suddenly, he realized, "I cannot stay here any longer." He then called the innkeeper, paid the fee, and excused himself and left.

The host went to call his wife and children back, and by nightfall they all went to bed together. Just as they were sleeping deeply, the monk indeed returned. He opened the window and directly reached the place where the living person was lying, finding his head and raising the knife to cut it with force. The husband was startled awake, quickly grabbing the monk's arm and shouting for help. The monk was greatly frightened, but realized it was the voice of the husband. He spoke softly, saying, "Do not make a sound, I am a humble monk." By then, everyone in the room had already stood up and looked around, seeing the monk holding a sharp blade, shining like snow, and the blood-stained pillow and mat, with his wife's head and body separated. The monk also cried bitterly. It was because his wife had originally been a monk and then married. The monk initially met the living person because of an appointment, though offered gold, he could not guarantee that the secret would not be revealed to others. Therefore, he returned, killing unexpectedly, not knowing that the person had already moved away. Thus, the monk was bound and sent to the county. The magistrate came to inspect and finished the inspection, calling the monk to interrogate and understand the situation. He ordered the servants to visit Wucheng Academy to find Dong. The academy official thoroughly checked the register and found no name, returning to inform the magistrate. The magistrate quickly brought the monk out and said, "The killer must die, why use Dong! But fear that cutting you will not cut the root of dust, not as good as to change to the method of cremation, to send you to heaven. Perhaps bones turn to ash, one day can avoid falling into karmic obstacles again." He then ordered to carry the monk to the parade ground, pile up firewood and burn him, taking his ashes and scattering them in the river. This matter Dong once self-described to people, when he submitted his card, it is said that had already changed his name, so could not be found. Heard that at that time the monk's hair was already gray.

Double Marriage In the northwest of my hometown, there was a village called Zhoujiahu, and in it lived a man named Zhou Mingshan. He had a son who, at the age of eighteen, began to marry a Yang family girl from the village, who was seventeen years old. Although she was plain and unadorned, she reached the peak of elegance and charm. Her family lived opposite Zhou's, as close as the distance between here and the fairyland. They looked at each other with longing in their hearts and eventually had a secret affair. Later, the woman felt a movement in her belly, and they talked about it in bed, fearing that her parents would find out, and she was unable to sleep happily. Before dawn, the Zhou family son got up and left. But his father woke up early, did not see his son, searched for him, and after several days of searching, he had given up hope. And the girl's parents also could not understand why, and they just sighed and wondered. After a long time, they saw that the girl's belly was as big as a pot, and they questioned her, but she initially did not speak. The father suspected that she had a secret lover and was going to put her to death. The girl then told the truth and also described what they had privately said at night. The father then consulted with Zhou, and Zhou was surprised and said, "If that is the case, then my son must have fled out of fear of punishment." His wife smiled at Zhou and said, "We are old, and now our son has gone and not returned. Fortunately, the new bride is pregnant. If she gives birth to a boy, we will have a grandson even though we have no son. Now that the new bride is due to give birth, it would be better to invite him home to witness it together, to avoid other worries." The husband thought his wife's plan was good, so he chose a day to welcome him back. Not long after, she gave birth, and when the baby was born, it was a boy. Both the husband and wife were delighted. The wife was also happy, but whenever she thought of her husband's absence, she would comfort and cry while holding the child.

After, the son was nineteen years old, and they arranged for him to marry. Just as the ceremony of bowing at the hall was finished, suddenly a man with a long, twisted beard and carrying a load entered through the door. Everyone present did not recognize him, not even his father. The man recounted his background. At that moment, his wife, who was hiding behind the door, had listened carefully. She suddenly stepped out and pointed at her husband, scolding him: "Heartless husband, you left this piece of flesh behind and departed suddenly and far away. I almost died several times because of you. Do you have the face to come back today?" The father laughed and said, "Foolish child, you left without saying goodbye and have been without any news for twenty years. Where will you leave us two old people?" The son tearfully apologized. He explained that he initially left out of fear of punishment, went to Songjiang to sell candy to make a living, and now had quite a bit of savings. However, because he missed his parents, he came back despite the risk of punishment.

The old man said, "We are fortunate to still be well, but you already have a son and a daughter-in-law. Your wife still has her hair in a messy, maiden-like style—come, see what she looks like!" The other guests who heard this also laughed heartily. Therefore, they all encouraged each other, and on that very day, they decided to marry the two of them. The wife was deeply ashamed and couldn't look up, so she went in. Uncle Zhou also went in and told his wife about it, and his wife also laughed uncontrollably. They then all urged the woman to get ready, but she refused. The others helped her comb her hair and apply makeup, and then dressed her in the embroidered robe and red skirt that the new bride had worn. They supported her out of the hall and ordered the music and instruments to be played. Then the music was played again, and the woman and her husband bowed to each other, and then bowed to their parents. They then ordered the son and daughter-in-law to pay respects. After the bows, they were sent into the room and the wedding cup was shared. At that time, the woman's father had already passed away, and Uncle Zhou and his wife were both over seventy years old.

**周烂面** In the western market port village of a town, there lived a man known as Zhou Lanmian. He had once been branded on his face for stealing, and after applying a medicine that caused the area to become severely ulcerated, people began calling him "Lanmian." After returning home, he became increasingly brazen, extorting money and goods, forcing women into sexual acts, and causing widespread harm. People feared his accusations and dared not confront him. Later, he stole from a wealthy household in the village, and the stolen items were recognized as his. The next day, he went to the market to buy a piece of pig liver, cooked it for his mother, and said, "After we finish eating tonight, I will go and hang myself at the gate of a certain family, so I am giving this to you to make you a well-fed ghost." His mother was over seventy years old, blind, and usually begged for food in the village. That night, she wept and went to hang herself at the gate of the family in question. The next day, Zhou Lanmian found her body and announced his intention to report the matter to the county magistrate. The family bribed him with a large sum of money, and Zhou Lanmian satisfied his desires and returned home. I once read *Chuyue Lou Jianwen Zaji* (杂记, Miscellaneous Records from the Chuyue Tower), which recounts: In Wuyuan, there was a man named Dong Fengqi, whose given name was Shiyuan. He was kind and did not quarrel with anyone. In the fourth year of the Shunzhi era, a severe famine struck the area. A rascal from the village made his father drink poison first, then went to his home, hoping that his father would die and he could receive a substantial bribe. Upon entering the house, he invited him to sit down. Suddenly, he reproached himself and said, "My son has deceived me. I cannot die at the house of a good man." He quickly rushed out and fell dead by the roadside. This led me to reflect on the fact that nothing happens in isolation; there is always a counterpart or parallel event.

After the disfigured face, the village people gathered firewood and burned him to death. Once there was a village woman who sold a dog to a butcher, and the dog escaped into Mr. Feng's home. The woman followed, called out a hundred times, but the dog did not come out, so she paid its value and sent it away. From then on, the dog always stayed close to Mr. Feng. When Mr. Feng died, the dog lay by the coffin and did not eat, dying after several days. Dog Stew and Rice During the Qianlong Jia Wu year (1784), during the rebellion of Wang Lun in Shandong, Mr. Shen Litting, a horse trainer, was martyred in Shouzhang. At that time, a black dog in his office lay day and night before the coffin, mourning and howling without eating. When the coffin was closed, the dog leaped wildly several times, struck the coffin with its head, and died. The family regarded it as righteous, carried it back, and buried it beside the ancestral grave. They agreed to visit the grave during seasonal rituals, always setting up a dog stew and rice offering to it, and this practice continues to this day. Mr. Litting, whose given name was Qi Yi, served as the magistrate of Shouzhang in Shandong. He had a daughter who was two years old when her mother, Madam Lu, passed away. Mr. Litting fed her with dates and chestnuts, adjusting to her comfort and temperature, using all his heart and effort. His stepmother, Madam Zhang, raised and nurtured her, and the girl was able to follow their teachings. As she grew older, she attended to her parents' needs and behaved like an adult. She was loving and affectionate with her sister and brothers. She enjoyed reading books, especially the *Classic of Filial Piety* and *The Book of Minor Learning*. Whenever she encountered stories of ancient people who sacrificed their lives for their principles, she would be deeply moved, her chest filled with sighs, her sense of filial piety and righteousness clearly a part of her nature.

In the year Dinghai, the girl was eleven years old. Master Liting sought an official post in Shandong, and the girl gazed at the clouds, weeping, always feeling regret for not being able to serve her parents. In the summer of Xinmao, after Master Liting recovered from his illness, he went to take up his post. As they were about to part, the girl held onto his clothes, her tears flowing profusely. She spoke loudly, saying, "My father is an official of the state, and I only wish for him to be a loyal minister." Master Liting was surprised by her sudden words, and her two brothers also found her words somewhat inappropriate, feeling uneasy and unable to put it aside, though they initially did not understand the depth of her pain. In the autumn of Jiawu, the rebel Wang Lun caused trouble. A relative of the girl, who had fled from Shouzhang County, returned and detailed the events of the upheaval. The girl, upon hearing the news, was shocked and fell from the stairs, in a state of confusion and deep sorrow. She carefully inquired about the details of Master Liting's death and the situation after his passing. At that time, her two brothers were traveling in Shandong, and they encouraged her to set up a memorial and mourn. As she knelt to offer a cup of wine, she let out a long wail, saying, "My father has already been a loyal minister; what more is there to regret! I have not been able to serve my mother, but I will now begin serving my father." At that moment, the family was in a state of confusion, unsure of what to say. After a while, the girl got up and went inside. People were surprised that she had not come out for so long, and when they looked, she had already hanged herself. It was the twenty-fourth day of the tenth month, at the twenty-second watch of the night. She was dressed in multiple layers of mourning clothes, her blood and tears flowing profusely, and those who saw her all wept. Some said, "The righteous daughter, upon hearing of the chaos, was in a state of shock, her soul and spirit scattered, and she often walked around the pillars." Others, placing their hands on their chests, mourned, "He is really dead!"

One night, I dreamed that Master Liti stood in the courtyard in his official ceremonial attire, his face soaked in blood and unrecognizable, saying, "I am fortunate to have something to offer to the country." Alas! The sincerity of filial piety and righteousness truly communicates with spirits, is it truly like this? In matters of literature, she could compose poetry and ancient music without learning, and her small cursive script was also neat and pleasing. This is not enough to make her a notable example of righteousness, but let us discuss how she died for righteousness. Note: The righteous daughter's name was Yulin, and she died at the age of eighteen. On a certain day in the 40th year of the Qianlong reign, the governor-general of Zhejiang, Sanbao, submitted a report. The Ministry of Rites approved the commendation, and an imperial decree was issued: "A filial daughter should be called a righteous daughter." A minister dies for his lord, a daughter dies for her father; the intensity of loyalty and righteousness is concentrated in one family. Record all the details to show that even the smallest creatures are reached, and its origin must have a basis.

Shao Shimei The magistrate of Songling, Shao Shimei, was born with the ability to remember past lives, except for the period before his marriage. At the age of eighteen, he married a woman named Lu, who was gentle, wise, and virtuous. However, she often said, "My life is short, and I cannot serve you for long." Her family found this strange. In 1706, when Lu was twenty, she suddenly said she would die that year. She wept and spoke incoherently, her words difficult to understand. One night, she told Shao, "Do not grieve, for I will die soon, but our fate is not yet ended. In another life, I will die young, and in yet another, I will be your wife again, and we will have children. If you pass the imperial examination in the future, in your first appointment, which is close to home, you will encounter something unusual. In your second appointment, you should return home quickly. Go to a quiet place and practice Daoism for several months, and at that time, we will meet again. If you wish to find me, my house is near a river where two rivers merge into one. The third house on the left side of a slope is where I live, and there is a well in front of the door. My surname is the third character in the family genealogy. I will be almost eighteen, and if you marry me in the second month of a leap year, you will still be able to introduce me to your father-in-law." The next morning, she suddenly bathed, closed the door, and died. Shao was deeply shocked and mourned her. After a year, he remarried. In 1721, he passed the provincial examination, and in 1729, he passed the imperial examination. He attended the selection ceremony and was appointed as a professor in Dengzhou, and soon moved to a position as a teacher in Qixia. When he entered the city wall of Qixia, he felt as if he had been there before, and he was surprised by this. A student named Li Wanzhen came to see him, and Shao recognized him, saying, "I reviewed the register of doctoral students and saw the name Li Keping. I felt as if I had seen his face before. When he entered, he looked exactly like him, so I recognized him." The students considered this strange.

Someone mentioned the beauty of the surrounding mountains, water, and springs, so Shao took his equipment and went. A few miles outside the city gate, a student approached to greet him. Shao immediately asked, "Is your home outside the city?" The student replied, "My home is at the Three Li Inn." Shao suddenly realized and said, "I must have lived in the Three Li Inn in my previous life." At that time, the students and attendants who were watching from the side of the road all stared in shock and could not speak. Shao walked to the inn and looked at the gate, but it was not the same. He said, "The place where I can look down at the city and see the mountain ranges is the right one." The student informed him that there used to be an old Three Li Inn at the site. Shao went back to search for it, gradually getting closer, and said, "This is it." He asked the student, "Is there an old temple here?" The student replied, "No." A few steps up the hill, Shao suddenly saw a dilapidated temple. It was the place where his soul had passed by the temple gate in the past, looking back with a sad gaze, thinking of home. Inside, a statue of a white-bearded deity still stood. When he reached the inn, the houses were still the same, and it was the house of the former Elder High of the East Sea.

When Shao Yi passed away, he left behind three sons and a granddaughter who was married to the Song family. All three sons died young, leaving only two grandsons alive. Shao Yi surveyed the left side of the neighborhood and remembered the past times of leisure, feasts, commercial activities, and rental properties in great detail. The old men of the village said, "The old man used to uphold righteousness and keep his promises. He was known for his straightforward and honest nature.
