If there were any unjust matters among the clan, he would always resolve them according to reason and only then would he stop." Shao Yi asked the old men, "Was there an old man with a beard and a prominent chin who, in the past, sold cloth and demanded payment for a drink? If the payment was not given immediately, he would carefully observe the person's appearance. Did you recognize him too?" They replied, "Yes, we did." Shao Yi then asked, "Where is the big stone at the foot of the city?" They answered, "It has been moved to the city god." He asked further, "Why is the temple's vermilion color so new?" They explained, "It was destroyed by fire, and then rebuilt." Shao Yi inquired, "There is a place called Danzi Xian on the mountain ridge twenty miles away, which is very dangerous and steep. Carrying firewood, walking with trembling hands, is that true?" They confirmed, "Yes, it is." There were many more words, which are not fully recorded here. Shao Yi stayed for several days, managing the family affairs and arranging a marriage for his grandson before departing. Those who heard about it from far and wide could not help but marvel at the unusual events.

In the year Wu Shen, I was appointed to govern the two counties of Wujiang and Zhending, which together encompass the area of Zhenze. The heavy taxation and the harsh methods used to collect it were inevitable, but they were not my preference. Within two months, I resigned due to illness. In the winter of Ji You, I visited Qingyuan for official business. Passing through Guantao, I arrived at a spacious and quiet temple. The walls were lined with Buddhist scriptures. I borrowed a room to read them, and suddenly felt a clarity as if I had read them before. A guest mentioned, "This year has an additional month in the late winter, which will shift the start of the following spring." I reacted with surprise, saying, "Was there a prediction about the leap month?" From then on, I felt uneasy and could not calm down. One day, I rode across the bank of the Wei River with only one attendant. It was evening, and I passed through a slope, arriving at a household where I saw a well. Suddenly, I remembered a previous agreement. I asked the third household, "Do you have a daughter?" They replied, "No." The neighbors gathered to inquire, and upon being told the reason, they were surprised and laughed, causing me to feel disappointed. An old man pointed out, "There is a village a few miles away, which seems similar to this area." I went to the village, reached the door, but found no well. I asked for the family name, which was Xiao, and the age of the daughter, who was 18. I informed the parents, who were angry and said, "This is like a ghost's words, why are you bothering us?" I thought about the two rivers merging into one, and this was where the Wei and Wei rivers meet. I rode back and forth between Qingyuan and Taoyi for several months without seeing anything. I recited a line from a Tang poem, "Bieluo Huangquan," and often sighed and wiped tears from my collar.

Returning to the Jizhou area, he then went again to Qingshui, where he saw a house reflected in the water, with its gate and well still as before. However, the family was of the surname Sun, and the daughter was seventeen years old. Shao, finding the surname unique and fitting, sent a letter to Taigong. Taigong drove a cow to the place, urging them to marry. But the girl's parents strongly resisted, and Taigong left in anger. Shao's steps became slow and hesitant. He passed through Guantao again, where he met an old man on the road. The old man bowed and said, "I have found it." He led Shao there. Following the stream and the slope, they came to a house with a well lined with stones, which was the third house, and the family was of the surname Dong. Shao straightened his clothes and entered. Mr. Dong invited him to sit and said, "The matchmaker had proposed several times, but the girl firmly refused. Who would have thought that a noble gentleman would remain steadfast in his promise?" He then happily agreed to the marriage. Shao's Taigong, hearing this, immediately arranged the wedding ceremony and sent a gift of a bird. It was in the month of a certain year in the Gengxu cycle. Shao had no children, and he was particularly concerned that the third character of the surname was not harmonious. Later, he read the "Thousand Surnames Comprehensive Spectrum," which arranges surnames by rhyme. The first is "Dong" in the upper level, the second is "Xian" in the lower level, and "Dong" in the upper tone is the third.

The historian remarks: The following is a record by my great-great-grandfather, Wu Changgeng, a historian. The text is purely narrative, concise, and varied. The beauty of the writing lies in its fragmented nature, surpassing the works of Han Yu and those who followed him. If Shao Gong could know past events, it would indeed be strange, yet such stories are also heard in the world. As for Lady Lu, she could know events three generations into the future. Is such a story unheard in legends? If the subject is strange, how could the text not be strange? I, who am already full of sorrow, am deeply moved. After reading, one realizes that what is called "wise and virtuous people" are simply "people of deep emotion." I want to present this text to the Moon God, asking him to expand his spiritual roots to fill the sky of separation and sorrow, so that all people in love will always be united. Will he agree to my request? As for Cai Zhi, the historian from Deqing, he claims that in a past life he was an elderly woman from Shaoqiao in Hangzhou. She was widowed at a young age and devoted to Buddhism, relying on her son-in-law for a living. On the brink of death, she revived and told her daughter, "I will be reborn into the Cai family. Since the Buddha's image has not yet been destroyed, I am returning temporarily. Please burn it for me, to fulfill my final wish." She then spoke in detail about the Cai family's residence and lineage: "We are very poor, but fortunately, I was born a man. Your husband will come to see me in the future." She then passed away. After her burial, her son-in-law went to visit and met Cai Taigong, informing him of the matter. Cai Taigong then showed his son, who was only a few months old, and the child looked at him and smiled, as if they were old acquaintances. As Cai Zhi grew up, he did not forget his past life and often spoke of it to others. Therefore, he still abstains from meat and alcohol, adhering strictly to the Buddhist precepts.

Se Bo Nian In the early years of the Yongzheng era, there was a man named Shen Bo Nian in our county, skilled in martial arts and brave, and excellent at leaping and jumping. One day, a household near the north side of Yinzijiao Bridge in the market caught fire, spreading southward almost half a mile. Only a building facing south on the southern bank was spared from the fire. The windows and doors were wide open, and inside, a young woman with a youthful appearance and tender teeth was sitting sideways on the edge of the bed inside a mosquito net, smiling as if in conversation with someone. From the bridge northward, the fire raged uncontrollably, blocking all paths. Firefighters called out to her from the bridge, but the woman remained calm and motionless. At that time, Bo Nian was also on the bridge. Across the river, flames were shooting, and the eaves were already on fire. He immediately leaped into the building through the flames. He saw a woman beside her, wearing a red robe, with fiery hair and beard, holding her wrists as if restraining her. Bo Nian could not pull her away, so he picked up a chair from beside the bed and struck the red-bearded figure on the head, who suddenly disappeared. He then carried the woman out of the building. Afterward, Bo Nian returned home, his entire body purple and swollen, crying out in pain. The next day, he called in a doctor to examine him. The doctor said, "The fire poison has already affected his heart, and there is no cure." But Bo Nian cried out and shifted in pain, and within half a meal's time, he was dead.

Last winter, early in the season, a certain nephew of Mr. Menglu happened to go to Beima. On the way, a northern wind suddenly arrived, and the cold air attacked him. In the boat, he suddenly fell ill, talking incessantly. The boatman hurriedly took him back home. The family surrounded and guarded him, but he could not sleep all night. Her voice was soft and gentle, but she used obscene words and displayed indecent behavior, which was so disgusting that people could not bear to look directly at her. When his brother Zhitang arrived, he slept soundly, but acted as usual when he came out. When asked, he said, "This strong and vigorous person has a manly spirit, not as warm and lovely as Lang." Wizards and doctors came in succession, but ultimately, it was ineffective. The whole family was at a loss.

After several days, an elderly woman from the village heard and came to visit immediately. She smiled and bowed deeply, saying, "Mother, today the good wind has blown here." While speaking, she reached into her skirt and began to fondle him. The elderly woman saw her cute and innocent appearance and then spoke kindly to persuade her to leave. She replied, "I and my husband have a predestined relationship, and my husband is not a jealous person. I feel so happy here that I don't want to leave." The elderly woman said, "Then I will arrange the marriage for the two of you. After the wedding ceremony, I will send the two of you back together. How about that?" She laughed and said, "If so, then Mom is giving a gift of beauty to a beautiful woman. How could I forget such great kindness!" The elderly woman then told her mother, made a statue of her with rice flour, cut her hair and nails and stuck them on the statue, and also bought flowers, candles, and other items, placing them in the main hall. She called four musicians to play music to celebrate the wedding. At that time, she was in the room, suddenly woke up as if from a dream, only groaning and calling out in exhaustion. The elderly woman then ordered her to paint her face with coal, cut her hair and stuck it under her chin, making a pensive expression. Just as she finished, she suddenly saw her eyes stand up and exclaimed in surprise, "Why did you suddenly change like this?" After she spoke, she made no further sound, as if the illness had vanished. Then they bound a colorful sedan chair with reeds, placed the statue inside, carried it in a boat, and played music to send it to its place and returned. She took care of her for half a month before she recovered.

The Historian said: The tears of the Xiang River goddesses transformed into bamboo, and the river named after the jealous woman are both expressions of profound emotion, yet they are also fueled by deep, hidden resentment. As for Chen San's young lady, it is said that she engaged in sexual misconduct before marriage, and thus was drowned by her parents. Even today, she still causes harm as a spirit. If she truly has the power of a ghost or deity, she should not be allowed to harm people in this way. If it is said that she does not exist, then where did this demon come from? I say: She was a beautiful woman around 18 or 19 years old, graceful and alluring, almost like a willow tree hanging down like smoke, or a peach tree with dew on its petals. When she walked gracefully on water, her embroidered socks and slender feet were truly captivating. At that moment, it was truly worth dying for. As for me, nearing middle age, with a flushed face like ochre, my appearance is far from being graceful and charming. How unusual is my encounter with her? However, since I have personally witnessed this, and there are others who have also encountered her harm, it seems that she does not have time to choose her victims. She is truly a demon in the realm of beauty.

Mr. Lu Xingcha was in Guangdong. One day, he attended an early morning meeting at the prefectural office. The sun was high in the sky when the vice-governor emerged. Five colleagues had already presented themselves, and after the formalities, the vice-governor sat down on the kang (a traditional heated bed). Before he could speak, a newly appointed official who had just arrived stood up and asked, "May I ask, Your Excellency, what county you come from?" The vice-governor replied, "My native county is Daxing." The official then asked, "Which prefecture does that county belong to?" The vice-governor answered, "Shuntian." The official nodded in acknowledgment. A moment later, he asked, "And what is Your Excellency's surname?" The vice-governor replied, "Among the Manchus, there are no surnames." After answering, he chuckled and asked, "What is the local climate and soil like?" The official replied, "Our county has very few local products, but there are many monkeys in the mountains." The vice-governor asked, "How big are these monkeys?" The official answered, "The small ones are no bigger than a child, but the large ones are as big as a person." Here, the term "大人" refers to a common person, but the official unknowingly offended the vice-governor. The other officials all suppressed their laughter. The vice-governor turned pale with anger and said, "Does this person also think he can be a leader of the people?" That very day, he ordered the official to resign on the grounds of illness and return to his hometown. Alas, this official, how could he understand what "大人" means? How dare he be so impertinent! Yet, I have seen officials today who lose their positions for speaking a single word that displeases their superiors. Their superiors are not just monkeys wearing hats, but tigers wearing hats. Alas for this official, who could not flatter his way to power and instead foolishly compared himself to a hare or a rat.

Cloud and Rain The phrase "morning as a wandering cloud, evening as a wandering rain" is not found in Cao Yu's *Rhapsody*, nor is there a clear explanation of it. However, later generations have taken it to mean words of male and female pleasure, though they do not seek a thorough understanding. When heaven sends rain, it is because the yin and yang are in harmony, and clouds bring rain. At this time, the qi of heaven descends, and the qi of earth rises, hence the saying: "Heaven and earth are interwoven, and male and female unite in essence." The *Yijing Commentary* uses these two phrases to form a coherent text, drawing a parallel to the union of heaven and earth. Someone asked: "Then during the time of cloud and rain, there could also be a wife on top and a husband below, what does this mean?" I replied: "This is precisely what is meant by 'turning clouds upside down and rain over'!" The guest laughed. Spring River Gentleman According to *The Garden of Leisurely Talk*, the Spring River Gentleman was handsome like a beautiful woman but did not get along with women. He liked to associate with young men, sometimes sleeping and waking up together, without knowing whether the crow was male or female. He once wrote a poem: "People have different temperaments, trees have different branches and leaves. Rather be the husband of Lady Xu, than the concubine of Zi Du." His father, the Governor, saw it and was angry. The Gentleman then wrote another poem: "The codes established by Duke Zhou, what deep and subtle intentions they have. There are temples for virtuous women, but none for virtuous men." The Governor laughed and said: "Have you reached such a level of twisting words and winning arguments?"

In the year of the ox, he entered the Hanlin Academy and once watched a play at the Temple of Heaven's Luck. A certain captain mistook an actor for a common performer and teased him. People felt it was unfair, but the young man said, "Those who are familiar with me, love me. Haven't you read about Yanzi's punishment of Yu in the *Spring and Autumn Annals of Yanzi*? Alas, that person was not my match. To be angry would be vulgar." This can be considered a skillful way to defuse tension, but I wonder what the point or interest of this matter is. These people truly do not know what is in their hearts. As a result, he playfully composed a judgment: From ancient times, men and women living together is the great principle of human relations; the union of husband and wife is the proper rule for having a family. Yet they attempt to cultivate barren land, abandoning the proper path and not following it, calling upon colleagues and marrying a sworn brother, recruiting actors to play with children. Thus, there are women with the appearance of men, willingly crouching like a rabbit; not knowing how to look over one's shoulder, they wish to emulate the Dragon Youth. Clearing this silk field, they must surely be mountain-clearing strongmen; carving out a path of the birds, they come to ask the fisherman for directions across the river. Suddenly, the buttocks grow a shovel handle, surely teaching them to consult and hesitate; the head should wear osmanthus, it is imagined that it cannot be approached closely. City walls by the ditch (as noted by Cheng Mianzhu in *The Songs of Zheng*).

The poem "Zi Jin," one chapter of which is traditionally interpreted as a love poem between two men, what harm is there in arousing clouds and rain? In the hidden corners and carriages, one should not hesitate to teach lust and lead to theft ("hidden corners" alludes to the Qin palace; "carriages" allude to Feng Zidu, both referring to those who are favored both inside and outside). Minor offenses may result in the dismissal of a supervisor, while major ones can lead to the cutting of sleeves and the downfall of a nation. Actions that displease the people and reverse the natural order are thus at their extreme. Indulgence in lust is illegal; why not release semen by hand? Both are strong males (as in the poem by Emperor Wu of Chen: "Who would worry about two strong males being together? The golden fox fur should yield to me"), who would say that barren land cannot be entered? *Strange Tales from a Chinese Studio* states: One should cut off the root of his probing and block the path of sending and receiving. An experienced official decides cases clearly and decisively. I believe that since he is already fond of the outside, it will be difficult to guard an empty room. There is also a magpie's nest; it is better to let a jay take over. That mischievous young man, perhaps he itches intensely; he can carry a bee sting instead of a fly's drill. Then in the wild mandarin duck society, perhaps the tortoise mirror will always be clear; in dreams of yellow eels, there is no need to bother with old affairs. Frost and Rime On the early part of the first month in the Chinese lunar calendar, someone who gets up early sees the whole field of grass and trees covered in white, like falling jade powder, like hanging ribbons; on people's braids, all forming into pearl-like clusters. The time is extremely cold, and there is still some leftover snow. Villagers are passing it on, calling it "plang cloud." According to the *Mao Shi*, the character "plang" is found in the phrase "rain and snow are plang." The pronunciation of the character "plang" is "pǔ láng" or "pū láng." The appearance of rain and snow, how can it be called the thing that the sky rains down?

The *Nanfeng Collection* contains a poem titled "Eulogy of Rime Ice" (the character for "淞" is used in the poem, but it is written as "松" in the *History of the Song Dynasty: Events in Chronological Order*). This phenomenon occurs in the north where it is bitterly cold at night, and when fog rises, it adheres to trees and forms beads of ice, hence the term "rime ice." It is a sign of a bountiful harvest. What village farmers see today is likely this very phenomenon. I once read in the *Xibao Pavilion Collection* a long poem titled "Observations on a Journey to Xin Cheng," which says: "Some say that signs of good fortune are abundant in the granaries, with thousands of carts loaded with grain and hemp. Others say that this misfortune frightens officials, as there are ghosts who wish to spy on the homes of the high and mighty." Thus, whether it is good or bad fortune cannot be determined. During the Xinsi year of the Chongzhen era, there was a major epidemic along the Yangtze River. There was a family where dozens of people died, lying one upon another. Anyone who came into contact with the air of the epidemic would die. Among the students, Master Chen Junshan and his family—father, mother, and children—five people died in one night. No relatives or neighbors dared to approach their door. Wang Yuxi alone stood firm and said, "If we do not act as teachers and disciples should, how can we bear to sit idly by!" He then led several beggars inside and buried each person in a coffin. One child was still in swaddling clothes and had already died, but still showed a slight breath. Wang Yuxi personally carried him out, administered medicine, and nursed him back to life. Chen's family was thus saved, and Wang Yuxi remained unharmed. Is it not that when people act out of benevolence, heaven cannot even restrain them?

Seventeen years later, the plague struck again. There were cases of people who, without being ill, would suddenly spit blood and die. People began to pray to spirits and deities, each family setting up incense altars, lighting sky lanterns, and performing plays and holding competitions, to the utmost of the bizarre and wonderful. The officials and soldiers in the temple were all replaced by living people. At times, the voice of the deity could be heard shouting in the air, along with the sounds of shackles and beatings. This continued for nearly a month. As recorded in the *Fujian County Gazetteer*, this matches the old saying that during a great plague in the capital, after midday, people and ghosts would mix on the streets, their voices audible and their shadows visible. Many paper money were collected in shops, so water basins were set up, and people were ordered to throw money into them. **Floods** According to the *Fujian County Gazetteer*, in the thirty-sixth year of the Wanli era, there was a great flood that submerged the higher fields, and the residents of the city all built elevated structures to live on. Fish, shrimp, snails, and clams filled the rooms, and one could bend down to pick them up from under the bed. The *Wuqing Gazetteer* also records that the water of that year was so high that boats could navigate on land. In the third year of the Daoguang era, from May onward, the streets and markets of the prefecture and counties saw much travel by boat. In the countryside, there were cases of people who, while sleeping soundly, fell into the water and died after rolling over in bed. Other houses and tombs were swept away by the flood, and the number was too great to count. On the last day of July, two monks from Tangxi were carried by the flood overnight to the front of the Baishizhi Yongfeng Temple. The temple monks rescued them, and both were unharmed, as the two monks were skilled in swimming.

At the time, I was a guest at Lake Tai, but I could not return home. By the end of the Mid-Autumn Festival, the water level had dropped somewhat, yet the boat could not yet pass the market bridge. Taking a detour through the fields, I finally arrived home, where there was still shallow water in the hall. Fish and shrimp swam and leaped everywhere, and water lilies and algae covered the walls. The water mark on the pillars was almost four feet high. How does this compare to the water levels during the Wanli era? There was also a legendary figure known as the "Valley Fairy." According to Niu Yuqiao's "Gu Sheng," he described a ship carved from a date pit, with the story of Su Dongpo's visit to the Red Cliff inscribed on it, marveling at the divine craftsmanship. The "Kunyu Waiji" records that in the Hot Spring Kingdom of Mariana, the people were skilled in their work, able to fit a self-striking clock inside a ring. Recently, there has been a clock made on a pendant stone, an object said to be crafted by the Ghost People, truly ingenious to the point of surpassing nature. Once, I saw a Valley Fairy created by the Fei family in Daochang Mountain in the county. It was carved from a small piece of camphor wood, shaped like a grain of rice, split into two halves, and carved into the figure of Lady Magu, a fairy. The head, belly, hands, and feet were all hollow and intricate, with beautiful eyebrows and eyes, finely detailed trousers and jacket, and sharp, long fingernails, all clearly defined and lifelike, rivaling even the famous miniature figures of Jie Zhou. Some say this object can ward off fire disasters. At that time, a certain official in the county tried to buy it for seven hundred gold pieces but failed to obtain it.

A White Magpie My father once told me: In the village, there was a white willow tree on the south bank of the Flower Family's board bridge. In the past, a white magpie came to roost at the top of the tree. The tree was large enough to provide shade for several acres. At that time, all the sparrows that followed it gathered around the tree almost completely. Until dusk, the white magpie flew away, and the other sparrows then scattered like falling leaves. Some say that this magpie was the king of the birds. Yang Zhuo, while in Ziqing, once saw a swallow's nest in a commoner's house. After the swallows had fled, one day various wild birds flew into the courtyard, gradually gathering on the rafters without leaving any space. The cook served food in the hall, and all the dishes were picked up and taken away by the birds. The old man could not predict whether this was a good or bad omen, so he hit the swallow's nest with a stick. Immediately, a white phoenix chick appeared, about three inches long, and fell out of the nest. Before it reached the ground, it suddenly flew out through the door, heading southwest and ascending into the sky. The other birds also scattered and flew away at the same time, and soon they were all gone. It is said that all birds follow the phoenix, like the rivers and streams that all flow into the sea. Perhaps this white magpie was a rare sighting of the phoenix chick?

The Turtle King From the *Jinhua Zi Zabian*: A turtle with straight central stripes is called the "Thousand Miles" turtle. Near its head, on the first level of its horizontal stripes, there are diagonal lines that connect to the "Thousand Miles" pattern, which is the mark of the Turtle King. When ordinary turtles are examined, none possess this pattern. Once, Huang Kun led a naval force to Guangnan, preparing to cross a small sea. Suddenly, a military officer found a small glass bottle, about the size of a child's palm, in a shallow part of the water. Inside the bottle was a small turtle, about an inch long, moving back and forth and spinning within it. The neck of the bottle was extremely narrow, and it was unclear how the turtle had entered. The officer took the bottle and kept it. That night, he suddenly felt the ship leaning heavily on one side. Upon looking, he saw many turtles piling up and climbing onto the ship. He was greatly alarmed, fearing that crossing the sea might bring unforeseen disaster. He then took the bottle he had kept, made a wish, and threw it into the sea. The turtles then dispersed. Later, he spoke to a foreigner on a sea vessel, who said, "This is what is called a 'Turtle Treasure,' a rare and spiritual creature. It is regrettable that you encountered it but could not possess it, for you are not a person of great fortune. If you had obtained it and kept it at home, why would you worry about your wealth not being abundant?" The foreigner sighed with regret. Could this not be the so-called Turtle King? Otherwise, why did so many turtles follow it?

Xue Jianyang Xue Jianyang, a man from Wu, lived in the Zuanzhu Lane. He was a loafer who drank and gambled, and his nature was extremely cruel and cunning. The villagers called him East Governor, and he particularly enjoyed seducing women. His neighbor, Li, had a concubine named Yang, who was extremely beautiful. Xue was attracted to her and often waited for her to draw water, then approached her to talk. Yang, being quiet and gentle, refused to respond. Xue had no way to persuade her, so he thought that Li was poor and could be tempted with money. He waited for Li to be in a difficult situation and then gave him money and rice. Li, though from an old family, was poor but maintained his integrity and disliked Xue's usual behavior, so he rejected the gifts. Xue left in shame and pointed to Li's door, saying, "Even if you lock her in an iron cabinet, I will eventually take her away."

Late in the summer, during a humid, hot rainstorm, Li was asleep when he was suddenly struck by lightning. His wife was awakened by the commotion and saw a flash of lightning, noticing a figure resembling the Thunder God, who flapped his wings and broke through the door. She saw a hole in Li's forehead, about half an inch wide and several inches deep, with blood gushing out. At first, Li cried out in pain, but soon after, he died. She wailed loudly, and Li's mother was also startled awake, touching his body and weeping bitterly, saying, "My son, who always lived a life of diligence and propriety, what wrong did he commit to offend Heaven?" She then turned to Yang, saying, "Our family has never had even a small amount of savings. Now we have suddenly encountered this disaster. How will we manage the funeral expenses, let alone the food for the future?" After speaking, she collapsed in grief. At that moment, the neighbors all gathered, and Xue also rushed in, seeing everyone standing idly and sighing. He rolled up his sleeves and said, "If we all stand by and do nothing, how can we call ourselves neighbors!" The mother thanked him with tears. Xue hurried back and brought thirty taels of silver, telling the mother, "With this money, the funeral expenses can be roughly covered, but you must sign a promissory note, and we can arrange repayment at a later date." The mother then signed the note and handed it to him. Xue continued to manage the funeral arrangements. The wife and mother repeatedly thanked him, and Xue finally left.

From then on, the elderly and weak sat alone, weeping together, sometimes going without food for days. Even in the severe cold, his wife still wore clothes as white as snow. The promissory note he had signed had reached its due date, so Xue went to demand repayment. The mother came out, tears streaming down her face, and agreed to repay it the following summer. Xue reluctantly agreed and left. When the time came, the mother again asked for an extension, but Xue did not respond, changed color, and stood up to leave. A short while later, he returned with his associate, Mr. Jia, who looked sullen as he entered. He took out the contract and threw it to Mr. Jia, saying, "You have already guaranteed this contract. If you have the ability to pay, then pay it. Otherwise, I will report to the officials. Do not worry about being involved." Mr. Jia pretended to plead on her behalf, but Xue did not allow it and took the contract, wanting to leave. Mr. Jia strongly held him back and said to the mother, "Given your old and feeble state, and your weak and frail condition, you are already worried about surviving until tomorrow. When can this debt be repaid? Now that Xue has been unmarried for a long time, it would be better to give your wife to him. You can get extra money to support your mother in her remaining years, and your wife can also have a place to eat. Wouldn't this be a win-win situation?" The mother sadly went inside to discuss with her wife. When the wife heard this, she silently wept without saying a word. After a long time, she choked up and said, "My short life is not worth pitying, but what about my old mother?" The mother cried, "The matter has come to this. Can I still consider myself?" She then went out to tell Mr. Jia. Mr. Jia discussed with Xue, made a new contract and guaranteed it, took the old contract, and burned it before leaving. By evening, an ornate carriage arrived, and the wife hastily got into it, crying bitterly as she left.

After returning to Xue, I began to reflect on the situation. In the room, only the boxes and chests belonging to the wife were allowed to be managed by her, while the rest were not permitted to be opened or looked at. One day, when Xue went elsewhere, the wife sat alone. Suddenly, she heard a rustling sound coming from one of the chests, as if a mouse were playing and chasing inside it. She got up and went to the back to remove the hinges, opened it, and found no other items inside, only a mask of the Thunder God, with two wings clearly visible, an axe and chisel all prepared, still bearing bloodstains. She was utterly shocked and finally realized what happened last summer, which was done by Xue. She told others about it, and they all agreed. So they all carried the chest to the officials. The officials arrested Xue and interrogated him, and he finally confessed the truth. It turned out that Xue had been planning this for a long time. That night, taking advantage of the thunderstorm, he sneaked in, hid under the bed, waited for her to sleep, and then secretly attacked her, quickly and directly fleeing, so that no one knew whether she was dead or alive. The official was surprised and said, "In the past, Pei Xie could create a three-mile fog, but later he was beaten for using the fog to commit crimes, yet he only did it to commit crimes. Now you want to become clouds and rain, but you first struck down your husband with lightning—how cunning and wicked you are!" So he ordered that Xue's hands be tied behind his back, and he was made to wear a mask, with two wings inserted under his armpits, an axe and chisel in his hands. He was dragged around all six gates, and then he was executed. Person A was charged with collusion and sent to military service.

The Yang woman returned home, shamed and disgraced, and hanged herself to death. Note: This was an event that occurred on a certain day in July of the sixteenth year of the Daoguang reign. From *The Unrecorded* (*Buke Lu*): A man from Qianshan became enamored with a neighbor's wife and attempted to court her, but she refused. When her husband was ill, and a great storm and heavy rain raged, the man donned a two-winged flowered robe and leapt into the neighbor's house, striking her down with an iron mallet, then leapt back out. Later, he sent a matchmaker to request the wife's hand in marriage, and the couple became very devoted to each other. One day, the wife opened her chest and saw the strange clothes, finding them unusual. Her husband laughed and explained the matter. The wife pretended to be amused, waited for him to leave, then took the clothes and went to the authorities to report him. He was sentenced to be hanged. On the day of his execution, another great storm and rain broke out, as if the sky itself were splitting open. Xue (or Xie) had followed the same method.

**Examination** Peng Yunmei, the Minister of Rites, oversaw the examination of students from the three prefectures under the jurisdiction of the Hu (Lake) region in Zhejiang Province. He posed the questions "没齿," "被发," and "易牙" for the students. Some students copied printed texts. Only Zhang Guisen from Gu'an searched for and bought almost all the copied versions after the examination, so he did not have time to check them in the office, and thus he was granted a stipend. Those who copied verbatim were placed in the second rank; those who made mistakes were placed in the third rank. Everyone laughed at this. When the results were announced, the Minister called the students near his desk and said, "I once oversaw education in a certain province. At the time of the examination, after the names were called, I withdrew to sit upstairs. I was about to write down the question, when a teacher suddenly approached and said, 'Report to the great person, this place is near the barbarians, and there has never been an example of writing an essay here.' I was startled and asked, 'Then what is examined?' The teacher replied, 'A single couplet is sufficient, but the word should not be too many, only one character is enough.' I was initially surprised and sighed, but there was nothing I could do, so I gave them the character '柴' (wood). Thus, the students all frowned and shook their heads, and by midday, one student came forward to submit his answer sheet."

Looking at the left side, I saw the character for "carbon" (炭). The teacher standing beside me said, "This scroll should be placed first." I thought that pairing 'carbon' with 'wood' (柴) should not make it the best, so why place it first? Suddenly, another person came to submit, and on the left side, they added the character for 'wood' (柴). I was angry and was going to scold him, but the teacher said, "Sir, please do not be angry, this scroll can already be placed second." I replied angrily, "This person still has the character for 'wood' (柴), how can it be placed second?" The teacher said, "If Sir does not believe, please look at the rest, and forget about this 'wood' (柴) character." After that, no one came to submit, and I began to marvel at the truth of his words. Now, you all, who have memorized and studied since childhood, copy without missing a single character, and your memory is good. Otherwise, the daily effort of reviewing old material is also commendable. Therefore, I will place it in the second class to encourage diligent readers." He then pointed to two others and said, "If your scrolls have too many omissions and errors, I think this style has not been played for a long time, so you should review it again in the future. If next year you still make the same mistakes, you will be placed in the lowest class and will not escape forty strokes." So he ordered the attendants to give him a copy of the text he had copied and then dismissed him.

In the past, an official was overseeing education in our province of Zhejiang. During the autumn of the Ren Xu year, he visited the county towns to conduct the examination for selecting candidates for the imperial examination. There was an essay titled "The Theory of Three in One." The entire examination hall only had one scroll from a student named Shao, which was based on the *Book of Han* to establish the argument. When the results were announced and he went to pay respects, the official explained to him saying: "The *Book of Han* states: 'The Supreme Ultimate and primordial qi contain three as one. The Supreme Ultimate is the center. Primordial qi is the beginning. It moves through the twelve earthly branches, first moving at Zi, then reaching Chou and becoming three, then reaching Yin and becoming nine, then reaching Mao and becoming twenty-seven, and through the twelve branches, it reaches seventeen thousand seven hundred and forty. This is the union of yin and yang, where qi is concentrated at Zi, transforming and generating all things.' But this is actually a method for calculating the length of the calendar cycle. Historians, seeing its complexity and depth, could not understand its use, and thus said: 'This is the union of yin and yang, transforming and generating all things.' Once, someone found a wooden mallet in the ground, brought it home to show the neighbors, and they were all surprised and shocked. Later, a scholar passed by and saw it, saying: 'This is a spiritual object. I have heard that Fangfeng had a body two zhang long, with bones so large they could fill a cart, and this is his shin bone.' The villagers were all delighted, built a temple to worship it, calling it the Shin Temple. Ban Gu's argument is similar to the Shin Temple. Now, your argument seems to be influenced by Ban Gu's error. However, at this time, even the Five Classics are rarely read in their entirety, let alone others. But you know the source of this question, so we can appreciate each other beyond the mundane world." Indeed, at that time, there was no other candidate in the examination hall who knew the source of the question.

There was a hunter from Tengyue who carried a wooden hut while traveling in the mountains. One day, he reached Moduan Mountain and suddenly saw a group of foxes and rabbits, numbering in the dozens, emerging in a crowd from the deep, dense forest. Following them, bears, tigers, civets, and elephants appeared, with their ears laid back and tails hanging down, running wildly one after another, as if being driven by something. The hunter found it strange and stopped by the roadside to secretly observe. After a while, he saw something that looked like a gibbon, but it was no more than four feet tall, with long hair, golden eyes, and a body covered in white fur. It came slowly from behind. The hunter quickly opened the window and fired a musket at the creature. With smoke, it rushed to the front of the house and used its hands to push the boards up twice. After seeing that there was no one inside, it left. The hunter watched it go far away, then went out to look at where it had pushed. It had already sunk in about an inch. What was not pierced was only as thick as a coin. He was greatly frightened, quickly went into the house, and carried it back. From then on, he did not dare to go there again, but he did not know what kind of animal it was.

A Strange Snake I was in the mountains of Hexi one summer night when I heard a tiger roar. The next day, I told people about it. They asked, "Did the sound shake the roof tiles?" I replied, "No." One person said, "Then it was not a tiger, but a snake. In the past, I used to walk in the mountains and suddenly heard a tiger's roar, which was very close by, and I was extremely frightened. Looking up, I saw a snake hanging upside down from a tree and making a sound. The snake was not more than a zhang in length and was covered in yellow spots. Every summer, it is seen, and people in the mountains often encounter it. When it makes a sound, it will rain heavily. However, the sound of a nearby tiger's roar can shake the roof tiles, but the snake's sound does not." I have heard that there is a snake in the borderlands of Menglang. Every day, it must climb a tree, fall, and break into pieces like powder. Soon after, it reassembles itself and slithers away. Its vital energy is strong and must be released. It is captured and used as a medicine for setting bones and treating injuries, and it is particularly effective. The *Dianqian Jilüe* says: A fragile snake is found in the Tusi region, over a foot in length. It lies in the grass and, when it sees a person, it jumps up, breaks into several pieces, and quickly reassembles itself. Its color is like white gold, bright and lovely. If picked up by mistake, it will cause death. The snake has a regular pattern of movement, and hunters place bamboo tubes near the path, thinking it is a den, and the snake enters it. If grabbed quickly, it remains intact, but if grabbed slowly, it breaks apart. It is dried and used to treat madness, and different parts of the snake are used to treat different body parts, all are effective, and it can also be used to set broken bones. This is the snake I am referring to.

There are also round snakes, which resemble stone eggs, with spotted and lovely patterns. If one mistakenly holds one, it absorbs human qi and immediately transforms into a snake. When it bites someone, they die instantly, and their corpse cannot be touched. Within a five-mile radius, people dare not travel, as being exposed to its foul odor causes swelling and death. The Miao people wait three days, then insert bamboo arrows into the place of death and collect it after seven days. Those hit by it die immediately. This snake's transformations become more fantastic, and its poison becomes more potent. There are also flat snakes, which look like cowhide. They are five inches tall and two feet long and wide. Their color is yellow and black, their movement is like an arrow, and their exhalation is like cooking smoke, with an unbearable foul odor. When they see a person, they immediately spray black water from their back, and those hit die instantly. They are found near Chu Mountain in the western part of Guangdong province. There are also flat snakes, which are five inches wide, five feet long, and one inch thick. Both ends are flat, their color is like felted cloth, and they have five colors interwoven into patterns and a square shape. Those who do not know it might mistake it for a combing and washing cloth. Their mouth is very large, their movement is like flying, and they can chase cunning rabbits. They are occasionally found in the mountains of Guangxi province and near the South China Sea. (The above two snakes are found in *The Book of Snakes*.)

The Weighing Snake According to popular belief, there is a snake known as the weighing snake. Those who are weighed by it are destined to die. When I was sixteen, I accompanied my younger brother Zai Xi to visit our maternal uncle, Jinkuaiting, at his home in the East Gate. After visiting him and returning, we reached the area west of the Great Mercy Bridge. Suddenly, I heard a splash behind me and turned to see a snake on the ground, raising its head and rushing towards us with incredible speed. Its body was covered in star-like spots that resembled a scale. It rose about four or five feet off the ground, with only its hind part touching the earth, moving as swiftly as the wind. Both my brother and I were so terrified that our souls seemed to fly away. We ran wildly until we reached the Zhao Family Tomb, where we finally dared to look back, but the snake was nowhere to be seen. Upon returning home, I asked my mother about it, and she told me it was indeed a weighing snake. Later, during late winter, my brother fell ill and eventually passed away in the spring of the following year, at the age of twelve. Even now, when I recall that incident, my heart still beats rapidly with fear. It is said that the snake measures people. If its length exceeds that of the person, the person will die. The method to deal with it is as follows: when the snake stands up, pick up any object nearby and throw it into the air, calling out, "Your length is not as great as mine." The snake will then turn over and lie down, stretching out its feet in a thousand directions. You must then remove your hair and show it, saying, "Your many feet are not as numerous as my many hairs." The snake will then retract its feet and lie flat on the ground. Immediately, cut all the ties and belts from your body, calling out, "I am leaving!" The snake will surely die. This method is described in Li Xuguang's *Taiwan Zaji* (Record of Taiwan), but unfortunately, I did not know about it at the time.

**Famous Physician** Wu was a renowned physician in the region of Hezhong. In his youth, he once studied commerce at a herbal shop. When he grew older, he briefly delved into medical texts. Later, after becoming unemployed and feeling aimless, he took up the practice of medicine to earn a living. In a certain village, a wealthy man, in his old age, had a son who was only seven years old and fell ill. At first, the boy simply lost his appetite, and gradually became emaciated, with his chest and abdomen swelling; soon, the swelling was as large as a leather wine bag. After a year of treatment, all medicines proved ineffective, and the father could only helplessly weep. Wu learned of this and went directly to his door. By then, it was already evening, so he stayed overnight there. The man came out to inquire about his name, and Wu feigned being a physician returning from treating a patient in another village, passing through this place, and dared to ask the elder for temporary shelter. Upon hearing this, the man was pleased and invited Wu inside to examine his son’s illness. After the examination, Wu stepped forward and said, “This illness I can cure, but I must be paid a thousand gold, and you must not allow other doctors to interfere with the treatment, for they would hinder my work.” The man carefully agreed to all conditions. Wu then requested to see the prescriptions the boy had previously taken, made slight modifications, compiled a new formula, and gave it to him. Upon receiving it, the man almost believed it to be a life-saving elixir. He then invited Wu to stay in his home for convenient and timely examinations. However, after taking several doses, the boy still showed no improvement. When questioned, Wu boldly replied, “The illness has been present for years; could it be cured in a short time? If you insist on a quick recovery, then you should consult a more skilled physician!” The man repeatedly apologized, and from then on, he served Wu with even greater reverence.

Wu Ming knew he was unable to serve, and planned to flee back home. Yet, being attached to his current situation, he thought of obtaining another place to spend a few days. One day, he went out to stroll in the fields and suddenly saw a mushroom as big as a fan. He thought this was a rare treasure and picked it up, putting it in his pocket. He hurried back, called the old man out, and gave it to him, saying, "The medicine your son is taking should have used this as an ingredient. Fortunately, I found this now. Isn't this a gift from heaven?" He then ordered the old man to take it and cook it into medicine to take. About the time it takes to cook rice, the son's stomach thundered, and he was extremely painful, almost dying. Soon after, he had a severe diarrhea, passing several dou of black blood, with a lump of blood clots in it. Carefully examining, they saw something wrapped in hair, as hard as iron. The son's abdomen had shrunk back to its original size, and the illness was gone. The old man was wildly joyful and ran to tell others. He also said, "From now on, the life of the dog child comes from the master's gift. But still, ask to stay a few more days to treat and restore to health, only then can we prepare a proper farewell and send you back." Wu Ming made a difficult face, but the old man agreed to pay three thousand gold, and finally agreed. However, he still didn't understand the reason. The next day, he returned to the place, dug and looked at it, seeing its root growing on a broken comb. He then realized that the object wrapped in hair must wait for this to understand. Nevertheless, Wu Ming's name became widely known from then on. At home, his door was always crowded like a market, and when he went out, he had to pay one coin per li. In a few years, he became wealthy with millions.

Later, Mr. Shen of Dongxi suffered from a long-standing condition of weakness and timidity, and always used ginseng and aconite in his medicine. In the autumn of the year Guiyou (1723), he suddenly contracted summer malaria and again invited Wu to treat him. Wu diagnosed him and believed that his long-standing weakness indicated a yin condition, so he prescribed Fuzi Lizhong Decoction. Shen drank it and became violently agitated, chewing and breaking his fingers. He immediately ordered to pour snow water down his throat, but the tea spoon was also bitten in half. Shortly thereafter, he died. Wu fled back home, and Shen's entire family was very angry and planned to take legal action. Upon hearing this, Wu was terrified and died from taking raw opium.

There is a person who can balance objects on their nose. Whenever in the market, they pick up an object at will, such as a table or chair, they support its legs on their nose, or a knife or axe, they hold it vertically by the handle. Even more remarkable, they pick up a scale, attach a weight to its end, and balance it on their nose. They also pick grass, cut off a length, and knead it thoroughly. Then they straighten it, tie twenty coins to its tip, and balance it on the tip of their nose. They have never failed to do so. In the market, someone brings out a small magic box. They open the lid and take out a horizontal piece of wood, about half a foot wide, an inch high, with four legs, arranged horizontally on a cabinet. They make several calls into the box, and suddenly a frog jumps out, placing its front feet on the horizontal wood and sitting facing south. Then several dozen small frogs jump out one by one, placing their front feet on the wood and sitting facing north. Once settled, the person takes a small board and taps it once. The main frog makes a sound, and the small frogs respond in sequence. Then the main frog makes a loud, disordered sound, and the small frogs also make a loud, continuous sound. After a while, the person taps the board again, and both the main frog and the small frogs stop. The person then makes several calls, and the frogs jump back into the box one by one. This is called "frogs teaching."

Another person cut bamboo into two tubes and kept two types of ants, one red and one white. When they wanted to play, they took two small red and white paper flags, inserted them east and west on the table. They took the tubes, removed the stoppers, and placed them on either side. They tapped their fingers several times at the tube openings, and the ants came out. Their movement naturally formed rows, and they separately moved towards and stopped under the flags, arranging like an army. The person then took out a small yellow flag, made a commanding gesture, and the ants immediately moved forward in unison. The two armies met, raising their feet to fight, two by two, each other's horns, circling and retreating, all in proper measure. After some time, one group immediately retreated, disorder as if fleeing in chaos; the other group rushed forward, their movement like flying, actually winning and chasing the fleeing ones. The person then raised the yellow flag to signal, and the winners immediately returned, entering the tubes in order; the other group also arrived in succession, rushing to enter, no longer forming a line. Frogs, as creatures, are small and simple, yet ants are even more so. How shameful it is for humans to be like the mythical creatures Qiongqi and Taowu, who are even more difficult to teach or correct!

A Record from *Xixun Rilu* by a follower from Gaojiang Village: To the southeast of the Nanhai Pond, outside the capital city, there is a mound known as the Ant Mound. On Qingming Festival, millions of ants are sure to gather here, hence its name. In Chaozhou, there is a mountain known as the Big Ant Mountain, and there is also a temple dedicated to the "ant ancestors." Every May, the ants gather in a ritual. These ants, it seems, also know how to respect their ancestors. According to *Shui Jing Zhu* (Commentary on the Waterways): In Yexu County of Yizhou, it was Tang Meng who first opened up the region. Eighty li northwest of the county, there is a mountain called Diao Niao Mountain. Hundreds or even thousands of birds gather here, their calls resonating in unison, and they come six times a year. People wait for them to gather and then light fires at night to capture them. Those that do not eat and seem particularly sorrowful are considered to be mourning, and they are not taken. It is said that a phoenix died here, so the birds gather to mourn, and thus the mountain is named. This can be compared to the Ant Mound in Gaojiang Village. There are also people who keep goldfish, dividing them into two colors, red and white, and store them in a single tank. Using two flags, one red and one white, they guide the fish. When the red flag is waved, the red fish follow it, swimming back and forth, turning sharply when the flag turns sharply and slowly when the flag turns slowly. When the flag is lowered, the fish all hide. The white fish behave similarly. When both flags are raised together, the red and white fish intertwine, rotating and moving in and out, like a military formation. After some time, when the two flags are separated, the red fish follow the red flag and remain in the red group, while the white fish follow the white flag and return to the white group. The *Book of Changes* says, "Trust extends even to pigs and fish." Is this indeed the case?

According to *Dream of the Eastern Capital: A Record of Splendor*, among the miscellaneous plays in the capital, there were performances such as Liu Baiqin playing with snakes and ants, and during the Lantern Festival in the palace, there were also shows featuring Li Woning's monkey performing a hundred plays, fish jumping over the Dragon Gate, and plays involving commanding bees, butterflies, snakes, and ants. It is said that all things with consciousness can be taught, such as the dragonfly tiger dancing to the music of Liangzhou, and their teaching methods are not new. Iron Child Iron Child was a person from Yiwu. His surname was Gu, and his given name was Xiaocheng. His father, Shu Mu, was renowned for his martial skills when he was young. He married a woman from the same village, surnamed Long. After a year, he was recommended by Xu Wei and followed Hu Zongxian to fight the Japanese pirates, and did not return for three years. Left alone, Long spent her summer nights cooling off in the garden. There was a small hill in the garden called Iron Boat, named after the iron column in the pavilion, which was the most beautiful spot in the garden. Around midnight, she walked alone to the top of the hill to watch the moon, looked at her reflection and felt sad, finding it hard to bear, and then entered the pavilion to rest. The iron column in front was dark and imposing, standing like a person. Long embraced it, feeling something. After several months, she gave birth to a child made of iron, with all facial features and limbs, but did not move or cry. Playfully, she wrote "Iron Child" on his back with chalk and ordered an old woman to abandon him by the dike.

The next day, a Guangxi military officer named Chen Daguang returned to his post with his troops after the defeat of the Japanese invaders. Passing through a certain area, he heard the crying of a child in the reeds. He followed the sound and found a tiger nursing a child, which fled upon seeing him. At the time, Chen had no son, so he was very pleased to take the child back and raise him as his own. As the child grew up, his skin was as black as lacquer, and thus he was named Tie'er. From a young age, Tie'er was brave and fierce, possessing the manner of his adoptive father, and his nature was exceptionally noble. As he grew older, he became bold and chivalrous, fond of making friends. Some laughed at him for not knowing books, so he humbled himself and followed a teacher. Once, he quarreled with a fellow student, who cursed him as a different breed. Tie'er was furious and went to Chen Daguang to complain. Chen told him the reason, and Tie'er cried bitterly, urgently wanting to go find his parents. Chen, thinking he was still young, did not permit it. Later, when Yang Yinglong rebelled in his state, Chen Daguang's troops were mobilized to join Liu Ting in the campaign, and Tie'er requested to go along. They fought their way to Sichuan and learned that the rebels had two valiant generals, Wu Rihua and Yang Zhu, who had previously formed a brotherhood with Tie'er. Tie'er asked Liu Ting to go persuade them to surrender. Yang Yinglong lost his support and eventually submitted. Liu Ting reported Tie'er's achievements, and he was appointed as the deputy general of Yongning.

After the second military campaign in Korea, Iron requested to lead his own troops and attack the capital directly by sea. His intention was to pray at Potala along the way and visit his parents in Zhejiang to inquire about their well-being. The court did not agree, so Iron bit his finger to draw blood and submitted a memorial to express his feelings, willing to resign his post and join the ranks to follow. The court finally agreed. Iron led a naval force out of Qiongzhou and set sail directly toward Potala. Just as a strong west wind arose, in half a day they arrived at the purification site. He ascended the mountain and asked the temple monk to see the Bodhisattva. An old monk stepped forward and said, "The Bodhisattva is not on this mountain. What do you wish to pray for?" Iron detailed his feelings. The monk exclaimed, "Filial son, filial son!" and led Iron to the back of the temple, allowing him to look across to a distant hillside. There, he saw an old woman with white hair, unkempt and dirty, as if she were a prisoner, weeping. Iron was confused and returned to ask the monk. The monk replied, "This is likely the Bodhisattva appearing in this form to convey a message." Iron asked for the monk to guide him further. The monk said, "This mountain can be seen but not reached. Even if you could fly over, your mother-in-law is not here either. Just remember this image, and there will be a time when you will meet again." Iron wept and returned to his ship, which then set sail.

Upon reaching Korea, the Japanese had already abandoned the capital. Upon hearing of Toyotomi Hideyoshi's death, they were about to retreat. Chen Lin ordered his deputy general Deng Zhilong to lead warships to intercept them, killing 300 of their followers. The enemy fled into E-san, where the cliffs were deep and the paths were dangerous, and the soldiers dared not advance. Tie'er accompanied his guest Jiao Ren and led a hundred brave soldiers, entering at night and surrounding the cave. The enemy relied on high ground to defend themselves, but Tie'er led the charge, and more than a hundred others followed. Not one of the enemy escaped.

Thus, they searched the cave and found mountains of gold and silk. Upon reaching a certain place, they found women bound together in chains, who were then released and sent away. Among them was an old woman who cried alone and spoke, saying, "I have no home to return to. If you show compassion, I wish to follow the noble official, to sew and mend in the army, to spend the remaining years, it would be fortunate." Tie'er stared blankly for a long time, then suddenly recalled the image of the mountain opposite Putuo. After verifying their hometowns, all corresponded with what Chen's father had said. Then, crying and bowing, he said, "Mother, do you also know if Tie'er is still alive?" The mother was greatly frightened and said, "My late husband followed General Hu to fight the Wokou, and only produced one Tie'er, who was already abandoned in the wilderness. Later, my husband died because he was falsely accused by Zhao Wenhua despite his achievements. I was captured and brought here, where I sew and mend for the bandits, struggling to survive until now. How could I have such a noble son?" Tie'er then showed his back, revealing two characters written in powder clearly visible, and recounted what Chen's father used to say. The mother began to doubt, but still could not understand. At this time, Lin also arrived, laughing and saying beside, "Mother, do not be surprised, it is because the child originally received the essence from the body, therefore, when exposed to wind, it solidifies. When the tiger came and covered it with its milk, then immediately melted and became human, therefore, it is the natural order of things." The mother suddenly realized, and they then hugged and cried together.

At that time, the houses in my hometown had already been completely destroyed by the ravages of war. I took care of him and returned him to Guangxi. Only then did I learn that Yang Yinglong had rebelled again and that a high-ranking official had been defeated. Chen's father died in battle at Songmenya, and the court had already bestowed a posthumous sacrifice and burial upon him. Furthermore, the achievements in Korea were commemorated, and Tie'er was promoted to the rank of Doudushi and transferred to the position of General Commander of Shanhaiguan. Tie'er strongly declined the promotion and requested to resign, but his request was denied. Tie'er resigned and returned directly home. Some criticized him for being hypocritical, but Tie'er said, "I crossed the sea on a distant campaign, not for the sake of seeking merit. Now that I can support my elderly mother, this joy is something even a marquis with ten thousand households could not exchange. If I were to cling to wealth and status, one day my body might be wrapped in horsehide and returned, or I might be affected by the storms of officialdom. Even if I wanted to stay by my mother's side, how could I?"

After the death of his mother, who had lived to be over eighty, he personally carried the soil for her burial. Suddenly, tens of thousands of birds appeared, carrying soil to form a grave, which people called the "Filial Birds' Grave." However, Iron Boy eventually died due to destruction. At the time of his burial, when his coffin was lifted, it seemed as if it were empty. His son opened it to look inside and found only a small iron figure, no more than two feet tall. Golden Butterfly He was a renowned person from Hanyang, named Xian Qin. In the early years of the Kangxi era, he was a scholar. Widely learned and knowledgeable, he was skilled in poetry, ancient prose, and classical literature, and good at painting plum blossoms. Changzhou Wen Dian once saw his poetry and paintings, considering them unparalleled in modern times. Upon hearing this, Xian Qin traveled thousands of miles to establish a friendship. He had a reserved and upright nature and was not good at writing popular articles. However, each time a piece was published, it was immediately circulated and praised. Subsequently, he failed repeatedly in official examinations and could only make a living by selling his writings and paintings. If not the right person, even if offered a thousand gold pieces, he would not pay attention. Therefore, people stopped asking about him. In his later years, he built a house on Yingezhou, entertaining himself with poetry and wine, and his feet did not step into the bustling city. Even though the cooking smoke often disappeared, he did not care. However, every time he got drunk, he would always carry his own poetry and writings to Mi Heng's tomb, recite and teach others, cry bitterly, and return.

When the new prefect of Hu Prefecture, Mr. W, arrived, he heard of the artist's name and summoned him to paint. The artist did not go, and the prefect became angry. At that time, the Confucian temple was being renovated, and the prefect issued an order to paint the walls as a way to humiliate him. The artist, Xian Qin, took his brush and went, painting a plum blossom on the wall. After the painting, he wrote after it, saying: "Accidentally accompanying the recluse with his qin and crane, not allowing the mountain alder to be considered his younger brother. As the moon sets and the aster is horizontal, no one sees, but only the clear fragrance fills the universe." After writing, he brushed his sleeves and returned home. Later, when the prefect arrived, he was greatly surprised. He sent a servant to personally visit his house and offered a hundred gold pieces as compensation. The artist refused. It was already the height of summer, and he still wore cotton clothing. Seeing this, the prefect looked at his servant and had him take some fine linen and gauze to give to the artist. The artist said, "I do not feel the heat, so I do not need this thing." So he stopped, left the gold, and went away. The artist chased after him, but could not catch up, so he returned, threw the gold into a broken basket, and never looked back. After several months, the gold turned into butterflies, one by one flying away. The artist later died of poverty and hunger. The Battle of Shizi Garden During the reign of Emperor Chongzhen, General Sun Chuanjing's campaign at Shizi Garden followed the emperor's order for the military inspector Su Jing to urge battle, leading to defeat. A certain guest in the tent said to him, "Yesterday, while I was napping during the day, I saw people all about a foot tall, wearing armor and holding spears, riding in chariots and horses, emerging from the battlefield, climbing onto the table and the stove." The Taoist priest Zhu Yingzi from Mount Jiangshan ordered boiling water to be poured where they entered. They dug and found a large number of dead ants about the size of a dou (a unit of volume) in the hole. Then he sighed and said, "I mistakenly listened to the Taoist priest, and thus killed millions of lives in a child's game. When they held their spears and climbed onto the table, were they not clearly soldiers going out to the frontier?" General Sun cried bitterly.

Huiniang A scholar from Hezhou, named Wanxia. Orphaned and poor in his youth, he was innately sharp and intelligent, reading five lines at once. At thirteen, he entered the county school and was subsequently admitted through annual exams. The famous scholars in the county all admired him as surpassing them. But despite his abundant talent and eloquence, the scholar had extremely high literary standards. From then on, he repeatedly failed in the examinations and lost his wife, becoming even more aimless. Previously, the scholar had an aunt on his mother's side who married into the Ma family of Xincheng and was quite wealthy. The scholar would visit her from time to time. The aunt loved his graceful and spirited appearance and would keep him for ten days, not sending him away. Her niece was named Hui娘, who had already passed the age of fifteen but had been widowed before marrying. She was skilled in writing and calligraphy, and was also good at playing the guqin and chess. She had a beautiful and graceful appearance and a gentle and pure nature. But when the scholar came, she would converse freely without reserve. In the autumn of Gengshen, the scholar failed the exams again and returned to Xincheng. The woman greeted him, asking about his well-being and offering comfort. She said, "With your talents, how could you be destined for a life of poverty and obscurity? But given the current trends, if you could lower your standards slightly, why worry about not ranking on the list?" The scholar replied, "There are indeed many scholars like Shi Kuang and He Jiao inside the examination hall, but if I must seek fame through such means, I will shave my head and retreat to the mountains, unwilling to seek a kindred spirit on the road ahead." He then shed tears. The woman also looked sad and approached him, wiping his tears with her handkerchief.

When Mother stepped out, he inquired about the reason and could not help but sigh deeply. Mother had always loved chess, so she called a servant to fetch the go board, and played chess with him to dispel his boredom. The daughter sat beside and watched. Soon, one corner of the black pieces was in great danger, and the daughter looked at him and said, "The southwest wind is strong now, will you be willing to abandon this corner?" He asked, "What do you mean?" The daughter roughly indicated and said, "This is what is called the 'reversing the boot' strategy." Mother smiled and said, "Why do you speak so closely, is it not because the daughter is leaning outward?" Before she finished speaking, the daughter's face turned red, and she rushed up and left hurriedly. He was also moved, pushed aside the chessboard, stood up, and bowed, saying, "If it were as Mother said, what else would be worth regretting!" Mother regretted her words. She then thought of her sister who had already declined, and considering the precious daughter and talented son, such a good match was hard to come by, so the next day, she conveyed her intention to the daughter's father, Feng Le. Feng Le was poor. Mother said he was talented enough to be trusted, but Feng Le replied, "What if fate is unfavorable? If it were as you suggest, let's wait until next year's literary competition." So they gave up and left. Upon hearing this, he felt aggrieved and wanted to return home. Mother tried to keep him by teaching his two sons, but he loved the daughter and was not ready to depart hastily, so he reluctantly agreed.

Soon after, the mother fell ill. The man entered to visit her, and just then the girl came to supervise the medicine. They met in the eastern wing of the house. Seeing no one around, the man whispered softly, "Do you know why I stayed here?" The girl sighed and said, "Deep feelings have long been engraved in my heart. With a single thought of cherishing talent, I have bound myself like a silkworm spinning its own cocoon. Upon hearing my father's words, I almost wished to die. From now on, if I can use writings to elevate the status of an unfortunate person, that will be enough; otherwise, we shall meet in the afterlife." The man replied, "If I cannot have you, I do not wish to remarry in this life. But I fear human affairs are unpredictable. May we establish a secret agreement, as a solemn oath, can we?" The girl's face turned red and she said, "If so, you would be betraying my father and also my aunt. How could you use such an unvirtuous woman?" She then took off a bracelet from her wrist and gave it to him, saying, "This is the token of our vow. Until the sea dries up and the rocks crumble, swear not to forget." The man put it in his pocket and left. From then on, he no longer spoke of returning home.

After the illness soon recovered, the stepmother would eat lotus seeds every morning. The daughter secretly gave a cup of lotus seeds to her maid to deliver to Sheng, who just happened to encounter Feng Le. Feng Le questioned the maid, who could not hide the truth and answered with the facts. Feng Le became angry and was about to return and question the daughter. Just then, a wealthy merchant named Wang in the neighborhood proposed marriage for his son, who was not intelligent. Feng Le, angered by the daughter, finally agreed. After several days, the engagement ceremony was scheduled. The daughter first heard about it and then fell ill. She stopped sleeping and eating, gradually becoming very weak. Unable to do anything else, she reluctantly called for a doctor. The doctor arrived and examined her, saying, "The illness was caused by suppressed anger and liver damage, causing the heart fluid to be burned away by fire. It is necessary to get human blood to mix with it, using the bark of the happy tree to make a decoction to drink, which might work. Otherwise, it is feared that it cannot be cured by medicine." Feng Le consulted with Wang, who laughed and said, "How foolish! You want to use your dead person's things to cut out my son's living heart." Feng Le returned feeling ashamed and angry. He went to Sheng to tell him what the doctor said and agreed to the marriage. Sheng smiled and said, "Uncle, don't you worry about cooking words to cure hunger in the future?" Feng Le wanted to say more, but Sheng took his book and got up, leaving to his mother's place. He told her about the matter and cried, saying, "If there is even a slight chance for my wise sister, how could I bear to live alone? Just now Uncle came and said, making me feel uneasy!" After finishing, he took out his sword and wanted to commit suicide. The mother quickly got up and stopped him, saying, "Foolish child, why would you harm yourself first? Stay here for now, and I will come back after I see your sister." Sheng asked to go with her.

After arriving, he lifted the curtain and saw the woman lying feeble and near death. Her mother asked, "Have you eaten a little today?" She replied, telling him about her condition and explaining why she had not eaten. The woman opened her eyes to see the man and looked at him silently, only shedding tears. She sighed and said, "I have betrayed you! Last night, I dreamed that you came to play with me. You took hold of my feet, removed my sleeping shoes, and put them in your sleeve. I hurriedly reached into your sleeve, but could not find them. You laughed and said, 'The embroidered shoes have already been taken by Ah Hong.' I was surprised and asked, 'How can this thing be in someone else's hands? What shall we do now?' You did not answer and left. I called out to you, but you did not look back. When I woke up, I realized that this matter could not be resolved. I had been unwilling to tarnish my reputation, but now my soul has already wandered into the graveyards. If you do this, it will inevitably cause your death, and I cannot come back to life. I don't know if there will be a next life." She then lay down on the pillow and wept bitterly. Her mother comforted her, saying, "My child, take care of yourself. Yesterday your father-in-law already agreed to our nephew, and this matter can still be resolved." She then took the man to where her son, Feng Le, was and fulfilled the promise she had made. She also said, "My child is so sick, how can you stand by and watch her die?" Feng Le agreed readily, and his mother returned to tell the woman, who was slightly relieved. From then on, she made an effort to eat well and within a month had already recovered.

Upon hearing this, the Wang family sent Bing once more, intending to arrange a marriage proposal, which Feng Le agreed to. But when the mother first heard of it, she was filled with rage and immediately went to accuse Feng Le of breaking his promise. Feng Le defended himself by saying that the Wang family had made the promise first. The mother, in anger, swept her sleeve and left. Just then, the daughter arrived and overheard some of the conversation, realizing that the matter had taken an unexpected turn. She stood there, on the verge of tears. The mother tried every way she could to comfort her, but in the end, the daughter could not be consoled. Soon after, she fell ill again and not long afterward passed away. When her husband arrived to mourn, the body was already being prepared for burial. As he stopped beside the corpse, it suddenly leaped up. The crowd was greatly terrified. The woman then recounted in detail what had happened in the afterlife, saying: "At the moment of my death, my soul drifted and wandered, and recalling my hometown, it all felt as though I had been born in another life. But I could not leave thinking of my husband. I privately thought and appealed to the King of Hell, hoping he might show mercy. So I walked forward at ease, and arrived at a place where the halls and buildings were grand and resplendent, with ghost attendants standing in a fearsome array. While hesitating, I suddenly saw an old man, who called out from within the gate: 'How could you come here? Your years have not yet run out, and your karmic bond with my son is not yet complete. Come with me, and I will ask the King of Hell to judge this case.' So I followed him in, and saw the King of Hell seated on the throne, his bearing solemn and dignified. The old man knelt and made his report for a long time. The King then looked and ordered that the wife be called to the case, saying, 'Your father is an ordinary person. The two of you were long ago bound by a red thread of fate. Though you died for love, you have not lost your virtue and righteousness. You should still be reunited, and the broken mirror should be mended.' He then summoned the ghost attendants to escort her back to life. Unexpectedly, she was able to return home in an instant." With this, the sorrow turned to joy. Only the husband carefully inquired about the old man’s appearance.

As they were laughing and crying alternately, suddenly they heard the sound of drums and gongs, shaking the tiles of the roof from afar. Soon a servant rushed in and said, "Xie Qian has rebelled, local bandits have led the enemy troops into the city, and great looting is about to begin!"
