The mother and Huiniang were in a state of panic when the rebel soldiers stormed in. The son fled away, and the mother's house was completely looted. The bandits saw the girl was beautiful and took her away. When the new city was recaptured, the son returned, and they learned that the girl had already been taken. They cried out in despair, and Fengle and the mother also cried. The son had a servant named Hongnu, brave and strong, capable of controlling armor and jumping ten zhang. At that time, he was by the side and advised the son, "I wish to go and investigate about Huiniang." If she is unharmed, I will do my best to return her. But ask Lady to tell me what reward she will give me." The mother did not have time to answer, and Fengle broke into tears and said, "You are like the ancient bailiff. One day when the girl returns, I will give you to your master." Hong bowed twice and said, "I humbly accept the order." He then got up and left with his sword.

At that time, the remaining bandits were encamped in Zichuan, and Hong went straight to their camp to beg for surrender. After staying for several days, a coerced individual spoke to him: "When Huiniang was captured, Xie Qian intended to take her as a concubine, but she refused. He threatened her with a knife, and Huiniang requested to wait until three months later, after completing the mourning period for her mother, before she would obey his commands. Otherwise, she asked to be killed. The bandits loved her beauty, so they have kept her locked up in a tower ever since." Hong secretly rejoiced and, after midnight, crept up to the side of the tower. Looking up, he saw the light flickering through the windows. He leaped up, peered through the window crack, and saw Huiniang sitting alone by the lamp, shedding tears. He broke through the window, and the two maidservants were startled and got up. Hong drew his sword and killed them, then carried Huiniang out. The guards only realized it and could not catch up. Just as dawn broke, they arrived at Xin Cheng and entered the gate. Huiniang saw her family gathered around, as if waking from a dream, and described in detail the situation of being coerced, feeling both sadness and joy. Then, the mother turned to Feng Le and said, "Can we now discuss marriage for my nephew?" Feng Le smiled and agreed. The protagonist asked to return and report to his mother. The mother smiled and said, "Is it still possible to wait a little longer?" The protagonist understood and stopped. Just after the wedding, the bandits were already defeated. The roads were clear, and the protagonist carried his wife back home, went to the hall to bow to his mother, and the mother asked about the previous events. She could not help but shed tears and said, "Then I should bow to this chaste woman." Relatives and friends came to congratulate, and those who saw it all admired it greatly, considering it a reward for righteousness and bravery. However, from then on, the protagonist became increasingly disinterested in power and wealth, spending his days idly with Huiniang, playing music and composing verses, and resolved not to seek further advancement.

Jia Quan Jiangyin's Jia Xingfang, courtesy name Shixiang, was a renowned scholar in his hometown. His family was not wealthy, but he maintained his integrity and refused to be approached with unjust requests. He had a younger sister named Quan, courtesy name Xinxiang. She was exceptionally beautiful, with a dignified and serene personality, skilled in composing poetry, and beloved by her brother and sister-in-law. At the age of sixteen, she was betrothed to Jiang Shitao, the son of a salt merchant from the same city. After a year, the wedding date was set. An old woman named Wang, who sold jewelry, heard about it and brought some pearls to sell. The sister bought several pearls and also took out dozens from her dowry to have a pearl phoenix made. After completing the work, the old woman gave it to the sister and said, "Even a figure in a painting looks better in simple attire, and now adorned with precious jewels and feathers, Jiang's son truly has good fortune." The sister smiled and paid for it. Just then, her sister-in-law came with a mirror to ask her to sketch a design. She saw the pearl phoenix on the table, picked it up, and exclaimed, "Is this the one the old woman made? These people should not be allowed to enter the house; my sister is being deceived now." The sister took it from her hand and closely examined it, realizing that the pearls had already been replaced. She felt regret and anger, but it was too late. Her sister-in-law returned and told Shixiang about it. Later, when Shixiang went out, he met the old woman at the door, refused to let her in, and scolded her for lacking shame.

The elderly woman left in shame. Already emboldened by her old shame, she went directly to the Jiang family and slandered Jiang's mother, saying that the daughter had once ordered her fellow villager Jin Ma to deliver a letter to a certain young man, and she had recently heard that the young woman was already pregnant. Yesterday, she had intentionally used the excuse of selling pearls to test whether it was true, and unintentionally found that it was indeed as she had said. The mother, after listening, told Jiang's father, who was half-believing and half-doubting. Several days later, a female servant led an old woman selling flowers into the house. When asked about her name, the old woman replied that she was Jin Ma, as the elderly woman had said. The girls ended up buying flowers and had already dispersed. The mother calmly asked about the daughter of the Ji family, and the old woman detailedly recounted the previous events, which matched exactly what the elderly woman had said. The mother immediately ordered the female servant to invite Jiang's father to come, to prove the matter. Therefore, they decided to divorce, and finally went to the matchmaker, throwing the marriage contract and making the dowry return. At that time, the matchmaker also heard rumors and did not dare to contend. She brought Ji's son, who was shocked and surprised and asked for the reason. The matchmaker hinted at the situation, and Ji's son angrily threw the marriage contract on the ground and entered.

The matchmaker, having no choice but to act, returned to inform the father, who then took the matter to the authorities, with Jinma as a witness. The young man also went to the county to file a complaint. When he appeared in court, he spoke with a forceful and passionate tone, which the judge could not suppress, and advised him, saying, "You may withdraw for now. Bring your sister with you tomorrow to hear the evidence." Returning home, he discussed the matter with his sister and said, "How could I let my sister be damaged to this extent!" The sister sighed and said, "Since I was nurtured and loved by my brother and sister-in-law, I often thought to strive to emulate Hao and Zhong, to console our parents in the afterlife. Now, I have suffered this humiliation unjustly. Can I still bear to endure this for now? My will is already decided. Do not be concerned for me, brother." After speaking, she wept bitterly all night. She hastily prepared herself, changing all her clothes and shoes to white mourning clothes. Bowing to her sister-in-law, she said, "My fate is thin; I cannot stay with my sister-in-law until the end. I have disappointed you!" At that moment, the sister-in-law could only weep, no longer able to distinguish what she was saying, while the sister had already climbed into the carriage with her brother and left.

Upon his arrival, the magistrate swore by heaven and sun, delivering his speech with clear and eloquent logic. He said, "The evidence is clear and undeniable; how can one argue forcefully?" He ordered her to be tortured. The woman replied, "Cruel punishment, I am too weak to bear it. I will inevitably confess falsely. To confess is worse than to die. How can the magistrate kill an innocent person?" The magistrate then quickly called the midwife and led the woman to another room to examine her. After the examination, the midwife reported, "The examination showed that Jia had been pregnant for four months." This matched what Jin had said. The magistrate laughed and asked the woman, "What can you say now?" The woman responded, "No, I believe it is better for the magistrate to personally verify the results!" Before she finished speaking, she pulled out a knife from her sleeve, removed her clothes, and stabbed her abdomen. She died before the act was completed. Shixiang rushed to the woman, took the knife, and forcefully cut open her abdomen, causing her intestines to spill out. He threw the knife and said, "Please examine, sir!" The magistrate urgently shouted to stop, but it was too late. The magistrate then reported the incident to a higher authority, who ordered a re-examination and confirmed the case. Jiang the old man, his wife Wang, and Jin were all sentenced to death by beheading. The magistrate was sentenced to death by strangulation for accepting bribes and perverting justice. A temple was built to honor Jia as a chaste widow.

Zhi Family** In Wuxi, there was a woman named Zhu Zhenfu of the Zhi family, who was the betrothed wife of Zhu Can at the age of twenty-four. After Zhu Can died, she returned to Zhu Shouzhen. She then adopted a son named Yingerjun, who had twenty-four acres of land. Later, Yingerjun died young, and they discussed adopting another heir. Yingerjun's biological father, Wen Yao, coveted his property and plotted with a relative named Cai Ren, saying, "To adopt an heir based on the mother; without a mother, how can there be a child?" They forced her to marry, but she refused. They humiliated her in countless ways. Zhu Zhenfu took a pair of scissors to harm herself and again tried to drown herself. She met her younger brother Xichang, told him the reason, and complained to the county magistrate. Wen Yao had a private connection with a certain Censor and went to the magistrate, claiming that Zhu Zhenfu had other intentions. The magistrate questioned her, and she undressed and asked for a knife to cut open her chest to prove her innocence. The magistrate sent an old woman to verify, and it turned out she was indeed a chaste woman. The magistrate then severely punished them and established an heir for Zhu Zhenfu, writing a biography to record it. This magistrate did not cause her to die due to corruption. As for the certain Censor, his plan was also very dangerous. Zhu Zhenfu's survival was fortunate! **Abortion** In the western part of the county, there was a village called Henanpu. A village woman named Li was promiscuous. After her husband died, she daily associated with a notorious young man in the village. Soon after, she became pregnant, and it was already past five months. A neighbor woman named Yang, who could perform abortions, used this to make a profit. Li had always been close to her, and now she gave her five pieces of foreign currency, asking her to help. Li accepted the money, invited her to dinner, and also gave her wine. Li was drunk, and Yang hastily performed the procedure. Before the fetus was aborted, Li had already died. Yang then called her husband to bind Li with stones and throw her into the river. No one knew about it.

After six years, a midwife from a neighboring village returned home after assisting in childbirth. Just as she entered the door, she suddenly slapped her own cheek and shouted, "Old harlot, you once told me: you induced an abortion for someone, and the person was safe afterward. I entrusted my life to your hands, who could have expected you to be so careless? You used boiling water to soak a straw shoe and rubbed it on me. The reason I called out in pain in a low voice was because I feared others would know. How could it still be ordinary abdominal pain? But you still rubbed with force without stopping, causing the fetus in my womb to rise up and die. And you already cheated me of my money and caused me to die, even to buy a coffin to prevent the burial of my body, perhaps it can still be forgiven. Then you dropped me with stones and sank me into the river, making both my bones and flesh buried in the fish's belly. Can this grudge still be forgiven?" After finishing speaking, she spat foam and fell. Her husband kowtowed and begged for mercy, promising to repent and elevate her. The woman suddenly glared and said, "Old turtle spirit, still want to explain with clever words? I formerly could not land for a moment, therefore swallowed hatred until now, only could spit out this evil breath. Your wife can die me, but I cannot die your wife?" All drowning ghosts must come ashore after three years, and then can seek retribution after another three years. When the body was sunk, Li's breath was still not gone, therefore until now came ashore to seek revenge. Therefore her wife became even more crazy, jumping and throwing herself about, calling out in pain, furrowing her brow and holding her chest, loudly calling out in pain, looking up with her eyes, imitating Li's dying state. Until midnight she finally died.

A Matter of the Late Jiaqing Era, Told to Me by Wu Xiangpu Locked in Her Womb Zhudun's Shen, originally a Confucian scholar, was a troublemaker from a young age. As he grew older, he abandoned his studies and indulged in a life of debauchery, with a nature that was both sexually depraved and cruel. After marrying, he squandered all her dowry to support his promiscuous activities; when that was not enough, he severely punished his wife to continue his ways. His wife, Min, was also beautiful. Shen preyed on women throughout the family and clan, and people feared his brutality, looking at each other in silence. Min was worried that he might bring disaster upon himself and tried to gently advise him. Shen flew into a rage and said, "You don't know that my husband is a hero in the bedroom, and you dare to be jealous?" He beat her naked, leaving her body with no intact skin. Min was on the verge of death, pleading pitifully for mercy. Shen said, "This time you should know my methods. I have had many other men's wives, and I am not satisfied yet. You are alone at home with me, and I often stay out at night. How can I ensure that you won't do something else?" After saying this, he went out and fetched a drill. He bound her and drilled into her private parts, then took out a small lock from his pocket and locked her with it. Min was severely injured, and with her lower body locked, she could hardly move. However, fearing his cruelty, she still got up every day to cook for him. One day, her brother came to visit and saw her walking unsteadily, looking emaciated and almost lifeless. He asked her about her condition, but she remained silent, weeping silently. Her niece, who was present, described her state to her mother and clan members. The clan members were all furious and called for boats to go together to Zhudun. When they arrived, they found Min lying curled up and unable to get up. Seeing her mother, she cried out and died.

At this time, the clan of Shen also gathered, and the clan leader, surnamed Shen, stepped forward and said, "Do not be angry, Mother. This son has accumulated enough evil deeds. There are three evils in the village that have not been eliminated, and this is one of them. Now we ask to eliminate him, to avenge our public anger." He then ordered the people to capture him and burn him with piled-up wood. Hundreds of spectators were all jumping for joy. In the past, there was a scholar in Bozhou who was intimate with his maid. His wife found out and put minced garlic inside the maid's vagina, then sewed it up with thread, causing the maid immense pain. The neighbors all felt it was unfair and collectively sued the official. The official was very angry and issued an order to bring the wife in, and called several leatherworkers with needles and thread to sew up the wife's vagina. The scholar feared that this would bring shame to his family and tried hard to avoid it. The official said, "Now the city tower is about to collapse. If you can rebuild it, perhaps you can be spared." The scholar used all his family's resources to finally complete the construction. To this day, the local people call the city tower "Sew-Vagina Tower." During the Shunzhi era, a certain official in Piling occasionally had an intimate relationship with a wet nurse. The official's wife found out and used a needle to pierce the wet nurse's vagina and locked it, throwing the key into the well. The wet nurse screamed and cried, almost dying. Reluctantly, she had to find a copperworker to pry it open with iron wire. To this day, people in Changzhou call her "Locked-Vagina Grandma."

Both events are recorded in the *Jian Hu Ji*, and unexpectedly, I have now seen them with my own eyes. Gunpowder Depot In the summer of the twenty-third year of the Daoguang reign, the gunpowder depot in Hangzhou was moved by thunderfire in a single night, leaving not a single tile or beam standing, nor even the bases of the columns remaining. It is unclear why this happened. Some said, "Perhaps the Thunder God needed this for some use, so he had the Six Gods and Six Officials take it down." This is not so. In recent times, warfare relies solely on fire attacks, and the harm caused by gunpowder is great, so it was taken as a warning. Otherwise, why would they move the entire depot? Flattery and Disaster Xi Muxuan, a jinshi (imperial scholar) at the end of the Ming dynasty, was from Lou Dong. After the change of the dynasty, he retired to cultivate his aspirations, living a simple and content life with few desires. During the reign of the Prince of Fu, he was repeatedly summoned but did not come. When the Prince of Yu came south to Hangzhou, he prepared a letter of respect, and upon seeing the king, he did not bow. He was recommended to be the chief of the Imperial Academy but did not accept the position. The king respected his character and integrity, and sent him away with generous gifts. Later, when Jin Shenghuan arrived in western Zhejiang, he already had rebellious intentions and summoned him to be the chief of the secretariat. When Xi arrived, he showed excessive respect and fear, bowing his head dozens of times. Jin Shenghuan became furious and said, "I treat you as a national hero, how can you treat me as a commoner! You did not bow to the Prince of Yu in the past, why do you bow to me now? Isn't it because you think I can't tolerate others and am playing with me?" He was then killed. (Helian Bobo also killed the hermit Wei Xuan for similar reasons.)

The historian says: Flattering words, pleasing appearance, and excessive deference, which Confucius despised. He also said: If one has the eloquence of Zhu Ta but lacks the beauty of Song Chao, it is difficult to avoid offending in this world. The world has been a place of fox-like flattery for millennia, yet there are those who, despite their arrogance, do not suffer misfortune, and those who, despite their flattery, cannot escape it. For example, Mu Xuan, who was killed due to his excessive caution, compared to Yin Hao, who was punished for presenting an empty letter—how tragic! Alas! Scholars who lived in times of chaos and were punished for refusing to bend to a false regime were many, how much more so for those like Sheng Huan, who killed people as if they were mere dirt? Mu Xuan did not die at the time of his defiance, but died on the day he was forced to submit; his death is indeed regrettable. Yet he was respected because he was not afraid to die; he was killed because he was too afraid to die. How wise were the words of Hou Chaozong: "Suppose two people encounter a tiger while walking. One is terrified, kneels down, and begs for mercy until he dies, while the other shouts, raises his arms, fights, and dies despite losing. I believe that Sheng Huan's ferocity is like a tiger; he could not be saved by mere kneeling and begging." A gentleman should act with foresight and have his own principles. Some say that flattery also has its methods, which involve being born with a servile nature and being skilled at flattery. For instance, the story of Ma Ling in *Zhuang Hui Tang Ji* and the account of a poor man learning flattery in *Xie Duo* are truly profound insights from experience and the legacy of flattery. As for Mu Xuan, he likely sought quick success through unconventional means, never serving at the doors of powerful officials, and lacking education and skill, leading to his downfall. Alas!

According to Zhang Zhuo's *Earmark of the Eyes and Ears*: During the Zhou dynasty, the Spring Official and Shangshu Yan Zhiwei was sent on a mission to negotiate peace with the Khagan of the Eastern Turkic Khaganate, Merkut. The Deputy Chief of the Foreign Guests Department, Tian Guaidao, accompanied him. Upon reaching Merkut's tent, Yan Zhiwei performed a graceful dance, embracing Merkut's boot nose and sucking on it. Tian Guaidao, however, only bowed deeply without kowtowing, which infuriated Merkut. He ordered Tian Guaidao to be hung upside down, and he remained in this state for an entire night before being released. Upon returning, Tian Guaidao and Yan Zhiwei argued in the palace courtyard, with Tian Guaidao asserting that Merkut should not be appeased. Yan Zhiwei, however, insisted that peace was necessary. Later, Merkut indeed rebelled, capturing Zhao and Ding, and Yan Zhiwei was executed along with his entire family. Tian Guaidao was appointed as the Chief of the Summer Official Department. Those skilled in flattery, their effects are even more evident. The *Earmark of the Eyes and Ears* also states: Right Remonstrant Zhao Liangbi was sent to the Xiongnu, where he sat under their tent. They offered him unclean food, and Zhao Liangbi ate an entire tray. When he was released, the court was ashamed of him. One wonders how he could swallow such food!

Sending Poetic Rhymes When Lord Ping of Shanyin remarried in the capital, Mr. Ji Xiaolan was sent to deliver congratulations and a gift, accompanied by a set of four volumes of poetic rhymes, each inscribed with the four characters "This Daughter Returns Home." Lord Ping did not understand the meaning. Later, when Mr. Ji came to join in a banquet, during the meal, Lord Ping asked calmly, "Last night, I received your favor, which included four volumes of poetic rhymes and the inscriptions on them, but I do not understand the intention behind them. I would like to humbly ask for your explanation." Mr. Ji replied, "There is no other meaning. The poetic rhymes are simply the four tones (level, rising, departing, and entering), and ‘This Daughter Returns Home’ naturally corresponds to them." Everyone present laughed heartily. I also heard that Mr. Ji had recently made a dragon robe and playfully said to one of his relatives, "Last night, my mother-in-law came to my home to visit my daughter and saw my new robe. She looked at it closely, and I once wrote a poem for her." The relative said, "I would like to hear this beautiful poem." Mr. Ji then recited, "Last night, my mother-in-law was overly affectionate, as she looked at the flowered robe and walked around the knees. She continued to look until the night deepened and people had quieted down." He stopped here. The relative asked, "Is there a closing line?" Mr. Ji replied, "There is no more." The relative said, "How can a poem have no closing line?" Mr. Ji said, "The closing line is simply ‘level, level, departing, departing, entering, entering’." His wit was just as playful as this.

**Tortoise Mirror** Se Shuto, from Tongxiang, was skilled in observing the wind and signs. Once, he helped someone choose a burial site. After selecting a location, he told the person, "If you bury your parents here, you will give birth to a noble son, and your family will have generations of high officials without interruption." The person was delighted and immediately had their parents buried there. Several years later, a monk named Purple Cloud came from Huolin, known for his long-standing skill in divination. The person, admiring the monk's reputation, invited him to the site to inquire about auspicious and ominous signs. The monk examined the site and said, "This place faces a major road, and your descendants have already been trodden upon. Moreover, it is situated in a position that invites the 'dragging knife' curse. Your family will be in decline without even time to flourish, let alone achieve greatness." He strongly advised the person to change the burial site. The person was convinced and asked the monk to find another location. They purchased a new site for a thousand gold pieces. As they were about to move the body, Se Shuto suddenly arrived and argued vehemently, "Who broke my method? There are already golden threads wrapped around the coffin. Why are you moving it?" The person did not believe him and ordered the grave to be opened. Indeed, there were golden threads of a plant wrapped around the coffin. The person regretted his decision and went to confront the monk. Se Shuto stopped him and said, "Such manipulation is their usual trick, but is it not fate?" He sighed and left. Later, a turtle appeared on the surface of a jar in Se Shuto's kitchen, as large as a tray. On its back were six characters written in seal script: "佩税殿削懵蒙." The meaning was unclear, so he asked others about it. Some said, "佩税殿 is in the Dragon Palace, and this turtle may have been punished by the Dragon King, hence its exile to the sea. But how did it end up here? Could it not bring misfortune to your family?" Se Shuto returned home, worshipped the turtle, and released it into the river. There was no other strange occurrence.

Tuotou, with his simple and clumsy nature, often removes his hat even in severe winter, so people call him by this name. I believe that Tuotou is skilled in his craft and does not engage in the devious schemes of this world. How can one know that he is not inviting divine scrutiny, thereby causing a turtle to come and foretell good omens? If someone like Tuotou can cut away ignorance, would he not be without shame in being a wise and discerning person? **A Tale of the Shadow** My hometown has a Mr. Zhu, who lost his wife in middle age and had no sons. He left behind a daughter, who was sixteen years old. She had a serene and graceful demeanor and was well-versed in the teachings of the inner chambers. Mr. Zhu regarded her as precious as a jade tablet. One day, when she was tired of embroidery and wanted to sleep, she was just about to lie down when she saw a scholar enter, his robes and shoes fluttering gracefully as he pulled aside the curtain. The girl was startled and tried to escape, but the scholar suddenly rushed forward and embraced her, restraining her hands and feet. She was about to cry out, but her tongue was already in her mouth, and she fainted and lost consciousness. From then on, she went mad day and night, laughing and singing at random, sometimes stripping off her clothes, which was too distressing to see. Mr. Zhu tried every possible means to drive the scholar away, but nothing worked. Someone suggested that the city god of the East Peak Temple was particularly renowned for his spiritual power and could be petitioned. Mr. Zhu was delighted and wrote a memorial himself, detailing the situation, and burned it in prayer. He then returned to look at his daughter. As soon as he entered the room, the girl suddenly sat up on the edge of the bed, pointing out the window and smilingly asked Mr. Zhu, "Father, did you also see him with shackles and chains, turning back and weeping as he left?" Mr. Zhu was surprised and asked her about the situation, but she covered her face in shame and could not speak. When he asked again, she was on the verge of tears, and her illness was suddenly as if she had woken from a dream.

In a certain district, there was a wealthy family. The son of this family, after marrying a woman surnamed Jin, was possessed by a malevolent spirit within a few months. Although her appearance was only average, after being possessed, she became unrestrained and licentious, almost never having any peaceful moments. However, when her husband was present and they shared the same bed, she slept peacefully and showed no signs of madness, but when he was away, she behaved as usual. Someone asked her about this, and she replied, "Two males sharing one female cannot help but feel a desire, so I temporarily leave. But how can I maintain this separation forever?" Her mother, overhearing this, spat in anger and said, "This lecherous ghost has caused so much trouble. I will report this to the Heavenly Master and send a judge to catch you, put you in a bottle, and boil you, to satisfy my hatred." The woman laughed and said, "Mother, do not be angry. I am the third prince of the city god of Guian, who loves your daughter's skin as smooth as jade and her breath as fragrant as orchids. In the future, I will choose an auspicious day to welcome her back to my residence, and we will have a lifelong union. I will not break off our relationship halfway." At the time, the father was also present and, upon hearing this, immediately wrote a complaint and burned it at the city god's temple. Upon returning, he found that his daughter had fallen into a deep sleep, sleeping peacefully as usual. However, after waking up, she seemed weak in spirit and required several doses of medicine to recover.

Both of these incidents occurred during the reign of Emperor Jiaqing. However, there are also cases that do not fit this pattern. According to the *Wujiang Gazetteer*, Mo Xuan, whose courtesy name was Ji'ang, was young and talented, skilled in calligraphy. During the reign of Emperor Yongle, he once visited the capital and, accompanied by guests, climbed Mount Sui and visited the temple of a Southern Dynasties princess. Facing the wind, he recited poetry and drunkenly slept under the eaves. He dreamed of a woman dressed in finery arriving and engaging in intimate relations until the moon set and the stars were in a diagonal position. Then he got up and parted. Mo Xuan returned home and soon fell ill, murmuring in a delirious state that "the princess is coming to welcome me," and he finally died. One year after Mo Xuan's death, his fellow scholar, Yuan Yue, entered the capital for tax matters. On the way, he suddenly dismounted his donkey and made empty bows three or four times, then remounted and bowed again as he left. The others asked him why, and he said, "I met Mo Ji'ang and bowed to him." They were greatly frightened. They sent Yuan Yue back home, but he became ill a few days later and said, "Brother Mo is welcoming me." He also died. This is a case of a ghost or spirit, nor is it necessarily controlled by a god. All of these events are inexplicable.

Quan Xieshan said, "The origins of the City God are unknown, and it is unclear which deity is being worshipped." According to *The Record of Returning from Office*, during the reign of Murong Yan of Qi and Emperor Wu of Liang, the City God was worshipped, and these events are recorded in history. The city is where the people gather, and the moat is the pool beneath the city walls. It is appropriate to worship it to invite blessings. During the Kai Cheng era of the Tang dynasty, the governor of Muzhou, Lü Shu, also believed that the practice aligned with the eight rituals of the Eight Sacrifices for the People and the Water and Ditches. By the Song dynasty, it had become widespread in the southeast. Those responsible for the city, without exception, would build temples to worship it. However, for market towns, they should only have village temples and should not have a City God. In our village, there is the Eastping Temple, which originally worshipped Yan Zhenqing, and Zhang Xun, who died at the same time, was also worshipped alongside him. It is simply named the Eastping King Temple, which has already lost its original meaning. Now, it is inscribed as the City God, not only making the name inappropriate but also completely erasing the old traces. Popular customs are baseless, and this is generally the case.

Rice Packet Boat In the eleventh month of the year 1827 during the Daoguang era, there was a village woman surnamed Wang living near her mother's house in the south of the Three Pagoda Temple in Huazhong. At the time, the new grain had just been harvested, and the woman made a dish of bobo in a container, intending to take it to her father. Her husband, who was going to the city the next day to trade cloth, asked her to return quickly. The woman agreed, took her child with her, and left. Soon, she did not return by dusk. The next day, they went to inquire, and it was found that she had not arrived home. They searched everywhere but could not find her, so they returned. Upon entering the house, she collapsed on the bed, turning over and over in thought, unsure whether she was alive or dead. Shortly after, she fell into a drowsy sleep. Suddenly, she saw her wife standing in front of the bed with her hair loose, covered in blood, weeping and saying, "I have been killed by a wicked beggar. Tomorrow, you should go along the South Pond road and find the bobo you carried yesterday. If you do, my wrongs can be cleared. But this life is forever farewell to you!" The village farmer hurriedly got up to hold her, but she vanished instantly. Startled, he woke up and got up. He sat and waited until dawn, then went out and followed the pond. Before reaching the northern part of the Wanshou Mountain, he saw a rice packet boat docked on the riverbank from across the river, which made him suspicious. He quickly called for a boat on the embankment, crossed to the boat, and saw two small beggars at the stern of the boat fighting over food. One of the beggars held two pieces of bobo in his hand and scolded, "Yesterday, the master did not let you eat because you did not beg for money, so he gave this basket to me."

"How dare you come back to steal food?" The villager, upon closely examining the buns, found them very similar to those made by his wife, and asked, "Where did your master get these from yesterday?" The young beggar replied, "Yesterday, a woman with a child asked my master to ferry them across. My master then rowed them to the other side, tricking them onto the boat. They had a basket of buns, and now only a few remain." The villager then ran to inform the woman's father-in-law, who gathered dozens of people, armed themselves, and went. They jumped onto the boat, but the two old beggars had already returned. They tied them up and searched them, finding several jars in the front and back holds, some fresh and some dried, all containing broken spines and arms. There were also small jars sealed with mud; when opened, they revealed the heads of the woman and her child, still fresh with blood. The villagers then took the jars together and went to the county magistrate, beating the drum to report the crime. The county magistrate immediately summoned the two beggars for interrogation, and they admitted without hesitation. When asked about the source of the dried meat, they firmly refused to confess. It was heard that the two beggars had a hawk-like gaze and deep-set eyes, with an ugly and fearsome appearance. During the interrogation, they did not cry out in pain either. It is unclear how this case was structured.

In the *Wu Qing Literature*, it is recorded that beggars from western Zhejiang formed gangs and used boats, traveling to various places. They lured and deceived children with drugs, gouged out their eyes, severed their tendons, and deformed their limbs, calling them "potted plants." They forced these victims to beg in the streets, demanding money daily. Beautiful girls were either sold into prostitution or forced into sexual acts. Young and fat children were boiled and eaten. As a result, the perpetrators were often strong and fierce, showing no fear of punishment. Older victims were also cut and their brain marrow, liver, and kidneys were extracted and sold as medicine. Thus, they accumulated great wealth. They bribed influential people to hide them, and when their crimes were discovered by the authorities, they were released through connections and bribes. In the sixth month of the year 1655 during the reign of Emperor Shunzhi, a blind girl of a few years old was begging in the market, and the details were thoroughly investigated. The gang members were tracked down and captured, totaling over ten people. They were brought to the police station, but the police official wanted to protect them. The public reacted angrily, and the gang members were executed and their boats burned. According to the annotation, this is the "Ruo Bao Boat" that Oubei described in his poetry. When I was young, I once saw two people captured in the police station. When their gang members were investigated, they endured torture without confessing and did not cry out in pain or beg for mercy. Their boat was searched, and several organs, including the liver and kidneys, were found. The boat was then sent to the county, and I do not know what happened afterward. Some say that this group often ate human brain marrow, which is why they could endure torture, and since they have connections everywhere, their kind cannot be completely eradicated.

In the middle of the Wanli era, Gao Cai supervised mining operations in Fujian. Originally recommended by Gao Cai, Wei Tianjue and Lin Zongwen used every trick to flatter him. They presented a method: "Take the brain marrow of young boys and girls, mix it with medicine, and consume it. This will restore one's sexual potency and enable one to mate with women and produce offspring." Gao Cai was greatly pleased and bought many young children, breaking their skulls and extracting their brain marrow. Poor families often sold their children out of love. Even evil youths used drugs to seduce young children and sell them to Gao Cai, earning a lot of money. In the tax office pool, there were many white bones. Later, he bought several young women and engaged in secret sexual activities to test the efficacy of the method. He also had young boys skilled in singing and dancing, who were not fewer than ten in number, and he reached the extreme of debauchery. Wei Zhongxian also used this method, which allowed him to have sexual relations with Ke Shi. Several palace maids became pregnant. These people caused disasters, and such practices have been going on for a long time, not just as mentioned in the literature, bribing powerful people for support. ### Translation of the Second Part Jin Jing, courtesy name Jianzhao, was from Guan County. He lost his father at a young age and was intelligent and studious. By the age of ten or so, he had read all the classics, and his writing was clear and impressive. His teacher encouraged him to take the exams. However, his older brother, who was concerned about the expenses of his education, forced him to learn business instead, causing him to abandon his studies, which was not what he truly desired. As he grew older, he became extremely reclusive and generous. His brother criticized him for not knowing how to save money, but Jin Jing remained unchanged. Eventually, his brother arranged for him to marry, and they lived separately. His wife, Meng, was beautiful and virtuous, and often helped him in his charitable endeavors. Despite this, Jin Jing's efforts were not successful, and his family's financial situation worsened over the years.

Once at the end of the year, one of his clan brothers informed him that his relative had died without a coffin. Zhao had no way to help, so he went to consult his brothers. His brother was just setting up a grand ceremony to welcome the gods with his wife. Upon hearing this, he looked at his wife and smiled. His wife also smiled and said, "Now your brother has debts piling up like mountains, and he himself has no means to cope. You have surplus funds and should be generous and fulfill your duty. Why are you doing this to your brother?" She said this and then went inside. His brother also went inside and did not come out again. Zhao returned disappointed, emptying his bag, leaving only a few hundred coins. He then consulted his wife and took off the hairpin from her hair, giving it to his brother. Soon after, the new year had arrived. His wife told him that there was no grain left in the jar. Zhao had no choice but to go back to his brother, asking for a loan of a thousand coins. His brother said, "Ah! There is only a bowl of rice left in my house. I have no way to borrow. How can I help you further?" He repeated this several times and then called his wife to measure out two sheng of red rice and gave it to him. Zhao refused it and cried bitterly as he returned home. His wife greeted him and comforted him, saying, "I have heard that Han Xin, when he was a guest, had the innkeeper pound the mortar for him. A man of high ideals should endure cold and hunger. How could you act like a child in a cow's coat?" Zhao wiped his tears and said, "Indeed. What I regret is that my brother has come to this state!" Before he finished speaking, his father-in-law's hired man brought five dou of rice. The couple then celebrated together.

After a month, food still remained scarce. One day, his father-in-law arrived, unable to prepare lunch, and after sitting and talking for a long time, a young child cried out for food. The father-in-law sighed and said, "If this continues, will the whole family not soon become starving corpses?" He then wrote a letter, recommending his friend Tao Jizhu to take charge of the accounting. After more than a year, he found Tao to be honest and cautious, and ordered him to go to Henan to sell medicine. Upon returning, Tao was robbed by bandits. Suddenly, a man in a splendid robe and fur cap, accompanied by a splendid entourage, galloped up and asked why he had come there. Jianzhao looked up and saw it was his fellow townsman, Mr. Jia. A few years ago, Mr. Jia had been unable to go to the capital with his quota of candidates, and Jianzhao had provided the funds. Now, having passed the imperial examinations and been selected as a county magistrate, Mr. Jia was about to take up his post in Guide. Jianzhao described his situation, and Mr. Jia was moved. He went to see the county magistrate, and the original stolen goods were recovered. Before leaving, Mr. Jia also gave him a hundred gold pieces. Jianzhao returned home and became somewhat prosperous. Previously, there were two branches of plum trees in his courtyard. When Jianzhao borrowed money and returned, he shed tears on them, and the trees withered and died. By this time, one of the trees on the western side suddenly blossomed with flowers. Jianzhao was delighted, taking it as an omen of good fortune. When Tao sent him to Fuliang to collect debts, Jianzhao secretly bought magnolia bark to take with him. Upon arrival, he found that there were too many sellers, and arriving slightly late, he could not sell it, and returned dejected. Coincidentally, the White Lotus rebels rose up, and in the areas where they were defeated, the magnolia trees they had planted were all cut down for firewood, and the price of magnolia bark soared. As soon as Jianzhao arrived, those who heard about it rushed to buy it, making a profit of several times the original price. Thus, he left Tao and returned home, building large mansions and shops, and within a few years he became immensely wealthy.

At that time, his brother's family had fallen into decline, and his sister-in-law had beaten a maid to death, leading to a legal case that had been ongoing for a year. Jianzhao lavished bribes worth thousands of gold pieces, but the case still remained unresolved. He then returned to Henan and consulted with a merchant. The merchant wrote a letter to the county magistrate, and finally, Jianzhao managed to secure a pardon and release. With over ten family members to feed, he was worried about their sustenance, and Jianzhao often helped them financially. His brother thought it was insufficient and even accused Jianzhao of encroaching on land when building his house. The official came to inspect, did not find in favor of his brother, and was going to punish him. Jianzhao begged for mercy, and he privately gave his brother fifty gold pieces to settle the matter. That night, thieves broke into his home, bound his wife and him, and branded them with fire, taking all their money. Later, his eldest son Qingyu passed the local examination and planned to entertain guests, so he went to invite his brother. His brother said, "You will soon be a father-in-law of a scholar-official. At this time, there are many guests coming to congratulate. Just look at your brother, who is in tattered clothes. Won't it be embarrassing for the grand banquet?" He declined to attend. Jianzhao returned and ordered his family to send a gift of sheep, wine, and several chickens and geese. It was late when the guests finally dispersed, and he suddenly heard that his brother's house had caught fire. He led the people to rush to help, but his brother's house burned down completely. It was because his sister-in-law was boiling water to scald chickens for the evening meal. Jianzhao sighed repeatedly and quickly returned, giving away his own house to his brother. Later, his brother and sister-in-law died in poverty. Jianzhao then arranged for their burial and often helped his brother's son.

外史氏曰：I have recounted this matter, and many times I have paused my writing to sigh. As for the character Jianshao, he found himself in dire straits at the end of the year with his food supplies exhausted, his tears wetting the *Tángdì* (a plant symbolizing close family ties), and he sighed, "The Tán family's *jīng* (a metaphorical reference to a family or lineage), has returned? How can the way of heaven be so perplexing!" When he rose to wealth through selling medicine and honored his parents by having a son, he suddenly said, "Could the heavens truly be devoid of compassion?" Yet, he thought of sharing gold in times of urgent need and repaying resentment with virtue. Among close family members, he was indeed so generous. As for his elder brother, his inaction in the face of his younger brother's dire straits, even standing by and doing nothing, showed that his shrewdness and meticulousness in daily life were evident, but ultimately, he failed to protect his family and property. However, the more he rewarded others, the more tragic the consequences became. For his elder brother, one can also reflect on the reasons behind this. When he observed his final words to his younger brother, he again lamented their estrangement, suspicion, and jealousy, even until death they did not realize. This heart, it is as if frozen and starving to death. Alas, is this not the human condition?

The Remedy of the Leftovers In the capital city, there was a wealthy young man named Zhou, who married a woman of exceptional beauty. They had a very close relationship. Later, he became obsessed with male lovers and often stayed away from his wife for several months. His wife stopped eating and drinking altogether, lying ill and languid. Zhou finally entered her room and ordered a doctor to examine her. The doctor arrived, but Zhou had gone elsewhere, so an old woman led the doctor into the room. After examining her, the doctor came out and told the old woman, "The illness is due to being confined for a long time, with suppressed emotions and heat. The treatment should use Yue Ju Wan to release the suppressed emotions. However, the illness did not start from external factors like cold, dampness, or indigestion. A vigorous young man must attend to her, making her happy and enjoying it, to quicken her spirit; melting and transforming her, to regulate her blood; giving her what she likes, to please her stomach; fulfilling her desires, to dissipate the heat. Then guide it to the proper channels, to facilitate the elimination of dampness; supplement it with yang energy, to alleviate the cold. After half a month, the illness will heal on its own. This is truly a precious medicine worth ten thousand gold pieces. Otherwise, it is unlikely that pills and powders alone can achieve the desired effect." The doctor finished speaking and left without writing a prescription. The old woman relayed the doctor's words to Zhou's wife, who believed them and secretly asked the old woman to find several young men. She treated herself as the doctor prescribed, and the illness disappeared as if by magic. After a month, Zhou returned and saw his wife glowing with vitality, as if a morning flower after the rain, with a radiant appearance beyond her usual self. He was greatly pleased and embraced her into the curtain, intending to be intimate with her. Suddenly, he saw several people standing behind the curtain, all pale and emaciated, looking like dried lumps of fat, huddled together. Shocked, he asked where they had come from. His wife hastily replied, "The leftovers, the leftovers."

The Historian said: This matter I once heard from a friend, and upon recalling it, I have written it down. Some say it has already been seen in ancient novels, but I have not yet encountered it myself. I recount this story to warn those who are close to and fond of mischievous children and who, through their own negligence, allow their lands to lie fallow, not daring to pick up the wisdom of others. Therefore, I have preserved it. Rice Cake Ah Liu Rice Cake Ah Liu, son of the Shen family from the northern part of the county, is named Feng Xiang. From a young age, he was mischievous and unscrupulous, and as he grew older, he made gambling his profession. He harbored prostitutes and thieves, and he did not hesitate to do so. All the salt smugglers and habitual thieves from far and near were his allies, and the local officials and law enforcement were his eyes and ears. Therefore, the government could not capture him. There is a temple dedicated to the Black General in the county, commonly known as the Land Temple, located near the Southern Commandery Office, about half a li away. In front of the temple, there are several small gambling dens. Those who run the gambling dens are all his henchmen, and people call them the guards in front of the temple. At that time, the Xu family from the eastern part of the county became wealthy through hoarding and speculation. Ah Liu's gang, including a carpenter and someone named Peach Blossom Bridge, first tried to extort money without success and were looking for an excuse to cause trouble, but they did not find an opportunity. One day, the Xu family sent a store employee to collect debts in the village. He returned to Three-Li Pond, and it was already dark, so he borrowed a candle lantern from a friend's house to continue his journey. Just as his gang members and the guards in front of the temple passed by his door, they discovered that he was a store employee of the Xu family. They then rushed in, falsely accused him of adultery, and bound him. They searched his bag and found more than fifty foreign coins, and they took all of it away.

Upon reporting to Mr. Xu, a complaint was lodged against Liu and others at the county level. The county magistrate, Mr. Wang, who had previously known Mr. Xu, was unable to deal with Liu. He then boasted to the crowd, saying, "With me around, what can a mere county magistrate do! Please tell Mr. Xu that if he cannot deal with me, I will have a way to retaliate." Upon hearing this, Mr. Xu went to the provincial capital to lodge a complaint with the governor. The governor sent officials to the county, but they were unable to arrest Liu. The officials then secretly arrived with several servants at night, unexpectedly capturing Liu first and handing him over to County Magistrate Mr. Zhang. They then went to Huzhou to inform Governor Lin, requesting two military officers and two thousand troops, who accompanied them back to the county and also arrested several soldiers from the county. The governor entrusted a certain official from Hangzhou Prefecture to personally handle the case, and Liu and six others were sentenced to penal servitude, while Village Head Yang Si was sentenced to military service. During the journey, three of the county soldiers escaped, leaving only Liu and several others to be taken to their destination. Within two months, Liu had already escaped back to Shaoxing, and Stone Old Bug, Little Carpenter, and others also returned from other counties. This was because they had substitutes who were answering the roll call in their place. Liu then reopened his gambling den at Beigong Bridge, and his power grew even stronger. In July, he married a woman surnamed Mr., and over a thousand people attended the wedding.

Before, a woman from a respectable family in Shaoxing married a man from another family, and their marriage was quite harmonious. Later, the man was lured by a gambler named Six, who took him away with a large sum of money to the county town, where he stayed for a long time without returning. His wife, missing her husband, followed him there and eventually settled down. The man had been gambling freely with Six, spending most of his wealth. By then, Six became attracted to his wife and found her attractive. He invited the man back to his home, and they gambled together. By evening, several courtesans emerged to entertain them, and as the water clock fell, indicating it was late, Six left. The man, already drunk, immediately embraced one of the courtesans and took her to a side room to play. Just as they lay down to sleep, Six led his gang with weapons and entered, seizing both the man and the courtesan, intending to kill them. The man offered to empty his purse to pay them, but they refused. The others urged him to go back and fetch another 500 gold pieces to add to it, promising to release them and even allow him to sell the courtesan. The man said the money in his bedchamber was already gone. They told him, "You have a thousand gold piece treasure at home, and you don't know it?" The man did not understand what they meant. They instructed him to give his wife to Six, and the 200 gold pieces he lost earlier could also be written off in one stroke. They forced him to sign the contract, and he wept and hesitated. Six drew his knife and moved forward, and the man signed the contract while crying.

The crowd surged to the man's house, calling out for his wife to come out and informing her that he had suddenly fallen ill and collapsed at the home of the six families, and despite efforts to save him, he did not regain consciousness, urging her to hurry and see him. The wife immediately followed, entering the house to find him unharmed, her initial shock subsiding as she saw him alive, but he suddenly rushed forward, grasping her arm and wailing loudly. After a long while, he choked back his tears and explained the reason. The wife was utterly shocked and wanted to run back. The crowd pulled her and said, "You have married Shen Lang, what regret do you have that makes you want to return? With things as they are, can you still come and go as you please?" The man, though rebellious and cunning, saw that the situation was beyond his control, so he gathered his tears and comforted his wife, saying, "It is truly best for you to stay here. If you follow me again, you will likely end up starving to death." After speaking, he wiped his sleeve and left on his own. However, his anger burned like a flame within him, and after walking several dozen steps, he returned to the gate, only to find it already closed. He then unbuckled his belt and attempted to hang himself from the eaves, but the belt broke and he fell to the ground. He returned, intending to take the rope and go again.

Upon entering, he saw the lights shining brightly, and looking around, no human shadow was seen. He cried uncontrollably. After thinking about whether his wife was still alive at this time, if he could just see her one last time before dying, he would die peacefully. Carrying the lamp to sleep, but turning and tossing unable to fall asleep, he recalled from the beginning when he first met Liu, bringing money together. Now what he brought has been completely spent, and his wife was also cheated. Suddenly jumping up, pounding the bed and shouting, "Ah Liu, you must be happy!" Soon after, it became dawn. Going out to the market, buying a short knife and hiding it, intending to wait for an opportunity to stab Liu, but not succeeding. Suddenly hearing that Liu was getting married, going to investigate, finding out that his wife had already left with another man. Furious, he rushed back, took out the hidden knife, sharpened it, and put it in his sleeve. From then on, he never returned.

One day, lightly snowing, I sought out Six to Tang's Lane. As I neared his door, I heard the sound of nailed shoes coming, and I hurried to look, it was Six. He spoke loudly, "Rice cake, have we met tonight?" He drew a knife and stabbed me. Six kicked his right foot up, hitting my wrist, and the knife fell. Because of the excessive force, his nailed shoe fell into the snow. I picked it up and struck him on the head, hitting the top of his head, and Six fell to the ground. I struck him again, and his head was riddled with holes, blood spurted like arrows, and the ground was covered in red snow, and Six could no longer move. It turned out that Six was bald and had a short and small body, so all the strikes were on his head. I discarded the shoe, took the knife, and stabbed him in the abdomen. He died immediately. I rushed to his home, kicked open the door, called out to his wife to come out and told her, and blamed her for betraying me, intending to kill her as well. His wife wept and said, "I endured humiliation and endured suffering until today, wanting to see your face before dying. Now that the great grudge has been avenged, how can I face you again?" She took the knife and stabbed her throat, I tried to stop her, but it had already penetrated half an inch, the blood flowed uncontrollably, and she fell to the ground. I carried her to the bed, tore cloth to wrap her wound. After dawn, she began to slowly recover.

This is the event of the tenth month of the sixteenth year of the Daoguang era. At that time, the magistrate of the prefecture was in the provincial capital, and the person went to the town office to surrender himself. The patrol inspector, a local official, inquired and found out that he was from Shaoxing, and temporarily ordered the archers to guard him. He secretly sent someone to instruct him to escape back home. The person found it difficult due to lack of funds, so he was given fifty taels of silver, and the person then took his wife and left. Now the old stone snake and others are still there. The historian says: "In the *Spring and Autumn Annals of the Sixteen Kingdoms*, Du Yu, when young, once followed a man from Puyang to become a bandit. His mother beat him, and Du Yu said, 'The world is about to be in chaos, and it's better to train one's courage. If things go well, one hopes to be ennobled; if not, at least one won't have others decapitating them.' Du Yu, as a bandit, wore three layers of armor and wielded a halberd, emerging like a thistle. Alas!" During the Five Dynasties period, Wang Jun obtained an official position by running faster than a galloping horse, and Ouyang Xiu once lamented the talents of the chaotic times. It is inevitable that in times of decline and chaos, extraordinary achievements often come from such people. I once told Yang Yiyu, "In times of turmoil, people like Li Bings and Ah Liux are all heroes of the common folk. If we were to bind them with the law, it would be overly rigid. Is this why those who uphold the law resort to lenient governance?" One sigh!

In the early years of the Shunzhi era, Wei Guoyi, the official from Wutai state who served as the Minister of Justice, once dreamed of reaching the underworld, where he took over the duties of the judicial officials in the afterlife. One day, Tang Wen Zheng Gong Bin visited him, and found him sleeping during the day. After waiting for a long time, Wei Guoyi finally emerged. Tang Wen Zheng Gong Bin advised him against sleeping during the day. Wei Guoyi smiled and said, "It wasn't just sleeping. I originally didn't want to talk about it, but since it involves matters of loyalty and integrity, I might as well share it with you. In my dream, I reached the underworld and was asked about the case of Qin Hui." Tang Wen Zheng Gong Bin was surprised and asked, "Is this case still unresolved?" Wei Guoyi replied, "It's not unresolved. In his previous life, Qin Hui was a dog in a wealthy family in Zhuozhou. One night, several bandits armed with knives broke in and tied up the master. The master dared not cry out and allowed them to search. The bandits were still unsatisfied and suspected there might be hidden treasures, threatening him with a knife to reveal their location. However, there was nothing left in the house. The bandits raised their knives to cut him down. The dog, seeing this, fiercely bit the bandit's foot, causing the bandit to turn around and sever the dog's head, allowing the master to escape in the confusion. The underworld officials praised the dog's loyalty and allowed it to be reborn as a son of the Qin family. Thus, Qin Hui was born with eyes that shone at night. Unaware of his true origins, he betrayed the virtuous and sold out his country, accumulating such great sins. The king of hell punished him to be reborn as a pig for thirty lifetimes as retribution for the murder of loyal ministers. Yet, Qin Hui still wishes to be reborn as a dog."

Master Tang said, "Could dogs possibly be superior to pigs?" The official smiled and replied, "This is why they are cunning and deceitful. Dogs are not completely killed, while pigs cannot escape being slaughtered. He was just beaten three hundred times, but he still did not confess. Then he was subjected to the torture of the red-hot iron, and finally he confessed. Now he is being taken to Bianzhou to be made into a pig." Asked, "Where was he before?" He said, "This case cannot be hastily concluded. Since the Prince of Ying entered Yan, he has been ordered to be reborn into the family of Yue, to be a mouse to feed his cat, so as to repay the resentment of Wumu. Now he is only allowed to be reborn elsewhere." Master Tang said, "Since the peace agreement was made, the Song Dynasty has been paying annual tributes of gold and silver, seeking temporary peace in half of its territory. The emperor and his ministers have been indulging in pleasure, with no intention of reclaiming the Central Plains, leading to their downfall. The policy of the South remaining in the South and the North remaining in the North was initiated by Ke, and Ke truly brought it to fruition. This is why his crime of betraying the country is even greater." He said, "This idea was given by the Jin people, under the leadership of Emperor Gaozong, who enjoyed a short reign after the southward migration, partly due to his own actions. After killing Ke's descendants, he was allowed to die before the Song Dynasty, which is enough to cover his guilt. But his crime of killing Wumu in prison and executing those who did not agree with the peace agreement, is particularly difficult to forgive. He needs to be reborn as a pig for thirty generations, in order to vent the anger of one loyal minister." Master Tang sighed and left.

Tang and Lu Qingxian were both recommended by the public. Furthermore, according to *Yishi Zihai*: In the third year of the Wanli era (1576), Wu Rubi from Jingkou traveled to Hangzhou. He saw a pig being slaughtered. All the hair was removed, and on its belly were five characters that read: "The tenth incarnation of Qin Hui." In the middle of the Kangxi era, an unnamed person from Zhening traveled to Wuling and happened to be at a butcher's house where a pig was being slaughtered. The words "Qin Hui" were found on the pig's hooves and lung tubes. No one dared to buy it. The person bought it resolutely, took it back, and gave it to a servant. After it was cooked, he led the group to Yue Wang Temple, worshiped in a circle, and offered it. After the sacrifice, they ate it freely. Those who heard this were greatly pleased. In the household of Xu Xiangguo, a high-ranking official from Qingzhou, they once slaughtered a pig. They scalded off the hair, and inside the meat were hidden words that read: "The seventh incarnation of Qin Hui." They cooked and ate it, but it smelled extremely foul. It is said that the ancestor of the high-ranking official was killed by Qin Hui in the Song dynasty. Therefore, he revered Yue Fei throughout his life and especially built a Yue Wang Temple in the northern part of Qingzhou city. He also cast statues of Qin Hui and Wan Qi Xi kneeling at the foot of the stairs. Is this pig to show compensation? If so, the theory of retribution is indeed proven. *Jianggu Ji* also records: In the third year of the Wanli era (1598), in Zhu Village, 30 li away from Fengyang city, a white cow was struck by lightning. All the hair burned off, and on its back were the words "Qin Hui." Is it to avoid its punishment, so it became a cow instead of a pig? And ultimately, it died from lightning. The treacherous minister could not escape the heavenly net, thus.

Furthermore, Qin Xi was originally a son of the Wang family, and Qin Hui had always disliked him. Qin Xi had a nature that feared his wife. His concubine was pregnant, and his wife drove her away, resulting in the birth of a son who became a son of Lin from Xianyou, named Yifei. Because of Qin Hui, he rose to the position of Shilang (a high-ranking official). The *Jin Lei Zi* section of the *Song Shi* (History of the Song Dynasty) records: Qin Hui's great-grandson, Qin Ju, served as Tongpan of Qi Prefecture. When the Jurchens invaded the border, he fought alongside the prefect Li Chengzhi with all his might. When the city fell, Qin Ju led the soldiers in street battles and then returned to his office, burning himself to death. His sons Jun and Bi also died. The descendants of a wicked minister, yet a whole family died for loyalty and filial piety—how could this be determined by their lineage? However, Qin Hui had no biological son and adopted his brother-in-law Wang Huan's son as his heir, thus the Qin family line ended with Qin Hui for a long time. This also suggests that Qin Xi was not Qin Hui's biological son, and the historical records are not entirely reliable. It is said that in the early Jiajing era, a descendant of Qin Hui's lineage served as the magistrate of Tangyin and had a good reputation. He always wanted to visit the Yue Zhongwu Temple but hesitated and did not succeed. As his term was about to end, he told his colleagues, "Yue Shaobao, although he had enmity with our ancestors, would not affect our descendants. I have served my office without shame to the gods, so going to visit will cause no harm." He then wrote an article to offer as a sacrifice. He bowed but could not stand up, spat out several dou of blood, was supported out of the temple gate, and then died. From this and what is recorded in the *Song Shi*, it is known that Qin Hui had a son. But how can we know that this is not a descendant of Wang Huan's son? The *Ming Shi* (History of the Ming Dynasty) records: Qiu Qiongshan said that Fan Zhongyan caused trouble, and Yue Fei might not have been able to restore the country, while Qin Hui had the merit of rebuilding the country. This is a startling statement! According to his words, the Southern Song Dynasty's reign depended on Qin Hui's efforts, and Wei Gong's action was an excessive punishment. Historians say he was broad in his arguments but often biased—indeed.

In the reign of Emperor Kangxi, Mr. Zhang Songcun once traveled to the Seven Provinces of Fujian, serving in the office of a certain governor of Fujian and quelling the rebellion of Zhu Yigui. On his return, he anchored his boat at Zhangpu. After dinner, the moon rose over the water, and the seagulls startled awake with a rustling sound, leaving him feeling melancholy and unable to sleep. He then went ashore, intending to visit the ruins of the Mulan Temple. Not knowing the way, he walked aimlessly and entered an ancient temple. Inside, he found three people sitting together, drinking. The green osmanthus trees flickered softly, and a beautiful woman, dressed in a blue-green silk and red gauze skirt, about eighteen or nineteen years old, sat gracefully beside a pipa. When Mr. Zhang arrived, they all stood up and bowed to greet him. He entered and asked about their surnames. One was named Yang Zixuan, and another was named Qian Xiangling. The third person spoke in the Wu dialect, introducing himself as Li Youzhu, a nephew of Mr. Fanxia. He loved mountains and water, admired the fame of Yandang, crossed the river to visit Dongou, and then traveled to Wuyi. He had been living there for ten years. After his words, he looked sad. Qian scolded, "Meeting a distinguished guest on such a fine night, why are you talking like captives in a gloomy manner?" They then rinsed their cups and poured more wine, drinking deeply and cheering loudly.

The gentleman usually enjoys drinking heavily, and after drinking several large cups of wine in succession, he recounted the events at the platform, causing Yang to sigh and lament without end. Qian clapped his hands and said, "I have come up with a drinking game." Lie said, "Good! How could we allow boorish drinking and noise when we are in the presence of refined scholars? We should not appear as uncouth. But it must be original; if anyone borrows from others, they shall be punished as severely as the penalty for drinking at the Jin Valley gathering." Qian then said while holding up a cup of wine, "Cut off Yang's head (as mentioned in the *Supplement to the History of the Five Dynasties*). The sheep's head rots, he becomes an official in Fujian." He asked the others if they could respond to these three phrases. Yang responded immediately, "Pierce through the coin's hole (also mentioned in the *Supplement to the History of the Five Dynasties*). The spring's eye is open, he dies in Zhejiang." Yang originally became a magistrate of Dachan by purchasing an office, and was later killed by Zhu Yigui; Qian died as a guest in Shaoxing. Hence, the two men mock each other. Next, Lie said, "I also have two words to respond with." He then declared, "The moon is full in heaven, half in the world." The others thought for a long time but could not respond, so they asked him to reveal the answer. Lie said, "This phrase has always been difficult to find a match for, so I have asked you all to help." The crowd became noisy, and they were about to take a large cup of wine to punish him.

Lai said, "At this time, I can no longer withstand the power of alcohol. How about I compose a small poem to repay the debt?" The crowd laughed and said, "That will do. We cannot bear to see you trembling, so let us put this aside for now." Lai then recited, "In the depth of night, I stand on the frost-covered board bridge, the tangerines and pomelos know the cold and have turned yellow. The endless green mountains and the lake road, only follow the smoke and moon in the dream of Qiantang." The second verse was, "The wind blows through the vast wilderness, strange birds cry, each leaf of the traveling clothes turns to mud. Tonight, I will send you off with a blast on the iron flute, the reeds and maple leaves also contain sorrow." Qian quickly covered his mouth and said, "When you open your mouth, it is filled with sorrow and sighing, making people unhappy." Turning to the beautiful lady, he said, "Sing beautifully for me, to replace the羯drum." The lady then smiled and took up the琵琶, softly singing a verse of "Three Laughs Under the Moon." The melody was tender and graceful, captivating the entire room. At that time, Yang was already drunk and immediately embraced her, placing her on his knee, and gave her his embroidered half-sleeve. Suddenly, a man arrived in a sedan chair at the door, burst in, and shouted, "You shameless wench, why are you here again to show off?" The lady fled in panic. Yang, angry, stood up and said, "You still act this way, who are you trying to scare?" He punched him, and the crowd persuaded him to sing in place of the punch.

The person, with a bashful face, took their seat and began to sing, keeping rhythm while Qian played the transverse flute to accompany them. The song went: "I resent this rascal, who flirts with his eyes and secretly casts his youthful passion. On the moss-covered ground, flowers have fallen, leaving an early imprint of a pair of spring traces. The flowers are unnamed, their fragrance just beginning to be smelled. It's like a lute played under the moon, captivating like the legendary beauty of Jiangshan. Partly unfastening the silk robe, the fragrance of orchids gradually spreads, as two butterflies rest in fragrant dust. In a sleepy and emotional state, feeling deeply moved, I gently cherish and carefully examine. Do not ask about the high wall of Song or the distant path to Han. Searching and seeking, again, the evening rain turns to a deep green. A flower path shrouded in mist, the river gate reflecting the moon, a moment worth a thousand gold pieces. Removing socks and the incense burner, hiding the lamp and clothes rack, letting the arm be wrapped in tilted golden cloth, the head being scratched with smooth jade. Even more resentful of my beloved, hating and pitying, deeply cherishing, trembling and swaying, circling around, tilting. Soft jade hooks, strangely and without cause, the phoenix pearl gradually slipping off."

The song had not yet ended when Yang stood up and struck the table, saying, "This is the *Ge Nong Qu* by Liao Yingzhong. We have agreed tonight not to repeat the old. You are originally a scoundrel from the marketplace, but you only accompany your favorites to commit theft and fraud. Surely you do not know there are matters of literature and writing in the world! Yet you pretend to admire scholarly elegance, hiring guests to publish books and inscriptions, associating yourself with famous people. Now you have forgotten your true nature. How can such a shameless person be tolerated?" He stood up and roared, and suddenly turned into a tiger, carrying the person away in his mouth. The crowd was shocked and scattered, and the master also stood up. Lai held him back and said, "Do not be afraid. The singer was none other than Cao Jia Sidao of the Song dynasty. Therefore, Yang transformed into this form to devour his soul. The earlier singer was Shen, whom Cao carried with him during his exile. Now I still have a letter, please deliver it to my home." He then took the letter from his chest, gave it to the master, saw him off to the boat, and parted with tears, bidding farewell solemnly.

After Mr. [Name] went to Hangzhou to visit the Li family, he indeed found such a person. He gave them a letter, and the son opened and read it, then realized that the person had been dead for a long time. At the end of the letter, he instructed them to quickly go and collect the bones. He sighed and returned. Ghost Boat My eldest brother, Dai Rong, was nicknamed Xue Lin. He was particularly intelligent and sincere and devoted in character, filial and respectful to his elders, and his demeanor was gentle. He especially liked me. Due to poverty, he could not attend school, and my second uncle taught him to read. However, he still could not afford it. He only managed to read two books, *The Analects* and *The Doctrine of the Mean*, so he gave up studying and learned to be a merchant. After my second uncle's parents passed away, my third brother went to Wulin to do business, and my brother also went to Meili to do business, but he never stopped reading. At that time, my parents were both healthy, and he often came to visit us. Whenever he had free time, he would always ask me about the teachings of the classics, and he especially liked to talk about poetry. He learned calligraphy from Mi and Dong and achieved a good likeness of their style. His love for learning was innate. Whenever I saw him, it was like meeting a good friend. We would talk until midnight. However, my brother had to work hard and could not stay long. Since I had no other brothers, after we parted, I often felt sad as if I had lost my hands and feet. Later, my brother gradually accumulated some wealth, and my father urged him to marry. So he married a woman from Meili in the 21st year of Jiaqing. The following spring, the wedding was scheduled. He then came home on a merchant ship, and he was going to ask my father and my third brother to go together. On the way to Changzhou, he drowned when the boat capsized.

Three of his brothers came to Changzhou to carry his coffin. The people of Changzhou reported: "That night, a great storm with wind and thunder occurred, followed by rain and snow. The boat could not move and was already anchored in a shallow area of the pond. Soon, they saw a boat at the shore, moving as swiftly as a horse. On top of the mast, a lamp shone brightly, and the characters 'deng xian' (ascending to immortality) were written on it. It seemed like someone called out to them, saying, 'The wind is so strong, why are you still anchored here?' Thus, those in the boat told each other that they could follow this boat and continue their journey. They quickly hoisted the sails and left, but the previous boat had already disappeared. The wind became even stronger. They hurriedly wanted to turn the boat around, but it was too late." At that time, seven people drowned together, but only the boatman, who was skilled in swimming, was spared. He was thirty-three years old.

The historian says: The world's drowned ones are many, but what sin did my brother commit to come to this? I often think of it and cannot sleep until dawn. I intend to emulate the style of Liu Zongyuan's "Invitation to the Sea Trader" to mourn him, but whenever I pick up the brush, I become overwhelmed and cannot complete a coherent piece. Until today, I often call myself foolish or unlucky. Now my third brother still lives, aged sixty-four, but he has no children. Is it that the line of my second uncle will end? This is another reason for deep mourning. Biography of Two Servants (Excerpt from *Gao Tang Wen Chao*) During the late Ming, the loyal and steadfast official of Yinzhou, Li Zhongyi, had two servants. One was named Ren Rui, tall and skilled in drinking, with an understanding of music and a clever nature. He enjoyed associating with frivolous people. The other was Kong Rui, dark-skinned, short, and cunning. His mother and younger brother both relied on the Li household. The Li household treated these two servants very well. When the Li household fell into trouble, they both left. Ren Rui joined the night guards in Haimen, and Kong Rui became a messenger in a certain general's camp.

After the public was brought in chains to Xiling, his wife sent someone with money to accompany him and provide for his needs. Just then, a servant on an errand to the provincial capital met the public on the road. Under the influence of alcohol, the servant called out the master's name and cursed, trying to intercept and seize the money he was carrying. The servant's former associates in Xiling, who were trying to help the public, all moved away to avoid him. Finally, the servant divided the money and then stopped.
