Meanwhile, a servant named Kong at home sometimes rushed into the public's home with two groups of soldiers, taking belongings and leaving. He then submitted a report of concealment to the deputy general, who forcefully took hundreds of gold coins from the public's home and presented a fur coat to the general officer. His treacherous acts against his master were all like this. Not long after, a certain deputy general sent the servant named Kong to carry an urgent letter to the provincial capital, which was delivered to the general's headquarters. The servant traveled for several days and saw a person on the road, wearing a hat with a sword and carrying a bag. Gradually, he approached and talked with the person, learning that each was a soldier from a certain camp, carrying letters to the provincial capital to report an alarm and prepare for it. So, they stayed together at night and drank together, sleeping on a single bed. The next night, the person bought even more wine, took beef and deer jerky from the bag, and drank freely, making an agreement to worship with incense and candles. Almost midnight, they were just sleeping deeply. They traveled to Qiantang, where the person said, "If you go first, I will wait for the rest of the group. We must go to the provincial capital in order. We will drink deeply at a certain wine shop in Wushan." Then, they parted. But Kong had a son in the provincial capital who met his father, greatly pleased, and they carried the letter to the general's headquarters. The general, sitting in his tent, opened the letter and suddenly became very angry, immediately ordering someone to drag them out and beheading them. The servant was in a panic, unable to say a word, and the heads of both father and son were cut off. The person they had met on the road was actually a spy from a mountain bandit camp, holding a surrender decree, which they had secretly replaced while they were sleeping deeply.

The person named "任" served as a night patrol for several years and was punished by having his rations taken away due to some offense. Daily indulging in gambling and eating, he used his boots and hats as collateral to pay off debts to the tavern, having nowhere to turn, he daily huddled in rags, lying on the bed with an empty stomach, ashamed to face people. One day, he happened to leave home and did not return. By dawn, a rumor spread that a body was floating in South Lake, holding another body, and one of the bodies was that of "任." Some believed that the servant could not bear the cold and hunger and drowned himself. Others said that the servant met an old drinking companion and got drunk. Returning home in the dark, he sat on a bridge, thinking he was lying on his bed at home, only to fall asleep and fall off the bridge, hitting a stone and dying with a crushed brain. Still others said that when a person drowns, their soul becomes a water ghost and needs to find a replacement to rest. This servant, after getting drunk, was dragged into the water by a ghost and was then pulled out by someone else. In summary, the cause of the servant "任"'s death remains unknown. As for the death of another servant named "孔," even he did not know how he died, and both father and son died. Heaven's punishment for evil people, punishing traitors and thieves, is indeed strange. How frightening! How frightening!

段珠 During the reign of Emperor Yongzheng, there was a man named Duan Qi in Shimen who was famous for his martial prowess across the land. His sister was named Zhu, who learned martial arts from her elder brother and mastered them to an extraordinary degree. She was around sixteen or seventeen years old, and her beauty was unparalleled in the world. One day, a Shaolin monk visited them, knocking on their door. Duan Qi was not home, so his sister responded from upstairs. The monk teased her, saying, "If you allow me to see your beautiful face, wouldn't it be even better than your brother's? This is a heavenly opportunity." The girl became angry and leaped down from the upstairs, kicking the monk in the temples with her toe, penetrating several inches. The monk's eyeballs popped out, and he died. In the early years of Emperor Jiaqing, the Miao rebels caused unrest in Sichuan and Hubei. Qi Lin, originally a leader of the White Lotus Society in Xiangyang, was executed after his case was broken. After his sons, including Zhi Fu, rebelled, they welcomed Qi Lin's widow, Lady Qi Wang, as their chief instructor. The bandits followed her commands, and she was known as "Qi Er Gufu" (Second Widow Qi). She was the most fierce and cruel, and they wrote "Avenging My Husband" on their flags, making their rebellion particularly rampant. After a long time, they were defeated. According to *The Compilation of Suppressing the Teaching Rebels*, she was described as having a very beautiful and seductive appearance, with small and delicate feet. She roamed and slept in the wild mountains with a pack of wolves and pigs, and her reputation was at the top among the bandits. She was truly a demon or monster. It is said that whenever Qi Er Gufu went into battle, she wore a pheasant feather headdress and a red brocade battle robe. On horseback, she wielded twin swords, moving with agility and strength like a flying bird, and was invincible in battle. Sometimes, she would lift one foot and rush down from the top of a mountain, crossing slopes and ravines without ever stumbling. Her agility and strength can be imagined. Lady Qi had a female servant named Hei Nüzi, who was also brave and skilled in combat, and was respected by the bandits. Later, they were defeated by government troops at Xiehua Slope and both threw themselves off a cliff and died.

Jin San, the Martial Arts Master** Jin San, the Martial Arts Master, was from Wuling. His martial arts skills were passed down from his ancestor Shi Yin. Once, he came to the county town to teach his disciples. One day, he performed martial arts at the Temple of General Wu. There was a young man named Kong Liu, who was in his prime and proud of his courage. He wanted to challenge Jin San. Unexpectedly, he jumped up with one foot. Jin San smiled and said, "Don't play tricks." He clasped two fingers and inserted them into the shoe seam, and his foot immediately could not lift. Upon inspection, the shoe buckle had come off, but his foot was not injured. It just happened to hit the concave part. Later, they went out to the mountain gate, where several sparrows were perched on the top of the southern stage of the theater. Jin San reached into his pocket, took out a pebble about the size of a mulberry seed, placed it on his index finger, and smiled at Kong Liu, saying, "I'll show you how to make the third sparrow fall." He flicked it away with his thumb, and the sparrow fell immediately. Kong Liu was greatly impressed. Kong Liu mentioned that Jin San once served as a bodyguard in Shanxi. One day, while traveling alone in the mountains, he encountered a mythical creature called Qing Si, which was as fast as a galloping horse. After walking for a mile, they came to a deep stream about ten feet wide. Jin San faced the stream and stood. When the creature was about to reach him, he suddenly leaped up and used his two feet to kick its back. The creature fell into the stream and died. Jin San was short and stocky, shorter than average height. However, the mace he used weighed no less than fifty catties. **Reading the Law

It is said that people from Jiangxi province are fond of lawsuits. There is a book titled "Deng Sixian," which contains methods for litigation. It initially teaches disrespectful writing, but if that fails, it resorts to deceit and slander. If deceit and slander fail, it seeks to accuse them of crimes to intimidate them. "Sixian" is a person's name, and people spread his methods, thus naming the book after him. In village schools, this method is often taught to students. Nowadays, many people from the Chen family in Dali Port, Huzhou, make a living as traveling officials. Their children are taught the law from a young age. One person reads the Four Books and does not read other classical texts, specializing in reading the law. He uses this knowledge to pass the imperial examinations and consistently achieves high ranks. The book he reads has detailed annotations and clear explanations, with citations mostly from classical texts and historical works, making it superior to "Deng Sixian." Min, from Lotus Pavilion, is the grandson of a certain official named Zhi Ting and the cousin of another official named Jian San. He passed the imperial examinations in the year Ding Mao. He is unrestrained and unconstrained by convention, loves drinking, and has a face red like ochre. He often wears tattered, coarse clothing and worn-out shoes that are as dirty and greasy as a mirror. He goes to the street every day to get drunk. When someone talks to him about literature and art, he immediately engages in grand and forceful debate, acting as if no one is around. He is especially skilled at writing poetry for the imperial court and often sells his poetry to make a living. Each poem costs fifty coins, and his poems and essays are written quickly, but he spends the money he earns immediately, often writing while hungry. According to "The Collection of Anecdotes from the Fishing Hermit," during the time when Qiu Wanqing was not yet famous, he used to sell his poetry with a sign, charging thirty coins per poem. If he stopped writing or messed up his ink, he would be fined fifteen coins. Now, Min does not need to put up a sign in the market, and his prices are much higher than Qiu's, indeed surpassing him in later years.

Poetic Omen Xu Hezhou, a poet from my hometown, was highly regarded by Mr. Cheng Junxuan for his poem "Plum Soul" when he was young, and Mr. Cheng arranged for him to marry his daughter. Shortly after, Xu contracted a debilitating illness and remained bedridden for thirty years, ultimately passing away without ever marrying. Mr. Cheng's daughter, who remained a maiden, is now over sixty years old. In the months before his death, Xu composed four chapters of a poem titled "Wilted Lotus," after which he ceased writing altogether. People all say this was a "poetic omen." Autumn Swallow Poem In the autumn of the year of Wu Zi, I was staying at the Ma family's residence in Xincheng. Ma Zhongying, a student, asked me to revise his poem "Autumn Swallow." I found the latter half of the poem to be overly formal and revised it to read: "The falling moon on the empty beam startles the broken dream, the autumn wind in the ancient alley resents the slanting evening light. The young peach and willow trees have all withered, yet still murmuring affection for the embroidered curtains." Just as I put down my brush, news arrived that my wife, Wu, had passed away. After returning home to perform the funeral rites, I immediately went to the prefectural examination in Wulin. Before I could return, my young daughter, A Ying, also died. Only then did I realize the ominous nature of the earlier poem. Fan Chi Temple I once accompanied Jin Guchun to visit the Fan Chi Temple in Chongming. The temple was bustling with worshippers. When it rained, the temple attendant covered a soy sauce jar with a piece of fish scales, as large as a mat. In front of the corridor, two large jars were hung, their bright and white color catching the eye. Upon closer inspection, they were neither glass nor clear horn. I asked the attendant, who replied, "These were made by carving fish eyes." We were amazed and discussed this for a long time.

I said to Gu Chun: "Fan Chi was originally from Qi. I have never heard that he had ever been to the South Sea. How could he be revered here?" The temple attendant smiled and said: "Do the two guests also know why Confucius is revered as a sage?" I said: "I do not know." The temple attendant said: "Once upon a time, a man from Lu got lost at sea. He arrived at Danzhou and saw Confucius and his seventy-two disciples playing in the sea. He gave a wooden staff to the man from Lu, instructing him to close his eyes and ride it back. He told the Duke of Lu to build a city to defend against enemies. When the man threw the staff, it turned into a dragon. He reported the whole story to the Duke of Lu. The Duke of Lu did not believe him. Soon after, tens of thousands of swallows brought earth to strengthen the city walls. The people of Lu then built a large city at Qufu. After it was completed, the Qi invaders came, but failed to capture the city and returned. This is why Confucius is revered as a sage. Fan Chi was always interested in profit. He came here to trade cotton, providing himself with clothing and food. Later, he traded further to the outer regions, exchanging for fur coats, which he sold in Wu, making enormous profits. Later, when our county suffered a great famine, Fan Chi brought grain to relieve the famine, and the starving people were able to survive thanks to him. After his death, the people of the county thought of him, and so his worship continues to this day."

I looked back and said to Gu Chun, "Have you heard? Nowadays, the temples with magnificent structures and deities worshipped in a specific region are mostly like Fan Chi selling cotton." I laughed and left. In the past, at the Fang Ta Temple in Changshu, there was a blue-faced demon-god, which was the trace of Zhang Gong from Suizhou. With red hair and a blue face, he held a large snake in his mouth, resembling a night demon. Some say that he vowed to become a vengeful ghost to kill the rebels upon his death, which is why he appears as a vengeful ghost. The temple in my hometown, Dongping, originally worshipped Zhang Gong alongside Yan Gong. Now it has been changed into a City God Temple, and the deity still has a dark face, curly beard, and glaring eyes, because the common people, being ignorant, still follow the old ways. Even Wu Zhi Xu and Du Shi Yi, are they not found everywhere?

施氏 In my village, there was a family named Shi. Her father once managed accounts for my family. After her father's death, she married a merchant. She often came to my family's home. Later, due to poverty, she could no longer support herself and hanged herself. At that time, there was an adopted daughter-in-law named Ah Fu who entered the room and screamed upon seeing her. Her son rushed in and found her hanging from the beam, with disheveled hair and erratic movements, white foam flowing from her mouth. He quickly untied her and rescued her, and she revived after some time. Later, she came to my family's home and said that she did not feel any pain at the time. Two years later in winter, when my mother was just finishing her meal, before noon, Shi came to cry bitterly. My sisters tried to console her, and after a long while, she finally stopped, but she continued to weep. When we tried to persuade her to eat, she refused. As evening approached, she told us she was leaving, and we could not persuade her to stay; she left, crying. By night, her second son, who was with many guests drinking, suddenly saw a neighbor rush over and shout, "Your mother has hanged herself!" The son rushed to save her, but she did not revive. I have heard that those who hang themselves on mulberry trees or bed rails cannot be saved. In our village, there was a man named Cai A San who was always a troublemaker. Later, he had a conflict with someone named Shen from the same village and went to his house, shouting and cursing. The Shen family, including father and son, all avoided him. One morning, it was suddenly reported that Cai had hanged himself in the mulberry grove of the Shen family. I went to see him and found him hanging from the mulberry tree, with one foot on the ground, his right foot also touching the ground, and his knee bent. Only his tongue slightly protruded, less than half an inch. At the time of his death, he probably did not feel any pain either.

Another of my cousin's wife, surnamed Zhang, was in her youth quite strong-willed. Later, she contracted a mental illness, which took years of treatment to cure, and she became even more gentle and yielding, taking up Buddhism. As a result, the whole family loved each other. However, by the time she was over forty, she had attempted suicide twice. After several more years, her family was about to worship the gods, and my cousin went in to fetch incense and candles. Just then, the servants and concubines were all absent, so Madam Zhang asked to go to the Buddha's basket to fetch them. She went up the stairs herself, and for a long time, there was silence. My cousin couldn't wait any longer, so he went to see, and found that she had hanged herself from the beam and died. Later, my elder sister told me: "Half a year ago, she seemed to say that whenever she walked, she was always accompanied by four people. One of them was a beautiful woman, wearing a purple silk jacket and a black half-sleeve, who often looked back and smiled. The one in front had white hair and beard, wore a square robe and a headband, resembling the image of a land god in a temple. The two behind also had gray hair and beards, resembling people in their fifties or sixties. Sometimes the three disappeared, and this woman would lead her into a hidden room. When she woke up, she realized she had already hanged herself. Now, she could not escape her fate." Observing this alongside the case of Shi, one sees that the swiftness or slowness of destiny cannot be forced.

**Kongkong'er** During the reign of Emperor Qianlong, Huang Taibao, the governor of the Two Jiangs, was touring the frontier and arrived at Zhenjiang Prefecture. His boat anchored at Jingkou when he suddenly lost the rosary hanging around his neck, which greatly alarmed him. He ordered the local authorities to conduct a strict search, setting a deadline of one month for the rosary to be returned. The magistrates and officials, upon receiving the order, immediately dispatched men to investigate everywhere, but the rosary left no trace. Soon, the deadline was approaching, and all leads had run dry. The magistrate, in deep thought and at a loss for a solution, decided to leave his office and secretly investigate. After several days, he reached the back of Gouqu Mountain, where he encountered a beautiful and graceful young woman. She wore a crimson silk robe, had pointed shoes and narrow sleeves, and was walking along the sheer cliffs to gather *nǚzhēn* (a type of plant). She climbed a tree with the agility of a bird, which astonished him. He waited for her to return and followed her to the streamside, where she entered a cave. He followed her inside as well. Inside, the cave was as large as several acres, with winding, shadowy passages that twisted like a snake, far from the human world. Near the end of the cave, there were several thatched huts, surrounded by a hedge of *jǐn* (honey locust) outside. An old woman was washing dishes at the stove when she saw him and asked in surprise, “Is this not Official [such-and-such]? How did you come here?” He bowed respectfully and explained his purpose. The old woman smiled and said, “Oh, I suppose it’s my daughter playing a trick on you again. This girl has not changed her childish ways, causing you such anxiety. I will certainly reprimand her. But I don’t know where she is now. Please return for now; tomorrow I will have her return the rosary. Official, you may come to the top of the pagoda at Baoren Temple in the afternoon and retrieve it.” The official was startled, respectfully agreed, and left. He hurried back to report to Taibao, who was greatly surprised and astonished.

The next day, the deputy general was ordered to lead troops to the Ring Pagoda, drawing their bows and loading their arrows in preparation. At midday, with many eyes watching, they looked up at the top of the pagoda. Suddenly, a streak of red light flickered like lightning, and the rosary was already hanging on the top. In an instant, thousands of crossbows fired simultaneously, but the target was elusive, like trying to catch a shadow. The deputy general then ordered strong soldiers to climb the pagoda using a ladder and retrieve the rosary. Attached to the rosary was a letter, inscribed as "Handwritten Seal of Kōngkōng," which was presented to the high-ranking official for reading. The letter roughly stated that since taking office, the official had used power to disturb the scholars and common people, cunning to deceive the ruler, authority to humiliate local officials, and spying to allow soldiers to act freely, fabricating charges against close advisors and bullying while claiming isolation. The official furthered greed with cruelty and practiced deceit with power, shouldering the responsibility of border defense but harboring plots of the underworld. One region suffered widespread suffering, with scholars having no recourse and censors daring not to criticize. Therefore, this item was taken from the official just to serve as a warning. If the official did not quickly plan to repent and reform, continuing past faults, the official's head would be taken immediately, to serve as a warning for high-ranking officials, and so on.

The Grand Tutor finished reading, and the person was so frightened that their hair stood on end and they shivered; their greed and violence were somewhat restrained from then on. Ghost Light In the three-family village of Tongxiang, there was Xu Xiaoshan. Once, after returning from the capital city, his boat reached Yongxing Ferry just as dusk was falling. Suddenly, dark clouds spread all around, and wind and rain mixed together, making the sky as black as ink, and it was impossible to distinguish east from west. The boatman was greatly terrified and at a loss whether to advance or retreat. In a state of bewilderment, he suddenly saw a single point of phosphorescent light in the dense undergrowth. The light was larger than a lamp, gradually moving closer to the shore, shining with a clear, brilliant light, illuminating the water as if it were daytime. As the boat moved, so did the phosphorescent light, as if it were guiding the way. It continued until it reached the large stone bridge in the village, where the light then disappeared. Calculating the distance illuminated by the light, it was over thirty miles. This is what Ke Shi said, and he believed it was a good omen for Xiao Shan. However, I once asked Xiao Shan, and he said that he had never before buried any remains or bones in this area. Xiao Shan was always skilled in divination, but this place had never been used for burials, nor were there any ancestral tombs.

The Historian says: In the *Unusual Events of the Diamond Sutra* by Duan Chengshi of the Tang Dynasty: During the reign of Emperor Dezong, my father traveled from Jing to Shu in response to an invitation from Wei Nankang. After Wei died, a usurper took control as the acting governor. My father had previously disagreed with the usurper and, upon hearing this, left the county at night. The usurper soon issued an order, forbidding all county officials from leaving their posts. That evening, it was raining, and as he left the county gates for two li, he saw two torches leading the way for a hundred steps. Initially, he thought it was the county officials coming to greet him and was surprised that they did not approach. The torches moved with precision in height, distance, and direction, disappearing just as they were about to reach the county gates. When he asked the county officials, they were still unaware of the usurper's order. At that time, my father had been reciting the *Diamond Sutra* for five or six years, and the torches that guided the way were actually traces left by the sutra, so they said. However, Xiao Shan (the author) had never recited sutras or mantras before, and the torches encountered by Duan Chengshi's father may not have been caused by reciting the sutra.

**The Oration to the Crocodile** Kundien, a kingdom located on the southeastern coast of Basa, is accessible by a journey of about a day’s sail with favorable winds. The harbor is also under the control of Dutch officials, and foreign ships anchor there. From there, one can hire a small boat to enter the inner port, travel about five miles, and then head northeast for another day to reach the Wanla harbor. Another day’s journey brings one to Dongwanli. To the northeast, several dozen miles away, lies the region of Shalaman, where Chinese people were engaged in gold mining. During the reign of Emperor Qianlong, a man from Guangdong named Luo Fangbo traded in this area. He was a brave and generous man skilled in martial arts, and he could win the hearts of the people. Once, there was an uprising by the local indigenous people, and Luo Fangbo led the people to suppress it. There were also crocodiles that were causing harm to the local population, and the king was unable to control them. Luo Fangbo erected an altar by the seaside, arranged sacrifices, and recited and burned the *Oration to the Crocodile* by Changli (Han Yu). Soon after, a fierce storm arose, and the crocodiles fled, thus ending the problem. As a result, both the Chinese and the local people revered him as a leader, and he was honored with a shrine after his death, a tradition that continues to this day. This is similar to the accounts of previous writings about Han Yu. It is said that literature has a divine power—indeed, it seems to be so!

A Wealthy Man from Taian and the Rabbit's Mysterious Transformation A wealthy man from Taian, Zhou, was known for his fondness for the outside world. He had a young male lover named Zhang Maodi, who was extremely charming. Zhang always slept and ate with Zhou, and they had a deep affection for each other. Not long after Zhang's death, Zhou buried him on the side of Qiguan Peak. Years later, a hunter entered the mountains with his bow and arrows to hunt. Far off in the snow, he saw a rabbit mating with a fox. He chased and shot the rabbit, hitting its buttocks. The rabbit fled with the arrow, running into an old coffin. When the hunter approached the coffin, he saw nothing but a dried skeleton remained. Some said this was the grave of Zhou's beloved Zhang, and it must now be paired with a fox spirit. The hunter was greatly alarmed, threw away his bow and arrows, and returned. From then on, he never hunted again.

**Ma Hongmo** Zhangde native Ma Sheng, whose name was Hongmo, had always considered himself a man of upright conduct and virtue. Though past his prime years, he had not yet married. He once remarked that people regard the man of Lu as having a heart of iron and stone, yet he had already blurred the distinction between men and women, and I secretly laughed at his unbroken tenderness. People said he was indeed worthy of those words. His father, Ruo Xu, was old, and served in the household of the wealthy Zhao family. Every night, he would go to bed first. One day, two of his students, unable to finish their literary exercises, toiled until midnight, finally completing their drafts. Suddenly, they saw the image of Lord Guan, hanging on the wall, slowly descending from the frame. The two men were greatly startled and about to flee, but Lord Guan stopped them, saying, "Do not be afraid. I am not here to harm you." He then asked to see their draft, corrected and explained it, and all of it became exquisite and profound. After a long while, he returned to the painting. The two men rewrote and carefully polished their work, and the next day presented it to Ruo Xu. Ruo Xu read it once and found it all excellent. Astonished by how quickly they had improved, he inquired and learned the reason: fearing that a spirit had come to harm them, he had excused himself and returned home, telling the family. Upon hearing this, Ma Hongmo laughed and said, "This is a spirit of the painting. From ancient times, demons arise from the hearts of men. How often have we seen wicked spirits able to bewitch upright and virtuous men? Since my father was timid and dared not stay any longer, I shall go in his place and take over the task. Let us see if this demon dares to bewitch me." Ruo Xu could not dissuade him, so Ma Hongmo packed his belongings and went directly. He went in his father’s name, and the host temporarily provided him with lodging. From then on, however, the deity in the study never descended again. People all said that the young man’s righteous spirit was so strong that even ghosts and spirits avoided him. Ma Hongmo himself grew even more self-confident as a result.

After the fifth day of the fifth month, all the students in the private school had dispersed, leaving the protagonist sitting alone in the study room, unable to help but feel homesick. He wandered aimlessly to the back garden, where the pavilions and buildings were very secluded and deep. Looking far to the east side, he saw a small pond with lotus flowers already in bloom. He rushed to it, and there was a pavilion over the pond, like a bird spreading its wings. Intending to climb up and enjoy the view to dispel his melancholy, he found the door locked very tightly. Just as he was hesitating, suddenly, the two doors opened by themselves. About twenty beautiful women greeted him at the door, their teeth slightly visible, and they waved their hands to invite him. Graceful and alluring, there was no match for them in the world. The protagonist, who had been long without a partner, followed them in, following his impulse. The women turned and went upstairs, and the protagonist also climbed up the stairs to follow them, embracing them directly. Suddenly, this woman turned into a fierce ghost, her hair disheveled, and she grabbed him. Terrified, he ran desperately and fell down the stairs. Suddenly, there was a loud noise on his head, and his left foot was crushed by the door, and his body was hanging upside down. He screamed loudly, and the people gathered to save him, trying their best to open the door but couldn't. His host looked up at it for a long time, knowing it was strange, and quickly called a dog, taking a weapon to hit it. The dog barked loudly, and the protagonist's foot came out easily. They helped him to the study room, asking him in detail about what happened. The protagonist was still shocked and bewildered, unconsciously telling the truth. The host laughed and said, "You didn't know that this pavilion has been a den for fox spirits, so it is always locked and never opened. I didn't expect that you would also be deceived by the fox spirits." Everyone laughed. The protagonist blushed and couldn't look up. The next day, he excused himself with a lame foot and returned home, and he never returned.

外史氏曰：马生外表严厉而内心软弱，似乎他一生都保持着纯正谨慎的态度，像微生高那样正直，张君瑞那样远离美色，足以赢得虚名。然而他没有真正的学问，所以缺乏坚定的意志。最终，他遇到一个美女而陷入混乱，以至于失去了常态。幸好这个妖精突然变化形态来戏弄他，虽然伤了他的脚，却没有让他彻底失去平衡。然而他已经失去了大半的自我控制。 茅山道士 戴旷如是戴家山村的人，专门治疗溃疡，因此门庭若市。一天，有一位游方道士，穿着葛巾布袍，来到他家化斋，自称来自茅山。戴旷如准备了鸡黍招待他，款待非常热情。道士感激他的好意，打开黑袋子拿出一卷丹方交给他，说：“这个方子传自孙真人，孙真人是从老龙那里得到的。以后你只要用这个方子救人，一生都不会缺乏东西的。”戴旷如感激他的心意，请求成为他的非正式朋友，道士也很高兴，于是与他结为朋友后离开。

A few days later, the Taoist returned and said to Dai, "The formula I previously gave you was indeed wonderful, but it requires distinguishing the symptoms and applying the treatment accordingly. I still have a small trick; are you indeed eager to learn it?" Dai was very pleased and asked to be taught. The Taoist took out a small bamboo tube from his sleeve and gave it to him, saying, "There is someone inside; calling him will make him come out. If you encounter difficulties, asking him will yield no wrong answers." He also taught Dai a spell. Dai was delighted. After removing the stopper and reciting the spell, a small man came out, about two inches tall, with clear and handsome eyebrows and eyes. As soon as he touched the ground, he suddenly grew to over ten feet tall, with golden eyes that flashed, a green face, and long hair, and his two teeth protruded an inch beyond his lips. Dai was greatly frightened and begged for him to be taken back. The Taoist said, "Since you are indeed a virtuous person, I am willing to pass on this secret technique to you, but why are you now doubting me?" Dai replied, "But once this thing has been summoned, it must be appeased with ten gold pieces, otherwise, it will not go away." The Taoist calmly took him back into the tube, without feeling any constraint, and put the tube back in his sleeve. He bowed deeply and left. The historian says: From ancient times, monks, Taoists, and other religious figures have often deceived people with their magic tricks. According to my observations, there are many who have been deceived and harmed by them, but none who have benefited from their tricks.

A Taoist Priest Named Yang In the past, there was a Taoist priest named Yang in the prefecture, who had once been a low-ranking official in the government office. He was skilled in using spells to heal illnesses. Whenever he was invited, he would go, but he did not accept payment. If a ritual was required, he would take the offering money, because it was certain that others would be invited as well. Because of this, people all believed him to be a deity. I once stayed at the house of the Niu family, and their third son was already on the brink of death. All the doctors were at a loss, so they went to invite Yang. When Yang arrived, he ordered to fetch one white rooster and one bucket of water. He went to the patient's tent, set up incense and candles, and muttered spells in his mouth. After a long time, he took the rooster, split its head, and threw it into the air. When it fell to the ground, he looked at it and said, "The illness can still be cured." Then he took the water, drew a talisman on it, and held it out to the patient, saying, "If you want to live, drink this water." At that time, the patient had not urinated or defecated for several days, and had not taken even a spoonful of water, and was almost unconscious. Hearing his words, the patient suddenly woke up as if from a dream, took the water from his hand in one gulp, and then fell asleep. By midnight, he woke up, and his urine filled a bucket. The whole family believed he was fortunate to be reborn. Yang said there was a karmic debt here, and he still needed to repent. The next day, he invited several Taoist priests and set up a large altar. By evening, the hall was filled with the sound of gongs and bells, and ten lanterns and drums were lit, making it as bright as day. Yang climbed the altar, his hair loose and a sword in hand, performing the ritual with the steps of the Great Sage. Suddenly, an old servant ran out and said, "The third young master has died; you can pack up and go back." Yang and his companions were all shocked. In a panic, all the lights in the hall went out, and there was no one left. This can be a good laugh.

Alas! Good or bad fortune depends on people, and poverty or prosperity is determined by fate. Those who hope for wealth and honor but seek what is beyond their rightful share are rarely not ridiculed by the Daoist priests from Mount Mao.

叶太史诗谶 秀水叶太史维庚，嘉庆甲戌进士。以翰林出宰江左，时嘉庆己卯秋试，应聘入帘。八月十五夜，梦有人邀至一处玩月，且示以东坡催试官考校之作及《水调歌头》一阕，俾和之。和毕，复引至一官署，游览殆遍。问其地，曰：“澄江。”亦不知其在何省也。遂醒。后丁内艰，由宝应令量移江阴，因忽忆前梦，盖江阴一名澄江也。故其《留别宝应绅士》诗中，有“料得下车圆旧梦，澄江真个月分明”之句。次年遂卒于澄江。一时以为前定。按公作宰有政绩，及卒之前一夕，二鼓后，宅门已闭，其门役忽见烛笼数十，掩映门外，于门隙窥之，见有“靖海伯”字样。靖海伯，江阴城隍封号也。既闻嗽声而没。阅日，城隍庙道士某，夜梦一神语云：“官舟适送叶太爷至东岳，为罗酆山都录司命。橹后为树枝所损，宜亟修之。”道士醒而异之。及晓，视丧司船左裂一缝，于是知公之没而为神也。 The Prophecy of Ye Taishi's Poem Ye Taishi Weigeng from Xiushui, a Jinshi in the Jiaxu year of the Jiaqing era. He served as a prefect in Jiangnan from the Hanlin Academy. At the time of the Jiaqing Gui Mao autumn examination, he was invited to serve as an examiner. On the night of the Mid-Autumn Festival, he dreamed that someone invited him to a place to enjoy the moon, and showed him Su Dongpo's work urging the examiners to grade the papers diligently and a quatrain of "Shui Diao Ge Tou." He was asked to compose a response. After finishing, he was again led to an official residence and toured it extensively. When he asked where he was, he was told, "Chengjiang." He did not know which province it was in. He then woke up. Later, when he was granted mourning leave due to the death of his mother, he was reassigned from Baoying to Jiang阴. He suddenly remembered his previous dream, as Jiang阴 is also known as Chengjiang. Therefore, in his poem "Farewell to the Gentlemen of Baoying," he wrote, "I predict that upon taking office, I will fulfill my old dream, and Chengjiang truly reflects the moon distinctly." The following year, he died in Chengjiang. At the time, people believed it was predestined. It is recorded that Ye Taishi had administrative achievements. On the night before his death, after the second watch, the main gate of his residence had already been closed. His servant suddenly saw dozens of candle lanterns outside the door, shining through the gaps. When he peeked through the crack, he saw the words "Jinghai Bo" (Lord of the Sea). Jinghai Bo is the title of the city god of Jiang阴. After hearing a cough, he passed away. The next day, a Taoist priest at the city god temple had a dream in which a deity said, "The official boat just sent Ye Taishi to Mount Tai, where he became the Du Lushen of Luofeng Mountain. The oar was damaged by a tree branch, so it should be repaired immediately." The priest was puzzled upon waking. By dawn, he noticed a crack on the left side of the funeral boat, and thus knew that Ye Taishi had died and become a deity.

The External Historian said: The Grand Historian had a reputation for writing when young. During the year Jiaxu of the Jiaqing era (1814 CE), I read his work *The Complete Chapter on Unrectified Virtue and Its Consequences*, and I admired his quick-witted and sharp-minded understanding, which was clear and sincere. Therefore, I copied it by hand to study and emulate. Later, I heard that before passing the imperial examinations, he had stayed with the Shen family of Baishi Bay. There was a servant who was a habitual troublemaker, and seeing the Grand Historian's gentle demeanor, he often insulted him while drunk. One evening, as the Grand Historian was having dinner, he smiled and stood up, pouring himself a drink and bowing to the servant, saying, "If I have offended you, I will apologize tomorrow with a branch of thorns, but can I have another drink now?" The servant felt ashamed and left. When the owner came out to ask what had happened, the Grand Historian replied, "Nothing else, just a moment ago, he pushed me to the ground while drunk, so I called for help." The Grand Historian was particularly fond of learning. One night, while reading under a paper window, he heard a rustling sound outside. Looking out, he saw a woman standing in front of the window, dressed simply and gracefully, who had already licked a hole in the paper and smiled invitingly. He then took a pen and wrote a poem on the window: "Digging a hole in the paper window is easy to patch, but damaging one's hidden virtue is the hardest to repair. If I were to follow you, Lady Wenjun, tonight, I fear your heart would also be ashamed."

Upon completing the task, a sudden, sharp sound like tearing silk was heard, and the woman transformed into a ghost of someone who died by hanging and vanished. Shortly thereafter, the gentleman attended the provincial examination and, along with his companions, sought a dream at the temple dedicated to Yu Zhongsu. In his dream, he arrived at a place where the temple was shrouded in a solemn and eerie atmosphere, with spirits standing by the sides. On the dais, a man dressed in the ceremonial robe and cap of a ruler sat in the center, looking like a king, with two attendants standing beside him, resembling judges. The gentleman hurried forward, bowed down at the foot of the stairs. The ruler ordered him to rise, granted him a seat, and smiled, saying, "I have heard that your resolve is very firm, and your literary reputation is well-known. You should be promoted to encourage good scholars. However, when I checked your record of official positions and salaries, it seems you should be a superior candidate for life. How is this?" He then turned to the left, ordered the retrieval of the record of good deeds, and reviewed it. Upon reaching a certain line, he carefully examined it and laughed, saying, "Excellent! This shows your kindness and measured behavior." He then continued to the part about refusing to pursue a woman and laughed again, saying, "This demonstrates your strictness and integrity. These two things are sufficient to petition the emperor, but you must continue to strive and not slack off." He then ordered the spirits to send the gentleman back to the mortal world. Upon waking, the gentleman found it strange. That year, he finally passed the examination. Given the gentleman's diligent study, he still had to rely on hidden virtue to become famous; how much more so for those who are less capable? This shows that the afterlife values virtue even more than writing.

Strange Case Mr. Zheng Mengbai, the magistrate of Xingzi County, once handled a peculiar case. There was an old man named Yang in the county who, in his old age, had a son named Mr. Yang. The boy was very obedient and well-behaved from a young age, and the old man loved him dearly. He arranged for a girl to be raised in their household as a future daughter-in-law, whose name was Miss Yang. She was gentle and kind-hearted. After both grew up, they were married. That night, they shared the same bed, and it seemed they were very compatible. However, the next morning after noon, neither of them got up. When they entered the room to check, they found the new bride lying naked and dead in bed, while the groom had disappeared without a trace. Upon examining the body, there were no signs of injury, only traces of passion on the bedding. Unable to understand what had happened and unable to find the groom, they ordered someone to report the incident to the bride's family. It was the hot season, and three days later, the bride's father arrived. By then, the body had already been buried in the countryside. The old man claimed that he was concerned about the body decaying. The father was greatly suspicious and accused the old man and his son of conspiring to kill his daughter, hiding the son and burying the body to cover up the crime. He immediately went to the county magistrate's office to report the case and request an investigation. When the coffin was opened, it was not the body of the bride, but an old man in his sixties or seventies. The old man's hair and beard were completely white, with axe marks on his back. The magistrate was even more shocked and asked the old man, who was also confused. When asked about the whereabouts of the groom, he also did not know. After applying torture, the old man could not provide any answers. The magistrate had no choice but to order the burial of the coffin and send the old man back home.

After being detained for over a month, a sudden report came that the son of the plaintiff had voluntarily surrendered. They quickly brought him out for questioning. He confessed that on that night, he had been intimate with his wife and playfully pinched her neck. As he was laughing, his wife suddenly became silent and motionless. He examined her by lamp-light, and she was dead. Fearing punishment, he fled. Yesterday, he heard that his father was being punished in another town and decided to come forward to clear his father's name. His son, who worked as a barber, knew how to manipulate for pleasure, but he only knew how to play and not how to resolve the situation, so he fled. They detained his son and released the plaintiff. However, the question remains: how did the female corpse suddenly become a male one, and why is there no relative to identify the body? This matter cannot be fully investigated. They were forced to extend the period for further investigation, but there was no clear direction on how to proceed. A month after the plaintiff's release, he happened to be in Jianchang on official business and passed through Zhouxi. From a distance, he saw a young woman washing clothes by the river. As he approached, it seemed to be his wife. He called out to her, and she raised her head and saw him, surprised and asked, "Are you my father-in-law? How did you come here?" She invited him to stop his boat and visit her home. At that moment, the plaintiff was shocked and suspicious, so he asked, "Are you a ghost or a living person?" The woman sadly replied, "I am not a ghost. Please come to my house, and I will tell you everything." The plaintiff followed her to a thatched cottage, which did not look like a peasant's home. She asked why he was there, and the woman wanted to speak but first burst into tears. After a long time, she explained everything in detail and said, "Fortunately, he has just left. I can meet you, Father-in-law. The truth has been revealed, and I wish to accompany you to the riverbank and let my body be eaten by fish."

At the beginning, the woman had been hastily buried alive but revived at midnight. By dawn, two uncle-nephew carpenters from Jianchang happened to pass by, heard her cries for help, and together pried open the coffin to save her. The woman was young and beautiful, having just recently been married, and was dressed in fine and neat attire. Her nephew, upon seeing her, was struck by desire and wanted to take her home with him, but his uncle refused, carefully asking about her place of residence and intending to send her back home. The nephew could not persuade his uncle otherwise and, in frustration, killed her with an axe. He then placed her body back in the coffin, covered it up, took the woman back, and forced her to be his wife. The woman did not dare to refuse and thus managed to see her father-in-law. After hearing the story, the father-in-law tearfully embraced her and wept, saying, "My daughter, unfortunately, suffered this violence. What sin has she committed? And if she does not return home, this case will never be resolved. You must go quickly; if you delay, it may be too late." They then both returned home.

As the old man was about to arrive home, he suddenly saw a young man carrying an axe and saw, wandering aimlessly. The young man caught sight of the woman and was greatly startled, intending to forcibly take her. The woman scolded him, saying, "I was weak and helpless before, taken by you. Today, I am fortunate to be pitied and allowed to meet my husband. You are dying soon, yet you still dare to commit such evil acts?" The old man, now realizing that the young man was someone he knew, became angry and fought with him. The villagers all gathered, seized and bound the young man, and took the woman as evidence to the county office. The magistrate, after one interrogation, got the young man to confess. The young man was then released from prison, and the woman, seeing him in restraints, could not help but hide her tears. The magistrate pitied the woman's innocence and her ability to clear her husband's crime, and forgave them both, ordering the old man to take her back and reunite with her. At that time, the young man had gone to Nankang to work as a hired laborer. When he returned, he took a detour to the town to investigate the matter, unexpectedly meeting the old man and the woman there.

The historian said: The son of Yang caused his wife's death through playful behavior, and his father-in-law rashly and mistakenly buried her, so it was fitting that they could not avoid legal punishment, though it was not their fault. As for the person named Kou, he originally acted out of lust and willingly and without hesitation accepted the death of his uncle. If it were not for the encounter between the husband and wife, this case could not even be decided by the legendary judge皋陶. Even if they had already met the father, and the person was also at home, this extraordinary injustice would still not be easily resolved. Fortunately, the person had already left, and the husband happened to pass by; the husband brought his wife back, and the person encountered her again. In this case, there must be some divine intervention! Yet, without the learned person's clear and cautious adjudication of the case, there might be those who would cover up and protect. All of these are worthy of being recorded. In the Qianlong era, there was a man from the Li family in Suzhou's Le Bridge. Every morning, he would sell vegetables in the market and earn money to support his mother. One day, while on the road, he picked up a sealed envelope containing forty-five taels of gold. Upon returning home and opening it, he found the amount written inside. His mother saw it and was startled, saying, "You are a poor man, and your daily earnings do not exceed one hundred coins; this is your due. Now, you have suddenly acquired a large sum of money, which may not be a blessing for you. Moreover, the person who lost the gold might have a different owner, and they could be beaten or even forced to pay the debt with their life." She urged him to take the gold to the place where the owner was waiting, and the owner arrived just then, so he returned the gold. The person who found the gold took it immediately and left, and the people in the market were all surprised that he did not thank anyone. They wanted to make him divide the gold as compensation, but he refused, saying deceitfully, "The gold originally amounted to fifty taels, and he has already hidden five of them; why should I compensate?" The people in the market were greatly disturbed.

When a certain official arrived, he inquired about the matter and pretended to be angry, punishing the vegetable seller with five strokes. He then handed over the gold, pointing to the inscription, and said to the person who lost it: "Your gold was originally fifty taels, but now it is labeled as forty-five taels. It is not your gold anymore." He then handed over the gold to the seller and said: "You are innocent and unjustly received my punishment. I have made a mistake. Now I will compensate you with this, and thus your mother's prophecy of misfortune has been proven true." He quickly told them to take it away, and the entire market applauded. In addition to this, there was Zhang Qianwen Yuchao from Kunshan, who in his early years demonstrated great filial piety. When his father was ill, he cut off part of his arm and mixed the meat with medicine to offer it to him, earning him the reputation for his filial conduct at the time. He was generous by nature and once helped a forty-year-old painter named Qi Tianzhang, who was poor and unable to marry. Zhang gave him money to encourage him to get married. Qi happily accepted the money and left. The next day, when Zhang visited him again, he noticed Qi had a worried expression. He questioned him, but Qi remained silent and wept. Upon inquiring among the neighbors, Zhang learned that Qi had lost the money on his way home. Zhang returned the money, just as he had given it before, and went to ask Qi: "Did you lose your money yesterday?" Qi replied, "No." Zhang said, "You are not deceiving me; I have already heard from others." He then took out the gold from his sleeve and said, "Is this not the money you lost?" Qi was greatly delighted, thinking it was indeed his lost money, and accepted it without hesitation. On another occasion, Zhang encountered a vegetable seller who had lost a hundred coins and was so distressed that he wanted to die. Zhang pretended to buy vegetables and called him to his home, asking his family to weigh the vegetables. He secretly placed the coins in the vegetables. When the coins fell to the ground, Zhang pretended to be surprised and said, "Your money is still here!" The man was overjoyed and picked up the coins, taking the remaining ones with him. Because of these acts of kindness, Zhang's family became less prosperous, but his generosity never waned, and people called him "Mr. Zhang the Good."

The Historian said: A son of the Li family, who was a humble vegetable seller, unexpectedly found a large sum of money. Who could give it back? Yet, he returned the money due to his mother's advice, without any difficulty or hesitation. It can be seen that he treated his mother well in his daily life. His mother, though a humble vegetable seller, was so wise and discerning. How different was she from the mother of Wang Ling? As for Qi Tianzhang, he had already returned the gold, and when asked about it, he did not reveal the details. His integrity can be seen. Both eventually benefited from it, and it can also be seen as a sign of divine retribution or reward. As for Zhang Gong, who helped others with such depth and sincerity! Excellent! Excellent! Who would have thought that such people still exist in this world today? Qian the Great Man Qian Zhongcheng, who began his career in Jiangxi, happened to pass through Longhu Mountain on official business and visited the Taoist priest. As soon as he entered and saw the Taoist priest, the priest greeted him with a smile and said, "You are a person of high rank. Just now, the city's city god's office came to see me. We were talking when suddenly he got up in a hurry and said, 'Mr. Qian from Pinghu is coming. I should be careful and avoid him.' He quickly left." The official did not believe it, and the Taoist priest smiled and said, "The city god just now ran too fast and reached the courtyard, and one foot stepped into the pond water. If you don't believe it, please go to his temple and check it." The official still humbly declined. Afterwards, he went out and tried to go to the temple to verify it. The left foot still had wet mud on it.

**夫妇重逢** During the reign of Emperor Kangxi, Geng Jingzhong, a rebel, caused unrest in Zhejiang and Fujian provinces. Local bandits were active, and travel was obstructed. Newly appointed County Magistrate Wang, accompanied by his family, was en route to his post when he was attacked by bandits. His wife was captured by a bandit leader, who intended to assault her. She wept and said, "I was originally accompanying my husband on his mission. Now, with war raging everywhere, our reunion is uncertain. I am weak and without support, but I hope you will show mercy. I am willing to serve you with my humble self, fulfilling my duties. However, I am a woman from a distinguished family; my ancestors were prominent officials. I cannot bear to marry without proper rites. If I could prepare proper rites and then share this bed, we could be together in old age. Otherwise, please kill me." The bandit leader agreed to her terms. The wife was known for her skill in drinking. During their wedding ceremony, she deliberately encouraged him to drink. The bandit leader became drunk and repeatedly tried to assault her. She fetched a golden cup, filled it, and drank herself. Then, she poured another cup, held it out, and said, "Tonight, my serving as your drinker is by destiny. Please finish this cup with me, and we can share this good fortune. Wouldn't that add more pleasure?" The bandit leader was even happier and laughed, saying, "The beautiful woman loves me!" He drank it all in one go from her hand but did not notice that he was already drunk. By then, it was past two o'clock at night. The wife still intended to drink alone and ordered a servant to fetch wine. As the servant left, she quickly stood up, drew a dagger from the bandit leader's waist, and stabbed him, killing him instantly. She then hid behind the door, waited for the servant to enter, and killed him. She locked the door, escaped secretly from the rear of the camp, and fortunately, no one noticed her during the night.

When dawn had come, she destroyed her makeup and smeared her face with dirt, begging for food in the fields. Upon reaching Xi'an, she bit her finger to draw blood and wrote a suicide note on the wall, intending to throw herself into a well to die. The villagers saved her and spared her life, and they reported the incident to the magistrate. The magistrate inquired about the entire event, was moved with compassion, and praised her virtue. He requested that she temporarily stay in his office and serve as a tutor for his daughter. He then displayed a document to find out where Wang was.

A few days later, Mr. Wang suddenly arrived, presenting a visiting card to pay respects to the magistrate. The magistrate invited him in, inquiring in detail about the hardships he had endured. Mr. Wang spoke of his late wife, and tears streamed down his face without ceasing. The magistrate was moved by his sorrow but did not reveal the full story to Mr. Wang's wife. Afterward, he secretly instructed his wife to prepare a meal. During the meal, Mr. Wang began to cry again. The magistrate pretended to ask why, to which Mr. Wang replied, "This dish is remarkably similar to what my late wife used to make. The way she chopped the scallions, even down to the inch, brings back such strong memories that I can't help but feel moved." The magistrate sighed as if in deep thought and then suggested that he marry his sister to Mr. Wang. Mr. Wang responded, "My late wife has passed away, and I do not know whether she is alive or dead. It is too painful to even speak of her." Despite the magistrate's repeated attempts to persuade him, Mr. Wang refused. The magistrate then arranged a separate residence, prepared bridal gifts, and sent his wife to Mr. Wang. He instructed the servants to cover Mrs. Wang with a veil and helped them perform the traditional exchange of bows. Mr. Wang turned away from them, facing the wall, and wept uncontrollably, refusing to look at them even once. Mrs. Wang, who had already been informed of the plan, was overwhelmed with a mix of sorrow and joy, finding it difficult to hold back her emotions. She wept and said, "Mr. Wang, Mr. Wang, do you still remember the hardships we endured together?" Mr. Wang was startled and turned to see that it was indeed his wife. They embraced and cried, each recounting their experiences of hardship. When they spoke of the events in the bandit camp, Mr. Wang wept even more bitterly. The magistrate, observing from the side, said, "Your wife's actions in this matter were both wise and courageous, worthy of being compared to Fei Gong'e. Her virtue and integrity are truly admirable. I believe you should be happy rather than sad." Mr. Wang then wiped his tears and bowed to the magistrate, saying, "Without the kindness of my parents in raising me, I would not have been able to see this day."

The imperial edict had been lost, and the official agreed to thoroughly investigate and provide compensation, allowing it to be carried to the official. The woman wished to pay homage to the official as a father, but the official humbly declined and did not dare. After entering Fujian, they continued to exchange gifts and visits regularly, as if they were brother and sister. Soon, the person was promoted to the position of censor through his deeds. Jiang Jiqing said, "I have seen this matter in *Xin Yu of the Prosperous Reign*. In this part, where the woman was captured by bandits, *Xin Yu* does not detail it, and there are also occasional additions and deletions before and after. Some say that this was refined by the respected scholar. However, the scholar was well-informed, and what he based it on was not necessarily all covered in *Xin Yu*. Thus, the text becomes vivid through its uniqueness." Gong Weilü Weilü, courtesy name Ziyang, art name Zixuan, was from Taizhou. He passed the imperial examinations during the reign of Emperor Chongzhen and served as an inspector in the Hanlin Academy. *Chu Bian Nan Chen Chao* states that he and Zheng Er Yang, Zeng Ying, Shi Kangzheng, Zhang Boying, Wang Weixiao, Wang Xiyu, Cheng Beixie, Chen Ziqi, Hu Yukai, Shi Shengli, Liangyou Shi, Xia Long, Yan Tong, Lin Chi, and Wang Chongjian were all able to hide themselves. When entering the new dynasty, he was twice recommended and appointed. Citing the precedent of returning home to care for one's parents, he resigned and returned home, building a house at the site of the small West Lake. He shut himself in to write books, producing *Chunyuyicang* in fifty volumes. Because his son Mengren was honored, he was posthumously bestowed the title of Grand Master of the Imperial Clan, making him a survivor of the old regime.

Con Wei Liao was the eighteenth-ranked jinshi (imperial scholar) in the year 1643 during the reign of the Chongzhen Emperor, and his grandson Mao Yan also became the eighteenth-ranked jinshi in the year 1703 during the reign of the Kangxi Emperor. Both were associated with the poetry category of the fourth room, and both were evaluated by scholars named Li from the Hanlin Academy. Initially, when Mao Yan traveled north for the imperial examination, he dreamed that his ancestor walked with him. Upon waking, he was delighted and said, "This is a sign that I will emulate my ancestor's achievements." Indeed, he passed the examination as he had predicted. Thus, the spirit of his ancestor had not yet faded. Could it be that the sentiment for the former country and former ruler had gradually faded from his heart, and instead, he took pride in the prominence of his descendants? Or could it be that he relied on something else? A Great Fish in the Sea According to the *Nanhui County Gazetteer*, at the beginning of the Qing dynasty, a great fish passed through the mouth of the river, moving slowly and weakly, as high as a mountain. It took seven days and nights for the fish to completely pass by, and its head and tail were never seen. In the year 1816, during the reign of the Jiaqing Emperor, a large fish was found along the coast of Hai Zhou. Its eyes had already been gouged out, it was thirty-six zhang long, and from spine to belly, it was over seven feet tall. The residents all cut it into pieces and ate it. Its fat was very thick, and its fishy smell was unbearable. However, when compared to what was recorded in the *Nanhui County Gazetteer*, it seemed small by comparison.

Some say at the beginning of the Chongzhen era, a giant fish suddenly appeared in the sea, stopping near the shore of Zhu Touyan. The back of the fish had a mountain, with trees, birds, and animals. Tourists moored their boats and climbed up, enjoying the view and gradually forming paths. Some would drink wine and compose poetry there. There were those who struck its scales with oars, and the fish, suffering from pain, shook once, causing waves to surge and boats to capsize. They then warned each other, saying, "This must be a divine fish, sent here by the Dragon King as punishment, temporarily out of water, do not disturb it." Later, during the autumn flood, huge waves came, and one night, they carried the fish and the mountain away. As for the cart driver, Governor Zhao Gong Yao of Huai'an once went to Xuzhou on official business. On the way, he saw a man pushing a small cart throw the guest's luggage to the side of the road, showing his anger and unwillingness to push. The guest was stunned and didn't know what to do. Zhao stopped his carriage and spoke to him. The cart driver then said, "I was hired in Xuzhou to push this guest for three days, but I still don't know his name. Today, I happened to ask, and I found out that he is surnamed Qin, and I am surnamed Yue, how can I serve as a servant for my enemy?" Zhao laughed and then told him, "The enmity between Qin and Yue is a matter of six hundred years ago. Why do you hold a grudge against the guest?" The cart driver then understood. Zhao gave him two thousand coins and ordered him to continue pushing the guest. This is similar to the story of the cobbler killing Qin Hui, truly a heart of a child. This is recorded in *Xici Xin Yu*.

When I was young, I often heard the elders say that a cobbler, while watching a performance of *Sweeping Qin*, could not contain his anger. He picked up his knife and rushed onto the stage, almost killing Qin Hui, and could not help but laugh. Now reading this record, I am even more delighted to see that this saying has evidence, and how deeply moving loyalty and righteousness are, as profound and far-reaching as they are. Mr. Liao Zhou, the loyal and righteous official, once served as the judicial officer of Hangzhou. After taking office, he attended a public banquet with his colleagues. During the performance of *Qin Hui's Plot at the East Window*, he suddenly stood up and almost beat Qin Hui to death. The banquet was immediately broken up. The next day, someone asked him, "What did the host do to cause such anger?" He smiled and said, "There's no reason; it was just a moment of righteous indignation." The innate nature of a person is not divided by intelligence or foolishness. A remarkable child from Wu County, just eight or nine years old, every day went to school to read until evening when he returned home, and it was already dark. His father was originally poor and lowly, and he did not have a strong interest in reading. Because his son was still young, one day he went to the teacher to ask why his son was returning so late. The teacher was surprised and said, "Every day when class ended, the sun had not yet set. Why is it considered late?" He described the situation, and the teacher suspected that he might have been playing with other children on the way.

On that day, after the child had left school, the narrator secretly followed behind and observed him. The child immediately galloped to the tomb of Fan, handed over the book bundle to the stone figure, and the stone figure raised its hands to hold it very carefully. The child then leaped onto the stone horse and galloped to the peak of the mountain, returning multiple times with confidence. The teacher was greatly astonished and waited until the child reached the flat ground before shouting urgently to urge him to return early. The child, startled and looking back, saw the teacher and spurred the horse to gallop away, never to return. This incident occurred in the year 20 of the Daoguang era. To this day, the stone figure still tightly holds the book bundle without letting go. Jia Yishi Jia Yishi, whose name is not recorded, was from Fenyang, Shanxi. People from Fenyang used his capital to lend money and make profits, often spreading their business throughout the country. Yishi once traveled to Anlu, Hubei. There, a man named Fan Yi was setting up a pharmacy in the market. Yishi lent him money, and they lived together, getting along very well. Yi was eleven years older than Yishi and called him his younger brother. After living together for over a year, Yi fell ill and was on the verge of death. He told Yishi, "At first, I lacked capital for my business, and my situation seemed dire. Since you came to my home, our household became slightly prosperous, and you have treated me like a brother. Now, unfortunately, we are separated in the middle of our journey, and I am about to die. I leave my wife and children to you." Yishi wept and agreed to his request.

Fan Yi's wife, surnamed [name], was exceptionally beautiful but cunning and promiscuous. Within three months of Fan Yi's death, she already thought of taking her wealth and going elsewhere. In the town, there was a wealthy man named Li who was attracted to her beauty and also coveted her substantial wealth. He immediately sent a matchmaker. The match was made, but the clan of the Fan family rose up to protest. They did not succeed; then they requested to complete the mourning period, but this was not allowed. They also requested to wait for a month, but this was also not allowed. The righteous man gently admonished her with great principles, but the woman angrily said, "Who are you! And you also want to interfere with our family affairs? I will return your wealth and expel you." The righteous man dared not speak again. However, he often felt depressed and did not want to live. After several days, he also fell ill. After seven days of illness, he suddenly got up and said, "I have figured it out." He went to tell the woman, "Are you really going to marry? All your family's accounts were managed by me. Most of your wealth was lent to me. If you repay it, how much wealth will you have left? Moreover, you have a son; where will you send him? I am here feeling lonely and have been wanting a family for a long time. If you become my wife, you will have a husband again. Everything in the shop can remain unchanged. And your son can become my son. Isn't this a win-win solution?" The woman was very happy and thus broke off the engagement with Li. She chose a good date to marry the righteous man. Li tried to contest it and was going to sue. The righteous man sent someone to gently explain to Li, "Someone from the Fan family had been with the merchant for a long time. Now they are going to reject the original engagement and take another lover. Their intentions are clear. Why would you use such a dishonest woman?" Li also suddenly understood and stopped. Thus, no one in Anlu did not curse the righteous man, and they laughed at how Fan Yi entrusted himself to the wrong person.

Upon marriage, the righteous man held a grand banquet, inviting his fellow villagers to drink, and he became thoroughly drunk. By the time night had deepened, the righteous man was as unsteady as a collapsing jade mountain. The guests helped him into the bridal chamber, covered him with a fragrant quilt, and then left. The woman sent her maid out, undressed, and went to bed, shaking him but unable to wake him. She called him softly, and he snored loudly in response. The woman tossed and turned, unable to fall asleep, and then approached him naked with her lower body. The righteous man woke up suddenly, whispered, "The beautiful woman loves me!" As soon as he finished speaking, he fell back into a deep sleep. Soon after, the rooster crowed, and the righteous man quickly got up, saying, "Was I really that drunk yesterday? Now my companions are about to go to Guangzhou for medicine, and there is still one matter I haven't handled. Has the boat already set sail?" He put on his shoes and left. From then on, he pretended to be ill and often stayed away, and the woman could not persuade him to come back. After several months, the woman could no longer bear it, and she began to scold him incessantly. The righteous man had someone deliver a polite message to her, saying, "I have been slightly ill, so I have left you alone for a long time. When I recover, I will come to you." After another few months, the woman began to suspect his intentions and demanded a divorce document. The righteous man agreed to meet her the next day.

On the next day, coinciding with the anniversary of Nui's death, the righteous man arose early, dressed in formal attire, and performed three bows before Nui's spirit, saying, "Brother, I have received your heavy trust, and if I fail to accomplish what you entrusted me with, may the gods and spirits punish me personally." Turning to the woman, he said, "You used to say that I was a stranger from afar, making it difficult to manage your family affairs. Now that you are my wife, can I control you?" He then took the woman, stripped her, and hung her from the beam. Drawing his dagger, he cut off her buttock flesh, heated charcoal in the stove, roasted it, and placed it on the spirit table. He then performed three more bows, saying, "This shameless woman has ruined my elder brother's family reputation. Please, brother, eat her flesh; I will also share a portion with you." He took a bite, and while eating, he cursed her. The woman cried out in despair, pleading for her life. He then imprisoned her in the upper room, digging a hole to provide food and water.

For ten years, the wife was forty, and their son was eighteen. A certain gentleman who was friends with Wang Gongsun had a daughter, and the gentleman arranged for the daughter to be married to Fan's son. He sent her to study, where she managed household affairs during the day and instructed Fan's son in his studies at night. After several years, he entered the school. They then chose a good day to complete the marriage and prepared food and drink to invite the village, the Fan clan, and other relatives. When the music began, the gentleman spoke, saying, "I was entrusted by Fan to marry his wife, and without doing so, I could not fulfill his promise. For ten years, we were in a false marriage, enduring people's ridicule and criticism, all to fulfill the promise and repay Fan. Now my son has grown up successfully, and the wife is old and will not remarry. I am now forty-seven years old, still without a child. My wife lives alone, waiting for me ten years for the sake of Fan. Now she will return and give birth to a child." He took out a ledger and handed it to his son, saying, "Your father left behind several hundred units of currency, which has now grown to several thousand. Guard it carefully, and do not forget how difficult it was for your father to start this!" He then wept with emotion, saying, "Fan, Fan, now you can rest in peace in the afterlife!"

He immediately hired a cart to transport his luggage and set off. Fan Zi wept and tried to stop him but could not, so he gave him a thousand gold coins. He waved his hand without looking back and left. Thus, the people of Anlu all praised Fan Yi for his ability to recognize talent and spoke in unison of Mr. Jia's virtue, saying, "Chivalrous man, chivalrous man!" The historian said: I obtained this story from the *Yu Yu Ji* (The Book of Stupidity) and made some minor edits before recording it. Between "and after the marriage" and the following section, I specially added the following. Since ancient times, loyal ministers and heroic men have all proclaimed themselves without deceit, firmly holding onto an unshakable resolve, and were willing to face boiling cauldrons. This is not something that bold and adventurous men who seek fame can easily achieve. Observing how the chivalrous man protected himself by pretending to be drunk, it was not that he was not awake all night, but rather that despite the wife's various attempts to seduce him, he ultimately could not move his heart. This event, compared to Mr. Huangshi Zhai's sleeping with a courtesan under the same blanket, has its own differences, but in terms of his resolve, it is truly unmatched. Because he had the heart not to betray his deceased friend, he could marry a woman, endure the laughter and abuse of a thousand people, and ultimately prove himself to the world. This is what is meant by making the dead rise again and ensuring that the living have no shame in their words. The chivalrous man indeed has no shame in his words. Following this resolve, wealth and honor cannot corrupt him, poverty and lowliness cannot sway him, and power and might cannot subdue him. What in the world cannot he accomplish? The more foolish son compared him to Cheng Ying, noting that Cheng Ying's act of saving the orphan involved willingly risking disgrace and taking on the name of selling out his master, which was even more difficult than the act of the old woman and the young man. Indeed.

Yao San Gongzi Yao San Gongzi was a noble from Renhe. His father, once the governor-general of Hunan, raised him in luxury. The young master enjoyed wandering and idling, neglecting poetry and books. As spring drew to a close, he accompanied a servant to the Fire God Temple on Wushan Mountain to watch a play. There, he encountered a middle-aged woman and a young girl returning from the temple after fulfilling a vow. The girl, about seventeen or eighteen years old, was exceptionally beautiful, yet dressed in a simple and elegant manner, quiet and pure like a hidden orchid in the snow. The young master became even more infatuated with her, following her to the side of the drum tower, where an old woman beckoned him inside. The woman descended from her carriage and led the girl in. The young master hesitated beside her, and his servant advised him to return, saying, "The sun is already setting. I recognize this old woman; she was once a seamstress in our household. If the young master still has unresolved feelings, please go back temporarily. Visit this old woman again tomorrow, and the matter can be resolved." The young master returned with a sense of loss, unable to sleep all night.

The sky had already dawned, and the young master immediately summoned his servant to go to the old woman's house to search for the girl's whereabouts, planning to take her as a concubine. The old woman shook her head and said, "Very difficult, very difficult! The girl's family was formerly moderately well-off, and she would not be able to endure being a servant or concubine, and she already has a family. The girl possesses a chaste and calm nature, so even if you want to make a secret agreement, who would dare to approach with empty words? The willow trees of Yongfeng cannot be transplanted." The young master had no choice but to ask the old woman to convey his best wishes and promised a substantial reward. The old woman said, "This is absolutely impossible. However, the girl sometimes comes to my house to learn embroidery and is excellent at drinking. The young master should come tomorrow afternoon. Please get her drunk with wine, and then she will follow orders. If she is not persuaded to drink, then hope is lost." The young master then gave her a hairpin and repeatedly and earnestly instructed her before parting ways. The next day, as planned, the young master went to the old woman's house. The old woman greeted him at the door and whispered, "The young master has great good fortune. She has just been drunk and has already had a dream of intimacy." She then led him through winding paths to a room and pointed to the bed, saying, "Do as you please. The soft and gentle girl is not used to such experiences." She then turned around and quickly locked the door. The young master approached the bed, lifted the curtain, and saw the girl's hairpin shining on the pillow, with drowsiness on her jade-like skin, just like a begonia sleeping in spring and not yet awake. At this point, the young master was overwhelmed by emotion, and he lay down and licked her face, reaching out to touch her embroidered skirt, where her private part rose. The girl seemed to have woken up, but her whole body was soft and melted, and she could no longer resist, letting him treat her lightly. Soon, the girl's family sent a maid to welcome her. The old woman hurriedly entered, urged the young master to get up, and opened the back door to send her out.

The woman was still lingering in a state of drunkenness, her cloud-like hairstyle loose and messy, as she forced herself to tidy her hair. Her maid had been waiting outside for a long time before entering to see her. The woman was looking into the mirror, tidying herself up. An elderly woman spoke to the maid from the side, saying, "Your sister just had a sudden illness, a fever that caused her stomach pain, and she is resting here. She was called twice before she got up." As she spoke, the woman raised her head and saw the maid, unable to hold back her tears. The maid asked, "Are you still feeling unwell inside?" The woman did not answer, hastily dressing herself, and then, holding the maid's hand, walked out. The elderly woman asked her to stay a little longer, but she paid no heed. Upon arriving home, as soon as she stepped through the door, she embraced her mother and cried, "I have let down my dear mother! What shall I do?" Her mother did not understand, so the maid recounted what she had seen. The mother comforted her, saying, "Did someone bully you? Tell me, and I will seek retribution."
