The woman cried even more bitterly, and after a long time, she fell into a deep sleep. They covered her with a green silk quilt and left. After the lamps were lit, the maid went to call her for dinner, but found that she had hanged herself on the bed. They rushed to tell her mother, and together they entered, but she could not be revived.

The mother embraced her daughter's body and wept bitterly, saying, "Alas, my child, you were unfortunately orphaned at a young age and had no brothers. Even if you had suffered an extraordinary injustice, it would not have been impossible to seek justice. Why did you suddenly abandon me in death?" At that time, the girl's father had already passed away. When they prepared to dress her lower body, they secretly found wounds, and they came to understand that her death was caused by shame and anger. They wished to thoroughly investigate the matter, but they could not bear to expose her shame, so they stopped. And her mother, grieving for her daughter, eventually died of depression. Her room remained locked and sealed shut. A year later, a man named Hu Youzheng from Guangzhou traveled to the provincial capital on official business and stayed there. One evening, as he was writing a letter by the light of a lamp, a woman appeared from the northwest corner of the room, approached the table, and bowed respectfully, saying, "You, sir, are a guest in a foreign land, feeling lonely and isolated. Do you miss the letters of your beloved sister-in-law?" The man was young and had a bold, unconventional spirit. Seeing her youthful beauty and delicate appearance, he felt a sense of affection. He stood up and bowed, saying, "I am indeed suffering from the loneliness of a solitary bed. Since you have deigned to visit me, I wish to enjoy your company throughout the night." He then invited her to sit with him. The woman declined, saying, "You have misunderstood. I know that you have always been a man of righteousness and chivalry, so I did not hesitate to risk suspicion and approach you. My earlier words were merely a test of your character. Now, I wish to tell you the truth. May I?" She then introduced herself, saying, "I am Feng, and my nickname is Huanqiu. I was raised to read and was well-versed in the teachings of the home. Last year, I was violated by a man of ill repute, and in my anger and shame, I took my own life. I risked suspicion to approach you because I had something to entrust to you."

If one were to play with pearls, the shame before death, even the vast West River could not wash it away." She finished speaking, her tears streaming endlessly. The man then asked, "Who is your enemy?" The woman replied, "This matter is beyond the scope of what the ancient chief officer could plan. My enemy is the son of the Yao family from the Golden Gate. I previously appealed to the underworld, but because I did not know his name, it was not approved. Now I hear he has been appointed to Guangdong as a military governor, and I will go to find him. I hear that your return in glory is near, and I wish to attach myself to your success and travel with you, how about it?" The man said, "People say that those who die unjustly are not bound in the afterlife, then you can also come and go freely?" The woman replied, "Indeed. But at the checkpoints along the way, you must rely on people from your hometown to escort you, just as in the human world, where a guarantee is required. Otherwise, the road gods will obstruct you." The man said, "This is an easy matter. But I still need to wait until autumn. To see your beautiful face would tug at my soul and dreams. Will you not make me feel suffocated with longing?" The woman agreed to wait until night.

From then on, every evening he would come, and when he arrived, they engaged in various playful and miscellaneous activities. The girl was particularly skilled at the board game of *shuanglu* (双陆), and when he lost, he would be penalized to make tea as compensation. Later, when he happened to win several points, he wanted to take the purple lotus bag she wore, but she refused. He grabbed her sleeve and pulled it off. Her cheeks blushed red, and she got up and left, not appearing for several evenings. He could not stop thinking about her, wandering around the room calling her name, and after a long time, she finally came out. Her eyebrows were still sad and green, and they remained silent. He tried to comfort her, and she sighed deeply and said, "Now I understand how difficult it is to seek someone out. When I died, I tied a piece of silk around my neck, and although it was later removed, it is still in the northeast room. When the weather is overcast and the rope is damp, I feel pain in my throat. I have always wanted to ask to have it burned away, but now I dare not ask again." He asked her to repent and change, and she laughed awkwardly and said, "I'm just afraid that the wild boy won't forget his old ways." Since it is this way, after burning the silk, each day I still trouble you to recite the *Diamond Sutra* once. After seven days, this misfortune will be resolved." He agreed. He immediately ordered his servant to go to the room and fetch the silk to burn it. Every morning, he would wash up and recite the *Diamond Sutra* once with solemnity.

Seven days later, the woman came to express her gratitude. Her laughter was different from usual, and she became even more charming. The man smiled and said, "From now on, we can spend the whole day together, like distant mountain peonies." He then told her that he would be leaving the next day. The woman said, "I think if I travel with you, it cannot avoid being noticed by others. Please write my age and name on a piece of paper and put it in the box. When you want to see me, call out my name in a secluded place, and I will come." The man followed her instructions and hid the paper. Later, on the journey, the woman took the man's pillow and embroidered the top with the words, "In the desolate village, sleep should be early in the rain and dew; in the wild inn, rise late in the wind and frost." The man received it and felt as if he had gained a precious treasure. The woman said, "I did not want to show my handwriting to anyone. You once complained that I could not stay with you for long. Now that we are together, our time is likely to be short, so I made this for you. In the future, wherever you go, it will be as if I am by your side." The man, moved to tears, said, "This time we will be separated for a long time, where will you put this?" The woman replied, "All things in the world that come together must eventually separate. When I die, the Lord of the Underworld will let me be reborn as a child in the family of the Chancellor Chen of Zezhou because I have shown loyalty. I have not yet avenged my great grudge, so I have followed you. Your great kindness should be repaid, but I cannot abandon my sense of shame. Why do you still love this unfaithful person?" She cried bitterly and then left. After half a month, they reached Guangzhou, and the woman bid farewell and departed.

When he returned home, because he missed his daughter, he often stayed alone in the study. At the end of the year, his daughter suddenly appeared. Seeing him, she was overjoyed, and told him, "It feels so good! The guilty one has been caught." He got up and asked for details. She said, "I first arrived in Huizhou, and there were door gods guarding the office. While I was wandering around, I suddenly heard the sound of a procession. Upon closer inspection, the person in the sedan chair was indeed the culprit. There was a name card half-visible in the bag hanging behind the sedan chair, so I filed a complaint with the provincial city god. Fortunately, it was approved, and the spirits were ordered to arrest Yao and his wife. They did not confess when interrogated, but they finally did after being tortured. After the case was finalized, it was reported to the underworld, and Yao was sentenced to be executed at sea. His wife and daughters were to be sold to repay the debts. Yao's wife and daughters were punished to be reborn as prostitutes. Later, they died of hunger and cold after losing their beauty. Now, Yao has been executed for colluding with pirates and his head has been displayed on a pole outside Xiangshan city. His daughter is extremely beautiful; you can quickly go and take her as a concubine to dispel your sorrow; I am also expressing gratitude for this responsibility." She hurried to leave, but then returned and said, "I almost forgot, you must take the examination next year; your success or failure depends on this attempt, do not forget!" She said goodbye with tears, and tried to hold back, but it was no use.

After the birth, the man recalled the girl's words and took the provincial examination in his own province. The examination paper had been rejected, and the chief examiner was just about to go to sleep, when it seemed like a woman in red clothing urged him to get up and said, "The paper with the identifier '驹字十号' is of the highest quality, how can you reject it because of your own stupidity?" The chief examiner was startled awake and saw a red-marked paper on the desk. He took it to read and found it was the same paper he had rejected earlier, but the writing was excellent. Knowing that something was unusual, he struggled to determine the first place. It was then that he realized the girl's words, "success in one's career depends on this one attempt." Later, the man was selected to become a magistrate in Wenzhou. Sao Sunyi, style name Song'e. His father, Jia'an, had only this son. When he was young, he was thin and delicate. As he grew older, he showed a keen and insightful nature, and he studied and entered the local school, receiving his stipend at an early age. His parents loved him even more, providing him with the best food and clothing. By the time he came of age, the family was even more impoverished, and they did not keep any servants. His parents worked hard themselves, not wanting to burden their son with any labor. He grew accustomed to this, not knowing that there were things in the world that children should do to help. After he married, the family was destitute, and he had no choice but to travel as a guest official. Because of his upright character, he often did not fit in where he went. At that time, his parents were old, and they could not avoid hunger and cold, but he treated them indifferently. Later, his father died of poverty, and the next year his mother also fell ill.

At that time, his wife had already passed away, leaving behind one daughter. The husband had always been unable to properly serve her, and in the household, there was only one servant to cook. All the medicines and care were managed by the daughter. When the mother died, the husband felt remorse for everything, but it was already too late. He then wept day and night, secretly wishing to follow her to the underworld, perhaps he could still atone for his past sins. It happened to be the Cold Food Festival, and he went to worship at the place where her coffin was placed, about to hang himself there. An old woman with white hair and a hunched posture came from the woods, leaning on a cane, and said in surprise, "Who is this young man? Why do you not enjoy life but love death?" The man stated his name and spoke of his feelings with tears. The woman said, "Are you the son of Zhao Jia'an? If so, then you are like my son." The man did not understand. The woman said, "Do you know that your father had another wife when he was alive? Since your father's death, I have been lonely and desolate, often regretting not having children to accompany me in the morning and evening. You should come with me, if you can take care of me, it will also be a way to repay your father, and perhaps you might see your father's face again someday." The man vaguely remembered hearing his mother say when he was young that his father originally had a fox wife. But the woman's eyes and eyebrows were somewhat similar to his mother's, and he felt a stirring in his heart first. He secretly thought that a reunion after death was still uncertain, but now getting to take care of someone who resembles his mother, and can predict the time of another meeting. The plan is indeed good. He then said, "There is still a young daughter at home, I am fortunate to receive your kindness, please come to my home so I can take care of you." The woman agreed, and they then went together to his home.

The son served his parents with joy morning and evening, fulfilling his duties as a son, and was only concerned about the family's well-being, fearing that resources might run out. The old woman sighed and said, "I came here intending to provide entertainment for my old age. Now, how can our family survive on air alone?" She then took charge of managing the household affairs, and whatever was needed, she could obtain without delay. She treated the son and daughter as if they were her own children. The son called her mother, and she did not object. Occasionally, when there was a slight disobedience, she punished him strictly, but the son would laugh and say, "I have corrected my mistake; please do not hurt the mother's hands." The old woman would also weep and then stop. When the daughter reached the age of marriageability, she was arranged to be married. The son was appointed to an official position in Tai'an through the selection and recommendation system and brought the old woman to his new post. He served his office with diligence and caution, and whenever there were difficult matters, the old woman would analyze them, her insight being as sharp as a deity's. Later, on the day of his father's memorial, the son wept and said, "A lavish sacrifice is not as good as a simple but sincere care." The old woman replied, "Do you, the ungrateful son, know that this day has come? However, it is not pleasant to see each other." The son was surprised and asked how she knew, and she smiled and entered. Following her, he saw a maid applying yellow tin to paper money to make it look like offerings. The old woman then took a pair of butterfly-patterned embroidered shoes from the table. The son asked what she was doing, and she said, "Tomorrow is the birthday of the goddess of Mount Tai, and your father will go to offer his respects. He must pass through this place, so I am preparing these offerings to send with him." The son asked where his father was now, and she said, "Your father, because he lived a life without hidden faults, was appointed as the governor of Linhu Kingdom. That region is under the jurisdiction of Mount Tai, so he must go there to pay his respects." The son silently remembered this information.

On the appointed day, he called for a cart and asked the old woman to accompany him to the outskirts. Standing there for a long time, he suddenly saw the palace gates bustling with activity, and a man in the carriage wearing ancient attire rushed towards him. From a distance, the man spoke to the young man: "Your reputation as an official is good, I have no regrets now." Looking closely, it was indeed his father, and the young man could not help but hold onto the carriage pole and weep: "Father, can you take your son with you?" The father refused, ordered the attendants to support him up, and drove the carriage away on his own. The young man tried to chase but could not catch up. He arrived at a place where he only saw rugged peaks and ridges, with white clouds spreading everywhere, and could not distinguish the path. While hesitating, suddenly the fox mother appeared from behind, carrying paper and other items, and called out: "Foolish child, you have bothered me, almost causing me to miss you. If you want to go with him, you can take this with you. Tell him to deliver this to Yuanjun." She then pointed out the way through winding paths and said: "From here to the temple of Yuanjun, it is no more than ten li." After speaking, she disappeared. The young man shed tears and continued searching for the path. When he arrived, there were red eaves and green tiles, with palaces and halls along the banks. There was distant music and dance, colorful banners and embroidered flags, coming and going like a tapestry. The young man stood back and looked from afar. Just then, his father came out from the inner court after offering sacrifices, and asked in surprise: "How did you get here?" The young man recounted his past regrets and delivered the items given by the fox mother. The father said: "These items can be left with the recording office to present to the king. I am in the realm, receiving generous treatment from the king, enjoying happiness, so there is no need to worry. Now your mother and wife are here, and since you know to repent, you can go to see them for now. But the paths of the living and the dead are separate, so you will eventually have to return." So he was loaded onto the carriage and returned together.

Upon reaching the land, he entered an official building, where ghost servants hurriedly gathered. An announcement was made for him to ascend the hall, and he was hastily summoned along with his living mother and wife. He hurriedly bowed to his mother, who, upon seeing him, was filled with surprise and uncertainty. He recounted his longing and suffering, clinging to her bosom and weeping bitterly. His mother also wept and led him inside, saying, "It's very good for you to come here. I will find a suitable match for you, for the sake of our family's sustenance. Your wife, in the afterlife, has been lonely and without support, so I previously summoned her. The King of Hell considers her to have fulfilled her duties as a wife in life and will allow her to be reborn as a boy." He replied, "She endured great hardships at home, and I often think of them with pain. Now that I can share in serving you, my wish is fulfilled, and I don't want to return." At that time, his living father just entered and sternly said, "Your lifespan has not yet ended, and you don't have a child. Why are you thinking of such things so hastily?" His mother had a maid named Qiuyan who just arrived with tea. The father pointed to her and told his wife, "This maid has the appearance of a man-pleasing woman. She can be given to you." The mother smiled and said, "I have already considered it. I heard that ghost girls who can walk in the snow leaving footprints can be paired with humans. I don't know if this maid can do that." He secretly observed the maid, who was smiling gently, graceful and charming. The maid shyly avoided and left. Later, at the evening meal, he served wine and danced to the Chu song to accompany the meal. After the two finished eating, they began to share the leftovers with the wife. When it was time to sleep, he wanted to sleep with his father, but the father scolded him away. As soon as the rooster crowed, he rushed to serve her side, supporting and massaging, never leaving her side for a moment.

For several days, the father urged him to return home. The son refused, and the father angrily said, "Why do we need you to serve us today? If you wish to stay, you must serve us. Now, the wood-chopping office lacks someone to chop firewood. If you can take on this task, then you may stay." The son said he was willing to go. The reason was that the land near the lake, bordering the northern sea, was bitterly cold, and in June people often froze stiff. Everywhere the axe was used, there were ice mountains, and these mountains were filled with sword-like trees, constantly requiring felling. Otherwise, the branches and vines would block the path and make it impassable. Those who cut them often bled all over their bodies. Upon receiving the order, the son immediately set out, going out at dawn and returning at dusk, enduring the task for ten days without tiring. The father secretly instructed his wife and daughter-in-law to persuade him to escape and return, but the son would not listen. Seeing no other option, the father submitted a petition to the underworld officials, ordering the ghost laborers to escort him back to the living world. Soon after, the ghost laborers arrived. The father went in and spoke to the son's mother, instructing her to find Autumn Swallow and have her accompany the son back. A kitchen servant said, "Recently, I went to the back garden and saw Autumn Swallow changing her embroidered shoes, walking lightly in the snow." The mother understood her meaning and immediately called her over, scolding her, "You shameless servant, are you testing yourself first?" The father laughed and sent an old woman to check. Each petal of the lotus remained clearly visible. She returned to report to the father, who ordered her to accompany the son back. Autumn Swallow, ashamed and angry, wept pitifully and refused to go. The son, especially sorrowful and affectionate, clung to her skirt, unable to say goodbye. So the father ordered the ghost laborers to take her away. The son turned back step by step, hoping the guards would be slightly careless, and he would find an opportunity to escape back.

They walked for three days, and on the way, a peak suddenly rose into the sky directly before them. The spirit pointed and told the living one, "This is called Thinking-of-Hometown Ridge. Travelers who climb here can see their hometown." The living one asked the spirit to lead them both to the summit, where they looked down upon the city walls and people of Taian. Everything was clearly visible, yet the office was completely empty of people. Only in the upper room was there someone stiffly lying on the bed, whose appearance was extremely similar to himself, with one or two old servants attending by his side. Just as he was beginning to feel puzzled and strange, the ghost servant pushed him from behind, and he fell down immediately, realizing that his body was now lying on the bed. Wiping his eyes and looking around, he saw the old servants clearly present by his side. He jumped up and asked, "Why are you still here?" The servant replied, "Since the day the master climbed the side of the road and was helped up, he still stood upright as if waiting for something, and calling to him produced no response. We servants then carried him back to the office, but he remained unconscious as before. Everyone suspected he was possessed by a demon or spirit. So diviners and shamans came in great numbers, but in the end, it had no effect. Now the office is empty, everyone has scattered like birds and beasts. We two, because we have received great favor from the master, could not bear to leave." The living one then realized that the figure who had followed his father was actually his own soul. But he did not know where Qiu Yan was now, and his thoughts remained restless. Suddenly, Qiu Yan gracefully flew down from the air and said, "Just now I saw my lord fall off the cliff, so I immediately pulled the ghost servant to go to the underworld to claim his life. But because my wrist was weak, I was instead pushed by them. Unexpectedly, we were reunited." The living one looked at her, and tears still gleamed on her eyelashes.

Before this, Shangguan Yi had fallen seriously ill and was on the verge of death, having already entrusted the new magistrate to take over. Though he had recovered, he was still unwilling to leave his post due to unfulfilled private desires, and so he resolved to resign on the grounds of a chronic illness, taking Autumn Yan and two servants back to his hometown. Autumn Yan's eating, cooking, and daily tasks were no different from those of an ordinary person. Yet at night, unless engaged in intimacy, she would sit alone without sleeping. The husband's affection for her exceeded the usual. One day, she seemed about to speak, and he pressed her repeatedly. Autumn Yan blushed, her cheeks flushed, and spoke softly: "For several days, I have been experiencing intermittent nausea. Earlier today, while picking flowers at the steps, I looked at myself and saw a small shadow." He asked why, and she replied: "Ghosts have no shadow in the sunlight; now I have a shadow, so I suppose I am pregnant with a young yang." After half a year, she indeed gave birth to a son, whom he named Nianci. When the boy was just four years old, he was sent to school. Autumn Yan thought it was too early, but he said, "You don't understand. In the future, there may be no one to teach and supervise him." Autumn Yan did not grasp what he meant and so reluctantly accepted it. A month later, he entrusted household affairs to Autumn Yan, claiming to be going to Mount Song to visit a friend, and never returned.

Yan the Attendant Grand Secretary In my hometown, Yan the Attendant Grand Secretary, once dreamed of arriving at a mountain hermitage. He saw his senior scholars and fellow scholars of the same year all dressed in monk's robes, which surprised him. The scholars asked, "Have you forgotten this place?" He then asked, "What is the name of the mountain?" The monk replied, "Mount Song." Suddenly, he realized he had left his shoes to dry on the steps, and they were still wet. He soon awoke, and a few days later, he died. He composed a verse, saying: "Mistaken for seventy years in the human world, today I return to the old forest and springs. Fellow Daoists of Mount Song come to visit, laughing and pointing to the yellow flowers and white birds before me." This story can be found in books such as *The Selected Poems of the Shi Wu Hall* and *The Wandering Garden Journal*.

According to records, there was a Lang named Cun'an, who once stayed at an inn by the side of Yifeng Bridge. One night, before dawn, he heard the sound of sweeping and cleaning on the bridge. A person asked, "What kind of deity has passed, and why is the path being cleansed with such special reverence?" The sweeper replied, "The Eight Immortals will pass through here at dawn tomorrow." The Lang secretly noted this. The following night, after the hour of midnight, he secretly went to the bridge to wait for them. It was the end of autumn, and the bright moon was in the sky, illuminating the bridge stones as if they were molten silver. The area was silent, and the cold dew touched his sleeves. After a while, he unconsciously became tired and leaned against the bridge railing, dozing off. Vaguely, he heard people talking and quickly opened his eyes to see a group of beggars passing by, with ugly and filthy appearances and repulsive intoxicated states. The last one had a lame foot and carried a burden as if he were a leatherworker. The Lang secretly counted them and found there were exactly eight people, so he rushed to meet them. The other seven had already gone far ahead, leaving only the lame beggar staggering and not moving forward. The Lang hugged his foot, knelt down, and begged for guidance. The lame beggar said, "I am a leatherworker who cannot support myself, so I joined the group of beggars to get drunk. What did you see that made me seem like an immortal?" The Lang persisted in annoying him, and the lame beggar then opened the bucket behind the burden to show him. Looking inside, it was vast like the sea, with huge waves splashing into the sky and fish and dragons appearing and disappearing. In a state of shock, the lame beggar lifted the burden and pushed with force, saying, "It's truly a fierce ox." And both the person and the burden disappeared.

In the year of the Kangxi era, the vice minister ranked first in the imperial examination and was recommended by the Hanlin Academy to be promoted to the position of Shao Zongbo. One day, Emperor Kangxi summoned him for a long discussion. The vice minister had always been tall and robust, but he found it difficult to bow and rise. The emperor ordered the attendants to support him and smiled, saying, "He is truly like a strong ox." The official understood the divine message. He then requested to retire due to old age, at the age of fifty-nine. He received stipends for over a decade after retirement before passing away. Astrological Fortune-Teller A man named Zhang from Wu traveled among the nobility and officials using his astrological predictions. He once predicted that Miao Nianzhai would become the top scholar in the imperial examination. In the year of the Kangxi era, Miao Nianzhai indeed became the top scholar. The people of Wu were amazed and regarded him as a deity, with carriages and horses constantly flowing at his door. Zhang also raised his own reputation and accumulated a fortune. Han Zongbo Tan, who was teaching in a humble lane, entrusted a friend to ask about his own fate. Zhang spoke loudly, "This person will die next year, why ask about his achievements?" When Han Zongbo Tan passed the examination, Zhang fled. The Residence of the Prince of Kai Ping in Jinling It is said that there is a monster inside the residence of the Prince of Kai Ping, so anyone who enters dies. Since the beginning of the dynasty, every county magistrate taking office would add a seal. No one dared to open it. Suddenly one night, the house was lit up by firelight, and they thought it was a fire. They all rushed to save it. They opened the door and entered, but they only felt the deep silence of the palace, dark and nothing could be seen. They were all puzzled. Suddenly a strong wind rose, and thunder and lightning flashed. In the northeast corner of the hall, an eighteen-foot-long spear emerged from the ground, transforming into a dragon. It coiled and flew up to the sky.

At the moment, everyone was marveling and wondering when a Taoist monk, wearing tattered robes and dragging a staff, passed by. Upon hearing their story, he laughed and said, "When the King of Kai Ping was alive, he once carried this spear, assisting the founding emperor in pacifying the world. Later, after returning from Beiping, he fell seriously ill on the way and left a dying wish to bury the spear beside the hall. This spear was originally a 'toxic dragon' collected by the King of Kai Ping during his time as a bandit with Liu Ju. Now, buried in the earth for five hundred years, it should have transformed away." The crowd asked for his name, but the monk did not answer. When asked again, he joined three fingers and said, "A person from Yangcheng." After speaking, he disappeared. Those who recognized him said, "In the early Ming Dynasty, Zhang Sanfeng was originally from Yangcheng. The one who joined three fingers is probably referring to Zhang Sanfeng." According to *The Chronicles of Northern Villa* in *The Record of the North Villa*, there is a section titled *The Brief Account of the Shadow of the Immortal Li E* which states: Leaving the city gate of Pingyue, walking six or seven miles, the path turned and cliffs rose on either side, with a high peak emerging from the sky, water flowing beneath it. Walking on a stone bridge and looking west, someone was seen. Wearing a hat and a robe, walking in a trance facing east, slowly descending. Looking closely, it was a shadow, having a form but no features. On the left side of the shadow were four small characters, reading "The Spirit Remains in the Universe." Travelers told each other, "This is the trace of the immortal Zhang Lata from the early Ming Dynasty. To avoid being summoned, he entered the stone and left this stone as a sign."

According to *The Biography of Zhang Xian*: Zhang Xian was from Yangcheng. As a child, he was in a school, where a maid served him fish soup, but his classmates hid the fish, and Zhang Xian angrily beat the maid, who hanged herself. Zhang Xian got the fish back, regretted it, and then abandoned his home to study the Dao. When the Dao was achieved, the master said, "The fault of the fish soup must be repaid." He became a minor official in Fujian, was implicated in a mistake, and was stationed in Pingyue. In Pingyue, there was Zhang Qianhu's son, who was skilled at chess, and Zhang Xian often defeated him. Zhang Qianhu's son concentrated and fell asleep, dreaming of an old woman teaching him, and then he defeated Zhang Xian. Zhang Xian laughed and said, "The Mother of Mount Li is very talkative." From this, he knew her divine nature. When he wanted to enter Chu, Zhang Qianhu sent him off, hesitating and holding the sleeves, unwilling to part. Zhang Xian pointed out the burial site, saying, "If buried here, you will be enfeoffed as a marquis, and we will meet again at the peak of Taihe in ten years." And then he left. After several years, the Rebellion of the Prince of Yan broke out, and Zhang Qianhu submitted a memorial, being enfeoffed as Longping Hou. He was ordered to perform a sacrifice at Wudang, where he met Zhang Xian at the side of a rock stream, who was tattered and disheveled, with an odor so foul that it could not be borne. Seeing the Lord, he commanded him to sit, and reaching into his bosom, he found a date to give to the Lord. The Lord did not eat it, but held it in his bosom. When he wanted to leave, Zhang Xian held his sleeves and said, "Can you stay here?" The Lord said, "I wish to wait for another day." Just as he descended the mountain, the date grew to a foot long. He was shocked and regretful, returning to seek Zhang Xian, who had already passed away. Later, the court issued an edict seeking Zhang Sanfeng, finding his disciple Qiu Yuanqing, but Zhang Sanfeng was never found. I have heard that Zhang Xian studied calligraphy with Leng Qian under the monk Yunhai. These four characters are indeed written by Zhang Xian. Therefore, the image is indeed that of Zhang Xian, and the writing is also that of Zhang Xian. Otherwise, how could the faint traces of ink and pigment, spanning several hundred years from Hongwu to Yongle, not be eroded by wind and rain?

I have examined the anecdotes about Zhang Lata, and there are many examples seen in other books. This record provides a detailed account of the origin of his Daoist learning, so it is specially appended here. Human-faced Beans From *Yishi Zixia*: In Jinling, there was a Chancellor's residence where Hu Weiyong lived. The garden had a Five Grains Tree, which bore leaves that indicated the abundance or scarcity of each of the five grains: if the wheat was ripe that year, the tree would sprout wheat leaves; if the millet was ripe, it would sprout millet leaves, and so on for all the grains. When Hu Weiyong was accused of treason, the tree sprouted beans, and all the beans had human faces. Suddenly, they all fell off, and shortly after, his entire family was exterminated. The tree seemed to sense the ominous atmosphere first. Last year, I was in the fields, and a friend once showed me several hundred grains, saying they were brought from Henan by a canal worker. They all had eyes and noses, resembling human faces, but they lacked beards and eyebrows. I don't know what this signifies.

Furthermore, according to "The Daoist Hermit's Wild Talk," human-faced beans are produced in southern Yunnan. Each pod contains several beans, which resemble human faces. Children who consume them can avoid smallpox, and those who are ill can recover. The locals also highly value these beans, though they are not easily obtained. Those who find them describe them as large, similar to broad beans, and white in color. In Jiangsu and Zhejiang, there used to be beans that resembled human faces. Scholars believed these were omens of war, similar to the beans in Yunnan, but they are rare and often lead to exaggerated or superstitious interpretations. This claim is incorrect. In Yunnan, there is a specific type of bean that naturally resembles human faces. In contrast, the beans found in Jiangsu and Zhejiang are rare and discovered by chance in yellow beans, and yellow beans are not typically as large as broad beans. During the provincial examination in the year of the fire ox (丁酉), I stayed at the Sea Goddess Temple, where the Feiyin Pagoda in Huzhou had just been completed. One day, a local official came to collect fees and told me, "Sir, you must pass this examination. The top scholar next year will be from Huzhou, and the opportunity must not be missed." I asked, "How do you know this?" The official explained, "This summer, on a certain evening, I suddenly saw a red light illuminating the sky above the Feiyin Pagoda. Everyone was alarmed, thinking a fire had started, and rushed to help. Upon reaching the pagoda, the red light had already dissipated, and there was no other unusual event. We then realized it was a sign of the star 'Kui,' indicating good fortune. Is this not a sign of becoming the top scholar?" The following year, Niu Songquan (Fubo) indeed became the top scholar. I had heard about unusual events associated with the creation of the Daoist Hermit's Wild Talk since childhood but did not believe them. Now, I wonder if the conventional beliefs are truly reliable.

Chen Xueshi My family treasures a single scroll of cursive script by Chen Xueshi, a scholar of the early Ming Dynasty, which reads: "Yan Junping, Sima Xiangru, and Yang Zihui will no longer appear." It consists of fourteen characters. The style is reminiscent of Wang Xizhi, with a robust and ancient appearance, suggesting it was influenced by Li Gongquan. Although Chen Xueshi was not renowned for his calligraphy, his brushwork is so outstanding. It is undoubtedly a work that will be passed down. It is said that when Chen Xueshi first entered school at the age of nineteen, he fell seriously ill and had a dream of a purple-robed monk who called himself Master Xuan Gui. The monk took his hand and said, "You have brought me to the human world; why don't you return?" Before Chen could respond, the monk laughed and said, "Wait a moment, wait a moment. You still have a cup of wine at the Jade Forest and a bowl of soup at the Island of the Immortals. It won't be too late to come back after you've had them." The monk counted on his fingers and said, "This farewell will take another seventeen years." After speaking, the monk disappeared. Chen awoke from the dream, and his illness soon subsided. He passed the imperial examinations in the year Jia Wu, entered the Hanlin Academy, and eventually became a scholar of the imperial reading hall. At the age of thirty-six, he fell ill with dysentery that did not subside. Recalling his previous dream, he smiled and told his family, "Master Xuan Gui has not come yet; perhaps the date has been changed again." One day, at dawn, he burned incense, bathed, and dressed in his official robes, saying, "My teacher has arrived; I am going now." He passed away in the lotus position.

The Filial Son Xu Xu the Filial Son, a native of Kunshan, was the great-grandson of Grand Justice Ganxue. His father, a local scholar, was carefree and neglected household affairs, leading to the loss of all family wealth and the scattering of his students. At the age of thirteen, Xu began copying documents for the county clerk, earning money to support his parents. His father, who loved drinking, could not eat without having three cups of wine. When Xu lacked the means to buy wine, he would borrow money from a shop. After a long time, he could not repay the debt, fearing that the shopkeeper would be angry with him. He would visit the shop daily, clapping hands and discussing *Romance of the Three Kingdoms* and *Romance of the Sui and Tang Dynasties*, mimicking the characters' voices and appearances so closely that the shop owner was pleased with him and did not ask for the wine money. Xu then pretended to be crazy while singing, using this method to exchange for wine and food to support his family. His father's excessive drinking caused his mother to fall ill, and Xu also suffered from an eye disease that prevented him from writing. Unexpectedly, he took up playing a stringed instrument and performing blind poetry, regarding it as his usual occupation.

Kunyi was divided into a new county in the tenth year of Emperor Yongzheng's reign, called Xinyang. A new City God Temple was built at the Luohan Bridge in the east of the city, which was the former site of Ye Wenshen's half-silk garden. The filial son sang there every day, and crowds gathered to listen. As noon approached, he always returned home, and if forced to stay, he would cry. The crowd found this strange, and some followed him, but he would use the money he earned to buy food and return home. His mother would finish eating before he returned. When asked about his family background, he would pretend to be deaf, smile foolishly, and not answer. It is said that he did this because his occupation was lowly, and he did not want to tarnish his ancestors' noble lineage. After his mother's death, the filial son disappeared. Some said he may have drowned in the river. The historian remarked: Xu the filial son is like the ancient figures who lowered their status and humbled themselves. The narrative recounts his life from the age of thirteen, when he stopped studying, until his mother's death and his disappearance. Readers can understand his intentions. Therefore, when he was forced to stay and cried, it shows that his singing and laughing were filled with tears. Alas, what misfortune he suffered! Given the Xu family's former noble status, it is surprising that they declined so quickly. However, someone recently modified a saying from the *Strategies of the Warring States* to say: "When one is poor and humble, relatives fear them; when one is wealthy and powerful, parents do not treat them as children."

I have read *Lè Jiāo Sī Yǔ*, which recounts that Cài Jīng had held power for a long time, and when his son Yōu's influence began to conflict with his, those who were superficial and insincere meddled in the situation. As a result, father and son each established their own factions and became enemies, living separately in their granted mansions. One day, Yōu visited Cài Jīng, quickly took his hand and pretended to take his pulse, saying, "The elder's pulse is slow and relaxed. Is there anything wrong with your body?" Cài Jīng replied, "No, there is nothing wrong." Yōu then took his leave. A guest secretly observed this and asked Cài Jīng about it, to which Cài Jīng responded, "You do not understand. My son wants to make me appear ill so that he can dismiss me from power." A few days later, Cài Jīng indeed retired as the Duke of Lu. In Tongcheng, there was a beggar who once went to Shěn Mèngyuān's residence to beg. Most of what he received he did not eat. Shěn Mèngyuān found this strange and had someone secretly observe where the beggar went. The beggar went to a place by the riverbank, where a simple but clean boat was moored, and an elderly woman lived there. The beggar would take out the items he received and arrange them before the old woman, pour wine, and kneel to offer it. He waited until the mother took the cup, then stood up, danced, and sang mountain songs, playing and entertaining her. He did this every day. When the mother died, the beggar was no longer seen.

Yu and Gai are both human beings. To be the father of Yu, how is that better than being the mother of Gai? Therefore, if the Xu family has a wealthy and noble son like Yu, how is that better than having a poor and humble son like Gai? This is how the heavens have not been unkind to the Xu family, and the filial son can face his ancestors with honor in the afterlife. What more reason does the filial son have to feel ashamed in this world, and to be ashamed of his family name? To the west of the Baishan region, there is a land of women. In this land, women are fierce and men are submissive; women are the rulers and men are concubines. There can be up to a hundred male concubines, chosen for their youth and beauty. Once, Princess Anle recommended her brother to Empress Wu, saying, "Your Majesty's longevity increases daily; it is appropriate to have many male consorts to enjoy your old age." This was likely influenced by something she had learned. In the pond in front of the provincial government office in Hangzhou, there are dozens of turtles of various sizes, and they are extremely numerous. Those who enjoy such things sometimes buy bread and pastries, break them into pieces, and throw them into the water. All the turtles come to the surface to compete for the food, stirring up waves and ripples, their movements graceful and entertaining to watch. It is said that at the beginning of the dynasty, silver from the provincial treasury was often stolen, and the thieves were not caught for a long time. Later, because the sewer was blocked, workers were called in to clear it. Upon opening it, they found two bodies, one moving forward and one moving backward, with their heads touching and filling the space. They realized that these were the thieves who had stolen the silver, entering through the pond. Therefore, they kept turtles to guard against further thefts, and from then on, the thefts ceased. It is said that this place could only accommodate one person at a time, and once inside, one could only move forward and not backward. Thus, the two thieves happened to meet, and neither could retreat nor escape, so they both died there.

Such as the turtle in the Shangzhi Spring of my county, it has existed since the Song Dynasty. In the historical record "The Record of the Temple of the Black General" by Mo Yuan, it states: During the year of Renwu in the Shaoxing era, there was a foreign envoy passing through, and the official of Suide heard about it. He ordered officials to capture the turtle and offer it as a gift. Soon after, the official fell ill, and the envoy also dreamed that the turtle complained and returned. Some said, "It is the spirit of the Black General, a divine creature." However, according to Mo's records, there were originally dozens of them. When I was young, I still saw one or two, but now they are never seen again. Is it that these spiritual creatures appear and disappear at times? Or is it due to the local conditions? The Tomb of Wu Song The Liuhetower is located on the Jinlong River. There used to be a statue of Lu Zhishen at the base of the tower, but it is now destroyed. At that time, he heard the tide and attained enlightenment here. Below the Jinlong River is the Iron Ridge Pass, which is said to be where Song Jiang hid his troops. In the past, there were bandits in the river who robbed merchants' ships and hid the loot there, but they were killed by hidden crossbows. Since then, no one dared to enter. At the beginning of the dynasty, people near the river dug up a stone tablet inscribed with "The Tomb of Wu Song." At that time, troops marched to Qingxi and fought here; what is recorded in unofficial histories is likely true. However, the popular belief that the Jinhua General at Yongjin Gate is the spirit of Zhang Shun is baseless. Now it has been mistakenly called the Frog General.

According to history, when Liu Yu surrendered to the Jurchens, the valiant general Guan Sheng refused to comply and was killed. This Guan Sheng was a real person, but he cannot be taken as the Guan Sheng in *Water Margin*. One died for loyalty, and the other was transmitted as a thief; this is the fault of La Zaisen (Nasi An). Died three times (or experienced a near-death experience three times). This year in spring, in the Wu Liujū of the Min family in Shengshe, three people died from an epidemic. But Ling, the sister-in-law of You Mei, died and revived twice. She said that when she first died, two blue-faced ghosts, dressed like constables and wearing red hats, with very fierce appearances, took her out of the house. The road was covered with yellow sand and white grass, vast and desolate with no one around. After traveling for dozens of miles, the ghost servants found her slow and were about to whip her. Just as she was in a state of panic, she suddenly saw an old man with a white beard coming from the front. Upon closer inspection, it was her late husband, Xiangcen. He had died over ten years ago. She then realized that she had died, wept, and begged for help. Her husband immediately stretched out his hands to stop her, saying, "What kind of place is this! And you are here too, in such a disheveled state, how can you go to see Yama?" When she was captured, she had not yet changed into her clothes. He turned and scolded the two ghosts, saying, "Wicked spirits, how dare you be disrespectful!" The two ghosts instantly shrank like children and scattered in a moment. Then, he led her in a winding manner back home, and she felt the world become bright. Just as they entered the door, her body was already lying on the bier. Thus, the whole family rejoiced, believing that the ghost soldiers had made a mistake in summoning her.

After two days, she died again. She revived after one day. She recounted that during this time of being detained, she was not on the same path as before. She felt a bleak and gloomy atmosphere with cold winds, and the sky and earth had a different color. Midway, she met an elderly man wearing a coarse robe. He was surprised to see her and said, "Are you not the wife of a certain family? Your life has not yet ended. You should return immediately; if you wait any longer, your body will already be rotten." He then spoke a few words to the ghostly officials to plead for her, and they released her and left, allowing her to return home. They gave her medicine, and her spirit gradually became calm. Everyone believed she should not have died in the end. Soon after, her illness worsened, and she died the next day. From then on, she never revived again.

外史氏曰：According to the storytellers, when a person dies, it is said that they are summoned by ghostly agents. Yet there are cases where the deceased was mistakenly summoned and then released back to the world of the living, and cases where they were implicated in another matter and then set free. For example, in *Zi Bu Yu*, there is a story of someone who encountered a land deity and was directed to appeal to the Lion King for justice, which was due to the corruption of officials in the afterlife, and the matter was clarified and they were returned to life. However, the case of the Ling family, who died three times and were never revived, was not due to a mistake in summoning. But why did they revive after their initial death? Truly, it is inexplicable. **Household Oddities** The home of Shen Xueqiao in Hongdun was destroyed by tenants during a rent collection event last winter. It was said that the Lan Hall was still intact. On the second day of the first month this year, while Shen Xueqiao and Song Cheng were sitting with guests in the main hall, suddenly green smoke emerged from the brick seams. Soon it filled the room, and the host and guests could not see each other. After a long time, it dissipated. The next day, there was an incident involving Yu Anan. Could this be the interaction between fire and earth as described in the *Wuxing* (Five Elements) theory?

Again, this spring, a jar in the kitchen of the Ke family suddenly started to make a sound by itself. The sound was clear and long, as if someone were striking it. After a short while, it made the sound again, like this for over ten days. The whole family thought it was an omen, and they moved it outside to stop it. According to the *Five Elements* chapter of the *Han* dynasty, citing *Zuo's Commentary* from the 8th year of the reign of Duke Zhao: "The stone spoke in the state of Jin," Master Shi Kuang said, "Stones cannot speak, but a spirit might be upon them. ... When actions are not timely, complaints arise among the people, and then things that do not speak will speak. Now the palaces are grand and luxurious, the people's strength is exhausted, complaints are widespread, and no one can preserve their nature; isn't it appropriate for the stone to speak?" Liu Xun said that metal and stone are of the same kind, which means the metal does not follow the changes, losing its nature. In the third year of the Hongjia era of Emperor Cheng, on the day Yi Hai of the fifth month, a large stone in the Jitian mountains of Tianshui made a loud sound like thunder. The stone was three zhang long, about as wide and thick, leaning against the cliff, about two hundred zhang from the ground, and the local people called it the Stone Drum. The Stone Drum's sound indicates war. In that year, a blacksmith in Guanghan planned to attack the prison, take over the execution of the death row prisoners, steal the weapons, and rob the officials and common people, calling himself the Mountain Lord. He was executed the following winter. In the fourth year, Fan Bing and others from Weishi plotted a rebellion, and they were executed after a year. At that time, the construction of Changling was just beginning. I think the jar is a type of stone. Now the country has not started any construction projects, but the barbarians are not peaceful, and they attack at inappropriate times (the barbarians have been invading for three years without interruption, regardless of the season). The jar's sound might also indicate a military omen.

《碣石剩谈》记载：罗田西门外一家民宅，水缸中发出小鸡的叫声。打破水缸，片片都发出鸡叫声不止。后来这家人竟遭遇水灾，而可石家至今安然无恙。 柜中熊 崇祯年间，流寇日益猖獗。驸马都尉巩永固亲眼目睹权奸当道，知道大势已去，心情抑郁，无法自慰，在居庸关附近打猎。见到草丛中有一只柜子，锁得非常牢固。命令打开一看，里面有一名少女。询问她的来历，少女自称姓莫，住在伯叔的庄园。昨夜遭到光火贼的袭击，贼中有两人是僧人，劫持她到这里。交谈中，她含着愁容，手腕轻动，显得妩媚动人。巩永固被她吸引，便将她载入自己的马车。当时帐下有一名叫慕荦的人刚刚捕获一只熊，便将熊放入柜中，像原来一样锁好。 当时周皇后正秘密派遣使者四处搜寻美女，驸马认为莫氏是官宦人家的女子，便立即上表推荐。过了三天，京兆府上奏：“昌平州一家食店中有两名僧人，用十千钱租住一昼夜，声称是做法事，只抬进一只柜子。夜深时，听到房中有奇怪的声音。天亮时门未开，破门查看，发现一只熊冲出门外，两名僧人不见踪影，只剩下骸骨。”皇帝看了奏折，大笑，将奏折草稿拿给驸马看，说：“驸马真能处理这些僧人。”于是将这名女子赐给了他。

The Transformation of Rice into Pearls It is said that on the day of the birth of Master Pan Zhixuan, the Grand Secretary of the Imperial College, a lingzhi mushroom suddenly sprouted in the courtyard before his home, fresh and lovely. Later, Master Pan adopted this as his self-given name. In the summer of the third year of the Daoguang era, he had previously offended the emperor's edict and was living at home. At that time, there was a great flood in Jiangsu and Zhejiang provinces, and starving people were begging for food along the roads. Master Pan took the initiative to provide relief, giving each person a measure of rice from morning until noon, but stopping after noon. One day, just before the hour of the horse (around 1:00 PM), the starving people had all dispersed. Suddenly, an old woman with white hair, carrying a blue cloth bag and looking feeble, arrived. The gatekeeper tried to stop her, but the old woman wept and would not leave. The gatekeeper, having no choice, went to inform Master Pan. Moved with compassion, Master Pan ordered the old woman to be brought in. He saw that her bag could hold about a measure of rice and had a small hole in it. He measured out the rice and gave it to her, but it was still not enough. The old woman stopped him and said, "That is enough. Such generous giving by you, the heavens must bestow blessings." She then took her bag and left, without any trace of the rice, leaving only a few handfuls on the desk. Master Pan called his servant to collect the rice, and to his astonishment, each grain was a pearl, the largest being round and lustrous like soybeans. Some suspected that the old woman was a transformation of a Bodhisattva.

**Menglu Master's Last Days** I arrived at Houzhucun on the 12th day of the seventh month. By then, Menglu Master's illness was critical. On the night before, after the second watch, he suddenly called for Xuecun and his brothers to prepare for a bath and change of clothes. Xuecun and others saw that his mind remained clear and calm, and they hesitated to disturb him. Menglu Master then pointed to the bed and urged them, saying, "There are two armored divine generals who have come to deliver documents from the Heavenly Emperor, summoning me to be the deputy city god of the world. I declined because of unfinished family matters, but they said, 'This is the Heavenly Emperor's command, which cannot be disobeyed. Soon, they will bring music and attendants to welcome me to take up my post.' I decided not to go, but the mission must not be disrespected. I heard that there are still four people coming, and we should quickly prepare a feast to entertain and send them away. Perhaps the fate is not to be avoided, so do not delay and let it affect my affairs." Reluctantly, they prepared for the bath and change of clothes. After the third watch, he suddenly called again to remove his clothes, saying, "If they do not come now, there will be no danger tonight. You can go and rest for a while." The others gradually dispersed. That night, he was fine, but his illness was beyond recovery. When I entered to see him, his eyes were looking upward, and he could not speak. In a moment, he passed away.

Alas! Is it that heaven has bestowed this talent, not wishing for it to remain in the world for long? Or is it that the needs of the underworld are indeed urgent, necessitating the swift taking of this talent? The divine being, wise, upright, and singular, as you were in character, truly does not disgrace the selection of the Almighty. Moreover, from the brink of death to the end, not a single word of confusion was heard. Thus, what it indicated must be true. Xuecun also said: "When he called out to his brothers, there was nothing unusual. Only the entire room was filled with a strange fragrance." For several years now, I have been dreaming that my home is no longer mine, and I am often left to spend the New Year at the Dream Pavilion. On this New Year's Day, when the sky had already dawned, I suddenly fell asleep again. In my dream, I saw the lotus pond in front of the Zhu Village thatched cottage, with lotus leaves surrounding it like clouds, so green and fresh they seemed about to drip. Among them, only one lotus flower stood tall, about a foot above the leaves, its petals large as a plate, standing tall and solitary, a different kind of bright red. As I wandered around admiring it, this flower suddenly shed its petals one by one until none remained, leaving only the sad green lotus leaves unchanged. At one time, unable to contain shock and surprise, I awoke and realized its ominous nature. Yet at that moment, I merely thought of my old and sickly body, originally destined to celebrate my birthday on the 23rd day of the 6th month in the year of Bingwu, fearing that by the time it comes, I cannot avoid withering before the autumn. How could I know that from spring to summer, your illness deepens day by day. On the 12th day of the 6th month, I went from Machi to visit, knowing that your illness is likely to be fatal. After parting, never once out of mind, then on the 19th day I wrote a letter to inquire. But the letter from Zhitang was vague in its consolation, reading it only increases worry and anxiety, yet still did not recall the dream.

On the 23rd day, I quietly reflect that today is my birthday. Looking at myself, I am unexpectedly unharmed, and thus I suddenly recall the dream I had on New Year's Day. Soon after, I remember your illness, and cannot help but feel moved. Folklore holds that the 24th day is the birthday of lotus flowers, and I secretly believe that beyond this day, I might be able to revive something that seems dead or barren, thus avoiding the remainder of my life. But I fear that the fulfillment of a bad dream might instead befall you, meaning my dream was actually a warning for you. Who would have thought that the fall season would be the time, and unfortunately, my prediction turned out to be true. Alas! I have heard that brothers are as close as hands and feet. You were born, and I regarded you as a younger brother, while I treated you as an elder brother. We shared joys and sorrows, and were no different from hands and feet being part of the same body. The spiritual connection appeared first in my dream, which is natural. Moreover, with your abundant talent and virtue, and your great reputation, how could you be any different from a pillar supporting a great hall in terms of your role in the world and your family? The lotus flower is often compared to a gentleman, and only you can match it without shame. Thus, the dream was a warning for you, not an accident. It is only that my loneliness, weakness, and exhaustion in this world have allowed me to live a full life despite not being particularly talented, while you, who have always been intelligent and strong, ultimately died suddenly like morning dew! Thus, the principle of the interplay between prosperity and decline is indeed difficult to question of heaven, and the fleeting nature of life in a hundred years is even more unreliable. Alas!

From the full moon of June to July, I had two more dreams of a light snow like frost. I still feel as though I am in mourning without formal attire for my friend's family. On the night before my friend's death, I dreamed of seeing him in a crowd, running ahead, and when I called out to him, he ignored me and left; upon waking, I knew for certain that it was an omen of misfortune. Yet, are not misfortunes and blessings predetermined? If my dream abode knows, perhaps there will be no regret. Appendix: Records of Several Dreams In the first month of summer in the year Wu Zi, I was staying at Xinxi, and at night I dreamed of standing on a terrace in my temporary residence, looking out, and seeing a widespread cloud cover with snowflakes flying across the sky, it was not a world of clear skies and bright days. I have heard that dreaming of snow out of season indicates the need for mourning attire. By the first month of autumn, my second wife Wu died. When my father passed away, I dreamed of heavy snow covering the ground by several inches; when my mother passed away, I also dreamed of snow, but it was less severe. Both times, it was during the second month of summer, and I then realized that the ancients' words were not false, and I had tested them myself. How pitiful!

On the day of the horse in the year of the ox, I was slightly tired in my study and took a midday nap. I dreamed of a place with a spacious and open courtyard, evoking a somewhat mountainous and forest-like atmosphere. An old man, who appeared to be a prominent figure, was sitting upright on the hall, presenting a theme for a painting called "The Melancholy of Autumn." I composed three lines of a seven-character quatrain and woke up, not knowing what omen it was. By September of the year of the rabbit, after encountering a great misfortune concerning my father, I began to understand that the words "melancholy of autumn" were a premonition. The lack of the final line was like the four seasons lacking one winter. At that time, my father was still healthy and unharmed, but the ominous dream had foretold it two years earlier. Reflecting on it now, I cannot help but feel a sense of foreboding! In the middle of spring of the year of the ox, I was staying at the Chengshou Hall of the Lu family. One night, I dreamed of a building, looked around and saw no one, but there were several paintings hanging on the walls. One of them depicted a boulder, adorned with several leaves of water lilies, with a seven-character regulated verse inscribed above it. In a daze, I realized it was a painting by Mrs. Ye Qinkē, the wife of Mr. Ye Qinkē, who had passed the imperial examinations. Upon waking, I remembered two lines: "Wearing cloth shoes and socks is an ordinary matter, but in my opinion, one must aim for the very best." I do not know how to interpret these words.

**Donating an Official Position** A man from Songjiang, Mr. Zhao, rose to prominence through cloth trading. Later, he donated a position as a vice-magistrate. When he was introduced to the emperor, the emperor asked about his background. Mr. Zhao replied that he had been a cloth trader. The emperor said, "Then why did you donate an official position?" Mr. Zhao replied, "I believe being an official is better than a cloth-trading life." The emperor was angry and immediately dismissed him from office. Mr. Zhao left in anger and loudly demanded money at the Ministry of Personnel, saying, "Since you have taken away my official position, you should return my donation." The official in charge heard this and ordered the staff to slap him fifty times, beat him one hundred times, and then dismiss him. **Clearing Up Slander** In the neighborhood, there was a local prostitute named Ms. Someone. Her husband used to work at Mìyìn Temple. The temple monks were quite wealthy, and someone incited her to accuse the monks of having sexual relations with her. Prefectural Governor Chen Youxue ordered an investigation in Wucheng. A certain magistrate conducted a cursory examination and beat the monk, reporting back. Governor Chen doubted this and personally re-examined the case. He secretly summoned a monk from Tiěfó Temple, placed him in a private room, and put her husband outside. He called Ms. Someone and asked, "Do you recognize the face of the monk you accused?" She said, "He has had sex with me for a long time and gave me something. How could I not recognize him?" The governor then quickly summoned the monk from Tiěfó Temple and asked Ms. Someone, "Is this the man?" She said, "Yes, it is." The governor laughed. He bound her husband, severely reprimanded him, and also removed her clothing and beat her with a stick. The spectators all praised the outcome.

This is not more remarkable than the rashness of common people and the judge's handling of cases, yet it is like the county magistrate's making the best of a bad situation, which is especially laughable and pitiable. Ms. Jin Zheng Zunqian, courtesy name Liguong, was from Kuaiji. His father, Zheng Zhiyin, was a deputy official of the provincial inspection bureau in Shanxi Province. Zheng Zunqian, when young, enjoyed the practice of valuing friendship and loyalty, spending money freely to make friends. He formed a close friendship with Xu Du from Dongyang, a man willing to die for each other. A famous courtesan named Ms. Jin, upon first seeing Zheng Zunqian, was pleased and said, "A true hero." Thus, they became partners. Zheng Zunqian teased one of her maidservants, and Ms. Jin killed her. Those who were displeased with Zheng Zunqian, through the maidservant's family, brought a lawsuit against him in court, implicating Zheng Zunqian in the charges. Zheng Zunqian did not appear in court, but instead gave away a thousand gold pieces and daily drank heavily in the prison with Ms. Jin. At the time, Chen Zilong, the magistrate of Shaoxing, was in charge. Xu Du rushed to tell Chen Zilong, saying, "There are significant events happening in the world. Why would you want to kill a hero?" Thus, Zheng Zunqian was released.

The Prince of Fu fled, and Hangzhou fell to the enemy. He then summoned the young men and officials he knew to raise an army. Just as the arrangements were completed, his father surrendered from Hangzhou and shaved his head to submit to the Qing. Seeing him, he was greatly shocked and supported him as he knelt to bow, saying, "You should spare my life, and don't let our family be destroyed." Zunqian paid no heed and tore off his robe to leave. Just then, an edict from the Prince of Lu, who was acting as regent, arrived. He then sent his son Mao Sheng, leading the deputy general Hu Mingjie, to escort the Prince to Shaoxing. The Prince ordered him to wear the seal of the General of Yixing and bestowed on him a second-rank official's uniform. In November, he was enfeoffed as the Marquis of Yixing for his merit. Zilong also raised an army in Songjiang. He sent a letter saying, "I am truly a young man from Huaiyin, and I do not recognize the Prince of Lu." The following year, his army was defeated, and the Prince of Longwu sent an envoy to summon him to Fujian. But the emperor was in peril, and the Prince stayed in Changyuan. Zunqian came to pay respects and stayed with Zheng Cai. Later, due to his conflict with Zheng Cai, he drowned at sea. At that time, Jin Shi was in the army, she tied grass to resemble Zheng Cai and, whenever she served food, would cut the straw figure as an offering. Zheng Cai heard about this and threw her into the river. (Zunqian had already forcibly seized two ships, and he was also dissatisfied with the death of the Grand Secretary Xiong Shilin, showing it in his words and expressions. Zheng Cai then beat the subordinate general Wu Hui, who, with his injuries, went to Zunqian to ask for a letter to be delivered to Zheng Hongkui. Zunqian entered Wu Hui's ship to deliver it, was captured, and drowned at sea.)

The historian said: Zunqian's actions were truly bold. The historian of anecdotes remarked that although he was not naturally loyal or filial, he eventually gained fame through this, unlike those who indulge in fine clothes, delicious food, and pleasures of the senses but whose names do not endure. Indeed! It is regrettable that he had great ambitions but lacked the talent to foresee troubles, which led to his death at the hands of a traitor. As for Jin, she was originally a courtesan. At first, she alone could recognize heroes amidst the dust and filth; in the end, she even gave her life to follow her husband. This is truly what a great hero would do. Compared to Gu Hengbo, Liu Risi, and others, the difference is vast. A courtesan, yet she is worthy of being remembered.

Lotus Princess Peng Defu, a talented scholar from Nanchang, had a free-spirited nature and an exceptionally graceful appearance, making him a dashing young gentleman. Once, while visiting a friend in Qiantang, he stayed at Zhaqing Temple. One day, he went to Nanping Mountain with his friend. On their way back, they found a large crab, as big as a tray, caught by a fisherman. Surprised by this, Peng Defu bought the crab and released it into the lake. As the crab entered the water, it raised its two claws towards the bow of the boat, bowing twice before leaving. A few days later, while walking alone along the embankment, he encountered a young woman, around seventeen or eighteen years old, dressed in a green silk robe, accompanied by an elderly woman, who had just come out from Shengyin Temple. Her beauty was unparalleled. Peng Defu was so captivated that he almost lost his soul upon seeing her. He smiled and asked, "Where does such a beauty come from?" The girl blushed and looked at the elderly woman, saying, "Mother, let's go." Her steps were hesitant, and she occasionally glanced back. Peng Defu was even more entranced, following her. He hurried after her, but she was too quick to catch. After several turns, she disappeared behind the Shuixian Temple. By then, it was already dusk, and Peng Defu stood there, gazing longingly, as if he were a lifeless tree. Just as his friend returned from Lingyin Temple, he pulled Peng Defu back home. After returning, Peng Defu lost his appetite and sleep. Every day, he would go to Gushan Island, searching for her, but found no trace. Thus, he fell ill, languishing in bed.

By night, a girl with two braids carried a lamp and pushed open the door, saying, "The princess has sent to welcome the young master." The protagonist did not respond, turned around and faced the wall, reciting the lines, "Having seen the vast ocean, no other waters can compare; having seen the clouds of Mount Wushan, no other clouds can match them." The girl then said, "The princess you speak of is none other than the one you met at the Temple of the Water God the other day." Upon hearing this, the protagonist felt a sudden burst of energy, sprang up, and followed her. They walked to the back of the temple, where he caught a glimpse of palaces and towers, rising against the mountains. The girl led him through winding paths to a secluded garden, where flowers and trees were abundant, and the hills and valleys had been exhausted, leading to two open caves. Above the entrance was inscribed "Crystal Cave." The courtyard was not very high or spacious, but it was adorned with pearl curtains and red railings, surrounded by water, where lotus flowers were just blooming. The windows and walls were all made of crystal. The princess was leaning on the railing, enjoying the moon, when she saw the protagonist enter. She greeted him with a smile, took his wrist, and said, "Foolish man, it has been several days since we last met, and you look so thin!" She then ordered a cup of the Elixir of the Green Azure to be brought, and personally handed it to the protagonist, saying, "This was given to me by Lady Green Euphorbia the other day. Drinking it can help you forget your worries." The protagonist took the drink, which was a deep blue, fragrant and sweet, and it seemed to seep into his heart and spleen. He then asked, "Where are we?" The princess playfully replied, "This is the Moon Palace. You should leave now, don't let your common nature defile my pure realm." Seeing her cute and pitiful expression, the protagonist impulsively embraced her and led her into the room, removing her embroidered robe. Though her eyes were like stars and held a hint of anger, she was too shy and embarrassed to move her limbs. Eventually, the bed was stained with red, and the princess repeatedly asked him to stop, and he finally did.

The woman extended her arm to replace the pillow and gently touched him, saying, "You are so thin, how could you be so reckless all of a sudden?" The man asked, "Aren't you the reincarnation of Consort He? Why do you have such a pleasant body odor?" He then smelled her body thoroughly. The woman covered her mouth and laughed, saying, "I am the spirit of a lotus flower, you are not afraid, right? Let me tell you the truth, I am the daughter of the Water Immortal King. Yesterday, when I met you, I knew you were so deeply in love, so I am willing to entrust my body to you. But both of us have strange appearances and behaviors. I was nurtured by my uncle, and his family rules are very strict. If there is any mistake, I would be ashamed to see you again." The man asked who her uncle was, and she replied, "He is the King of Crabs. In the past, he was honored by the Water Realm for his merits and was bestowed the title of Zhonghuangbo. Now, he is the official in charge of West Lake." As she spoke, they embraced and fell asleep. When they woke up, they heard the distant temple bell ringing, and the man quickly got up. The woman made repeated promises and then left. From then on, they would meet under the stars, almost every night.

One night, they spent the entire night together, forgetting the dawn. The nanny noticed this and reported it to the boy's uncle. The uncle ordered the boy to be brought to him. The boy looked up at the person sitting on the dais, wearing a black hat and green robe, with an extraordinary appearance, and felt frightened and unable to move forward. The person suddenly startled and got up from the seat, descended the steps to greet and kneel, saying, "Do you still remember our encounter on the fishing boat? Since you graciously saved me, this kindness I have no way to repay. Recently, the old maid came and said that she did not know where a rough man came from, disturbing my nephew's inner chambers, thus causing this disrespect. My crime is great." He then got up, invited the boy to sit, but the boy still felt uneasy and constrained. The person recounted past events, and the boy finally realized that he was the so-called Judge of West Lake. The person then inquired about the boy's clan and also asked about who was inside the inner chamber. The boy said, "Previously, the betrothal gifts were transformed, and I am still seeking a phoenix." The person was pleased and said, "If so, isn't this a predestined fate? My nephew has quite an extraordinary appearance and talent. Now that you have found a match, it is no different from a military officer. If you do not consider us an inappropriate match, then I wish to marry you." Upon hearing this, the boy was overjoyed beyond expectation and bowed forward to express gratitude. The person then ordered the maid to summon the daughter, explained the offer to her, and the daughter was shy and could not look up. Just then, the person's wife heard the news and came out, seeing the boy standing tall and graceful, and was also pleased. They supported the match enthusiastically and brought the daughter in. The person then chose a favorable day for the wedding and sent them to the Crystal Domain Pavilion.

The woman was skilled in singing and particularly fond of playing the zither. She once cut paper into two white phoenixes and, carrying the zither, crossed them, traveling to the TianTai and YanDang mountains. She played the piece "Colorful Phoenix's Marriage," and the man leaned on the zither and sang the "Water Tune." He patted her shoulder and said, "I am old and belong here, I do not wish to emulate Emperor Wu seeking the White Cloud Land." A year later, on the Dragon Boat Festival, the woman accompanied the man to a lake to watch the dragon boat races. Suddenly, a friend called out to the man from a neighboring boat, asking where he had been. The friend then handed him a letter, saying, "This is a message entrusted by my elder brother." The man opened the letter and found it stated that his mother's illness was critical and urged him to return home quickly. After reading the letter, he shed tears and hurried back to his lodging to pack and prepare for his journey. He hesitated to leave the woman, unable to say goodbye. The woman looked sorrowful and said, "How can I abandon my husband because of me? But how can I let you go alone?" She then took the man back to inform her uncle of their plans to travel together. The uncle refused, saying, "You are weak and cannot bear the journey. I think your mother should have recovered by now. You are filial and should return to visit." He then gave the man a pill and said, "Give this to your mother; it can prevent aging. Just come quickly, do not delay." The man bowed and accepted the pill. He then packed his belongings and made an agreement with the woman to meet again in autumn. The woman wept and said, "In the past few months, there has been movement in my womb. At that time, you should remember. I fear that circumstances may not align, and we may not meet again." The man also shed tears and bid her farewell.

Upon arriving home, my mother's illness had indeed recovered, which greatly comforted me. I detailed what I had experienced and planned to take my mother with me to Zhejiang. My mother was not happy about traveling far, so after living there for several months, I bid farewell to my mother and brother, crossed the river, and stayed at Zaoqing again. The next day, I went to look for the woman, but upon arrival, I found that the path was blocked by weeds, and there were no houses. As evening approached, I reluctantly returned. Upon reaching Xiling Bridge, I saw the woman dressed in fine attire, slowly approaching from the east. I greeted her in advance and told her about the strange things I had seen. She said, "My family previously suffered a disaster and has since moved to Hunan. Now you can cross here." We called a boat, and upon reaching the side of the Leifeng Pagoda, we saw the tower emerge from the mist. She ordered the boat to dock there. She took me to the shore, ordered wine, and we talked about our separation. Before the wine was finished, she suddenly got up, embraced me into the bedchamber, and we were extremely intimate. I was almost unable to bear it, and the next day I fell ill. The woman personally prepared medicine and never left my side. But after sleeping, she would insist on being intimate with me, and although I was disgusted, I had no choice. As a result, I became increasingly weak and was on the verge of death.
