Suddenly, a woman rushed to the bedside, hugged me, and wept profusely. After a long while, she pointed at the other woman and scolded, "Foolish woman! Now that my lord is this ill, why are you still clinging to him?" Before she finished speaking, I suddenly opened my eyes and saw that the woman's appearance, clothing, and shoes were exactly the same as the previous woman, and she was indeed another princess. She sighed and said, "You are done! I already know that your life hangs by a thread, so why bother with the double axe to cut you down?" The woman wept bitterly and, after a moment, left the room in a huff.

As the day was drawing to a close, a woman accompanied by a maid arrived carrying a black crane with a red top. As soon as they entered the door, the woman in front suddenly shrunk like a hedgehog, lying on the ground, not daring to move. The maid released the crane to attack her, and the woman's head split open, transforming her body into a white snake. Cutting open her abdomen, they found a pearl the size of an inch. The woman showed it to the young man and said, "This is the result of the snake spirit from the Leifeng Pagoda. I had previously gone with my maternal uncle to the Jade Pool to celebrate the Queen Mother's birthday, and the evil creature caused this mischief, deceiving the young man. When I saw the young man was too ill to recover, I was unable to explain or justify myself, and even my uncle could not control this evil. So I went back to see my mother, asking for the black crane kept in the garden to come and eliminate it. Now the evil creature is fortunately eliminated, but the young man has been deeply poisoned. You must use this pearl mixed with realgar to treat him, and the illness can then be cured." The young man, in a state of confusion, heard the woman's words, like waking up from a dream. He sighed and said, "This creature was once in bed with me, but I found its breath and scent not as fragrant as your body, and it was very disturbing. When you came to see me, my heart became more shocked and surprised, but how could I suddenly let go of it?" The woman then gave the pearl to the maid, hurriedly ordering the preparation of medicine. After three days, the young man was already up, and they took her along and returned home together.

At this point, the child had been born for two months. The man held him, his joy turning to sorrow, and said, "This is no less than a reincarnation of Wei Xiao! This child can be named Lai Fu." The woman suddenly choked back tears and said to the man, "Take good care of him, for your family lineage depends on this one thread. I cannot see him grow up, is this not fate?" The man was startled and asked, "Why do you say this?" The woman replied, "I was originally a servant of the Purple Palace, bound by a foolish thought. Before, I went to the layered city, where the Queen Mother demoted me to be reborn in the family of Liu, a scholar from Huanggang, because I broke the color taboo. My time here has come." She stood up, was about to leave, then returned, took the child from the man's arms, and nursed him. Afterward, she was about to go, but the man made her sit down. The woman said, "Even if I stay for a short while, I must eventually leave. Take good care of yourself, do not think of this unfaithful person." She left with tears, and even after ten steps, she looked back. The man followed her, but she suddenly disappeared. He cried bitterly, took the child home, and never remarried.

Night Demons In the early years of the Daoguang era, there was a man named Li in Wangdian who lost his wife in middle age and left behind a son who was already ten years old. One day, two old women knocked on his door to seek a match. The man hated them because they were old and rejected them. The women asked to stay temporarily in his room, but he refused, saying he could not provide for them. The women said, "Just accommodate us, and do not worry about daily expenses." The man finally agreed. After staying for several days, the man's resources had been exhausted, and he was about to search his chest for clothes to pawn at the pawnshop. When he opened it, there was a silver ingot shining brightly on top. He took it and weighed it, and it just happened to weigh ten taels. He knew it was done by the two old women and became even more respectful. From then on, all kinds of cloth, grains, and food items, if there was any need, nothing was refused immediately, and the man's household became slightly prosperous. Later, the man went out for some business and returned at dusk, losing the child's whereabouts. He asked the two old women, and both said they did not know. He could not find him, and although he slept, he did not sleep well. While turning over, something seemed to be stuck under the bed. He took a light and lifted the mat to see, and there was a human skin, folded underneath. Its eyebrows, eyes, and limbs were just like the child, but the bones and flesh were all empty. Greatly frightened, he told others, and they all went to see. From afar, they saw the two old women were over ten feet tall, with sawed teeth and green faces, and mouths like blood basins. They then knew they were night demons. Frightened to the point of wanting to run away, but the object had already disappeared.

The historian remarks: When something unexpected comes before one, even a night-glowing jade or a moon-like pearl cannot escape being met with suspicion and vigilance, let alone a person. How much more so when the unexpected comes from the person themselves? Now, in the case of Mr. Li, who was confronted by two women suddenly and unexpectedly, he was unable to discern the danger early on, and when it came to the sudden appearance of wealth, he failed to consider the consequences fully. In the end, what did he gain, and how far did he fall into the clutches of the demons? Alas, alas! Yet, how many people in the world can take on the form of demons, not just these two women? A Strange Illness This summer, Shen Yuanxiang recounted: In a wealthy household in Hezhong, the wife contracted an illness. Every day, two men and two women would appear before her. Whenever they appeared, she would faint and lose consciousness, but she would remain in a dazed state, sitting silently without speaking. Her complexion would turn red, like that of a peach blossom, and she would become even more beautiful. When the two men and two women left, she would spit out a mouthful of clear water and recover. This happened several times a day, and her spirit gradually weakened. When asked what the two men and two women were doing, she would never speak. Doctors were called in to examine her, and some said her pulse showed signs of a ghostly presence and that the illness was incurable. On their way back, they would surely encounter trouble. Therefore, whenever a doctor was called, they would first warn them. Yuanxiang also once went to visit her.

According to the aspirations of the Old Man of the Su Garden: Xu Lingtai, the esteemed physician, recounted that the patient, Nengshi of Luzhu, had been ill for six days, neither eating nor speaking, with his eyes fixed and bright. Xu said, "This is a struggle between yin and yang energies." After administering a single dose, the patient's eyes closed and he could speak; upon drinking more medicine, he suddenly leaped up and exclaimed, "When I was gravely ill, two red and black figures appeared to torment me. Suddenly, I saw the black figure struck down by lightning, and shortly after, the red figure was carried away by a white tiger." Xu smiled and said, "The lightning strike was caused by the Fuzi Pill I administered; the white tiger was the effect of the Tian Sheng Zhibu Decoction I gave him." According to medical texts, there are indeed cases where illness leads to hallucinations of ghosts. However, in this case, the patient's words clearly indicate that some malevolent spirit was influencing him. Zhen Pu From Wuyuan, Zhen Pu, also known as Zhen Jingshan, was a man of exceptional talent. He was once mentored by the respected scholar Wang Shen'an, who praised his exam papers, saying they were spirited and full of extraordinary talent. Wang believed that with further effort, Zhen could establish himself as a renowned writer. As a result, Zhen was selected as an outstanding candidate for a special scholarship. However, he repeatedly failed the provincial examinations, leaving him feeling frustrated and unfulfilled. Once, he showed some of his writings to others, who were all amazed by the extraordinary energy and brilliance that filled the pages, finding no trace of mediocrity. Zhen merely smiled and set the writings aside. Yet, due to his poverty, he hoped to support himself by working the land. His attempts to secure positions through recommendations were also met with rejection. Zhen sighed, "Is this what it means to be poor?" He then withdrew from social engagements, locked himself in his room, and devoted himself to study. Whenever he finished a piece of writing, he would go to the mountains, pick up a skull, bring it home, place it on his desk, pour wine as an offering, and while reading, drink and weep deeply.

One day, while he was still crying, the skull also shed tears. The protagonist was startled and did not dare to hold it back. By night, when he was reading by the light of a lamp, a beautiful woman suddenly entered, scolding, "Grave robber, do you not fear death?" The protagonist looked at her and saw that she had a youthful appearance, a palace-style hairstyle, and a furrowed brow, but there was no anger in her expression. He realized where she came from and stood up to bow, saying, "To meet a true connoisseur, dying would be no regret! But how can I face the spirit of Lu Guimeng?" The woman said, "I am also a weak woman, unable to protect my remains, how could I help with matters of fame and fortune?" The protagonist promised to bury her properly, and the woman then smiled and bowed in gratitude. The protagonist found her charming and gentle, like a willow swaying in the wind, and invited her to share the bed. The woman changed color and said, "I came out of pity for your talent and to show compassion for the dead. I am a woman from the noble family of Haiyan, whose ancestors died in the capital during the fall of the Ming Dynasty. My family fled south, and I was selected to enter the palace during the reign of the Prince of Fu. Before I could be favored, the Qing forces broke through Nanjing, and I was taken by a low-ranking general. He wanted to make me his wife, and I asked to bathe first before submitting to his will. I went into the bathroom and killed myself with my dagger. He also pitied me and buried me here. Now, if you are a woman from Hejian, this is your fate." She tore her skirt and left.

The protagonist returned dispiritedly to bed. The next day, he carried the skull to its original place and made every effort to arrange a proper burial. If there was not enough, he followed it with pawning his possessions. He also carved a stone tablet to commemorate her loyalty and virtue, and after several days, the work was completed. That night, the woman returned, smiling and saying, "From now on, I know you are truly a man of deep feeling. I cannot repay your great kindness by sacrificing my virtue, but since childhood, I have been taught in the ways of literature and have gained some insight into its depths. Now, I wish to serve you by your writing desk, as a literary assistant to Master Zheng, is that acceptable?" The protagonist was overjoyed. He took out his recent writings for her to read, and she would revise a few sentences, which he admired for their excellence. She threw down her pen and sighed, "How could I possibly add to your work?" She then pointed to one piece and said, "This piece is not lacking in depth and beauty, but it might be too highbrow, and those who see it might not appreciate it as the common sun of Shu, but rather as the rare snow of Yue." The protagonist was disappointed. From then on, the woman came every night, and he read to her, often forgetting his fatigue. Seeing his hard work, she would set up a board game to play together, sometimes brewing tea and playing the zither, often until dawn.

One night, the girl arrived. The student had just finished recording a lesson and presented it to her. She took the work, placed it on the table, and remained silent, sitting beside him in a lingering manner. The student questioned her, and she responded with a sad expression, saying, "I originally intended to serve as a tool for refining you, to help you achieve fame and to repay your great kindness. Now, my father was recorded by the Heavenly Deity for his loyalty and integrity and appointed as a celestial official in the Lingzhi Pavilion. Because I am adrift and without support in this place, I was summoned to serve as a scribe in the Purple Palace. The jade talisman arrived when I returned home yesterday. I wanted to tell you recently, but I was afraid it would hurt your heart. I recall the night of old when you commanded me to sing, something I was too shy to do before. Now, parting is imminent, so please allow me to sing one song as a final farewell." She then stood up and lifted her sleeves, singing a quatrain from Zhang Hu's "Palace Song." Each word elicited multiple turns, and each turn brought tears. The song ended, and she choked and could not continue singing. Soon after, she fell to the ground and vanished. He could not find her. He followed her to the tomb and called out, "Where is Wu Nü?" But her fragrant soul finally disappeared, and he cried bitterly and returned. From then on, he developed a hemoptysis disease. It was time for the autumn examination, and he entered the examination hall while ill. His exam paper was already accepted, but because of the question "If Yi Yin and Lai Zhu...," he used words like "gold plate" and "jade basket" in his essay. The examiner did not understand, and he was ultimately rejected, which was exactly what the girl had described as profound and beautiful. After the results were announced, his illness worsened, and he soon died.

Gu Sheng did not even realize he was dead, stepping out casually, intending to look for his daughter, but wandering aimlessly, not knowing where to go. As he roamed the open fields, he suddenly saw a banner with embroidered curtains, accompanied by several maids coming from the east. A woman with white wrists pulled back the curtain and looked at him, saying in surprise, "Is this the real husband? How did you get here?" Gu Sheng told with tears about the reason for searching. The woman smiled and said, "You are too infatuated. I thought your illness had not yet healed, and after we parted, I often couldn't let go of my feelings, so I took a detour to see if you were well recently. Now I have good news to tell you. Yesterday I heard that the true official Han Yu reported that this time the examination was extremely unfair. Next year's favor-granting examination must first screen the examiners to prevent the suppression of talented people. The emperor immediately appointed Han Yu, who believed that Shuntian (Beijing) was still a hub of culture and talent, and he proposed to appoint Wang Tingzhen as the chief examiner of Shuntian. This person has always been your friend. If you take the exam, you should naturally fit well." She then pulled out a tortoise-shell hairpin from her hair and gave it to him, saying, "I am now going to attend a banquet hosted by the Lady of Nanyue and cannot stay long. You must take this and hurry back to prepare. Strive for success in the future, and do not be attached to this unfaithful person." Gu Sheng accepted it and saw that the hairpin had two pearls embedded on top, as large as broad beans, shining brightly. As he was about to ask more, the fragrant carriage had already sped away.

The protagonist was about to return when he happened to meet a fellow student from She County who was also going to the capital. The fellow student invited him to travel together. The protagonist was worried about the lack of funds, and Baochai was even more reluctant to sell something. The fellow student took on the responsibility of covering the expenses and agreed that they could plan things gradually once they arrived in the capital. The protagonist was happy and decided to travel with him. They finally arrived at the end of winter and presented their visiting cards to Mr. Wang. Mr. Wang was also pleased and invited them to stay at his residence. The following year, in the year of Wu Yin, the imperial examinations were indeed held during the emperor's birthday celebration. Mr. Wang, as the chief examiner for the imperial examinations, decided that the protagonist was a famous scholar from Jiangnan, selected him as the top scorer in the provincial examination, and he went on to pass the metropolitan examination without interruption. He was then appointed as a compiler in the Hanlin Academy and took leave to return home. Upon entering, he saw his wife wearing mourning clothes and crying in the hall. She shouted, "I have now returned, having passed the examination!" She turned around and saw the protagonist, dressed in a splendid coat and riding a magnificent horse, and was greatly startled. She said, "You died from crying after failing an exam, exactly one year ago. Do not use this state to scare people." The protagonist heard this and, as if waking from a dream, sighed deeply and suddenly passed away, dying with his clothes and hat on.

Many years later, someone encountered him on Mount Qingcheng. He was dressed in a simple headband and Taoist robe, and his complexion had become noticeably younger. Accompanied by a beautiful woman who appeared like a fairy, he rode in a green carriage, followed by more than ten horsemen, about to enter the mountains. He called out to the person, asking about their hometown, and instructed his servant to take two books from a colorful bag and send them back: one for his wife, stating that he had recently found a female companion and was now considered a local immortal; one for Cao Sheng, thanking him for the favor of sharing clothes and also entrusting his wife and children. It was clearly written in his usual style. In the late Ming dynasty, there were eunuchs who served in the imperial court during the reign of Emperor Kangxi. They said that during the period when the Ming dynasty was in decline, the emperor had a son who was now a descendant in the imperial service. During the early Ming period, Emperor Tianqi referred to Wei Zhongxian as "Old Companion" and entrusted him with all matters, while he himself did not intervene. Yang Lian and Zuo Guangdu were flogged, and the old eunuch still had witnesses to these events. The court was extravagant, spending 400,000 taels of silver on cosmetics and millions of taels on supplies. The ground bricks in the Forbidden City were laid in seven layers, both horizontally and vertically. There were 9,000 palace maids and 100,000 eunuchs. Food was often insufficient, and there were daily deaths from hunger. The court used Ma Kou wood and red snail charcoal, consuming tens of thousands of pounds daily. Ma Kou wood is about three to four feet long, with two carved mouths at each end, and is completely white with no black spots. Today, it is only used for sacrifices at the Altar of Heaven.

During the time, many actions were impractical and laughable. After the Hall of Supreme Harmony, there was a stone step several zhang high and thick. When it was transported to the capital, it could not be carried into the Meridian Gate. The eunuch in charge of transporting the stone reported that the stone refused to enter the Meridian Gate, and the emperor ordered that the stone be beaten sixty times with the imperial rod. Emperor Chongzhen once tried to learn to ride a horse, with two people holding the reins, two holding the stirrups, and two supporting him. As soon as he mounted, he fell off, and the emperor then ordered that the horse be punished forty times and sent to a harsh post for service. Such actions, how could they not make one burst out laughing! During the reign of Emperor Kangxi, a man from Shunde once entered the mountains of Deqing to gather herbs. Suddenly, he heard a baby's cry from the top of a tree. Looking up, he saw a misty vapor rising from an ancient tree, and all the birds that flew past it fell. He then cut down the tree and found a person inside, whose appearance was like solidified fat. When he asked it, it did not respond, but when he touched it, it laughed. One of his companions said, "This is not a bad thing." They cooked and ate it. After eating, they felt hot and soon bathed in a stream, and the flesh eventually rotted and split open, leading to death. No one knew what kind of strange thing it was. I once saw in *Beihu Lu* (Northern Gate Records) that it said: The Great Food Country is bordered by a great sea to the west. On the western shore of the sea, there is a large rock, on which stands a tree with a red trunk and green leaves. The tree grows small children, about six to seven inches long, who laugh when they see people and move their hands and feet. If you were to pick a branch, the children would die immediately. This is the origin of the ginseng fruit in *Journey to the West*. While that grows on trees, this one is formed by illusion, hence its ability to cause disasters is so great.

I have heard of the mythical country of Jün Rén Guó, where the inhabitants are exceedingly rare, living only a single day, like mushrooms. In this land, there is a mountain of silver, and on its trees grow children who can walk when the sun rises and disappear when it sets. This phenomenon of children growing on trees is not unique to the Great Food Country. The Death of Chen Zhongmǐn, the Righteous Man His name was Huàchénɡ, with the courtesy name Liánfēnɡ, and he was from Tóng'ān in Fujian Province. He began his military career at a young age, assisting General Li Zhōnɡyì, also known as Chánɡɡēnɡ, in defeating the pirate leader Cài Qiān. He was recognized by Emperor Rénzōnɡ of the Qing dynasty and was repeatedly promoted, eventually becoming the Commander of the Naval Forces in Fujian. In the winter of the nineteenth year of the Dàoguāng era (1839), the British, under the pretext of the opium ban, invaded Guangdong, Zhejiang, and Fujian, capturing Dinghai and threatening Zhaobao Mountain, resulting in the loss of several high-ranking generals. In the summer of the twentieth year, he was transferred to Songjiang. Just a few days later, Dinghai fell, and Yù Gōnɡ Qiān committed suicide. (At the time, Chen was on the city walls overseeing the battle, knowing that the situation was hopeless, he leaped from the wall to the ground, but did not die; he then threw himself into the water, but was rescued by his followers, and ultimately died by swallowing gold.) The Wusong River and the sea are located southwest of Zhoushan, with Chongming to the east and Fusong and Langshan to the northeast, mutually dependent like lips and teeth. He defended the area for three years, organizing and fortifying the camp, managing and leading the soldiers with strictness and kindness. Throughout the year, he lived in the tent, and when offered an official residence, he refused to move in, saying, "The soldiers all sleep outdoors, how can I rest easy while they suffer?" When offered food and drink, he said, "There are many under my command, I cannot provide for all; it is not right for me to enjoy alone." He refused to accept it. The eastern bank of the river relied on him heavily.

Two years later in April, the barbarian bandits captured Jiaxing, which is about two hundred miles from Wusong. I was ordered to cooperate with a certain general of Hubei to jointly defend, and I was in charge of the western artillery platform. At the time, the Governor-General of the Two Jiangs, Governor Niu, was in charge of the eastern artillery platform. On the fifth day of the fifth month, the barbarians suddenly arrived and attacked the western artillery platform. The general led the soldiers personally and damaged three steamships, one large warship, and thousands of barbarian bandits. On the seventh day, the barbarians raised their cannons at the top of the masts and fired them in succession, with lead bullets raining down like rain, and foreign guns and rockets converging, destroying the platform. At that time, the troops of Songjiang and Taihu were in front, the troops of Xuzhou were behind, and the troops of Anhui were hidden inside the earth wall to guard the eastern route. The general saw the situation was dangerous and galloped to seek help from Governor Niu, but Governor Niu had already retreated, so he had no intention of providing aid. He only sent riders to invite the general to flee together twice. The general rebuked them and then sighed, saying, "I have no help while they come in droves, the situation is difficult to handle!" He removed his seal and belt and entrusted them to a thousand-man commander to take to Songjiang Prefecture, and he still sat under the western artillery platform, continuing to direct the battle. The barbarians dared not advance. However, the left wing was already weakened, and the troops of Xuzhou took the opportunity to flee, followed by the troops of Anhui. As the sun approached noon, the barbarians entered through the eastern artillery platform's land route. Rockets hit the curtains, and armor and shields were all hit. The general's thigh was severely wounded, but he still stood firm. The barbarians had already swarmed in, and his right side was hit by seven foreign guns, with blood dripping onto his robe. He still held the flag and urged the battle, saying, "Do not be afraid, fire your guns and cannons." Soon, his voice gradually weakened, and he then knelt facing north twice before dying.

Those who died in battle at the same time included the guard commander Chang Yinfu, the hundred-man commander Qian Jinyu, the fifty-man commander Gong Lingzeng, the deputies Xu Lin and Xu Pangui, and the additional deputy Xu Dahua. The military jinshi Liu Guobiao seized the public body and hid it in the reeds. After twelve days, he was buried in the city of Jiading. His skin and body did not decay, and his face looked as if he were alive. He was sixty-nine years old. When the matter was reported, the emperor granted a thousand taels of silver. A special shrine was built at the place of his sacrifice and in his hometown. The Ministry was instructed to discuss the matter and posthumously bestowed the title 'Zhongmin' (Loyal and Compassionate).

Previously, in the defeat at Xiangshan, those who died in the cause of their duty included the Inspector-General Guan Gong Tianpei; in Dinghai, those who fell in battle were Prince Wang Xipeng, Prince Ge Yunfei, Prince Zheng Guohong, Prince Jiang Jishan, Prince Xie Chaoyun, and Prince Xiang Fu, while the rest mostly fled at the first sign of trouble. By the time Zha浦 was broken through, there was not a single person who died, nor was there anyone who fired a single arrow or cannon to resist. Since the use of military force in Guangdong, the emperor had appointed the imperial minister, Prince Yishan, as the Pacifying Reverse General, with two other ministers serving as advisors. When the barbarians broke through several counties in Zhejiang Province and occupied Ningbo Prefecture and Dinghai, the emperor then appointed Prince Yijing, the assistant to the Grand Secretary, as the Exerting Valor General, with Wen Wei and others serving as advisors, and the barbarians again broke through Zha浦. However, when the official first arrived in Songjiang, he immediately told his subordinates, "I am good at swimming, and I can handle maritime defense matters. Do not be afraid." He also taught them a trick for avoiding cannon fire: "White smoke indicates an empty cannon, but black smoke should be avoided immediately." In dealing with soldiers, he could use kindness to complement strictness and share both joys and sorrows. At the time, everyone said that there was still a good official here. Once, he captured a foreigner named Yan Shi Dala Ma, who said that the foreigners were afraid to attack because of the many cannons in Wusong. And the merchants from Fujian and Guangdong who came to Shanghai, passing on the words of the foreign merchants in Guangdong, said that the foreigners usually feared his reputation, and that he could still act according to his own will, and manage his subordinates as he did before. Thus, among the foreigners, there was a saying: "They do not fear a million soldiers in Jiangnan, but they fear Chen Huacheng of Jiangnan." They waited until three years later before entering, but ultimately, due to lack of support, he died with his aspirations unfulfilled.

After the death of the general, the barbarians entered the treasure mountain, reaching Jingkou, and in the year of Gengshen, steamships arrived at Chunshenpu. They then crossed the Sanmao, broke through Songjiang, and directly threatened Jinshan, causing the major cities of Suzhou, Changzhou, Jiangning, and Zhenjiang to be shaken and placed under martial law. The plans of several high-ranking officials to negotiate trade and peace were sold. Alas! If at that time all the generals outside the capital had been as capable as the general, how could they have reached this point so quickly? It is said that the barbarian ghosts once saw the general's form as a black tiger in a telescope. In March, a fire broke out at the Shanghai powder depot, which was likely done by traitorous merchants who had dealings with the barbarians. There were millions of swimming fish, some as large as a man, floating from the Huangpu River to the Maopu. There were also giant alligators and long snakes emerging from the waters outside the artillery platforms. In April, the barbarian bandits broke through Zha浦 and advanced on Songjiang. And then they withdrew. The general knew they would come again, so he feasted his soldiers and instructed them in the great principles, saying, "Even if there is nothing more to be done, I will die to the utmost." He also gave each soldier a pill and said, "Take it in your mouth in battle, and it will strengthen your courage." All were moved to tears and bowed in acceptance. Before the enemy came, strange omens had already foretold it; but the general's resolve had been firmly established from the beginning.

In the second year of the Deyou era of the Daoguang reign, on the first day of the fifth lunar month, the foreign invaders arrived at Songjiang, eighty li away from the city. The high-ranking officials and county magistrates each bought a boat as a means of escape. Yang Qingen, the Shanghai city magistrate, heard of this and sought an audience with the high-ranking official, but was unsuccessful. He then advised the county magistrate with moral principles, to which the magistrate replied, "Yes." When Wusong fell, the high-ranking officials and county magistrates all fled by boat. Yang Qingen stamped his foot in frustration and anger, wrote a letter to his superior, and finally declared, "I will also depart from here!" A loyal servant named Gao Sheng secretly followed him. He saw Yang Qingen hastily leave through the small eastern gate, call a small boat to cross the Chunshen River, and take out a hundred coins to give to the boatman. When they reached the middle of the river, Yang Qingen dove into the water, and the boatman cried out in shock. Gao Sheng pointed from afar and said, "This is Inspector Yang of Shanghai." At that time, the foreign invaders had already led their troops into the city. Gao Sheng hurried back, led his family to find a fishing boat, traveled upstream in search of him, and found the bodies of over ten people in a reed-filled area near Zhoujia Dock, one of which was Yang Qingen. They buried him in a coffin and returned. The matter was reported to higher authorities, and an imperial decree was issued: "Yang Qingen sacrificed his life for his duty, a deed that is particularly pitiable. He is to be granted posthumous honors and rewards according to regulations."

Alas, your death was indeed heroic! Yet, when Songjiang fell, from the Governor-General down to the Provincial Governors and Censors, there were many officials of higher rank than the Chief Magistrate, but they all fled in panic like frightened beasts and scattered birds, just as Chen Qingyong described in his memorial. The one who died in loyalty was merely a lowly Chief Magistrate. How difficult it is to accept a mission in times of danger, as you have shown! (England, also known as Yinguili, is a people from the northwest with red hair, thousands of miles away from Guangdong, who had never communicated with China since ancient times. During the 58th year of the Kangxi reign, they first came to engage in trade. In the 7th year of the Yongzheng reign, trade continued without interruption. Subsequently, they came to pay homage again, but were unable to complete the ceremony and left. Following the old tactics of the Dutch and the Portuguese, they produced opium to seduce the Chinese people. Since the 13th year of the Jiaqing reign, they have repeatedly plotted to seize Macau.) **The Chinese Tallow Tree** Several years ago, I was in Houzhucun. A neighbor came to tell me that his family had a Chinese tallow tree, which was just over a span in diameter, but because the field was quite narrow, it hindered the harvest, and they were about to cut it down and sell it. Just as they began to saw one of its branches, they saw that its center was black, with a resinous liquid flowing out, the color of amber, so they stopped cutting. They intended to find a buyer to cut it down for them. I heard that the tree was planted by his great-grandfather, and it must be two or three hundred years old.

According to *The Records of the Wei Dynasty*: In the 25th year of the Jian'an era, the Martial Emperor died. A commentary in *The World Sayings* states: The King traveled from Hanzhong to Luoyang, where he constructed the Jianshi Hall and felled a tree at the Zhuolong Shrine, and blood flowed from the tree. According to *The Biography of Cao Cao*: The King sent Su Yue to transplant a *meili* tree, and blood flowed from its roots. Su Yue reported this to the King, who personally viewed the site and was displeased. Soon after, he fell ill. This matter seems strange, yet trees that have lived for many years—could they not have a spirit? *The Record of Buddhist Temples* records: Zhao Yi Temple has a pond, which is the Zhiquan mentioned in the *Spring and Autumn Annals*. Later it became the pond of Shi Chong, an official of the Jin dynasty. To the south of the pond stands the Lvzhu Tower, and to the southwest lies Yuanyan Temple. In front of the Buddha hall, a branch of a mulberry tree grew, rising straight up five feet, with branches circling horizontally and leaves spreading out, forming a shape like a feathered canopy. Later it grew another five feet, and the same pattern repeated. In total, it formed five layers, each with different leaves and mulberries. Viewers gathered in crowds, and many made generous offerings. When the emperor heard of this, he became displeased and ordered Yuan Ji, the Attendant-in-Attendance and Minister of the Yellow Gate, to cut it down. On that day, clouds and mist obscured the sky, and where the axe was struck, blood flowed to the ground. All who witnessed this wept bitterly. *The Record of Campaigns* states: Mount Tai has three temples—upper, middle, and lower—each with walls and gate towers in strict order. On both sides of the steps stood cypress trees with a circumference of twenty *wei*, planted by Emperor Wu of Han. The Red Eyebrows once cut a branch of one of these trees and blood flowed. Even now, the axe marks remain. Thus, such phenomena are not unique to the Cao family.

毕秋帆先生巡抚陕西，曾上华山顶，宿僧舍，梦有人长身玉立，著古衣冠揖之曰：“某住此山中已千年。近有僧人以大铜钟挂吾左臂，吾甚苦之，乞为解释。”明日入寺，果见有钟一口挂大银杏树上。因命山僧移置他所。盖树老成精，理所固然也。 During his tenure as governor of Shaanxi, Mr. Bi Qiu Fan once ascended Mount Hua, stayed in a monastery, and had a dream where someone tall and noble appeared, dressed in ancient attire and bowed to him, saying, "I have lived in this mountain for a thousand years. Recently, a monk hung a large copper bell on my left arm, which greatly troubled me. Please help me resolve this." The next day, when he entered the temple, he indeed saw a bell hanging on a large ginkgo tree. He then ordered the mountain monk to move it to another place. It is said that old trees can become spirits, so this is a natural thing. 狮子 元魏时，波斯国献狮子，为万俟丑奴所获。丑奴破，始达京师。庄帝谓侍中李彧曰：“朕闻虎见狮必伏，可觅试之。”于是诏近山郡县捕虎以送。巩县山阳并送二虎一豹。帝在华林园观之，于是虎见狮子，并皆瞑目不敢动。园中素有一盲熊，性甚驯，帝令取试之。熊至，闻狮子气，惊怖跳跃，曳锁而走。帝大笑。 During the Northern Wei Dynasty, the Persian kingdom presented lions to the court, which were captured by Wan Qi Chounu. After Wan Qi Chounu's defeat, the lions finally reached the capital. Emperor Yuan told the high-ranking official Li Yu, "I have heard that tigers see lions and must submit. Can we test this?" He then ordered nearby mountain counties to catch tigers and send them. Gong County and Shan Yang both sent two tigers and one leopard. The emperor watched in the Hua Lin Garden, and when the tigers saw the lions, they all closed their eyes and did not move. There was a blind bear in the garden, which was very tame. The emperor ordered it to be tested. When the bear arrived, it was terrified by the lion's presence, jumped around, and dragged its chain away. The emperor laughed. 又，国朝康熙间，西域贡狮子二，形如图画。后口外打围遇两罴，人不能胜，召狮子搏得之。老狮力尽而毙，小狮继亦逸去，其罴皮实之以草，置雍和宫，悬牌腰间，一重一千三百余斤，一重八百余斤。是熊之与罴，勇怯又悬殊矣。 During the Kangxi period of the Qing Dynasty, two lions were presented by the Western Regions, and their shapes were like paintings. Later, during a hunt outside the city, they encountered two bears, which people could not defeat. They summoned the lions to fight and defeat them. The old lion died of exhaustion, and the young lion also escaped. Their bear skins were stuffed with grass and placed in Yonghe Temple, where a plaque was hung on their waist. One weighed over 1300 pounds, and the other weighed over 800 pounds. Thus, the courage and cowardice of bears and bears differ greatly.

According to *Erya: Explanation of Animals*, "Suan Ni eats tigers and leopards." The note states, "That is, the lion." *Zhengyi* cites *Shuowen Jiezi*, which says, "Xiao, the lion." *Da Ya: Chang Wu* states, "As fierce as a roaring tiger." Although the tiger is mentioned alongside the lion, in reality, the tiger's strength is formidable, but how could it compare to a lion? However, *Classic of Curious Facts* also records that during the reign of Emperor Wei Wu, who was attacking Modu, he passed through White Wolf Mountain and encountered a lion. He sent a man to fight it, causing many casualties. Suddenly, an animal emerged from the forest, as large as a weasel, and stood up on the cart yoke. As the lion approached, this animal leaped up, stood on the lion's head, and killed it. When they reached thirty miles from Luoyang, all chickens and dogs cowered, and none barked or crowed. It is unknown what this creature was. It also shows that even a creature as fierce as a lion can be controlled by a stronger one. Therefore, all those in the world who boast of their courage are no different from pigs from Liao Dong. Flattery and Imitation During the reign of Emperor Qianlong, a certain magistrate of Guangping was about to be presented to the emperor. He met Grand Secretary He Gong in the ante-chamber of the palace. The magistrate bowed, and Grand Secretary He Gong helped him up. The magistrate insisted on bowing again, and in the process, he accidentally pulled the prayer beads off Grand Secretary He Gong, scattering them like rain on the ground. Grand Secretary He Gong was startled. Thinking that there must be a way to vent his anger, he laughed and asked the magistrate's name, remembering it in his heart. A few days later, the emperor summoned the magistrate for an audience. After the audience, the position of magistrate of Cizhou became vacant, and the emperor asked who could fill the position. In a hurry, all of Grand Secretary He Gong's close associates could not recall anyone, so he had to mention the name of the magistrate. The emperor agreed to his suggestion and immediately ordered the magistrate to be appointed. This is also the effect of flattery.

**The Drunken Monk** At the beginning of the dynasty, the Zhou family of Floating Stone had three monks: Tongcheng, who pretended to be mad until death, known as the mad monk; Sinan, who drowned in drink until death, known as the drunken monk; and Shunde, who endured hardship and abstained from city life until death, known as the wild monk. Their spirits were always extraordinary, especially not like Sinan. Sinan's given name was Yuanmao, his courtesy name was Zhuchu, and he was the grandson of Wenmu Gong Yingbin. Through the influence of Wenmu Gong, he was promoted through the ranks to become the Director of the Office of Affairs in Nanjing and the Chief Clerk of the Department of Agriculture. He returned from a mission to Shu and governed Guizhou. During the national crisis, the master was unrestrained and pleased with himself. Originally, he thought of placing himself in the imperial library through his family's influence, but when he received the favor of his family's influence, it was not his preference. The Inspector General Liao Daxing comforted him by saying, "Family influence is not enough to humble a person; it is just that people humiliate themselves. Was Li Wei Gong not promoted from here?" The master was greatly pleased. When the state of Jiangdong was established, Dongguan (a respectful title for Qian) invited him, and his old friend Xu Jin Yi Qirui also invited him. The master, who was in mourning for a family tragedy, firmly declined, but he did not hesitate to spend his family's resources to contribute to the war effort. In the sixth month of the year Bingshen, his family reported that the city of Jiang had been lost. The master wept bitterly and drowned himself in the water, but was rescued to avoid death.

The master used to be good at drinking. He shaved his head and entered Ganting Mountain, where he drank even more daily. Soon, he no longer liked drinking alone. He called the mountain monks to drink with him, regardless of whether they could drink or not, forcing them to drink from night until dawn. The monks were troubled by this and fled or hid. Then he called the woodcutters to drink with him. The woodcutters, as the day was ending, kneeled repeatedly and begged to leave. Having no one to share it with, he then made his attendant drink. Soon the attendant was drunk and lying down, so he called upon the moon to drink with him. When the moon set, he called upon the clouds to drink with him. Finding it difficult to find a drinking companion, he finally returned to the Branch-Hidden Pavilion in the west of the city. Every morning, he called his disciples to drink with him. When the disciples left, he called others. If those people went elsewhere, he carried wine to where they went; if he did not meet them, he seized a passerby and drank with him. As a result, whenever the locals in Floating Stone saw him, they would gather and hide. Having no choice, he drank alone. Accumulated drinking and falling ill, countless people tried to persuade him to stop drinking. Most of them argued that the master had no heir, and the master would pour a large cup of wine over their heads, or else open his eyes and not respond. A like-minded person advised him, saying, "Don't you think about nurturing your body to wait for the right moment?" The master was startled and did not drink for three days, but soon resumed drinking as before.

The Master, though ensnared by wine, would immediately rise to receive rivers and lakes martial guests who came to him with matters to entrust. He gave all he had, fearing it would not be enough. Because of this, he lost his family. In the year Gengyin, he vomited blood that could not be stopped, and he finally died at the age of forty. Gongren Yu Shi also died from grief shortly after. Before, the former Chief Doctor Wang Gong Yushu mourned him, saying, "De Lin's stubbornly wild and unrestrained in the wine, almost did not know what the world outside was, what world. Without this and did not know where to put himself. An ancient poem says, 'Wine does not last all night, matters fill the heart at dawn.' De Lin probably hoped to be in a state of no waking to preserve himself." A fellow scholar and high official Gao Guo Qi eulogized him, saying, "Knowing the strong, guarding the weak, being the valley of the world; knowing the white, guarding the black, being the valley of the world. De Lin did not hear, so he sacrificed himself. Alas!" (The matter is recorded in *Jiaqi Pavilion Collection*.)

The Historian said: When the country was broken and the king died, De Lin sought death but could not find it, and ultimately died from drinking day and night. Compared to Xin Ling's fine wine and women, his aspirations are even more pitiable. I have heard that during the Qianlong era, there were three strange people in Chengdu. One of them was called Laughing Monk, who did not speak to people and only laughed foolishly. He liked smoking and asked people for it. Those who gave him cigarettes were considered to have many auspicious things, so people competed to give him cigarettes, and some even refused to give him. He lived in Baoguang Temple, where the temple monks disliked his laziness and delayed his meals. Sometimes, he would eat before dawn, and when he lifted his chopsticks, Laughing Monk would be there. A neighbor named Zhang the tailor knew he was not an ordinary person and would always follow him. One day, Laughing Monk told Zhang, "You must not be idle through the cold and hot seasons. Wait for six years, and you will surely have something you want. But I am lazy and cannot bear to be a teacher. There is a Crazy Xu in the East Cave Gate here, who can be your teacher. I will take you to him." They went together. Just then, Crazy Xu was roasting dead mice over a fire and drinking white vinegar.

A monk was seen from afar, and someone scolded and laughed at him, saying, "You cannot tolerate being a teacher, and why bother dragging others along?" The laughing monk laughed uncontrollably. At the time, the north wind was strong, and the cold outside the city gates was especially severe. The laughing monk and the madman were barefoot and bareheaded, yet they seemed completely at ease. By midnight, the madman took off his tattered robe and gave it to Zhang, saying, "Wear this to ward off the cold." Zhang put it on, and though it was neither cotton nor silk, it was strangely warm and fragrant. From then on, Zhang followed the madman and would not leave him. After several years, the two of them together went to visit the laughing monk. The monk greeted them with laughter, saying, "Have you two come? Good! Good!" He embraced Zhang's neck and laughed wildly. His voice was like that of a phoenix, making one's heart and soul tremble. The madman scolded from the side, saying, "Simple monk, you laughed until today and still think it's not enough?" The monk prostrated in reverence and said, "I know I have sinned. But the old monk will not die, and laughing cannot be stopped." Struggling to hold back laughter, he climbed onto the bed, sat cross-legged, and died. Slowly laughing, he looked at Zhang and said, "You can go now." They held hands and walked out the door, and suddenly disappeared. Immortal, indeed! Some say that the laughing monk grew up in peaceful times and died in joy, which cannot be compared to those born in times of chaos. However, considering his final words, how can one know that there was no time for a long song to mourn within them? This monk's obsession with laughter is probably like the drunk monk's obsession with drinking, not in the drinking itself, but in the deeper emotions. Thus, his laughter can also be passed down.

In the Ma Jun Alley of the city, there lived Zheng Sheng, whose given name was Fuliang. He was the grandson of Warm Field Mr., a respected scholar or official. From a young age, Zheng Sheng was exceptionally intelligent, able to read and memorize texts at a glance. He was a straightforward and strong-willed man, fond of drinking, skilled in medicine, and widely read in many books. However, when he drank, he would invite others to join him, but they would not come. On one occasion, he beat his maid to the point of unconsciousness, and she only revived afterward. His wife then forbade him from drinking. Zheng Sheng had no recourse but to sit by the door every day, waiting for his relatives and friends to pass by, inviting them in to drink together. Sometimes, they would claim to have something to do and refuse to enter, often leading Zheng Sheng to kneel and cry uncontrollably. Later, his wife realized this was Zheng Sheng's plan, so when guests came, she would take a stick to chase them away. One guest was once beaten and injured his thigh. As a result, anyone passing through the Ma Jun Alley would warn each other to take a detour and not pass through his door. It is truly laughable, yet the skilled craftsman suffers deeply. Xiang Shu, the Grand Secretary During the twelfth year of the Yongle era, the crown prince sent an envoy to welcome the emperor, but the envoy was delayed, and the emperor became angry. Huang Huai arrived and was imprisoned. When Yang Shiqi and Jin Wen arrived, the emperor became even angrier, saying, "Who is Jin Wen, and how did he get to serve the crown prince?" They were sent to the judicial department for interrogation, and Yang Pu was implicated. They were detained in the Jin Yi prison for ten years, during which they read the classics, histories, and works of various schools of thought several times. When Renzong ascended the throne, he released Yang Pu. Upon release, Yang Pu cried at the funeral, and could not get up. The emperor also cried, and appointed Yang Pu as a Hanlin scholar, entering the cabinet to handle important affairs, and later promoted him to Grand Sacrificial Minister, still serving as a scholar. He secretly lamented that the meeting of the emperor and his ministers was no different from that of family and father.

According to tradition, Minister Qian Xiangshu was sent on a mission to a vassal state during the late years of Emperor Yongzheng's reign. Upon his return, it was already the early years of Emperor Qianlong's reign. The emperor asked about matters during the late emperor's time, and Minister Qian could not help but burst into tears, and the emperor also wept. From then on, Minister Qian gained the emperor's favor and was promoted to Vice Minister. Later, he and Mr. Shen Guiyu were both recognized by Emperor Qianlong for their poetry. The emperor once said, "The two elders are the great figures of Jiangsu and Zhejiang." The emperor's favor for Minister Qian was no different from that of Emperor Renzong for Yang Pu. In the *Biography of Yang Pu*: When Emperor Yingzong first ascended the throne, Yang Pu later entered the Grand Council. The Grand Empress Dowager presided over the court, and one day she sat in a private hall with the emperor standing to her west. She summoned Shishi, Rong, Pu, and the Grand Secretary Hu Ying, instructing them, "You are all senior officials, and the young successor ruler needs your support. May you work together to ensure the stability of the state." She then called Yang Pu forward and said, "The late emperor often expressed sorrow and concern for your loyalty, and I did not expect to see you again today." Yang Pu wept, and the empress dowager also wept, and those around them were all moved to tears. Initially, when Emperor Renzong was still a crown prince, many officials and ministers were imprisoned due to slander. Yang Pu and Huai were imprisoned for ten years, and they nearly died several times. Emperor Renzong often spoke of the matters of the crown prince's time in the palace and wept bitterly. Hence, the empress dowager mentioned him.

The Empress Dowager then looked at the emperor and said, "These five ministers were appointed by the previous dynasty to assist future generations. The myriad affairs of the emperor should be discussed with these five ministers." Reading these words, one can imagine the close relationship between ruler and minister during times of hardship, a bond that is difficult to forget. One can also see how, in the court, sage and virtuous people met, like family and father-son, no different from the time when Empress Dowager Cao extinguished the golden lotus candle and sent Su Dongpo back to his residence. Quan Quan Quan Quan was born in Baixiang. He was a scholar in the county. Being the eighth son, people called him Quan Ba. His family originally dealt in trade. After his father's death, Quan Quan did not engage in production, but loved reading and was willing to help others in their urgent needs, which led to the gradual decline of the family. He worked as a hired scribe to make a living, but was not always paid, and eventually died of poverty and hunger. He left behind one concubine and one son and one daughter. His son was named Chunlin and was still young, and there was no money for his burial. His friend Zhu Xuhou, a man of high spirits and a lover of reading and swordsmanship, was a poor acquaintance of Quan Quan. Upon hearing the news, Zhu Xuhou went to see the funeral and sought help from the family, but no one responded. Filled with grief and anger, Zhu returned home, took off his hairpins and searched through his boxes, even selling his musical instruments and books, and relied on his mother to handle the funeral arrangements. The mother and her two children were left crying and standing against the wall, and Zhu could no longer attend to them.

A servant named Jin Zhong, simple and naive, has always been loyal to his master. When the household was in dire straits, he pitied the young and helpless, and hesitated to leave. He often faced hunger, but he sold shoes and wove mats to provide for the family, even starving, he did not stop. The whole family depended on him for survival. However, his wife was still young, and since her birth, she had not stopped using cosmetics, and was not accustomed to hardship. In the town, there was a wealthy young man named Pan, who was a good-for-nothing and loved to pursue women. When his wife owed rent to the master and planned to move, Pan was attracted to her beauty and offered her a place to live near his house. Zhu had sneakedly observed the situation and hurriedly went to warn her not to accept, but she did not listen. From then on, Zhu began to avoid the situation. Later, when the daughter grew older, Pan also became involved with her. As the scandal gradually became known, the servant informed Pan's wife about the situation, warning her to stop Pan from coming, and threatened to kill him if he did. At the same time, Zhu also heard the rumors spreading and called his nephew Chun Lin to ask if he knew about his mother's actions. Chun Lin angrily struck the table and said, "Pan is my enemy! If not for my uncle's call, I would have gone to my uncle to report this and repay this grudge. Even if I die, I still hope to collect my bones!" He then knelt and asked for his uncle's sword to avenge his mother. Zhu said, "You are not yet old enough. If you fail, my father's spirit will be disappointed. Your father devoted his life to his friends, and at the end, he entrusted your mother and you to me. His words still echo in my ears, how can I sit idly by?" Chun Lin left, crying.

After several days, the deceased was suddenly killed by someone, and his body was abandoned in the fields. His wife recalled the words of the servant and reported to the authorities based on this. The county magistrate came to examine the body, but the head was not found. He questioned the servant, who said he did not know. Then he hurriedly summoned the concubine and questioned her. She confessed that she had never been unfaithful to the deceased and asked how she would know anything else. The magistrate ordered her to be tortured, and the concubine originally suspected the servant because he had often advised her to change her ways. She had long suspected that the servant was the killer, and at this point, she confessed the truth and recounted the servant's previous advice to prove her point. The magistrate then summoned the servant and used torture to question him, applying all five tortures. The servant also falsely confessed. When asked where the head was, he replied that at the time he had cooked it to sacrifice to the deceased's tomb, and after the sacrifice, he fed it to the dogs. Then the magistrate released the concubine and imprisoned the servant. Shortly after, the concubine went home and was killed by someone. The magistrate learned that the servant's son was usually fierce and domineering in the village and suspected that he was acting on the servant's orders. He then arrested the son and fabricated evidence to convict him. At the time, the magistrate had already accepted bribes from Pan and convicted them of killing each other out of jealousy. He sentenced the servant and his son to death, finalized the case, and reported it.

Spring Rain heard the news and ran to the county hall, weeping and taking full responsibility, in order to clear the name of the accused. The magistrate doubted his youth and turned to interrogating the instigator, threatening him by saying, "If you kill your own mother, are you not afraid of the death penalty?" Spring Rain replied, "To avenge my father's death is more honorable than living, even if it means dying." The magistrate was furious and imprisoned Spring Rain, intending to execute him along with the accused. That night, as he was about to sleep, he suddenly heard a very loud sound in front of the tent. He lit a candle and saw a dagger stuck in the case, shining like snow and about to move. Beside it was a piece of paper that read, "The one who previously killed the adulterer and the adulteress did so to avenge my dead friend. Now you, for five hundred gold, have falsely accused and killed three filial and righteous people. Am I not capable of killing you?" and so on.

He was ordered to read, and his face turned as pale as earth. Immediately, he was released, and three people were freed from prison. The next day, he went to report to the higher authority with a dagger and a book. The higher authority praised the righteousness of killing and released him without questioning. Chunlin was rewarded with fifty gold coins to commend his filial piety. He was ordered to falsely report obtaining stolen goods and was dismissed from office. At the time, Chunlin was only sixteen years old. Zhou Lannasi, a monk at the Yuanyi Temple in our town, loved drinking and did not adhere to minor details. When young, he once entered a brothel, which caused his nose to rot. Later, he felt remorse and voluntarily took vows to become a Buddhist disciple. He was upright and honest, never lying, and people trusted him because of this. Seeing the main hall collapsing, he beat the drum to raise funds for repairs. People trusted him for his honesty and rushed to donate. Though he has devoted himself to Buddhism, he still cannot abstain from drinking. He drinks every day and gets drunk every time. He often goes to market stalls, stands by the stove, and sings loudly. Those who listen around secretly cover their mouths and laugh. Sometimes he walks slowly through the streets, singing "The Great River Flows East." Children clap and laugh, follow in groups, and also show disdain. Master Zhengfa in the village (commonly known as Tang Xuanhong) highly respects him, saying, "This is a reincarnated Arhat." Zhou lives a floating life in the world and is already over seventy years old.

One day, Zhou Lanbi wished to invite more than a hundred people he frequently interacted with to return to the spiritual path. His disciples refused, saying, "Such guidance is rare among good teachers; how can it be found among those who indulge in meat and drink?" He insisted repeatedly, and they finally agreed to invite the guests. The guests laughed and said, "Is Zhou Lanbi going to achieve enlightenment too?" On the appointed day, everyone gathered, and Zhou met with them as if they were old friends. At noon, he bathed and changed his clothes, burned incense in the halls and corridors, paid respects to all the Buddhas, and then returned to the main hall. He took a chair made of a type of vine and sat cross-legged facing south, waving goodbye to the crowd. Observing him, it was clear that his eyes had closed. The crowd was making offerings and praising him when suddenly he opened his eyes and shouted, "There is still a pot of cooked meat in the kitchen; bring it so we can finish eating it." His attendant brought the meat, and he ate it all with great pleasure, not letting go until he passed away.

The Historian says: I once read the "Biography of the Drunken Woman" and admired the eccentricity of Daoji as something beyond emulation. When all are drunk, how can one not carry a spade and follow along to coexist in this world? When all are blind, without carrying wood and lifting coffins, who can display their supernatural abilities and serve as the morning bell to awaken others? Yet, without a good friend or teacher like the Blind Hall Monk, even though the Buddhist path is vast, who could accommodate such a person? If Zhou Lannose knew this meaning, would he also understand it? Therefore, being drunk in the streets and singing wildly in the market, it means that those who practice long-term vegetarianism and devotion to Buddha, discuss scriptures and recite verses openly, yet secretly indulge in fragrant incense and stolen drinks, are not worthy of being counted among them, and it is better to sing and drink with children for joy. The records describe this person as upright and straightforward, indeed, he does not愧to his upright and straightforward nature! Otherwise, if one were to seek Arhats solely in alcohol and meat, then Arhats would already be found everywhere in the world. Pan Lantou Pan Lantou was a Taoist priest from the northeast of the town at the Qianzhu Temple. He could summon rain and wind, travel to Puchuan, and once played the moon with someone. When the person was disrespectful to Pan, Pan drew a moon on the wall and stuck a piece of paper over it, causing the moon to become obscured by clouds. The person asked Pan to remove the paper, and the moon remained bright as before. One day, he summoned a heavenly deity, but nothing happened. The deity marked his head with a red pen, causing it to rot. People called him Pan Lantou. (See "Tongxiang County Gazetteer") According to "Wuqing Literature": "There is no such person in this temple according to the tradition of master-disciple succession." However, Tang Zhi Feng's "Inscription on the Dongyue Temple in Qianxi Village" states: "He was associated with this temple." It is unclear which account is correct.

**Buttock Itch** In the village of Yaozhuang, Gu Wenhǔ, a man of noble lineage, was accustomed to a life of luxury and indulgence. One day, he ordered his family to hold bamboo strips and had him undress to receive twenty strokes. Over time, this became a habit, and his family grew tired of it. If the strokes were too light, he would scold them, and sometimes he would even strike them back, only finding satisfaction when the strokes were heavy. After several years, he gradually felt pain and stopped. A doctor, hearing of this, said, "His excessive consumption of spicy and stimulating foods has caused heat toxins to attack internally, resulting in an unusual itch. The beating dispersed the heat toxins and prevented them from ascending; otherwise, he would have developed gangrene on his back and died." I, however, do not agree. He was indulging in pleasures, eating all day without using his mind, and thus had no way to release his energy, leading to the heat toxins descending and causing an unbearable itch. This was not due to his excessive consumption of spicy foods. Therefore, those who enjoy a life of luxury today, like animals in a pen, should be given this stick. **Grass Hut Monk** Xú Rǔlián from Haichang was strong and skilled in martial arts. One day, I met him at the home of Shěn Jūn in Shānpíng and asked, "Do you enjoy martial arts? Have you ever encountered extraordinary people?" Rǔlián replied, "In the past, I accompanied others to visit the Grass Hut Monk by the shores of Taihu Lake. Seeing his imposing appearance, I knew he was no ordinary person. When I discussed martial arts with him, my heart was moved, and I did not even realize I was sweating. I then asked, 'With your talent and skills, you should be serving the state, why do you mingle with Buddhist monks? Are you hiding something and escaping?'"

The monk sighed and said, "This is my fate. In the past, I served as a guard in the inner court, following the imperial command to accompany General [name] on a campaign against the Miao people. One day, our large force advanced together, and we suddenly encountered enemy spears. I stepped forward to fight, and after a brief struggle, the enemy was cornered and abandoned their horses, fleeing into the forest. I pursued them with all my strength, but the dense underbrush made it impossible to use my weapons effectively. I then abandoned my sword and fought hand-to-hand. The enemy's fists were extremely skilled, and they resisted and fled. We crossed dozens of hills until we reached a sheer cliff, where I strangled his throat and pushed him. He also forcefully grasped my arm and dragged me into a large ravine. I took advantage of the moment to draw out my dagger and stabbed him, decapitating him. At that time, I was exhausted, so I jumped out to rest briefly. Climbing a hill, I looked around and saw chaotic clouds swirling together, thousands of trees blocking the sky, with no sign of human presence. That place was about fifty miles away from the main army. I found a way back, returned to the central camp, presented the head to report, and then withdrew. I thought to myself that no one's achievements surpassed mine. Someone in the army who knew the situation secretly told me, 'Eliminating the leader is not a small achievement. But you are in danger. The general said you died in battle, had already listed your name, and submitted a report. Your achievements have been taken by someone else. If you don't leave, you will be in danger.' I believed this to be true and dared not stay, so I fled at night. Since then, I have always lived in the wilderness of mountains and thickets. Silently reflecting, I realized that high achievements without reward instead led to disaster—it is fate. Thus, I wandered beyond the world, spending the rest of my life there. How could it be that becoming a Buddhist monk allowed me to retreat to this extent?"

Ruolian asked to observe his skills, and the monk said, "It is not easy for you to come here; try to see it." Thus, he saw the two shoulders moving in coordination, and the upper part of his body was extended by six to seven inches. He asked to shorten it, and the lower part was also shortened by the same amount. Once settled, he lifted his clothes and descended the stairs. In the courtyard, there was a tree with a girth of ten spans. When he shook it, the branches and leaves all swayed. Those who were proud of their own talents and skills were all amazed, speechless, and lost. The monk said, "This is a practice of qi cultivation. You are not qualified to discuss this." The monk's name is not known, as he lived in a simple hermitage, so people called him the Grass Hermitage Monk.

Yang Yu heard this and sighed, saying, "The world is not without extraordinary and heroic talents, yet they often fail to find their place. If the monk is not an exception among the common people, how strange it is that his talents are so extraordinary yet his fate is so odd! Yet he was able to discard like a shoe and escape harm, his ability to see the signs and act wisely is particularly beyond reach. Yet his high achievements were taken away, his name and surname are not passed down, lost in the lakes, mountains, and wilds, alas!" This biography, I copied from *The Easy Peace Studio Collection* in the year Gui Chou. The original version is lively in style but somewhat loose in structure and somewhat redundant in sequence, so I added and deleted several sentences. Reading it now, one still feels the vitality of the paper, and the monk of the thatched hut is as if he lives on. Throughout history, there are countless cases of those with great achievements who are not rewarded and are instead envied and slandered, reading this one cannot help but weep for the annals of history! On the day before the Mid-Autumn Festival of Xin Chou, I re-read this biography at the Jade Village Thatched Hall. Counting back, it has been ten years of cold and heat. I, the Fool, have hair as white as snow, and I too am plagued by aging and illness, no longer enjoying the joy of writing with a brush and ink as I did before. How can one not sigh three times!

A Man Named Fan The Man from Siming, whose family had long been engaged in reading and learning, rose to prominence when he was appointed to office through the imperial examination system (甲榜) and became wealthy by engaging in the trade of pawnshops. He had two sons, whom he taught to read, and after several years, both passed the local examination (游泮). However, they failed the higher-level exams multiple times. His younger son, who was still young and vibrant, was fond of debauchery and immoral pursuits (狭邪游), and also addicted to gambling. He spent day and night engaging in debauchery with a few disreputable companions. As a result, the elder and more upright scholars he had known treated him as an enemy or rival. Consequently, the family's fortunes suddenly declined, and all their assets were sold off. With no other choice, he borrowed money from relatives and friends. Citing an example, he was appointed as an assistant governor (别驾) and assigned to Guangdong. After serving in this position for one year, he captured seven individuals involved in illegal activities with foreigners (通夷匪), all of whom were leaders of criminal gangs. He interrogated them thoroughly and temporarily detained them. The gang offered him 3,000 gold pieces to request their release. The younger son, upon seeing the gold, was tempted and accepted it. He then made a request for their release, but it was repeatedly rejected. The governor-general personally interrogated them strictly, and all were executed. The younger son had no recourse, but every time he thought about the events, he experienced heart palpitations for several days.

One day, slightly intoxicated, he sat in the upper room, and the servant was massaging his back. A four-year-old child was playing by his side. Just as the wet nurse brought another child, the child was just asking to be held. Suddenly, a person rushed in front of him, with fierce eyes and a curly beard, an urgent and fierce demeanor, drawing a sharp blade like snow, stabbing directly at his head, killing both children, and also hitting the wet nurse. The servant let out a loud cry. When people arrived, the person had already disappeared. Questioning the gatekeeper, the gatekeeper also did not know where the person had entered from. Together, they reported to the high officials, drawing a portrait to apprehend the culprit. But throughout the year, the true culprit was not caught. So the entire family returned south, carrying the coffin to the gate of the village. The procession was grand and impressive, the whole area covered in white mourning clothes, still extending beyond a mile. But those who knew the truth said that the coffin contained only a headless ghost. Before this, when the official was about to take up his post, afraid that he would not be familiar with the formalities during court appearances, he invited some troublemakers to his home and had them take turns practicing. When it was his turn, after bowing and standing up, suddenly he looked back and saw no head. At noon, everyone saw it, and they were greatly startled. Those who understood knew it was an omen of misfortune. When the coffin arrived at the gate, his brother was just setting up a banquet in honor of their mother, calling in entertainers to assist with the drinking. After several performances, three people appeared wearing the attire of the current dynasty, addressing each other as brothers. A bystander asked for their names, and the eldest said his name was Fan Chi. The bystander asked who he had taken, and he said Confucius. The second said his name was Fan Kuai, and the bystander asked who he had taken, and he said Han Gaozu (Emperor Gao of Han). The youngest said his name was Fan Nao, and the bystander asked who he had taken, and he said himself. Everyone laughed uproariously. His brother suddenly remembered the earlier incident and realized that there was a hint of criticism. He cried bitterly and ended the banquet.

During the Jiaqing era, the Lin Qing Rebellion erupted on a day when the sky was clear and sunny. When the rebellion began, several bandits had already scaled the palace walls, and the imperial guards were hastily assembling but not yet fully gathered. The bandits moved from the kitchen area to the west and then northward. The emperor's second son (who later became the reigning emperor) fired a musket at them, killing one bandit. Those who arrived later held white flags to command, and he fired again, killing another. Prince Mian Zhi, a member of the royal family, also fired at the bandits, killing yet another. The emperor's son then galloped to the Western Factory on the Western Long Street, where he led a group of eunuchs under the command of Chang Yonggui to attack the bandits. As the day was drawing to a close, the bandits' strength gradually waned. They were about to set fire to the palace when suddenly it rained heavily and thunderbolts struck. Two bandits were killed by the lightning and fell into the imperial river of the Wu Ying Hall. In the lightning, they vaguely saw Guan Di, the God of War, seated majestically at the Meridian Gate. The bandits trembled with fear and could not flee, and all were captured. It was said that the bandits had accomplices in all the provinces. Earlier, the Qing court had sent spies to Jiangxi, where they arranged with the bandits' accomplices to attack on a fixed date. These bandits were tired and took a short rest at the foot of a mountain. Nearby, a Taoist priest breathed on them, and they fell asleep. When they woke up, the Taoist priest had disappeared. When their accomplices received the message, the fixed date was set for the ninth month. By that time, the Qing had already suppressed the rebellion. The governor and provincial administrator of Jiangxi also captured the bandits' accomplices and surrendered them. When they were brought to prison, the Qing court asked them why they had been delayed. They replied that the fixed date had not arrived, so they dared not act rashly. When asked about the spies, they mentioned the Taoist priest they had encountered. The Qing court then released the spies and sent them to guide the search for the Taoist priest in Jiangxi, but they could not find him. They happened to rest at the temple of Xu Zhenjun, a Taoist deity, and saw a statue that looked exactly like the Taoist priest they had met. They reported this to the court, and the deity was posthumously enfeoffed. With the sage ruler on the throne, the hundred gods manifest their power, and this principle is indeed not false.

A Daoist Practitioner from Mount Mao Jin Mingru, a man from Hangzhou, lost his first wife and subsequently married a woman surnamed Yu, who was the sister of Yu Gong, a magistrate in Yuqian. She was known for her fierce and violent temper. Not yet a full month after the marriage, Jin Mingru habitually stayed out overnight. One day, a quarrel erupted between the husband and wife, and the wife swallowed a gold hairpin from her head. Soon, mucus choked her chest, causing her to lose consciousness. The whole family was in panic and at a loss. Suddenly, a Daoist practitioner appeared at the door and said to the gatekeeper, "Your master is in urgent danger, and I have divined this by observing the atmospheric phenomena." The gatekeeper was shocked and asked, "Master, can you save her?" The Daoist practitioner replied, "I am here for this very reason. Report to your master immediately; if we delay, it will be too late." He then entered the house with the gatekeeper, and the whole family was greatly relieved. The family also asked how much they should pay, and the Daoist practitioner said, "As practitioners of the Dao, we uphold compassion as our foundation. We do not covet wealth. Quickly prepare a bowl of clean water." When the water was brought, the Daoist practitioner wrote a talisman with a sword-like gesture and gave it to the patient to swallow. Soon, the wife's chest became slightly more comfortable, and the family all bowed in gratitude. The Daoist practitioner smiled and said, "Not yet. Earlier, her life hung in the balance, but now the object has entered her intestines. Soon, she will die from bloating and tearing of the intestines. I am a celestial being from the Jade Peak of Mount Mao, and I am raising funds outside because our temple has collapsed. If you can give me three thousand gold pieces, your wife can be saved. Otherwise, I must take my leave."

The Taoist priest offered 800 taels of silver, saying, "There is no one poorer than a salt merchant. If you promise me 800 taels of silver, you may place the exact amount at the foot of the steps, so that it can be taken away when the matter is completed." The crowd was surprised by the contradiction in his words, but they temporarily accepted his proposal to see what would happen. The Taoist priest then wrote three talismans on yellow paper, had them burned, and poured the ashes over the woman. He also ordered a chamber pot to be quickly prepared nearby and said, "The malevolent star is about to appear." Soon after, the woman passed blood in the bucket, more than a standard measure, and a golden hairpin shone brightly in the blood. The Taoist priest said, "This is cursed gold; if not removed, it will surely cause further harm. It should be removed and cast into an image of a heavenly general to ward off evil for your family." He commanded the gold to be taken out, washed, and placed in his sleeve. Slowly, he took a small bag from his waist, about seven inches long, and blew air into it for a short while. He then slowly put the money into the bag, and in a moment, it was all gone, without feeling cramped. He tied the bag to his waist, looked at the gold, and said, "I, the humble Taoist, have disturbed you today." He raised his hand to express gratitude and then left.

The historian says: Is the Daoist of Mount Mao truly a man of virtue? At first, he could predict misfortune by observing the qi; later, he could resolve calamities by writing talismans; and finally, he could obtain money. With such divine power, he nearly transformed the small into the vast, like a mustard seed containing a universe.
