Yet, when asked what he desired, he said, "We take compassion as our foundation and do not covet wealth," but once the gold had entered his belly, he then demanded a heavy interest. Moreover, he called the gold "malevolent" and claimed he must carry and cast heavenly generals to ward it off. Is there anyone in the world who would search for gold in filth to cast a divine image? He said, "No one is poorer than salt merchants." It seems the gold had always guarded his wealth as if his life depended on it, and he could not even part with a single hair for his relatives in times of need. Thus, the Daoist displayed this神通 to warn him of his stinginess. Otherwise, how could his words be so contradictory and absurd? Some say: Is this Daoist perhaps a transformation of the Three Mow, playing in the human world? This is uncertain. In Chengdu, when Zhang Chuan Shan, the magistrate, was in office, a patrol inspector came to report. Zhang said, "My lord has had a hard journey, but your demeanor is quite good." The inspector misunderstood his meaning and, stroking his beard, half-kneeled and said, "Your humble servant has been favored by your lord's grace and often thinks of repaying it. Alas, with age, I have kept this beard, and I cannot devote myself entirely to repayment." Zhang laughed heartily and dismissed him.

#### 梁山州 Fu Hai Fan, while governing Zhejiang, would often discuss poetry and prose with his colleagues during his leisure time. When the governor of Hangzhou was vacant, he appointed a county magistrate to act as the governor. One day, in the morning assembly, Fu Hai Fan asked the acting governor about the matter of Liangshan State. The acting governor replied, "Under my jurisdiction, there is only Hai Ning State, no Liangshan State. Please check the *Jinshenlu* (Register of Officials)." Fu Hai Fan laughed heartily and left. #### 诗嘲 Jiang Taoci said: There was a man surnamed Wang, who was moderately well-off and had donated to obtain a ninth-grade official rank. He liked to boast about his family background. Whenever he saw a painting by Shi Gu, he would say, "This is the great-great-grandfather of my second branch of the family." Whenever he saw a fan with a calligraphy by Meng Lou, he would say, "This is my cousin who is not yet out of mourning." Whenever he saw a Wang surname official, he would claim they were of the same clan. His colleagues all secretly laughed at him. Later, he was assigned to Jiangxi, where Bai Tian Yuan, a high-ranking official known for his wit, was the provincial governor. One day, the officials came to pay respects, and Yuan laughed and said to them, "I have some crude words, I want to give to Wang Zuo Tang, let me recite them for you." At the time, Wang was also present. Yuan recited: "All three Wangs come from the same family, whether you pull them east or west. The one on the left of Tai Chang is called the same clan (Lingye), the one in Jiangnan is called Meng Hua (at that time, there was a Jiangnan provincial governor, also surnamed Wang). My younger brother sent silk from Guangdong, my elder brother sent tea from Guizhou. If passing by the Lingguan Temple, an uncle from five hundred years ago." Everyone present burst out laughing.

Tao Zhi Jun Shu's Anecdotes When Tao Zhi Jun Shu had not yet succeeded in his examination, his family was extremely poor, and he supported himself by teaching students. He was a man of great ambition, loving to drink and skilled at gambling, and despite having no stored grain at home, he did not care. Later, as the end of the year approached, his wife Cui wept and said to him, "We are so poor, I truly cannot continue to starve together with you. For your sake, selling myself can also help us get through the year. Otherwise, I wish you would grant me a divorce certificate, allowing me to seek another way to live." The husband replied, "How shallow your understanding is! I have not yet met my great fortune. The diviner said my fate would reach the first rank. Let us wait patiently; do not worry about wealth and status." The wife said, "If you truly have such great fortune, there will be someone to share it with you. I dare not think of such a thing. I will take my leave of you and listen for good news from you." The husband, unable to refuse, wrote a divorce certificate for her. Meanwhile, a baker in the same neighborhood was planning to marry a wife. Upon receiving the divorce certificate, the wife happily left with him. After the divorce, the husband became even more aimless.

At first, there was a Taoist priest who had always been good friends with a man named Gong. Gong often stayed at the temple during his leisure time. The Taoist priest had a strong liking for playing chess, though his skills were extremely poor, he was very competitive. There were people who taught guests how to play chess, harboring resentment. Sometimes, the Taoist priest would prepare food and drink to please the guests, making them happy and conveying his intentions. Those who knew his habits always let him win when they played chess with him. Gong, who had become close friends with the Taoist priest, always played chess with him throughout the year without winning once, so the Taoist priest deeply admired him. Later, Gong moved into the temple and helped the Taoist priest write documents. Whenever he earned money, he spent it all on drinking and gambling. People called him "Tao A'er." The upper class avoided him and did not associate with him. In Jieshi Village in Shan Yin, there was a man named Liu, who was skilled in astrology, divination, and various divinatory techniques. His wealth and reputation attracted many visitors, and he accumulated a huge amount of wealth. He was generous and liked to give, so people called him "Yuanwai." Later, he set up a dye business in Fuyang, using fair weights and measures and not deceiving customers. Wealthy traders would definitely come to trade with him. Fuyang was a place where Anhui, Fujian, and Zhejiang provinces met, a region known for its trade and commerce. Many merchants gathered there, and all kinds of food and drink establishments and boats were always present along the riverbank. Liu sometimes went out with guests, and people called him "Liu Sanye" and followed him everywhere. Women who were not well-off or in need would ask for a look or attention from Liu, and their status would suddenly rise. At night, they would gamble until dawn, and if guests owed money, Liu would mediate and settle the disputes. Liu had a special reputation for gambling and always won. He would place the money he won on his bed, and if guests took it, he would not ask for it back. Sometimes, when asked about it, he would laugh and say, "Silver is a living thing, it must have transformed itself." His generosity and broad-mindedness were all like this.

Wearing Folly, a follower of Lord Lu, was known for his extreme filial piety. Because he could not support his parents, he chose not to marry. Before every meal, he would first offer a small portion of food to his ancestors before eating himself. He loved martial arts, was heroic and thrifty. Therefore, the salary he earned was often stored with the host. Whenever he saw someone in need, he would not hesitate to spend thousands of gold coins, and people often regarded him as foolish. He also enjoyed drinking and regretted not having drinking companions. One evening, while drinking in the market, he saw a man named Gong drinking without a shirt, who drank quite boldly. He called out to ask who he was, and Gong replied that his surname was Tao. Wearing Folly said, "There is a Tao A'er in the market. Are you not him? Seeing your appearance, you do not seem to be an ordinary person. How much can you drink?" Gong said, "I enjoy drinking, but I have never been able to get drunk by anyone. Can you be as skilled as the famous historian Zha? Why bother asking such questions?" Wearing Folly was very pleased and said, "I will challenge you to a drinking contest." They then bought two large jars of turbid wine and drank together. After the two jars were empty, Gong was slightly tipsy, while Wearing Folly was already completely drunk and collapsed. Gong left. The next day, when Wearing Folly woke up and remembered the previous night, he sought out Gong again for a drink, and they had a great time. From then on, they became drinking companions.

A Wealthy Vinegar Seller Named Duan There was a wealthy man named Duan who sold vinegar, and he had only one daughter. She was extremely ugly, with blue scars covering her face, a broad forehead, and widely spaced teeth. A diviner once told him that his daughter would receive a first-rank title, but Duan suspected that the diviner was joking with him. As the daughter grew older, those who came to ask for her hand in marriage always died of illness, so she was still unmarried at thirty. One day, she suddenly had a dream where a black ape leaped onto her body, and she woke up in shock. She told her father about the dream, and Duan said, "Could it be that someone from the same clan is asking for your daughter's hand in marriage?" The next day, Duan Chi came to buy vinegar, saw the daughter, and asked if she had ever been asked for her hand in marriage. Duan replied that she had not, and added, "I am poor and my daughter is ugly, who else would want her?" Duan Chi took upon himself the task like an axe handle and mentioned this to Duan. Duan said, "Is this not Tao A'er? He is addicted to gambling and excessive drinking. In the future, will I let my daughter live by sucking the wind?" Duan Chi exclaimed, "I'm afraid your daughter doesn't have this good fortune. Otherwise, if Tao the scholar remains poor and lowly for a long time, I will gouge out my own eyes." Duan asked him about his birth year, and he replied, "I was born in the Year of the Monkey." Duan remembered his daughter's dream and was slightly moved, telling Duan Chi, "You can come tomorrow." The next day, Duan invited Mr. Duan to his home, and upon their first meeting, they agreed to the marriage. Duan Chi excused himself by saying he lives in a temple and has no money, so how can he marry? So they discussed the idea of having him live in their home.

The woman could weave and spin, and she would not burden her husband. Mr. Dai said, "Even now, it is necessary to have a little preparation. Being empty-handed despite skill, how can we manage?" Mr. Dai encouraged him from the side and volunteered to cover the expenses. He went to Mr. Lü to request thirty taels of silver. Mr. Lü asked the purpose, and Mr. Dai explained the reason. Mr. Lü was astonished and said, "He is a scholar! How did you recognize him?" Mr. Dai replied, "This person is not someone to be underestimated, so I got close to him." Mr. Lü wanted to see him, so he asked Mr. Dai to invite Mr. Dai. Upon seeing him, Mr. Lü exclaimed, "This is a man of great importance! But he will be obscure in his early years. From then on, his career will be very successful, but those with a wooden shape (referring to his appearance) will not live to old age, but it is enough." He looked at Mr. Dai and said, "I will help with this matter." He immediately gave him fifty taels of silver and said to Mr. Dai, "After the wedding, I hope to visit your new wife." Mr. Dai agreed and said, "This kindness must be repaid." Mr. Lü said, "Such trivial matters are not worth mentioning, but if you remember, I would be grateful for your mercy." He called his grandson out to bow, and Mr. Dai remembered him. He received the money and returned home. Three days after the wedding, he took his wife to visit Mr. Lü. Mr. Lü also promised to make her a first-rank lady, and they enjoyed a feast all day and returned.

Since the couple lived in harmony, the sounds of weaving alternated with the sounds of reading. The official's studies greatly progressed, and the following year he passed the local examination. He was appointed as a magistrate in the capital, assigned to Hubei, and gained a reputation as an able official. Within less than ten years, he reached a high-ranking position. Later, when he served as the governor-general of Jiangnan, a famine occurred, and the official petitioned the court for relief and tax remission, saving tens of thousands of people. The people of Jiangnan revered him as a deity. Subsequently, he reformed the salt administration, received the emperor's attention, and was highly favored. However, he died in the office of the Viceroy of the Two Jiangs. At that time, Mr. Dou had also passed away. On his deathbed, the official instructed his descendants to forever honor the ancestor in the ancestral hall. During the official's tenure as governor-general of Jiangsu, Mr. Lü's grandson, due to their old acquaintance, arrived in Suzhou suddenly and beat someone severely, causing their death. When the case was brought to court, the official redeemed the person from punishment and released them, gifting them a thousand gold pieces. In capturing the salt bandit Wang Yi, all the officials were apprehensive, fearing it might provoke unrest. The official secretly ordered a military officer to lead troops to capture him. When the bandit was publicly executed, a chess-playing Taoist happened to be in the governor-general's office and remarked, "Who would have thought that Tao Er (the official) would take such a harsh action." The official did not resent it. Previously, a Buddhist monk from Guangdong traveled to Shaoxing and was skilled in physiognomy. He once predicted that Dai Chi, after turning forty, would become prominent in a military role and receive a third-rank title. Dai laughed and said, "How can there be someone who receives an imperial decree for being of value?" When the official gained prominence, he supported Dai according to the rules and donated him the position of a defender. During the Sichuan uprising led by Zhao Jinlong, the official recommended Dai to join the campaign. After the victorious return, Dai was promoted to deputy commander by military merit.

After several years, I informed them of my intention to return to my hometown, and my retinue was splendid and impressive. Cui's wife was dragging a staff and begging for food on the roadside. She questioned those around her and learned about Dai's origins. She lay down across the road to beg for mercy, and Dai questioned her origins before ordering her to leave. The woman returned home and wept all night, and in the end, she hanged herself. The man she married, the baker, had long since died of cold and hunger. The historian said: I learned of this matter from the records of Wan Yizhai, and upon reading it, I was moved to tears, not knowing where they came from. The Lord and others were not only generous and righteous; their ability to recognize heroes when they are unrecognized, is not it a keen eye in the world? With a sigh, he said: Is there still such a person in the world like Zhang Fu and the washerwoman? Thus, I wept for them. But what they gave to others, they also gave to themselves, and what they received themselves, they also received from others. This is enough to make one weep for them. As for Tao Gong, who was abandoned by others and took refuge in a temple, he sighed and said: Is it possible to see Su Jizi and Zhu Wengzi again today? Thus, I wept for Tao Gong. When he died, the baker had already left, and the beggar woman clung to the carriage, how could she know that withered leeks cannot be planted in the garden, and spilled water cannot be collected again? Thus, I wept for Cui, and for those who are not Cui but learn to be like Cui, I wept endlessly. Alas, it is indeed a lesson to be learned.

According to the *Qingjie Jinlu* by Liang Jingshu, Wen Yi and his father were both Jinshi (imperial scholars) of the same cohort in the year 1422, and they served together in the capital. The two families had close ties, with their wives and children frequently visiting each other. Vang Furen, Wen Yi's mother, once noticed that Tao Furen, the wife of Wen Yi, had a wart on the back of her right hand. When Vang asked about it, Tao frowned and said, "I come from a humble background and had to work hard when I was young, and this hand was injured by handling a grinding tool." It is said that Wen Yi was extremely poor when he was young and was engaged to a woman from the same county. A wealthy family named Wu heard that the woman was beautiful and plotted to marry her as a second wife, offering substantial financial incentives to her father. The father agreed and pressured Wen Yi to break the engagement, but Wen Yi refused. The woman's mother also did not want her to marry the Wu family, but the woman was attracted to the wealth of the Wu family and decided to marry them. Her father strongly supported the marriage, making it difficult to change the situation. A servant offered to take Wen Yi's place, and both the woman's mother and Wen Yi accepted this arrangement. Tao Furen later became a lady of the first rank, receiving imperial favor. Later, the Wu family got into a land dispute with another family, and a member of the Wu family was beaten to death. The patriarch of the Wu family also died. The Wu family exploited the vulnerability of the woman, seizing most of her property. At the time, Wen Yi had already achieved high status and returned home after the death of a close relative. He heard about the woman's plight and provided her with fifty taels of silver. The woman felt guilty and regretful, holding the silver and crying all day, unable to use it. Soon, the silver was stolen by a thief, and she, in anger, committed suicide. Later, Zhu Wending, a scholar-official who had returned to the capital after serving as a provincial examiner in Zhejiang, visited the Wu residence. Zhu and Wen Yi were also from the same cohort in 1422. During the visit, Wen Yi entertained Zhu with a performance, which included the play *Shuang Guan Gao* (Double Imperial Decree). Wen Yi was moved to tears by the performance. Zhu said, "This is a great oversight on my part. I forgot that the Yun Ting family also had a sister named Bi Lian."

This record and the biography differ in their narratives at the beginning and end. In summary, the Huang woman's betrayal of her duty to the wealthy man is consistent. However, the claim that the woman who received the imperial title was actually her family's maid is based on different accounts or rumors. Nevertheless, the fact of the divorce is more credible. Change Name A man from Hangzhou, surnamed Feng, enjoyed humor. Later, he obtained a position as a local official in Zhili through financial means and was on the waiting list for an appointment in Anhui. One day, after attending morning court and leaving, he met a fellow official named Xu Bèi from Ganxian in the office. At the time, Xu was waiting for inspection and they briefly chatted. Xu then stood up to urinate. Seizing the opportunity, Feng took Xu's business card and added a hook to the character "王" (Wang) without Xu noticing. After entering the court and completing the ceremony, a certain governor-general, after briefly questioning Xu a few times, smiled and said to Xu, "Your Excellency, your demeanor is gentle and refined, but your name is not very elegant, is it?" Xu stared in disbelief for a long time, unable to understand. The governor-general then ordered Xu's report to be fetched and shown to him. Xu was horrified and blushed with shame, unable to stay for long. He retreated to the office and confronted the gatekeeper, intending to beat him. Feng then stepped in and laughed, saying, "This was my doing. Please forgive my first offense, and I am willing to offer a box of money boxes used in intimate settings as compensation for my crime. How about it?" Xu had no choice but to leave in anger. His fellow officials could not help but burst out laughing. The money boxes used in intimate settings are objects placed under the waist during sexual intercourse. In the past, a wealthy man's son in a place called He was newly married, and his wife's dowry included a box containing all such items. The wealthy man's son did not know what they were for and secretly took one, showing it to his father and asking what it was for. His father covered his mouth and could not answer. Those who saw it could not help but laugh silently.

The Debt-Bearing Ghost In my village, there were two friends, Jia and Yi, both of whom were poor. During the Cold Food Festival, Jia returned from visiting his ancestors' graves and saw a broken coffin with exposed bones by the roadside. Moved with compassion, he went home, took shovels and baskets, and covered the remains. That night, he dreamed of a person dressed in a silk robe who came to him, weeping gratefully and saying, "I am deeply grateful to you for your kindness to these dead bones. There is nothing I can repay in the netherworld. In my lifetime, I learned the Six Rhyne (a form of divination). From now on, you can hang a curtain in the market, and I will help you a little, which can also be a way to alleviate poverty." Jia doubted that he had never studied this before, but the ghost said, "Just listen to what I say, and it will surely be effective." Jia thanked him and woke up feeling strange. He thought to himself that if he was so poor, why not try this method? So he hung up a sign to invite people to consult him. Whenever someone came to ask for a divination, the ghost would teach Jia how to interpret it. One day, a person came to report a lost item, and the ghost whispered to Jia, "This item is hidden inside a false wall in the northwest corner of their house, but it was not stolen by a person." Jia told this to the person, and indeed, the item was found exactly where the ghost had said. It turned out that the person's wife had placed a pearl ring on the mirror stand before going to bed, and it was taken by a mouse. In the village, a certain old man had a white dog that suddenly rose in the air and walked to the top of the wall. The old man then fell ill with typhoid fever, which was very severe. Jia went to consult the ghost, who said, "There are wild ghosts seeking food, and worshipping them can cure the illness." The old man's family worshipped the ghosts, and the illness was cured. From then on, Jia's place became as busy as a market, and after a year, he accumulated wealth in the thousands.

Yi happened to visit Jia and asked why he had suddenly become so effective. Jia explained his method, and Yi admired it. After returning home, Yi also carried a basket and a spade to the outskirts, found an old, broken coffin, and did it according to Jia's method before returning. That night, indeed, a ghost came to thank him. The ghost had a frowning and tattered appearance, looking like a beggar. Yi then told the ghost what he wanted, and the ghost happily agreed to serve. Yi was greatly pleased, thinking he would soon become a wealthy man, and thus relied on the ghost's method. Soon after, when he asked about divination matters, the ghost always said, "Come tomorrow." When he changed the person, the ghost said the same thing. Yi followed the ghost's words, and each time the person left with the divination money. The next day, no one came to ask anymore. Yi returned and blamed the ghost, who said, "Before my death, whenever I encountered someone demanding payment, I used this method to respond. I was not familiar with other things." After speaking, the ghost fell silent and never came again. Yi was very regretful. He went to the county town to inquire and found out that the person had died because he was overwhelmed with debts and became ill due to depression.

The historian says: Laozi once said, "The highest virtue does not appear as virtue." In today's world, it is hard to find someone who gives generously without expecting anything in return. Yet, to hope for a return and seek it in decayed and decomposed remains is foolish indeed. How much more so for someone like Mr. Yi, who stubbornly pursues an unattainable goal? It is no wonder that such a person is mocked by spirits. Once upon a time, Xi Shi fainted in pain and made a pained expression that became beautiful. A plain-looking woman from her village saw this and thought it was beautiful, so she too held her heart and made a pained expression when she returned to her village. When the rich people saw her, they firmly closed their doors and did not come out; when the poor people saw her, they took their wives and children and fled. She knew that the pained expression was beautiful, but she did not understand why it was beautiful. Similarly, Mr. Yi's clever attempts turned into clumsiness. If the spirits were standing beside him, could they not clap their hands in amusement?

Snake Anomaly** In the early years of the Kangxi reign, two snakes suddenly emerged from beneath the new bridge's pillars on the Eastern River to fight, and they did not stop for a long time. The number of spectators grew gradually. Suddenly, the bridge collapsed, crushing and killing thousands of people, and the snakes disappeared. This event is recorded in Li Fanxia's *Eastsidetown Miscellany*. Were these two snakes perhaps sent by heaven? **Ghost Servants Engaging in Promiscuity** The Imperial Mint in the capital city had a shrine with four statues of ghost servants inside, which were quite renowned for their supernatural abilities. Several craftsmen stayed near the entrance and often found themselves defiled in their dreams. When the spirits came, they felt as if their hands and feet were bound, and they could not shout. Upon waking, they would find green mud filling the area between their thighs, causing swelling and pain that made them unable to stand. At first, they did not know what was causing this mischief. Later, one clever craftsman was again defiled, and he silently memorized the appearance of the spirit in his dream. Upon waking, he recalled it and realized it was the ghost servants. Together, they informed the officials in charge, and the statues were destroyed, thus eliminating the evil spirit.

**Fox Mother** The father of Da Ji, a senior military officer in Shengjing, once went hunting in the mountains. As evening fell, he met a young woman on his way home. She was about twenty years old, with extraordinary beauty, and she told him that she was lost and asked to ride along with him. The father thought, how could there be such a woman in this remote area? Could she be a fox spirit? He had heard that human blood could control ghosts and fox spirits, preventing them from escaping their true form. Deciding to test this, he allowed her to ride with him. As night fell, he secretly broke his nose and let blood flow onto her forehead. The woman became extremely angry and cursed him, saying, "Black-hearted man, do you not fear death?" However, she could not escape. He took her back home and forced her into marriage. The following year, she gave birth to Da Ji. The woman was polite to the family, and everyone treated her without suspicion. Those who saw her were amazed by her beauty and forgot that she was a fox spirit. Da Ji once told people, "My mother looks like a normal person in all respects, except that she always wears a gauze hat on her head, regardless of the weather. Her head is hollow, and one can see her internal organs from below." After her death, the family verified this, and it was indeed true. **Prince the Seventh** During the reign of Emperor Jiaqing, Chengde attempted an assassination. He waited until Emperor Renzong was presiding over court and suddenly shot an arrow from his sleeve. A guard saw the arrow coming but was unable to block it in time. He immediately threw himself over the imperial throne, and the arrow pierced his chest, killing him. At that moment, the Seventh Prince was nearby. He quickly grabbed Chengde with both hands. The other guards rushed over and seized him. Chengde was then executed by dismemberment and cooking.

It is said that Chengde was skilled in martial arts, and there was no one among the guards who could match him. Someone would drive a row of short stakes into the ground, and Chengde would leap on one foot to sweep them away, uprooting all the stakes. The seventh prince also had this ability, but he could only sweep seven stakes, while Chengde could sweep up to twelve stakes, they say. Later, the emperor visited Mulan for a hunting expedition, and the officials were riding and chasing. A bear suddenly appeared before the emperor, injuring several guards. The seventh prince stepped forward to fight the bear barehanded, and after a long time, the bear caught him and sat on his body, and he could not escape. The prince quickly bent his right foot and pushed the bear away with all his strength, causing the bear to fall at the foot of the mountain and die, its body rotting. But the prince's foot was permanently crippled from then on. Qu Gong At first, when the imperial army entered Guilin, Qu Gong was wearing a scholar's cap and a loose robe, sitting upright in his office. Hu Yiqing came on horseback, urging him to leave. Qu Gong raised his cup and said, "Can you drink?" Yiqing replied, "Is this the time for drinking?" and then he rode away. Just then, General Zhang Tongchang, the governor of the Chu forces, returned from Lingzhou. Qu Gong was delighted and said, "When Zhang comes, I will not die alone." Zhang said, "I will do whatever it takes. If you give the order, I dare not die!" They stayed, and drank. General Qi Liangxun of the provincial forces, the governor's bodyguard, led a horse and urged Qu Gong to leave the city to try to restore the situation. The family members wept and begged him to wait a moment, until the second son arrived. But he refused.

They were then captured and brought before Duke Dingnan Kong Youde. Kong said, "Are you the Grand Secretary? What an excellent Grand Secretary!" The Grand Secretary replied, "Are you the prince? What an excellent prince!" Kong sat with his legs spread out on the ground and said, "Sit down." The Grand Secretary said, "I am not accustomed to sitting cross-legged." Kong stood up respectfully and bowed to him. When they saw Tong Chang, the attendants ordered him to kneel, but Tong Chang loudly scolded them. One of the guards struck him on the leg with the back of a blade and forced him to kneel, but Tong Chang remained firm and was dragged away to be beheaded. The Grand Secretary spoke with a serious expression and scolded, "Zhang Shizhao is a minister of the state; you must not be disrespectful! If you kill him, I will die with him." Kong usually respected the Grand Secretary and, with a start of alarm, stopped. He tried to persuade them to surrender in many ways, but they finally did not yield. Kong respected them even more and lodged the two officials in a separate place. The defense was very strict, but they were provided with food and drink like guests of honor, and the two officials continued to sing in response as if nothing happened.

During the governor's time, he sent a loyal follower to leave a message for Jiao Lian, strongly stating that the Qing army was weak and exhausted, and advised Jiao Lian to quickly mobilize troops to reach Guilin, saying, "The plans for the revival of the state, do not worry about me." The patrol soldiers obtained the message and presented it, causing Yude to be greatly alarmed. On the morning of the seventeenth day of the twelfth lunar month, the governor invited the two officials. The official was eating, and when the food was removed, he smiled and said, "We have lived forty-one more days with the governor, and now the matter is over." Tong Chang said, "How delightful this journey will be! Today I have found a proper place to die." Those who saw them were all moved to tears. The two officials did not change their expressions, and they remained cheerful as usual. The governor hid a white headband in his bosom and put it on, saying, "This is for the deceased emperor, and I will wear this to meet the deceased emperor." Arriving at the foot of the Duxiu Rock, the official pointed and said, "All my life I have loved springs and stones, I wish to die here." They tidied their clothes and rushed to the executioner's blade.

At the time of his execution, a great thunderstorm erupted in winter, and men and women far and near wept bitterly. Ma Jiaolin, who carried out the execution, held the gentleman in high regard and ordered that he be covered with reed mats. Three days later, Guan Duan, a subordinate of the official and an attendant in the Imperial Court, together with Yang Ruo, entered the prince's residence and planned to prepare the funeral rites for the two gentlemen. When they opened the body, they saw the blood from the blade still on his neck, his head and body not yet separated, and his face remained unchanged. They caressed him and wept, saying: "Your loyal spirit remains, do you know that we are burying you?" Suddenly, he opened his eyes and looked left and right. Yang comforted him, saying: "Has your second son come to see you? Has your eldest son lost his way?" His eyes still looked about, and Guan Duan knelt and said: "I now understand your thoughts. The emperor has already taken residence in Nanning, and your disciples have gathered in great numbers. Lord Jiao is safe and unharmed." At this, his eyes finally closed. They then dressed him in full ceremonial attire and shallowly buried the two gentlemen in the open land of Fengdong Mountain. A house was built nearby, and they remained to guard the tomb, never leaving — so it is said. Gong Sun Changwen, a censor in the Hanlin Academy, was sent in October to the Prince of Yongming, resigned from his hereditary title of Lord of Linge, and also presented a statement on why Guilin could not be held. Upon hearing the alarm, he returned home. Earlier, Wei Yuanyi of Zhejiang, a corrupt official who had been dismissed, harbored resentment toward Changwen and intended to take pleasure in harming him. One day before he arrived, in Wei Yuanyi’s home, iron chains clanged loudly, making a sound that echoed around the room. Wei Yuanyi fell to the ground and begged for punishment, then suddenly spoke in the Wu dialect, saying: "You are unfaithful and unjust, and you wish to kill my grandson?" Wei Yuanyi knelt and begged for three more days to finish his household affairs. Then, suddenly speaking in the Chu dialect, he said: "This unrighteous servant, kill him quickly — why ask anything at all?" Blood flowed from all nine orifices, and he died.

有德患病，派遣将领到城隍庙祈祷，忽然看到“宫侯司马”四个大字。进入殿内，见到总督正襟危坐，大为震惊，便向他行礼。回来后将此事告知有德，有德感到非常惊讶，便在铁佛寺为两位忠烈之士设立了神位。昌文恰好到来，有德因此对他厚加礼遇。昌文随后将留守的遗骸迁葬于明月洞，清凝也将总督的遗骸迁出，并与夫人合葬。 起初，安仁王英明特达，才略过人，有知人之明。他曾说：“在安定时期可以托付社稷，在危难时刻不丧失大节的，只有瞿先生一人而已。”一日宴会结束后，半夜突然发病。急忙召见公入内，托付后事。握住他的手流泪说：“我辜负了先生。”又对永明王说：“国家之事全听先生裁决。”并自言其前世：“我重生为伽蓝，而王是第一罗汉，先生最好辅佐他。”说完便去世了。相传永明王曾到宝鼎寺，礼拜肉身无量佛，佛像忽然站起。如此看来，罗汉转世的说法果然不是虚言。

The Qing forces attacked Jixi and captured the commander-in-chief and censor Jin Sheng. When Jin Sheng was executed, Hong Chengchou oversaw the execution. After his death, his body did not fall. Hong entered the execution chamber and saw Jin Sheng sitting upright with his attire neat and dignified. Hong was so shocked that he retreated inside and remained disoriented and afraid to come out for several days. This was similar to the posthumous manifestations of other historical figures like Qu the Governor and Zhang the Military Governor. The loyal spirit and righteous soul of Jin Sheng should be as enduring as rivers, mountains, the sun, and stars, and cannot be suppressed. The historian commented: I once read Shen Tingfang's *Inscription on the Rededication of the Left Gong Shrine of the Right Censor of the Ming Dynasty's Ministry of War*. In the subsequent section, it said: "On the 20th day of the leap month in the second year of the Shunzhi era, the official was executed. On that day, villagers from Laizhou saw him wearing white clothes, riding a white donkey, entering through the south gate and going home. His wife, Lady Liu, asked him, 'Have you returned?' He replied, 'I was imprisoned by the new dynasty.' When asked about other matters, he said, 'I can now end the chaos.' At that time, there was a wooden bench under the north window. He sat there for a long time before leaving. The villagers still saw him leave through the south gate. Shortly after, Maotai sent someone to escort his coffin back home. The next day, people who knew him came from the south, saying they met him in Yangzhou at dusk, where he said he wanted to go to Nanjing to pay respects to the late emperor, and his attire and mode of transport were the same. This shows that his loyalty never wavered in life or death, never forgetting his king and country. Until now, villagers call him 'Greatly Loyal Master.' I heard this from Zhao Yuanrui."

According to the records, when the public figure accompanied Chen Hongfan and Ma Shaoyu, dressed in mourning attire, entered the capital, they requested to offer sacrifices and inform the tombs of the former emperor and to reinter him. If this was not possible, they would set up the grand sacrificial offerings on the court, cry, and perform the ritual. They were then immediately sent back to the capital. Hongfan requested that the public figure be retained and not sent away, so he was recalled and moved to the Imperial Medical Hospital. The public figure inscribed the hospital gate with the words: "In life, I am a loyal minister of the Great Ming; in death, I am a loyal ghost of the Great Ming." He also hung a portrait of Su Wu, the envoy, on the wall. Later, upon hearing of the fall of Nanjing, the public figure mourned deeply, facing south, and fasted for seven days, vomiting blood. He wrote a poem that included the lines: "The cold soul of a loyal heart cannot be extinguished, like cold smoke that will never fade." When ordered to surrender, he refused and was executed along with Chen Yongji, a low-ranking clerk from the Ministry of War who was traveling with him. His servants, Zuo Xia and Wang Lianzhou, also chose to die with him and were killed as well.

Ever since the most loyal and great principles, all have their inherent determination, therefore, even after death, they still possess such vitality. Some say that if Nanjing had not fallen, the public official could have survived. However, even if he had survived, he would still have been a guest in Zhenzhou like Hao Jing, how could he then betray his lord and compromise his principles? A jade can be broken, but its whiteness cannot be destroyed, this is what these several officials share. As for what is recorded after the stele, the spirit of the public official is especially solemn and awe-inspiring, therefore, it is recorded together. Sun Yanling Li Dingguo attacked Guilin. Kong Youde said to his wife, "I have received great favor from the state, I vow to die with my body. You should make plans for yourselves." His wife said, "You do not need to worry about me dying." She pointed to her son and daughter and said, "What sins have these children committed, and why are they also caught in this disaster!" She instructed an old woman to take them away and sent them off with tears, saying, "If this child escapes from danger, you should raise him as a novice monk. If he does not follow in his father's footsteps, running around the country his whole life, he will end up in this situation." She finished speaking and hanged herself. Kong Youde set fire to his mansion and cut his own throat, killing himself. His son was soon captured by Li Dingguo's soldiers and died in Anlong. His daughter, being young, was raised in the military.

Guangxi was pacified, and the woman was allowed to return home. The Shizu Emperor and the Grand Empress Dowager, moved by the death of Kong Youde in the service of the state, ordered her to be brought into the palace, where she was taken as a foster daughter of the Empress Dowager and given the name Kong Sizhen. When Kong Sizhen was sixteen, the Empress Dowager sought a husband for her, but Kong Sizhen herself declared that she already had a husband. It was because Kong Youde, while alive, had already betrothed her to Sun Yanling, the son of a subordinate general. The emperor then issued a decree to find Sun Yanling, and under the command of the Empress Dowager, they were made husband and wife, and a residence was granted to them outside the Xihua Gate. When Guangxi was pacified again, the emperor, considering that there were no descendants of the Kong family and worrying that the Kong family would have no one to manage their affairs, bestowed upon Kong Sizhen the title of Hezhuo Princess and entrusted her with the affairs of the Dingnan Prince, exercising remote control over the armies of Guangxi. Sun Yanling was appointed as the Hezhuo Prince Consort and an Inner Minister of State, inheriting the title of First Rank Asinihafan. Sun Yanling was handsome and well-proportioned, skilled in music and proficient in martial arts. His body was strong and agile, capable of leaping over a nine-foot screen. However, he did not like to read books, but whenever there were imperial edicts or memorials, he could always discern their merits and demerits. In his dealings with others, he was always sincere and could tolerate others' mistakes. Kong Sizhen was both beautiful and talented, considering herself as the Empress Dowager's foster daughter and in charge of the affairs of the vassal state, she looked down upon Sun Yanling. Sun Yanling, due to the Empress Dowager, appeared respectful and obedient to her will. Kong Sizhen was pleased with this, and she frequently praised his abilities when entering and leaving the palace. The Empress Dowager also favored her, bestowing her with favors second only to those of a prince. Kong Sizhen did not realize that Sun Yanling was being deceived, believing that his gentle and compliant nature would make him easy to control, and she took more and more decisive actions. Sun Yanling, however, grew increasingly discontented within, and every day he thought of ways to seize power from her.

When the three commanders, including Dai Liangchen, seized power, Si Zhen deeply regretted it and reconciled with Yanling. He reported the illegal and disorderly actions of Liangchen et al. to the emperor, and the three commanders also accused him. The emperor ordered Supervisor Jin Guangzu to investigate the matter, and the ministers all found Yanling to be unjust. In the twelfth year, Wu Sangui rebelled and wrote a letter to invite Yanling. Yanling invited Liangchen et al. to discuss matters, and then had strongmen throw cups as a signal to bind and kill them all. He immediately raised an army, and Wu Sangui enfeoffed him as King of Linjiang. The governor of Guangxi, Ma Xiong, also surrendered. Ma Xiong was originally assisted by the three commanders, and Yanling feared his pressure. Si Zhen was constantly grateful for the emperor's grace and advised him to surrender. The plan was about to be decided, but Ma Xiong discovered it and secretly informed Wu Sangui. Wu Sangui appointed his nephew Shibi as Grand General Jinwu, leading troops under the pretext of restoring Guangdong, and stationed them outside the city of Guilin. Yanling went out to meet him, recounted old acquaintances, and they were very happy together. When he saw him off at the main gate, dozens of Miao soldiers suddenly attacked from the horse's head. Yanling drew a sharp blade from his whip and fiercely attacked, killing several people, but could not hold out and was killed. Shibi sent his head to Ma Xiong, who, looking at it, laughed loudly and said, "Yanling has come to this too!" The head suddenly opened its eyes and mouth, leaped up, and directly attacked Ma Xiong. Ma Xiong shouted, "Yanling killed me!" He vomited several liters of blood and died.

This story is extremely similar to the one in *Romance of the Three Kingdoms*, where Sun Quan ordered the execution of Guan Yu and sent his head to Cao Cao, who then opened the box and asked, "Has your health been good, Cloud Long?" However, this account is a fabricated and baseless rumor, while the true story of Si Zhen is recorded in the biographies of the Four Kings. Alas, is this actually the case? Si Zhen was once raised as a daughter by Wu Sangui, and she was forcibly taken to Yunnan. Her son was also killed by Shi Bin. After Yunnan was pacified, Si Zhen returned to the capital and was honored with a sacrifice. 缢鬼 Wang Ruyang of Xiushui, whose courtesy name was Yunhe, was a scholar who had not yet passed the imperial examinations. He was teaching at the home of a local gentry family. One night, after reading until past midnight, a young woman dressed in white sleeves and plain clothing pushed open the door and entered. Wang was surprised and asked her where she came from. The woman said she was acquainted with the master's daughter, Fragrant Aunt, and intended to take a shortcut to visit her. Wang, suspecting her behavior, tried to stop her. The woman could not argue and returned to crouch outside the door, reaching into the threshold with her hand. After some time, she left. Wang became even more suspicious and quickly returned, moving the lamp to examine the spot. He found a circle about a foot in circumference. He brought it back and examined the object by the light. It was neither a rope nor a belt, but a ring with no end. Realizing something was amiss, he immediately burned it with fire, and the stench was so foul that it was unbearable to the nose.

Suddenly, the sound of weeping came from inside. Asking the attendant, he learned that the master's daughter had already hanged herself. In the midst of his astonishment, the previous woman suddenly arrived at the railing, unable to find her hoop, she went back inside and demanded it from Wang. Wang replied, "It was just burned," and scolded her to leave quickly. The woman angrily said, "We have no grudge against you, how could you resort to such a cruel act? But you are a noble person." She wept bitterly and left. Shortly after, the attendant came again to report that the master's daughter had been rescued and revived. Later, Wang passed the imperial examination in the Gengzi year and was appointed as an educational inspector in Yunnan. He soon died. At the time of his death, an old monk came to the door, calling him to return, and the master also mentioned that he had been a monk on Mount Emei in his previous life. The Sack of Chashu In the summer last year, the British broke into Chashu, and the massacre and pillage were so brutal that corpses blocked the roads, and some were thrown into the river, causing it to stop flowing. The most horrifying aspect was the treatment of women. The bandits were divided into two groups, black and white. The black ones were almost as stupid as dogs and sheep, and were driven by the white ones, not knowing fear of death. Therefore, they were made to fire muskets in battle. However, when the city was breached, they also knew to rape and plunder. All the women they captured, the young and beautiful ones were given to the white bandits, while the black ones mostly took the old and ugly ones. There were cases where several women were raped in turn by one man, leading to their deaths.

A young man named Yang, talented and handsome, entered the local school. He married a woman of great intelligence and beauty, and their marriage had lasted only a year. The day before, his wife heard an alarm and urged Yang to go immediately to find a boat and escape, saying that if they waited until the city was captured, they might not be able to commit suicide. Yang, attached to his home, hesitated to leave. When the foreign bandits arrived, he finally went out to find a boat, but the entire city was in chaos, and no boats could be found. He rushed back and heard his wife's cries echoing outside. He hurried inside and saw six or seven black ghosts pulling her hair, about to violate her. Yang knelt down to beg for mercy, but the bandits became angry and immediately tied his hands and feet to the door. They then seized the woman, stripped her clothes, and took turns violating her. After a long time, she writhed and cried out until she died, and then they left her. Later, they found and killed several servants. An old servant, hiding under the bed, jumped out, removed the nails, and carried Yang down. Yang could not stand up and wept over his wife's body. After a long time, he limped out of the door, intending to seek death. He happened to meet several white ghosts, who asked about the situation and took him back. They ordered him to identify and kill the seven black ghosts.

There was a certain Guo, a traitor to the Chinese nation, who was always supported by foreign bandits and held military power. He was rewarded with thirty pieces of gold and made to remarry. He brought the money back and ordered his old servant to the market to buy two coffins. He prepared his wife's body for burial, wailed several times, and then struck his head against the coffin and died. The old servant immediately took an empty coffin and wrapped him in it, and then hanged himself. Others who suffered his cruelty are too numerous to count. Recently reading *The Ten Days of Yangzhou*, it chronologically narrates the suffering of the people when the city fell, making one unable to finish reading. At a time of turmoil and exile, the situation is generally the same.

The white ghost is also known for its lewd and poisonous nature, almost as dangerous as the black ghost. The women it captures are favored with great affection. Every day, it must use drum and music for a ceremonial bowing, followed by a banquet, as if it were a wedding. However, it also has a fondness for writing and painting, and whenever it enters a household, it will strive to obtain famous calligraphy and paintings, leaving nothing behind. **The Tiger Whips Its Own Tail** A man from Guangling once took his son on a trip to Chu. On the way, they entered the Jiuyi Mountains. By chance, it was evening, and they borrowed lodging at a temple. It was the 15th day of the 10th month in the lunar calendar, and the man was feeling lonely and aimless. He leaned against the window to watch the moon. Suddenly, the wind picked up, shaking the trees and causing leaves to fall. He saw a tiger leap into the back garden, sit on a large stone, and then begin to cry loudly, its voice filled with deep sorrow. After a while, the tiger stretched out its tail and whipped its own back hundreds of times before leaving. The father and son were greatly frightened and dared not sleep again. They sat up until dawn and told the temple monk about what they had seen. The monk said, "This is a common occurrence here." When asked why the tiger cried, the monk replied, "The tiger has a forgetful nature. When it eats a person, it does not realize that it is eating a human until it is too late. However, the claws of the person it eats cannot be digested and often get stuck in its chest. On clear nights with bright moonlight, the tiger necessarily regrets its actions and cries. It believes that heaven and earth are good at giving life, but it has eaten a person, so it whips its own back to punish itself. However, when it encounters a time of fierce wind and thunder, if someone happens to be there, it will revert to its old ways."

The Historian said: From childhood, I heard the elders say that when a tiger eats a person, it necessarily begins at the heel and moves upward. When it eats up to the head, it then realizes it is a human, and sheds tears before abandoning it. At that time, I already felt this story was a fabrication. Later, while reading a collection of Tang Dynasty writings, I came across an account from the reign of Emperor Muzong. There were two men, Sun Sheng and a certain Li Sheng, who had always been close friends. One day, Li Sheng suddenly disappeared. His family searched for him for a long time without success, and it was said that he had transformed into a tiger. Later, Sun Sheng left the capital on official business and, on his journey, passed through the foot of Huayin Mountain. Suddenly, he encountered a tiger in the grass calling out to him, asking, “Is my old friend well?” The tiger then explained that it had become a tiger, and inquired about news from his family, after which it wept bitterly. Sun Sheng called out to it, telling it to come forward, but the tiger replied that it was ashamed of its own ugliness and feared startling its old friend, and therefore did not wish to be seen. It said that whenever it encountered people, it knew it should not eat them, but its saliva could not be controlled. It also warned Sun Sheng not to return, for fear that it might then devour him. Sun Sheng was deeply moved and solemnly promised. The tiger then composed two seven-character regulated verse poems on the spot and gave them to Sun Sheng, weeping greatly before departing. The reason the tiger gave for eating people was slightly different from what I had previously heard, but its self-hatred for being an animal was the same in both accounts.

I alone find strange the people who wear the tiger's crown, for their forgetfulness is even greater than that of a tiger, and those who are cruel and kill people without remorse, do not even know regret. Alas! Even tigers are like this, how can we call them human and yet they are worse than tigers? A foreign ship Several years ago, there was a rumor that a ship suddenly appeared outside the borders of Qiongzhou. Its length exceeded that of ocean-going ships, and it was greatly praised. It had three decks, with high towers and masts, grand and majestic, sailing faster than the wind, and there was no one on board. In the middle were large bronze cannons, with a circumference of about a zhang, which could fire without anyone, and Chinese cannons could not match it. At the time, people were in a state of panic, thinking that there must be island barbarians who would cause trouble in the interior, so they were making a show of strength to intimidate. According to records: Among the foreigners, the Dutch are the best at using ships and cannons. Their largest ships are thirty zhang long, five or six zhang wide, with boards over two feet thick, overlapping like fish scales. They have five masts on the ship, with iron nets, and are painted with horse oil, shining like a mirror. The ship has three decks, with small windows on the sides, each containing a bronze mirror. Each cannon has a mechanism, and when fired, the window is pushed open to release it, and after firing, it retracts on its own, without the need for human effort. Below the mast are large cannons, over thirty zhang long, hollow like a four-foot wheel. It is said that firing this can penetrate stone walls, shaking for dozens of li, and if the enemy approaches, it can be broken and sunk, not falling into the enemy's hands. They use black demons as laborers, who often jump from high places into the sea, walking slowly out of the waves as if walking on flat ground. Behind the rudder is a large copper disk, several feet in diameter, which is said to be a sea mirror, and knowing this can prevent getting lost at sea.

Now the British barbarians have invaded, arriving at Dinghai on the sixteenth day of the sixth month. It is said that their general, Bai Mei, and Burley, who command their ships, are still anchored at Zhao Bao Mountain. Their ships are over forty zhang long, and their construction is no different from that of Dutch ships. The hulls of these ships are all copper-plated. Our troops once sent skilled swimmers to dive underwater and attempt to breach them, but they were unable to do so. According to Yang Bingnan's *Hai Lu*, the Kingdom of England is the same as the Red-haired barbarians, while the *Examination of the Outside Seas* states that the Red-haired barbarians call themselves Holland. Thus, these ships must have come from the British barbarians. The Red-haired barbarians in Fujian were originally a vassal state of Japan, frequently visiting Fujian for trade. In the late years of the Ming dynasty, they built fortresses in the coastal areas to establish a long-term presence. In the spring of the year Jiazi, a man surnamed Li from Zhangzhou returned from Japan, claiming to be the son-in-law of the Japanese king. Li was originally a performer from Fujian who, after losing the wind during a sea crossing, was shipwrecked in Japan. The Japanese king loved his appearance and character, and married him to his niece. After several years, Li wished to return to his homeland to honor his ancestors, so he returned. At the time, Governor Nan Juyi heard of this and summoned Li to inquire about matters in Japan, and requested a plan to disband the Red-haired barbarians. Li said, "These are vassals of our country; if we instruct them, they will leave." He then sent messengers to return, and they abandoned their fortresses. Thus, their constructions were destroyed. The core concern of Fujian was immediately resolved. At that time, although there was a concern for the coastal areas, it was merely due to the Red-haired barbarians being a vassal state of Japan. Unlike now, when they are powerful and rival China.

### Appendix According to *The Outer Ocean Examination* and *The Sea Record*: The English barbarians are the descendants of the Dutch, also known as the red-haired foreigners. *The Outer Ocean Examination* states that their greatest skill lies in ships and firearms; *The Sea Record* also mentions their proficiency with rapid-fire guns, and their ship construction is particularly ingenious. Their military system closely follows the spirit of the *Rites of Zhou*. They follow the Catholic faith, and they show no reverence for the deities of the inland regions. However, they always pay homage and worship the White Spirit of Death, a figure depicted in temples. Even the image of Confucius, the sage, is treated with disrespect, and some have even used it as firewood. It is said that last year, when they invaded Ningbo, their land commander, Bulley, entered the Temple of the City God and removed the cap and robe of the city god, intending to change their attire. Upon returning to his ship, he suddenly spoke as if he were a god, saying, "I am the god of this land, appointed by God. Although our own country has never imposed its system on us, you insist on making me change my clothes. How dare you destroy my cap and robe?" After finishing his words, he immediately took his sword and stabbed himself to death. The other foreigners were shocked and terrified. The next day, they still made clothes according to the old system, prepared offerings, and sent them to the temple. After dressing the god in the offerings, they prostrated themselves and apologized, then left. Although this event seems strange, it is also a sign of their disrespect for the gods.

### 瓮间手 The *Qixiu Ligao* records: I once compiled *Tanpu*, which mentions that during the reign of Emperor Shenzong, when the city walls were being repaired, an object was unearthed that was alive and resembled a person but lacked eyebrows and eyes. Some called it the Tai Sui. At the end of the Zhengde era, in the village of Gao Tiantun in Chongde (now part of Tongxiang), a household dug up a living child and burned it to death immediately. This is another mystery that remains unexplained. I believe this might be something buried by humans, and thus not particularly unusual. In my second elder sister's family, there was a cohabiting sister-in-law named Ms. Zhang, who had long suffered from coughing up blood. One day, at noon, she went to the kitchen to cook lunch and suddenly saw a hand emerging from beside a water jar, with all five fingers intact, resembling a human hand. The woman was greatly alarmed and called out to others to come see, but the hand had disappeared by the time they arrived. They dug at the spot where it had appeared, but found nothing. However, the woman began to suffer from frequent palpitations and died shortly thereafter. According to *Xichao Xinyu*: Xu Taishi Yongxi, before he passed the imperial examinations, once went to the toilet and saw a large meaty block covered in eyes. Recalling a passage in a book that said whipping the Tai Sui could turn misfortune into good fortune, he struck it. Each time he struck an eye, the entire block became even more vividly illuminated. From then on, he passed the provincial examinations and advanced through the imperial examinations, eventually reaching the rank of Shilang. Thus, it is not accurate to say that the Tai Sui is a being without eyes, for there may be various types of such entities. ### 挖眼 The *Draft History of the Ming Dynasty* records: Han Yong, a native of Changzhou, led military campaigns against the Yao and Tung tribes in Guangxi. Once, while discussing military matters with his subordinates at the camp gate, he took several captives, probed and ate their hearts and brains, finishing immediately. Those who witnessed this were horrified, but Han Yong spoke and laughed as if nothing were unusual. This truly exemplifies the concept of "overcoming one's love with might."

A certain governor-general, who was stationed at the sea frontier, would gouge out the eyes of notorious bandits whenever he encountered them. Once, while disguised in a tea house, he saw an Englishman engaged in a quarrel with his companion, who was drawing a knife to stab him. The companion fled. The Englishman pursued to kill him, but when questioned, he admitted that he was going to kill his enemy, and that his enemy had tried to dissuade him, so he intended to kill him first. The governor-general said in a soothing tone, "In your country, the law is the same: those who kill will die. The one who dissuaded you loves you. Why do you want to kill him?" The Englishman became extremely angry and said, "Who are you? Dare you come to plead for him? Leave immediately, or I will let you feel the sharpness of my blade." The governor-general immediately returned to his office, ordered several subordinates to capture the Englishman, and brought him before him. The governor-general, dressed in formal attire, sat on the dais and commanded the prisoner to look up. The Englishman looked up and realized that he was the same person he had met in the tea house, so he cowered in fear and dared not move. The governor-general then stood up, walked to the steps, and with one hand grabbed his hair, inserted two fingers into his eyes, and the blood gushed out, and the eyeballs came out with them. He then wiped his fingers with his sleeve and said, "Thief, let me first teach you the methods of my department. I will wait to capture your companion and kill him as well." Whenever he gouged out eyes, he would do it personally. After gouging them out, he would wipe his fingers with his sleeve, so the front of his robe was dyed red like cinnabar. This was something that the subordinates could not perform.

I privately think this method is for dealing with notorious thieves and cunning rascals. Even if they are not immediately executed, it can eliminate future troubles, not just to establish authority. However, you have now been investigated by the censor for using excessive punishment. A Fox Spirit In the early years of the dynasty, a person in a town was invited by a relative for a drink and returned home late at night. As he passed through Iron Shop Alley, he met a beautiful maiden with slow, hesitant steps, coming gracefully. When the autumn rain had just stopped, the road was muddy, and the woman asked him to carry her across the muddy part. He gladly agreed and carried her straight to his home. The woman learned that it was his home and smiled, her eyes turning sideways. "Foolish boy, you carried me here, what do you want?" she said. He also smiled and said, "You try to guess." "Then you should be stiff," she replied. He was wildly happy, pulled her into the room, and she did not reject it lightly. After intimate relations, the woman looked around and saw the bare walls and the bed with worn quilts and mats. She laughed and said, "Poor to this extent, yet still think of being a dandy or scholar?" The man felt a bit ashamed and then suspected her of being a spirit. The woman already knew and said, "I am indeed not human, but I will not harm you, do not be afraid." The man longed for her beauty and was not afraid, but worried about her being too weak to endure hardship. "If we can vow to be together until old age, this is also easy," she said. He was even happier.

The next day, he happened to go out, and when he returned to his room, the furniture and bedding were suddenly and brightly renewed. Surprised, he asked where this came from, and the woman replied, "I just borrowed them from my sister's house." At dinner, he sighed and said, "With guests but no wine, facing each other is also pointless." The woman did not answer. In an instant, then a bowl of wine and a chicken were displayed on the table. From then on, whenever he needed something, it would arrive without delay. Once, he jokingly asked, "You have such supernatural power, why not earn a thousand gold to wash away this scholarly pretense?" She replied, "I have an old connection with you, so I take the risk to do this. For ordinary people, eating and drinking are determined, going beyond this might not be a blessing for you." After a month or so, the woman claimed to go to visit her sister's house and returned after several days. When questioned, she said, "My sister happened to catch a minor illness, so I stayed behind to help her." He suspected she had another secret and told her, "The house of Shen Zongshan is good, don't go to trouble him." The woman said, "That house has high walls and many dogs. Besides, he is a fortunate person, it's not good to get close to him."

Not long after, I was imprisoned for accidentally injuring a life while drunk. The woman visited me morning and evening, often bringing food and medicine; the jailers were unaware of her identity. When the rebel leader Qi rose in revolt in Shandong, one day the official army was about to engage in battle when suddenly a woman appeared in a white brocade battle robe, wearing a pheasant's tail on her head, wielding a green-sunk spear, and leaping onto her horse, leading a dozen or so warriors into the enemy ranks. The rebels were utterly defeated, and Qi was captured. When asked who she was, the woman identified herself as my wife. The general reported her deed, and I too received a reduced sentence, being sent to Jinzhou for military service. On the day of my departure, the woman asked to go with me, but I refused, saying, “There are guards present.” She replied, “What does he know? When we reach the Huai River, I have another destination.” When they reached the Huai River, she parted from me. Two years later, I received a pardon. Returning past the Huai River, the innkeeper said, “Since the guest left last year, there has been a great demon causing disturbances here; I dare not ask you to stay.” I suspected it was the woman and insisted on staying in the upper room. That night, I drank alone by the lamp. Suddenly, the woman arrived in elegant attire, bowing deeply to me and saying, “Are you also unharmed, my lord?” I was overjoyed and invited her to drink with me, asking about what had transpired. She said, “It was only for your sake that I caused the bedchamber to be exclusive to me, allowing no one else. Now, I am glad we can go together.” The next day, I took her with me. Passing through Suzhou, five days later there was a dragon boat festival, and I accompanied the woman to watch it. She drank heavily and fell asleep with her head resting on my knee, and immediately transformed into a fox.

At first, a local official named Mr. Zhang on a neighboring boat saw the woman through her window and found her beautiful. By then he invited her to leave, offering to buy her for five hundred gold coins. Mr. Zhang thought to himself: "She is of a different kind, and ultimately not a suitable match. If I remain faithful to my principles, when will I attain wealth and status?" So he signed the contract and returned. The woman had already realized what had happened and scolded him, saying, "Heartless thief! I have treated you kindly, and now that I have just managed to return alive, how could you be so heartless as to discard me for a few hundred gold coins? I cannot bring myself to kill you, but the jewelry box in the chest must be returned to me." After saying this, she reached into the chest, took the box, and put it in her bosom, then walked straight out onto the shore and left, shedding tears. This box was a gift she had given Mr. Zhang when he went to Jinzhou. Whenever he was in a difficult situation, if he opened it, there would be several gold coins inside. Because of this, Mr. Zhang was able to avoid freezing and starving in his military service. Now he regretted betraying her, but it was too late to go back. With a sense of regret, he untied the boat and went home. After a year, he eventually died of poverty and hunger.

Appendix: The Biography of Yuan's Family During the Guangde period, there was a scholar named Sun Ke who, having failed the imperial examinations, traveled to Luoyang. He arrived at the side of Hui Wang Pool and saw a large mansion. The locals pointed out that this was the residence of the Yuan family. Sun Ke went straight to the door and knocked on it. After a long time, a woman suddenly opened the door. Her radiant face was captivating and stunningly beautiful, like pearls reflecting moonlight and willows just beginning to show their graceful charm. The fragrant and clean room was as pure as jade, free from dust. Sun Ke suspected that the woman was the daughter of the house and secretly peeked at her. The woman picked up a daylily in the courtyard and stood there in deep thought for a long time, then composed a poem: "Those who see this forget their worries, while this is seen as the same as rotten grass. Only the green mountains and white clouds can express my feelings." After reciting the poem, she came to pull back the curtain. Suddenly seeing Sun Ke, she was startled and blushed, then entered the house. She sent a servant to question him and said, "This young lady lost her parents at a young age and has no relatives or family connections. She has not yet married and is looking for a match." After a long time, the woman came out, even more beautiful than what was seen before. She ordered the maidservant to bring tea and fruits and said, "The young master does not have a house. You can move your belongings here." Sun Ke, who was not yet married, saw the woman's grace and beauty and sent a matchmaker to marry her.

Three or four years old, he suddenly met his maternal or paternal cousin Zhang Xianyun and stayed at his cousin's house. Zhang Xianyun held his hand and whispered secretly, saying, "Brother, have you ever received teachings from the Daoist tradition? I just observed your appearance, and there is a strong aura of evil. I wonder what else you have encountered?" The subject denied having any prior encounters. Zhang said, "Humans are endowed with yang energy, while demons receive yin energy. When the soul suppresses the spirit completely, a person can achieve immortality; when the spirit suppresses the soul completely, the person will die immediately. Therefore, ghosts and monsters are formless, embodying pure yin, while immortals have no shadow, embodying pure yang. The waxing and waning of yin and yang, the battle between soul and spirit, are all reflected in one's appearance. I observed your appearance, and I see that yin and yang have encroached upon their rightful places, evil has interfered with the righteous, true essence has been depleted, intellect and ability have gradually declined; vital fluids have been drained, roots and foundations are unstable, bones will turn to dust, and your face is no longer rosy. You will surely be consumed by strange phenomena, how can you remain hidden?" The subject was startled and realized, then explained the reasons for his marriage. Zhang was shocked and said, "This is it!" The subject said, "My life has been full of difficulties, and I have long suffered from hunger and cold. Because of this marriage, it seems like a respite. I cannot betray righteousness, what should I do?" Zhang became angry and said, "A true man cannot serve others, how can he serve ghosts? And which is closer, righteousness or life? Suffering the consequences, and consider the favors of ghosts and monsters?" He gave the subject a precious sword and said, "This is also a lesser version of Gan Jiang. Any ghost or monster who sees it will disappear. If you carry it into a secluded room, you will surely see their plight." The subject accepted the sword, and Zhang left.

Kek carried the sword and hid it indoors, but eventually showed a difficult expression. The Yuan family suddenly realized this and became greatly angry, saying, "Your poverty and sorrow, I have made you happy and carefree. Ignoring gratitude and righteousness, you thus engaged in wrongful acts. Using your heart in this way, even dogs and pigs would not eat the rest!" Kek felt ashamed and knelt down, saying, "I was taught by my cousin, not out of preconceived intention." The Yuan family then searched for the sword, broke it into small pieces, as if breaking a light lotus root. The Yuan family laughed and said, "Zhang, a mere youth, did not teach his cousin with righteousness, causing him to act so cruelly. But observing your heart, definitely not like this. I have been your match for several years now, what do you worry about?" Kek only slightly felt at ease. After more than ten years, the Yuan family had raised two children. They managed the household very strictly and did not like mixed elements.

After Ke arrived in Chang'an, he visited his old friend Wang Jìn, a high-ranking official, and was subsequently recommended by Wang to Zhang Wànqǐng in Nankang, where he was appointed as a military or administrative official. Ke took his family with him, and whenever Yuan encountered green pines and high mountains, she would gaze fixedly for a long time, as if feeling uneasy. Upon arriving in Duanzhou, Yuan said, "Halfway from here, there is Xiasan Temple. My family once had a disciple monk named Huì, who lived in seclusion in this temple. It has been several decades since we last parted. The monk's conduct is very high, he can distinguish form and substance, and is good at removing impurities. If he prepares a meal for us, it would be quite beneficial for our southern journey." Ke then prepared the necessary food for the meal. When they arrived at the temple, Yuan joyfully changed her clothes and groomed herself, took her two sons to the monk's monastery, as if she were familiar with the path. She then held a jade ring and offered it to the monk, saying, "This is an old object from the monastery." The monk did not understand. After the meal, several dozen wild monkeys jumped down from tall pines, ate on the platform, and then screamed, grasping vines and jumping. Yuan was shocked, and soon took a pen and wrote on the temple wall, saying, "Tearing apart affection and serving this heart, without reason, changes almost submerge. It would be better to follow companions and return to the mountains, emitting a long cry deep in the mist." She then threw the pen on the ground. Comforting her two sons, she wept silently and told Ke, "Farewell, farewell, I will say goodbye forever!" She then tore her clothes and transformed into an old monkey, following the screaming ones and jumping from tree to tree. As she approached the deep mountains, she looked back. Ke was shocked for a long time, comforted his two sons, and wept deeply.

Inquiring of an old monk, the monk then realized and said, "This monkey was raised by me when I was a novice monk. The jade ring, a precious gift from a person from the Hela Kingdom, was also carried around the monkey's neck at that time. Now I have finally realized." Kek, filled with profound sorrow, moored the boat for six or seven days, took his two sons back on the boat, and could no longer take up the post. This account was written by Tang Dynasty's Gu Xiong. I love how it excels in description in the narrative. In the middle, it discusses the boundary between humans and demons with the precision of the *Yellow Court* and *Yin Fu* classics, and the story itself serves as a powerful warning. Therefore, I excerpt it and append it here.

The External Historian said: "The Grand Historian said, 'A commoner once said, "Who knows what is benevolence and righteousness? Those who pursue their own benefit are considered virtuous."'" Gu Yijie, the Master of Guizhong, said, "When people are in distress, they never forget a kind word from someone." How much more so for the descendants of the Yuan family who are still alive? Moreover, the young man Sun, being poor and unable to marry, suddenly obtained a person of supernatural qualities who could raise a child and manage the household, ensuring the continuation of the Sun family's lineage forever. They lived together for over a decade, and their relationship was always harmonious. What wrong did the Yuan family commit against him? If they did not wrong him, then it was acceptable for him to be with them and to marry into their family. How could one be so cruel as to use a sharp sword based on the words of one person? If the young man were misled by Zhang's alarming words and ignored the old friendship, how could he escape the same fate as the certain student who was harmed by a fox spirit? Fortunately, his conscience was not completely lost, he hesitated, and in the end, he was understood by the Yuan family and managed to preserve his life. Nevertheless, the situation was still perilous.

**A Wedding in ZhiLi** A certain old man from ZhiLi was wealthy. He had a son whose nature was bright and refined, and the old man regarded him as the family's treasure. When the boy grew a little older, he was betrothed to the daughter of a family from the same town. At the age of eighteen, he was married to her, though the daughter had just reached the age of *jī* (the traditional coming-of-age ceremony for women). Not long after, the wedding date had arrived, but the groom suddenly fell seriously ill. The old man then sent a matchmaker to the bride's family, explaining his intentions: "Though the groom's illness is not life-threatening, the physician has said that if he were to be made to go out and fetch the bride at this time, there might be an unexpected disaster. If you would kindly grant us some leeway and allow us to skip the ceremony of the offering of geese, we will still prepare a carriage and attendants, and bring your daughter to complete the ceremony. This would save a great deal." The parents of the bride agreed. The matchmaker returned and reported that the next day they would go to fetch the bride. However, the groom's illness had become critical, and he was truly unable to perform the ceremony. She was hastily sent into the bridal chamber, but throughout the night there was commotion, and he could not even lie down to rest. The next day, his son died, and the bride had not yet undergone the *miaojian* ceremony (the formal ritual introducing her to the husband's ancestors). This was an event that occurred in September of the 25th year of the Daoguang era.

Before this, the master was about to host a banquet and summoned a butcher to slaughter a pig. The pig was repeatedly slaughtered without any blood coming out, and the same happened with chickens, ducks, and other animals.
