The cook who prepared the pig's feet found that each one turned a deep red, as if smeared with fresh blood. Those who understood recognized this as an ominous sign, but the master remained deluded and did not know when to stop, leading to this mistake. Alas! The historian remarked: I heard this from Ding Zixiang. At the time, Xu Ruqiao was also present and said sympathetically, "This woman has not yet had the temple ceremony; it is acceptable to marry her." I said: If she has already had the temple ceremony and has consummated the marriage with a certain man, then forcing a young woman of sixteen or seventeen, who has no orphaned children to care for, to end her life as a widow—can one feel at ease about it? However, this woman has already entered her husband's household; this is not like an object, which cannot be used and can therefore be resold to others. Especially in today's world, even within the confines of the home, people all like to pretend to uphold names and principles; some even marry without being formally engaged, or commit suicide to follow their husband. This is certainly the impulsive act of young people, which gentlemen do not wish to see, but the law still does not prohibit it.

Was her husband's death already after the wedding? In ancient times, Song Boji refused to leave the hall and met with disaster; the virtuous ones called her a daughter but not a wife. A woman's departure from her home should not be taken lightly, and even more so during the time of marriage. Therefore, the *Zengzi's Questions* states: "When the wedding day was set as auspicious, but the bride died, the groom would wear mourning clothes and pay his respects, and after the burial, he would remove them. The same is true if the husband dies." The phrase "the same is true" means that the groom would also wear mourning clothes to pay his respects. "After the burial, he removes them" indicates that the mourning period is not completed. The reason the mourning period is not completed is that it is not the proper attire for a wife. If it is not considered the proper attire for a wife, then she could be remarried to another family. However, this is said for those who have not yet entered the door. For those who have already entered the door, how could this example be applied again? Alas, the old man requested it, and the girl's parents agreed; all these are ordinary people troubling themselves unnecessarily. As for this girl, her situation is now beyond recovery. How can one help but sigh! A Ship Official from Linyi named Xu Langchuan reported that the foreign barbarians all go naked; men cover their bodies with bamboo tubes, and women hide their forms with leaves—this is what is called the Land of the Naked. Though accustomed to being naked, they still feel ashamed of being uncovered. They only trade with others at night, sniffing gold in the dark to discern good and bad. In the morning, all is as he said. According to *The Eight Continents' Translation History*, they are the people of the land of the Rakshasas, to the east of Pali. Their people have red hair and black bodies, beast-like teeth, and eagle-like claws. They trade with the people of Linyi only at night, and during the day, they cover their faces.

There is also a land of the Rakshasas, situated in the vast ocean of the East. When Tian Yiting Wen was serving as the governor of Guangzhou, there was a scholar named Huang Zican, who could not hear with his ears but instead heard with his eyebrows. This is especially remarkable, for it is not only that cows hear with their noses and dragons hear with their horns, but also that unusual qi (vital energy) is concentrated in objects. It is said that Song Gongluo of Shangqiu was skilled in appreciation, able to distinguish the authenticity of paintings and calligraphy in the dark, never making a mistake. This skill was different from simply feeling the quality of the paper and silk. In our county, there was Shen Binggu, the son of Mr. Qingzai, who was blind in both eyes and could not take one step outside. However, he enjoyed playing cards with others, feeling the cards and pairing them up to discard them, and even the most skilled players could not defeat him. This is particularly strange. During the Gengchen year of Daoguang, Mr. Yao Qiunong was the chief examiner in Guangdong. There were candidates in the examination whose essays used the word "Buddha's time." When recommending the candidates, Mr. Yao dismissed those who used the word "Buddha's time" because it came from Buddhist texts. Later, during the Gengchen year of Daoguang, Mr. Yao was the chief examiner as the president of the metropolitan examination. Among the candidates in the third exam, one used the year "Zhen Guan" in his red scroll, and he was dismissed because "Zhen Guan" is a year of the Han dynasty. Someone composed a couplet to mock him: "Buddha's time comes from Buddhist scriptures, and the Bodhisattva calls out in pain while the scholar laughs." "Zhen Guan" is called a year of the Han dynasty, and Emperor Wu sighs while Emperor Taizong is surprised. The matter is indeed laughable. However, Mr. Yao has a solid foundation in learning, and such mistakes should not have occurred. Perhaps it was done by his colleagues in the examination hall.

Liu Xie, courtesy name Yin Yuan, was from Wu Prefecture. His father once served as an official in Jiangyin and accumulated considerable wealth during his career. Liu Xie was vulgar and dull-witted, and he often spent all day struggling to write, yet could never produce coherent prose. When he finally managed to write something, it was filled with errors and confusion, such as writing "golden roots" incorrectly and "purple phoenixes" in reverse, leaving anyone who saw it utterly amazed. His father, therefore, sought to help him enter the imperial academy by connections. After his father's death, every day of mourning was marked by extremely inadequate rituals. His wife criticized him for this, and he retorted, "They never thought about providing for their descendants; how could they ever have thought to feed their descendants?" He explained that his father had been fond of forming friendships, and with the protection of his grandmother, his youngest son was called an old prostitute. Liu Xie was extremely frugal, counting rice grains while cooking, and from his servants to his children, they often went hungry. He was especially harsh on his wife, and he knew nothing of relatives or friends, indulging only in luxury for himself. He kept a maid named Zhang, who was fierce and violent. Because she was skilled in the bedroom, he took her as a concubine. The concubine demanded gluttonous food, and Liu Xie would not be satisfied without meat. One day, the concubine wanted to eat eel and ordered a maid to buy it. She thought the amount was too small and suspected the maid of stealing some, so she threw the container to the ground and scolded her. The maid strongly defended herself against the false accusation, but the concubine grew even angrier and ordered a servant to seize her, cut off her tongue, and kill her immediately. It turned out that the maid was exceptionally beautiful, and Liu Xie had been intimate with her. The concubine discovered this and, being extremely jealous, had long wanted to kill her but had not acted on it. At this point, she finally repaid her long-standing grudge. When the maid's father reported the matter to the authorities, Liu Xie bribed a certain county magistrate to cover up the crime by blaming another maid. The maid could not withstand the torture and was forced to falsely confess. After detailed reporting, the higher officials quickly consulted the Ministry, and the maid was led away to await execution in autumn.

Liu's family suddenly fell into ruin, and his concubine could not endure the poverty and hardship. She then plotted with Liu to resort to prostitution for survival. Liu readily agreed, saying, "When hunger and cold come, one does not care about shame; the ancients have already taught me this." He consented. Although the woman was only of average beauty, her skill in prostitution attracted many customers, and their door was filled with carriages and horses. Liu was grateful for the gift of life she had given him and also feared her power, going to great lengths to please her. Once, he purchased a white-line drawing of a spring palace by Master Shi Tao and ordered wine to appreciate it. As the wine arrived, he was just about to open the first page when he suddenly heard a very urgent knocking on the door. Startled, he got up to see several officials from the county arrive with official documents, arresting Liu and the woman and taking them away. At that time, the previous magistrate had been dismissed from office for embezzling relief grain, and the new magistrate, a Jinshi, came to replace him. Upon entering the office, he saw a rabbit crouching by the door and found it strange. After a while, he realized, "There is a rabbit in the door, which indicates injustice. Is there perhaps an unjust case in the county?" Later, he dreamed of a woman with her hair loose kneeling in front of his bed, opening her mouth to spit blood, as if pleading for justice. She had no tongue. In a daze, another woman was crying beside her, and after a while, she got up, went to the courtyard, took a bow, and drew it with great effort, about to shoot the magistrate. The magistrate woke up in alarm. In the morning, he ordered the arrest of a maid who cried out for justice. Upon examination, she was the same woman he had dreamed of the night before. He then thought that the person drawing the bow to shoot was the character "张." He immediately called officials to bring Liu and the woman, and they confessed after interrogation. The maid was released, and the woman was executed. Liu was charged with being an accomplice in embezzlement and sentenced to death by hanging, dying in prison from illness.

According to the *Yi Jing* (Medical Canon), the tongue is the sprout of the heart. Therefore, severing the tongue leads to death, but there are also cases where it does not. In Zhili, there was a man named Wu Zhiquan who was morally corrupt and loved to pursue women, even among his own relatives. One day, he attempted to rape his daughter. She pretended to agree and let him enter her bedroom. Just as her skirt was about to be unfastened, she first demanded his tongue. Wu was wildly delighted and extended his tongue to lick hers. In one bite, she bit off most of his tongue. He cried for help, and family members all gathered. They seized him and took him to the authorities, who sentenced him to death for committing incest. However, he did not die immediately. In a certain town, there was a magistrate named Shen who had once served as a military secretary and became wealthy through legal expertise. He was appointed as the magistrate of Donghe County through official channels. Shen had a fondness for young boys. One such boy, favored for his youth and beauty, was later dismissed for being arrogant and disobeying orders. The boy harbored resentment and asked someone to help him get back into service. Shen agreed, and the boy returned, prostrated himself, and apologized. Shen found him charming and pitiable and embraced him. The boy intentionally engaged in intimate behavior and bit off half of Shen's tongue, causing Shen to faint to the ground. The boy then went out to the office, shouting that Shen had attempted to rape him and that he had bitten off his tongue in anger. He threw himself into the Yellow River to die. Shen was dismissed from his position for tarnishing his official reputation, but he did not die.

All these are events of the Jiaqing Wu Yin year (1818). Liu Shao Bao, also known as Liu Sheng Wu, served as a general at home. At that time, bandits broke out in Ningzhou, and the situation was very alarming. The governor sent the county magistrate and the prefect to request aid, but Liu Shao Bao refused on the grounds of illness. The governor then sent the provincial governor and the censor to request aid, but Liu Shao Bao firmly declined. They were all very angry and ordered a doctor to go and verify. If it was found to be false, they would impeach him. The doctor arrived and found Liu Shao Bao lying weakly on his bed. Everyone was in a state of panic and had no idea what to do. Soon after, there was a sudden report that General Liu had defeated the bandits and returned. Everyone was greatly startled and said, "The general left, and we certainly did not know, but why did he use such cunning?" Liu Shao Bao replied, "The bandits are descendants of Chen Youliang, who have been plotting for generations to wait for an opportunity. Now that they have acted, it is not easy to stop them. If they knew I was coming, they would be well-prepared, so I attacked them secretly. This is military strategy." Everyone then admired his wisdom. At that time, the emphasis was on literature, and at every official gathering, Liu Shao Bao sat below the scholars. When the local gentry held a public banquet, the funds were insufficient, and they would look at Liu Shao Bao and call him by name, saying, "Sheng Wu will handle this." Liu Shao Bao always contributed ten times more than the others. Sometimes when he was drunk, he would have his attendants ride horses to entertain the guests. Liu Shao Bao would often get on horseback and dance with two swords. The spectators only saw white mist swirling around, dazzling their eyes, and could not see his face. Although they found his skills amazing, they only regarded it as a form of entertainment.

The Son of the Grand Tutor, named Nianshu, was agile and possessed his father's style. However, the Grand Tutor's skill with hidden arrows was supreme, allowing him to penetrate tough armor from a distance of fifty to sixty steps; Nianshu could only reach about twenty steps and was unable to pierce the armor, thus lacking his father's bravery. The Grand Tutor also had a daughter who was brave. She married into a certain family, and her bridal dowry was rich and abundant. One day, a group of bandits broke into her home, causing the entire household to be terrified. The daughter ordered her maid to fetch soft armor and put it on herself, then led the maid out with a knife to kill the bandits. The bandits could not withstand her and fled. According to the *Ming History*, the Grand Tutor's achievements in pacifying Myanmar, Luo Xiong, Bo Chieftain, the Japanese, and the Luos are detailed, but the pacification of Ningzhou was omitted because the bandits were quickly defeated. Nevertheless, his ingenious tactics were greatly significant. As for the current trend of despising martial prowess, influential officials who married into the family of the Grand Tutor were stripped of their official status. The decline of the Ming dynasty had its reasons. Note: The Grand Tutor was most skilled in performing the feat of standing on one's toes, capable of jumping vertically and horizontally ten zhang. The performer of this feat, as described in the *Zuo Zhuan*, is Wei Cuo, who could jump three hundred times and leap three hundred times. The *Book of Han* mentions Gan Yanshou, a young man from a good family who served as a member of the Imperial Guards, skilled in archery, throwing stones, and performing the feat of standing on one's toes, and who once jumped over the guard towers of the Imperial Guards.

Furthermore, this piece can be found in the *Zhang Guatian Collection*. In the original text, it states, "The *History of Ming* lists the achievements of the Shao Bao in pacifying the Miao, the Luo, the Bo, the Wokou, Korea, and the Luoluo," which seems to be an error, as the Shao Bao's campaign against the Wokou was originally conducted in collaboration with Korean forces. Therefore, these three characters have been specifically deleted. Huang Zizhong During the reign of the Chongzhen Emperor, Yu Zhongcheng's collection and Tan Yu formed a literary society in Nanjing. Huang Zizhong, also known as Shizhai, visited and joined the society, forming a close friendship with them. Huang Zizhong was always mindful of propriety, and the others admired him, but they found him somewhat rigid. They decided to test him. There was a woman named Gu, who was exceptionally beautiful, intelligent, and well-versed in literature and history. She played music and sang, and anyone who saw her was captivated. One day, it snowed heavily, and a banquet was held at Yu's garden. Gu was called to serve the wine, and Huang Zizhong showed no displeasure. The others took turns toasting and drinking, becoming thoroughly drunk. When they sent Huang Zizhong to bed, they specially arranged a bed with a pillow, quilt, and mat. They asked Gu to remove her outer clothes. They then locked the door, and the others waited outside. Huang Zizhong woke up in a panic, unable to find his clothes. He covered himself with the quilt and told Gu to lie on the mat. The mat was thick and narrow, making it impossible to turn over, so he asked her to join him under the quilt. Gu then moved closer to him, and Huang Zizhong calmly said, "It's unnecessary." He turned his body inward, and after a few breaths, he fell asleep. At the fourth watch of the night, he woke up and turned his face outward. Gu pretended to be asleep and did not notice, but she lay close to him. Huang Zizhong slept soundly as before. The next morning, Gu came out and described the situation. She also said, "You gentlemen are scholars, composing poetry and drinking wine, which is all for pleasure. Achieving the highest spiritual and moral ideals, becoming loyal and filial, ultimately belongs to Mr. Huang."

When the Ming dynasty fell, he was captured in Jinling (Nanjing). In prison, he daily recited the *Shangshu* (Book of Documents) and *Zhouyi* (Book of Changes), and after several days, his appearance became more robust. The night before his execution, an elderly jailer, holding needle and thread, wept and said to him, "This is the final service I render to my master!" The prisoner replied, "I die upright, which is called a proper end. Why do you grieve?" Friends brought wine and meat to bid farewell, and he drank and ate as usual. He slept soundly until dawn, washed, combed, and changed clothes, then said to his servant, "I previously promised to write a scroll. I cannot neglect my word." He mixed ink and spread paper, first writing in small regular script, then in running script. The scroll was very long, so he finished it with large characters. He sealed it with an official stamp, then went out to face execution. The scroll was kept in a certain family in Jinling. Concubine Gu, upon receiving the news, blamed her husband for his failure to die and decided to hang herself first. Her husband did not use her.

The historian comments: These anecdotes of Mr. Huang, recorded in the *Wangxi Wenji*, are written alongside those of Left Zhongyi Gong. Loyal ministers and righteous scholars from ancient times have all taken the world as their responsibility. Even in times of turmoil and great change, they still strive to support the world single-handedly, and if necessary, they will die for the country, considering themselves unworthy of fulfilling their duties. Therefore, when they calmly accept their fate, even the names of loyal and righteous individuals are difficult to speak of, and they have no regard for personal interests, pleasures, or material gains. I have read Buddhist texts, and Mahakasyapa said, "The body of a Buddha is indestructible by worldly fire." The *Yogacarabhumi-sastra* states, "Mara has four daughters, who are extremely beautiful. They come before the Bodhisattva and display various postures. The Bodhisattva, through his moral resolve, causes the four daughters to appear old and ugly, leading them to retreat in shame." The power of desire can overcome reason, enough to make even Guan Yu and Wu Zixu lose their courage, Zhang Liang and Zhuge Liang lose their wisdom, but only immortals and Buddhas can control it. However, immortals and Buddhas remain unmoved, while sages and virtuous men have both active and passive states. Despite the severe misfortune that befell Left Gong, his few solemn words still carry a sense of life and vitality. If he had a person like Gu Shu in his daily life, and they lived together in a closed room and shared the same bed, could one expect to move him even slightly? This is the commentator's intention to reveal hidden truths and subtle meanings. As for the final words of Mr. Huang before his death, destiny is predetermined, and this is also true for both Left Gong and Mr. Huang. But was this their original intention? This is something to mourn deeply for both Left Gong and Mr. Huang.

**Title:** Hanging Together **Text:** "Thus have I heard": In the capital, there was a wealthy family named Lu who married a woman. The couple was both handsome and graceful, and their relatives and friends all regarded them as if they were divine beings. Observing their demeanor, the couple also greatly enjoyed each other. The next day, at dawn, the door did not open. Peeping through a hole in the window, they saw the couple hanging side by side. Looking at their bedding, it was already in a state of harmony. The maids and servants all said, "They had already taken off their clothes and makeup the previous day, so why did they die dressed in their finest attire?" Even if it were heard by Gao Yao, the most just judge of antiquity, it would be incomprehensible.

According to the *Zhongxing Jueyao Ci Xuan* by Huā'ān: Qiantang Wu Li San, courtesy name Zihe, has five volumes of *Shunshou Lao Ren Ci*. There was a Taoist named Tao, who had a deep emotional bond with Wang Sheng. Although they met briefly like floating萍 (萍水相逢), they would ultimately be separated forever after death, so they both drowned in West Lake on a moonlit night. He composed the poem *Shuang Tian Xiao Jiao* to mourn them: "Linked rings are easy to break, but the heart-tie is hard to untie. The foolish but talented man and woman, their emotional bond is heavy, and they fear separation. Their feelings are sincere, their paths are cut off, and they both sink into the vast misty waters. Where does the fragrant soul drift? The moon over the long bridge, the moon over the short bridge." This event is also recorded in the *Xihu Zhi*. Thus, there are indeed such emotionally痴 (痴) people in the world. After the union of the Lü couple, didn't they also die for love? **Sacrifice** In the fifteenth year of the Chongzhen era, Hong Chengchou was defeated by our dynasty. At the time, it was rumored that he had died a martyr's death, and Emperor Chongzhen granted sixteen altars and personally composed a eulogy to honor him. Later, when our dynasty's forces advanced south of the Yangtze River, Hong went on to govern Jiangnan, Sichuan, and Hubei provinces. When he entered the pass, a local person welcomed him and asked to meet, and Hong agreed. The person entered and knelt for a long time, took out the emperor's personally composed eulogy from his sleeve and read it once, then cried and left.

A Note: Chengzhou's talent, among the officials at the end of the Ming dynasty, seemed suitable for military affairs. The historical records of his military achievements are also noteworthy. However, Huang Lirou, a respected scholar, once criticized the exaggerated claims of his military achievements, indicating that they cannot be fully trusted. The *Yanbo Zaji* (Notes on the Roof) states: During Chengzhou's defeat, his family had already prepared and distributed his biography to mourners. The Chongzhen Emperor was performing a ritual involving fourteen altars when news of Chengzhou's surrender reached him. Later, Jin Sheng raised an army in Huizhou but was defeated along with his disciple Jiang Tianyi. Chengzhou ordered Jin Sheng to surrender, and Jiang Tianyi recited a royal decree to shame him. After joining the new dynasty, Jiang Tianyi returned to the capital and died there, and his family prepared a new biography that did not mention his previous loyalty to the Ming dynasty, starting instead from his joining the new regime. Someone with a light-hearted attitude compiled the two biographies into one book for amusement. The *Ming Shi* (History of the Ming Dynasty) also states: In the fourteenth year of the Chongzhen era, Qing forces besieged Songshan. Chengzhou and Qiu Minyang swore to defend the city to the death, but without external support, their supplies of fodder and grain were depleted. By the second month of the following year, the siege had lasted for half a year. Due to exhaustion, the city was captured, and Chengzhou surrendered. Qiu Minyang refused to surrender and was killed, posthumously receiving the title of Right Deputy Censor-in-Chief, being honored with six altars, and having a temple built for him in the capital alongside Chengzhou. The emperor intended to personally attend the memorial service but stopped upon hearing that Chengzhou had surrendered. Anecdotes about Xiong Xiangmeng

The *Quan Xieshan Collection* records: Nisheng from Shining told me: His father once saw a book titled "Autumn Magistrate's Diary" written by his ancestor at the home of Chen from Cangqiao Village. The person who wrote this book was a prison official during the reign of Emperor Xizong. All the gentlemen killed by Wei Zhongxian, who were not sent to the Eastern Depot but to the Ministry of Justice, are recorded in the prison records. He said that Xiang Han, upon entering prison, had every meal and drink reported to Wei Zhongxian by the prison officials. However, Xiang Han showed no difference in his demeanor. He always slept with a vine pillow, regardless of the season, and never took it off. Every night when it was quiet, he would bow twice to the North Star and then place the vine pillow in front of it, but no one could understand his intention. When asked about this by Xiang Han, he would just smile and not answer. When the day of his execution came, Xiang Han remained calm, wrote a final memorial to the emperor, still speaking of border affairs, and also composed a final poem. The draft of his memorial was intercepted by the officials of the Ministry of Justice, who said, "How can a prisoner submit a memorial?" Xiang Han replied, "This is Zhao Gao's words. (Note: Twelve characters are missing here.) How can a holy court have such things?" He then calmly accepted his execution. At the time, there was an order to send his head to the nine border regions. When the officials of the Ministry of Justice tried to record his head, they found only an empty execution ground, with just a vine pillow. They were greatly shocked and warned each other not to reveal this. They secretly reported this to Wei Zhongxian, who then ordered the family members of the Xiong clan to be interrogated. A thorough search was conducted, but nothing was found. Wei Zhongxian had no solution and thus kept the matter secret. The head sent to the nine border regions was not the real one. After Wei Zhongxian's downfall, Zhao Bi repeatedly petitioned for the return of the public's head to be buried in Puzhou, knowing full well that it was not the real head, but merely using it to resolve this wrongful case.

This account is not mentioned in the wild histories of the Ming dynasty. I believe that Li Gong Yingbi's *Three Halls Notes* strongly describes the tragic execution of Xiang Min, which does not match this account. However, Chen Shi was a direct witness, so it is unlikely to be false. (This refers to Han Kuang, the Grand Secretary of Puzhou.) According to the draft history, it only records Xiang Min's achievements in protecting Liaodong, but does not mention his knowledge of divination. However, during his inspection of Liaodong in the 35th year of Wanli, there was a severe drought. He traveled to Jinzhou, prayed to the City God, and vowed to destroy the temple if it did not rain within three days. When he reached Guangning, after three days, he wrote a large white notice, sealed his sword, and sent someone to destroy the temple. Before they arrived, strong winds and thunderstorms broke out, and it rained heavily. The people of Liaodong considered it a divine intervention. Based on this, the events in prison are not groundless. Earthquake From *The Outline of Celestial Changes*: On the sixth day of May, the temple priest of the Fire God Temple in Hadamen saw the Fire God moving vigorously, as if descending from the hall, and hurriedly offered incense and knelt to say, "Lord Fire God, it is early outside, please do not move." The Fire God lifted his foot to step out, and the temple priest cried in sorrow and held him back. Just as they were pushing and resisting, the sound of the earthquake suddenly arose. There was a younger brother of a Zhou magistrate from Shaoxing, who was selected due to his brother's honor. He wanted to be a public servant, and had only been in the capital for three days when he bought a blue silk jacket from Caishikou and returned, swaying. On the way, he met six people. They had not finished bowing and greeting when his head suddenly flew off and sank into the wall by an inch. His eyes flew onto the wall of the opposite house, stuck there, and still moved. His eyebrows also stuck together. The six people were unharmed.

At the intersection of a guildhall in Guangdong, a private tutor opened school with thirty-two boys. After a loud noise, both the teacher and students disappeared. In Xuanfu, a newly appointed general was on Yuanhong Temple Street when, after another loud noise, he and his seven servants also disappeared. The injured men and women were all completely naked, not a thread on their bodies, and no one knew why. During the earthquake, a servant saw a woman passing by naked. Some covered their private parts with tiles, others with half a foot strap, some with half a quilt, and others with a piece of blanket. In an instant, dozens of people appeared. In the area around Chang'an Street, heads were flying down from the sky, some with eyebrows and noses, others with even a forehead, falling in a chaotic manner. A 5,000-pound stone lion flew out of the city gate. After the earthquake, someone reported that clothes all floated to the western hills, hanging on tree branches. In the training ground of Changping County, clothes were piled up, and household items, money, and jewelry were all there. Outside the De胜 Gate, there were especially many heads and arms falling.

Before, on the first day of the fifth month, the magistrate of Jinan, Shandong, went to the city god temple to burn incense. Upon reaching the temple gate, suddenly all of the magistrate's attendants fell into a trance. One attendant's wife came to see her husband and saw that her former husband, who had died many years ago, was serving as an attendant in the temple. The former husband said, "One cannot enter the temple; all the city gods are here registering." The *Chronicles of Strange Events* records: During the Xining era of the Song dynasty, in Wucheng County of Enzhou, a whirlwind came from the southwest, lifting roofs and uprooting trees. The magistrate's family and the people were all swept up into the clouds, and those who fell to the ground and died were countless. In the year Gengyin of the Daoguang era, the area around Zhili was split by an earthquake for at least a thousand li, and the number of those crushed to death was in the tens of thousands. However, none were blown away with their clothes and limbs. But this record states it so clearly. According to the *Ming History* (Emperor Chronicles and Five Elements Records), there is no record of a disaster on the sixth day of the fifth month. However, the *Ming History* has many omissions and errors. For example, on the day Jia Yin of the third month of the fourth year of Tianqi, and the day Bing Zi of the sixth month, the capital experienced an earthquake, which is recorded in both the *Emperor Chronicles* and the *Five Elements Records*. Only the *Records* mention three earthquakes in the capital in the third year, but the *Emperor Chronicles* do not record them. The *Emperor Chronicles* mention a second earthquake in the capital on the night of the day Wu Wu of the third month of the fourth year, but the *Records* do not mention it. On the night of the day Geng Shen, there were three more tremors, but the *Records* only say there was a second tremor on the day Geng Shen. Thus, the records are not entirely reliable. Wang Qiushan

Mr. Qiuquan Wang was a renowned physician in our county. There was a wealthy man who was gravely ill and on the brink of death, and he invited Mr. Qiuquan. However, Mr. Qiuquan was just treating a nobleman and could not go. The wealthy man, unable to stop thinking about it, was deeply distressed in the middle of the night and told his son, "If I could see Mr. Qiuquan once before dying, I would not regret it." The son then hurried to Mr. Qiuquan's residence, knelt and urged him earnestly. Just as the illness of the nobleman Mr. Qiuquan was treating had improved, and after several more days the nobleman had recovered, Mr. Qiuquan was prepared a feast and entertained with wine, and valuable gifts were presented to him as a gesture of respect and gratitude. Mr. Qiuquan drank until he was thoroughly drunk and returned home. When he returned to his boat, he told his family, "Now I can keep my appointment with the rich man." Meanwhile, the servant sent by the wealthy man's son was already eagerly untied the boat and took up the oars. When they arrived at the wealthy man's house, they shouted out, "Mr. Wang has arrived!" The entire family was surprised and joyfully came out to greet him. Mr. Qiuquan was sound asleep, and his family woke him from his dream. The host had already put on his finest clothes and bowed respectfully to welcome Mr. Qiuquan into the boat. Mr. Qiuquan thanked the host for the late hour and requested to bathe and groom himself before seeing him the next morning. The host insisted, saying, "My father has been waiting to see you, clinging to life. Please honor us by coming, why should you bother to bathe and groom yourself?" He forced Mr. Qiuquan to come in. After diagnosing his pulse and giving him medicine, Mr. Qiuquan left. The host prepared a feast and bowed to Mr. Qiuquan, but Mr. Qiuquan merely waved his hand and thanked him. Returning to the boat, he undressed and lay down. When he woke up from his drunken sleep, he called his family and scolded them, "Lazy servant, neglecting your duties! Moreover, the rich man has been waiting for me for a long time, I should have gone to him at night, why are we still here?" His family replied, "We already diagnosed his pulse and gave him medicine, have you forgotten?" Mr. Qiuquan was greatly surprised and said, "Did you really give him medicine? I was truly drunk, I must have killed him!" He stamped his foot and hurriedly ordered them to untie the boat and return, saying that if they did not leave, they would be disgraced.

The family hurriedly untied the mooring ropes, while the host had already sent a servant to wait for Mr. Qiuquan. Upon hearing that Mr. Qiuquan was leaving, the servant immediately went inside to report. Soon after, the door opened, and several dozen lanterns were seen on the shore, conveying a message to stop Mr. Qiuquan. Mr. Qiuquan was unsure of what to do. Shortly thereafter, the host stumbled in, entered the boat, and bowed his head, tears streaming down his cheeks, and thanked him, saying, "My father has taken some of your medicine, and he has been sleeping soundly, as if the illness has vanished. If you stay, my father will live; if you leave, my father will surely die. Only you can continue to show compassion for him." Mr. Qiuquan doubted to himself, "Could such a thing be true? Surely they are deceiving me." However, he had no choice but to reluctantly follow them into the hall, the door closing behind him, his heart still pounding. After settling down, the host repeatedly expressed his gratitude, "How miraculous is your medicine, Mr. Qiuquan?" Mr. Qiuquan casually replied, "I have grasped the general idea from yesterday, but I would like to examine further." He then went inside to take a look, asked for the residue of the medicine to inspect, and privately reassured himself, "Fortunately, I was not mistaken!" He gave several more doses, which cured the illness, and returned home with great gains. People began to call him "Mr. Drunkard."

The historian says: This event is recorded in the *Wuqing Zhì*. In a drunken dream, the miraculous efficacy of medicine is so profound, is there any ghost or spirit involved? Yet, it also shows that medical knowledge is not entirely reliable, and life and death are determined by fate. A wry smile. In the *Shui Jing Zhu*, it is said that in the Gold Creek of the Jiu Mountain in Jiaozhi, there is a large snake, known as the *rán shé*. It is ten zhang long and seven or eight chi in circumference. It often waits in trees to ambush deer and other animals. When a deer or other animal passes, the snake lowers its head and coils around it. After a short while, the snake moistens its prey and then eats the head and horns. After three months, the bones of the prey emerge through the snake's skin. The local people first see the snake when it is motionless, and they kill it by inserting a bamboo stake from head to tail, then eat it, considering it a delicacy. Another account suggests that if a woman's garment is thrown at the snake, it will coil up and remain still, making it easy to capture. In the *Yì Wù Zhì* by Yang, the southern scholar, it is said: "The *rán shé* is a great snake, both large and long. Its colors are varied, and its skin has patterns like brocade. It eats pigs and swallows deer whole, and its fat is used to heal wounds. At banquets, it is served as a delicacy, either as a dish or a drink." This refers to the time when the snake's fat is used to nourish and heal, making it a valuable ingredient for banquets. Unfortunately, the snake's habit of swallowing deer whole means that it is often overfed, providing ample food for gluttons.

The large python is capable of swallowing deer and humans. It is extremely lascivious, often picking up discarded women's trousers and wearing them on its head, finding great pleasure in its playful movements. To capture it, one must first estimate the areas where it moves in and out, then set up several rows of stakes or poles, narrow enough to accommodate only the snake's body. A brave man armed with an olive stick lies in ambush among the stakes, while another person stands outside, waving a woman's skirt and trousers to lure the snake. The snake will raise its head six to seven feet high and follow the lure. The person retreats into the stakes, and the snake, being large, finds it difficult to turn in the narrow space. The brave man strikes the snake with the stick, retreating as he strikes, and several people take turns, watching as the snake's head lowers to the ground, at which point they are no longer afraid. With each strike, the snake's skin and flesh contract. There is a bubble or sac that, when burst, causes the snake to die due to blood coagulation (this is its protective gall), greatly reducing its strength. Often, this bubble is mistaken for a true gall, which is worth a double price. This account is consistent with the descriptions in *Lingnan Zaji* and *Shui Jing Zhu*. However, *Gui Hai Yu Heng Zhi* states that the large python is as thick as a pillar and often chases and eats deer. The village guards are skilled in capturing it; several of them wear flowers in their hair and approach the snake. The snake, attracted to the flowers, will watch them, gradually lowering its head. They shout "Red Nanny," and the snake lowers its head even more and remains still. A brave man cuts off the snake's head with a large knife, and the guards scatter, observing from a distance. After a while, the snake makes sudden and vigorous movements, uprooting small trees along the path, and dies when it exhausts its strength. The entire village feasts on the snake's meat. This method is even more extraordinary. However, the accounts from Shi Hu are based on personal experience and are therefore reliable.

Furthermore, according to *Lingbiao Lüyi* (Record of the Strange in Lingbiao), in Pu'an State, there are households that raise snakes. Every fifth day of the fifth lunar month, they carry the king cobras to the government office to wait on and extract their bile. I have personally witnessed this: the snakes are all placed in large cages lined with soft grass, coiling on top. Two people carry one snake on the ground, using several sticks to flip its body from the head, unable to turn. They then measure the size of its abdomen and use a sharp blade to cut open. The liver and gallbladder are exposed, and the bile is cut out, dried in the sun, and prepared for offering as tribute. The liver is then sewn back in, the wound closed with thread, and the snake returned to its cage. Some say that the snake is carried back and released into rivers or lakes. According to various texts, the king cobra is as powerful as they say, and it is impossible to achieve this while the snake is alive. Now observing what is recorded in this text, then obtaining it is indeed easy. Is this indeed true?

**The Records of Strange Things from the Southern Regions:** The fang of the python is six to seven inches long. The local people value it especially, believing it wards off misfortune and is beneficial for long journeys. Selling one such fang is worth several oxen. **Picking Lychees** The branches of the lychee tree are very soft and brittle. When the fruit is ripe, they hire experienced climbers to pick it. To prevent them from eating freely, they make a pact with them: "Do not stop singing, for if you stop, we will not pay you." The leaves are sparse and spread out, and people sit in the green shade, their voices rising and falling, singing continuously. Occasionally listening to this is quite pleasant to the ear, but upon careful thought, it makes one want to laugh. **Grandiose Talk** When I was young, I read the *Biography of Wang Mang*, and I noticed that all his legal decrees, commands, and even the names of officials and places were copied or imitated from ancient texts, used to indulge in superficial embellishment—not only did he imitate works like the *Da Gao*, but he did so in many other writings as well. One can imagine that at the time, he lived in this manner without doubt, in a dazed and foolish state. Later, I read the *Biography of the Later Shu Kingdom* from the *Old Book of Tang*: Emperor Taizong of Song sent Wang Quanbin and others to attack Shu, and Meng Chang sent Wang Zhao Yuan to resist them. Wang Zhao Yuan loved to read military treatises and considered himself an expert in strategy. When the army first set out from Chengdu, Meng Chang sent Li Hao and others to see them off. Wang Zhao Yuan held an iron *ruiyi* in his hand, comparing himself to Zhuge Liang. When he was drunk, he said to Li Hao: "This campaign is not just about defeating the enemy. I shall lead these two or three thousand fierce young warriors and take the Central Plains as easily as turning over one's palm." Later, he fought a battle with Wang Quanbin at Sanquan and was defeated, and in another battle at Jianmen Pass, he was captured. Truly, this is a tale worth a laugh!

Indeed, from ancient times there have been not a few such deluded individuals. In the Southern Yan kingdom, there was a man named Wang Shi from Laiwu. In the fourth year of the Jianping era of Murong De, Wang Shi used witchcraft to deceive the people, gathering a following of several thousand who gathered in the Laiwu Valley of Mount Tai. He proclaimed himself Emperor of Peace, appointed a hundred officials, and titled his father as the Supreme Emperor, his brother Lin as General of the East, and his younger brother Tai as General of the West. The emperor dispatched General-in-Chief Prince Guiyang to suppress and capture him, and Wang Shi was executed in the capital city. At the time of his execution, the people all cursed him for bringing about the destruction of his family. Someone asked where his father and brothers had gone, and Wang Shi replied, "The Supreme Emperor is in exile, and the Generals of the East and West were killed by rebellious troops. Though my body still lives, what is the use?" His wife Zhao scolded him, saying, "It is precisely because of this tongue of yours that you have come to this fate—how can you still speak such wild words as you face death?" Wang Shi replied, "Why does the empress not understand the will of Heaven? Since ancient times, has there ever been a state that did not fall, or a family that did not collapse?" The executioner struck his mouth with the ring of the blade, and Wang Shi looked up to the heavens and said, "I am about to die, but I will never change my imperial title." This is particularly laughable, and can be compared with Wang Mang’s words: "Heaven has bestowed virtue upon me; how can the Han army be of any use against me?"

During the years of Jia Tai and Kai Xi (1201-1207), Guo Ni held a high position in the court and often referred to himself as the reincarnation of Zhuge Liang (卧龙). After drinking, he would recite the lines from Du Fu's poem "Chancellor of Shu": "Three times the ruler sought him, twice he helped the state." Tchen Jingjun, a military official responsible for logistics, invited him to a banquet and said, "The complex machinery and strategies (木牛流马) will be entrusted to you." After the army suffered a defeat at Fuping, Guo Ni realized he could no longer recover and wept in front of his guests. At that time, Peng Fachuang was present and commented to others, "This is a Zhuge Liang with a position (此带汁诸葛也)."

When Lu Shike, a scholar from Yinzhou County, was staying at the home of a wealthy man in the county, during a period of dampness and mold, he ordered a servant to dry the bedding. The servant passed the task to a maid, who carried the bedding to the mistress's room to dry it. By evening, the maid secretly carried the mistress's sleeping slippers out with the bedding. Lu Shike, wanting to sleep, unfolded the bedding and saw the slippers, throwing them onto the bed canopy. Later, the host entered the study and saw the slippers. He waited until Lu Shike left, then took the slippers away in his sleeve. By late at night, he secretly ordered the mistress to knock on Lu Shike's door, holding a knife and following her. Lu Shike asked who it was, and the mistress softly replied, "It's me." "How can there be a female guest visiting a gentleman at night?" Lu Shike asked in disbelief. "Just open the door, I have something to say," the mistress said. "What can a female guest and a gentleman possibly have to say to each other?" Lu Shike asked. "If there is something to say, come together with the host tomorrow," Lu Shike replied. "If you don't come again, I will catch and give you to your master, don't think it's shameful," the mistress threatened. Seeing Lu Shike's resolute and principled stance, the host immediately replied, "Please open the door, I am here." Once inside, Lu Shike saw the host holding a knife and was greatly alarmed. The host said, "Do not be afraid." He showed Lu Shike the slippers and explained the situation in detail. Lu Shike smiled and said, "Fortunately, I am impartial, otherwise your knife would have been stained." The next day, he finally left.

After passing the imperial examination in the year 1589 (Wanli Jichou) of the Ming dynasty, he became an imperial scholar and eventually rose to the position of Minister of Justice. During this time, many people were following or supporting Wei Zhongxian, a powerful eunuch, but he stood alone and did not flatter anyone, ending his life with complete integrity. The story is recorded in the *Wuqing Zhi* (Records of Wuqing). Some say that this same event is also recorded in *Zi Bu Yu* (Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio), where the person is described as a military commander of a certain place, but it is unclear which account is correct. According to the *Jingxin Lu* (Record of Warnings), Chen Chunzhu was a guest of Jia Sidao, who upheld principles and was disliked by other guests and eunuchs. One day, the concubines competed for favor, and one of them secretly took a shoe from another and hid it under Chen Chunzhu's bed, intending to frame him. Jia Sidao entered the room and saw the shoe, becoming suspicious. At night, he called the concubine to the door and tried to question her, but Chen Chunzhu remained silent, which made Jia Sidao angry. Jia Sidao then realized there was nothing wrong and investigated the concubines to find out the truth. As a result, he deeply respected Chen Chunzhu and later appointed him as the military commander of Nan'an. The story is also performed in Yuan drama, which is similar to this account. It is possible that the story was intentionally created or fabricated. According to the records, it is unclear whether the story is based on real events or is a fabrication.

The Orangutan Non-Nonzi said: "In dense forests and deep waters, fish and birds have their own happy places. Yet, they are ultimately controlled by humans because they are greedy for the bait." According to the *Water Classic Annotations*: "The orangutan looks like a yellow dog and also resembles a hog or wild boar. It has a human face, a clear appearance, and a beautiful voice." The Prince of Taiyuan, Wang Gang, said: "Orangutans love wine and clogs. People placed them in the mountains and valleys. They often gather in groups of several, seeing the wine, they know that people have set it out, and they take it. Knowing the names of the ancestors who set it out, they curse and say, 'You want to kill me, quickly leave!' Then they return and say, 'Let's taste the wine.' Until they are drunk, they take the clogs and put them on, and are finally caught by people." Lu You wrote in his poem: "Even when drunk, the orangutan still wears clogs, and even in autumn, the swallows still build nests." This animal loves wine and clogs, as other books also describe, and this is not an empty claim. According to a Tang Dynasty novel: In the Fengxi area of Wuping Prefecture in Annam, there are orangutans. They look like beautiful women, understand human language, and know past events. Because of their love for wine, they are captured with clogs. Hundreds of them were kept in cages together, and when people wanted to eat them, the group would push the fattest one forward, and they would send him off with tears. Sometimes, they would offer the orangutan to the governor of Fengxi, covering the cage with a cloth. The governor would ask what was inside, and the orangutan in the cage would say, "There is only me and a pot of wine!" The governor would laugh and accept it. Indeed, these animals are so intelligent, their wisdom surpasses that of Lu You's dog, Huang'e, which could carry messages. Yet, they are still ultimately captured because of their love for wine and clogs. The *Rites of Zhou* says, "Orangutans can speak, but they are still not different from animals." Indeed, but are they the only animals?

**燕妒** In the home of the Niu family in Guangling, a pair of swallows were raising their young. The female was killed by a cat, and the male chirped incessantly for a long time before suddenly flying away. Soon after, he returned with another female and together they fed their chicks. The next day, one of the chicks fell to the ground, and by evening, all the chicks had died. Upon examination, it was found that their throats were full of thorns, indicating that the new female had poisoned them. Alas! Even birds show such malice towards their former offspring, yet the male remained oblivious, a tragic fate indeed! Last midsummer, in the distant fragrance study of Shen Litang's home, the swallows had already raised their chicks. One female was killed by a snake. The next day, the male returned with another female and together they fed the chicks. Soon, the snake came again, but the male was out and had not yet returned. The new female, startled, flew up and pecked the snake in the eye. The snake, just as it was about to swallow one chick, could not bite back and quickly spat it out, slithering away. The chick was nearly dead, but the female covered and protected it with her wings, occasionally bringing in herbs from the courtyard to feed it. Soon, the chick recovered. From then on, the new female would return quickly whenever she ventured out, never leaving her side day or night, and the snake never came back. Thus, this female could be considered a righteous kite for the chicks. **戒贪** According to *The Golden Tower*: During the reign of Duke Huan of Qi, he lay in his bed in the palace. White birds were flitting about, hungry and seeking food. The king opened the kitchen made of green silk and offered them food. Some birds, knowing the proper etiquette, did not eat and flew away; others, being content, ate a little and then left; some, being greedy, ate greedily and noisily. When they were full, their stomachs burst. This is a warning against greed.

I have seen a mosquito with a swollen abdomen that has turned red, its tail dripping blood continuously, yet it continues to suck blood. When I drove it away, it perched on a screen or table and could not fly again. At that moment, I wonder if the mosquito regrets its excessive greed. Teacher's Admonition In our neighborhood, there was a person who performed the role of a "wandering soul" (无常). Once, he slept for several days. One day, he suddenly woke up and immediately asked his family, "Did Mr. Cen from our neighborhood die yesterday?" His family replied, "Yes. Did you see him in the afterlife?" He said, "Yesterday, at the city god's hall in the temple, I saw the ghost soldiers take Mr. Cen there. The city god slammed the table in anger and said, 'What good deeds have you done in the world?' Mr. Cen bowed and wept, saying, 'I did not dare to commit any evil deeds in my life.' The city god angrily said, 'Look at your rabbit-like face and mouse-like features; do you have even a speck of education? How could you kill people as a cow doctor and then pretend to be a teacher to deceive money and mislead students?' He ordered the ghost soldiers to pull Mr. Cen down and beat him. Mr. Cen bowed again and said, 'Although I claim to be a doctor, I never had the opportunity to treat patients, so I did not kill anyone. I was forced by hunger and cold to teach children temporarily. My education consists of only a small part of the *Four Books*, and I did not fully understand them. Therefore, the fees I charged were very low, not exceeding two taels. I mainly taught the children of common laborers and uneducated people, who only wanted to save money and did not intend to study deeply. If it came to teaching anything beyond the *Four Books*, I did not dare to teach it, so I did not cause significant harm.' The city god's expression softened slightly.

Judge Gu took the book and examined it, reaching the first line of Cen’s entry, where it was annotated: “The gentle-sounding yellow bird (read with a variant tone).” The City God angrily said, “These people should only be reincarnated into the animal realm.” He then checked the next line, where it was noted: “To hate what is hateful (both read as *wū*), to love what is lovely (both read with the rising tone).” The City God laughed and said, “If these two characters are read this way, what interpretation would you give?” Cen replied, “This should be read as four sentences: to be truly evil, one must be like an evil stench; to be truly good, one must be like a beautiful person. Then the meaning of good and evil becomes sincere.” The City God said, “Then what is the meaning of the later passage, which speaks of hating something yet knowing its beauty?” Cen replied, “In this case, the character *恶* should be read with the falling tone. For *恶* as a thing, there is nothing in the world that is considered beautiful. However, according to the *Bencao* (Classic of Materia Medica), *renzhong huang* (human middle yellow) is highly beneficial to people. Its flavor is more delicious than *hui*. Hence, Mencius said: *恶* (in the sentence) lies in not respecting one’s uncle.”

The city god scolded, saying, "Beast! You have been teaching people this way, still claiming that you have never misled anyone?" Then he ordered him to be punished by being made into a dog, allowing him to eat evil things as retribution. Cen bowed his head again and said, "In life, I ate twice as much as others, and had a lot of phlegm and fire in my body. Every time the summer heat caused me to wheeze, my suffering was unbearable. I beg Your Majesty to forgive me, and instead, punish me by becoming a cow." The city god was surprised and asked, "What is this explanation?" Cen replied, "I once read in the *Thousand Poems* that 'cows do not know the scorching sun.' Only cows can withstand the heat." The city god laughed, ordered the ghost soldiers to drag him down, first beating him one hundred strokes, then taking him back to his hometown, so that he would be reborn as a cow, to warn those who teach.

The family did not believe it. The next day, they heard that the neighbor had raised a cow and given birth to a calf. They went to check on it, and indeed, it was true. They playfully called the calf's name, and the calf would always raise its head, wag its tail, and neigh, as if responding to the call. In *Ri Zhi Lu* (Daily Knowledge Record), it is recorded that Shandong people carved *Jin Shi Lu* (Record of Metal and Stone), and in Li Qingzhao's *Hou Xu* ("Postface"), they changed "strong month" to "Peony" without knowing that "strong month" comes from *Er Ya*. All books carved since ancient times are like the "Peony" in this way. In *Fu Zhang Lu* (Record of Laughter), it is recorded that in the ninth year of Shaoxing, the Jin dynasty returned the Henan region to the Song dynasty. Merchants carried Qin and Han steles from Chang'an and sought to sell them to scholars and officials. Mr. Wang Xilao obtained a stele, but not a single character could be recognized. He praised it continuously. A guest asked, "What dynasty's stele is this?" Mr. Wang could not answer. The guest said, "I know, it's called the 'nameless stele.'" They laughed and parted ways. Today's connoisseurs are mostly like the "nameless stele."

Willow Painting In the month of October in the year 1751 (Qianlong's reign period), Lu Jingxuan from Xiaoshan was the magistrate of Yongcheng. There was an old willow tree in the courtyard that had been half-dead for a long time. He ordered workers to cut it down, and the wood grain inside was patterned like ink wash painting: on both sides were sharp, steep rocks, and on a cliff above stood a pine tree with vines entwined around it. Below was an old tree with branches and leaves hanging downward. Beneath this stood an old man holding a staff, wearing a tall hat and long sleeves, with his beard and eyebrows clearly visible. His left hand was in his sleeve, resting on his chest, his right foot forward showing his shoes, and his left foot hidden under his robe. He looked back as if listening to a stream. Even a master painter could not surpass this. Such patterns in wood grain are rare, and finding them in a willow tree is unheard of. The ingenuity of creation, how can it be conceived! In the year 1672 (Kangxi's reign period), an order was issued in Jingkou to build warships. In the garden of Liu's family in Jiangdu, there was an old ginkgo tree over a hundred years old, which was also cut down. When it was sawn open, two images of Guanyin Bodhisattva appeared in the wood grain, graceful and natural. Everyone was amazed, and they were placed in the Southern Fortune Temple. This seems to be the work of a divine presence, especially strange.

A man from Fujian named Wu Yuchangbi once traveled to Hangzhou. He was invited to a banquet at the home of Jin Zhongcheng. The cook prepared a round fish. After it was cooked, it was cut open, revealing a meat statue of Guanyin. The statue wore a golden crown and was adorned with elaborate decorations. Its eyebrows, eyes, and clothing folds were all like paintings. Its right hand hung down, and its left hand pressed down, with its feet stepping on a single lotus flower. All the guests were amazed and saddened, and then ordered the soup to be poured over it. This event is recorded in Fan Xie's *Chengdong Zaji*. Could this be the Bodhisattva appearing in person, as a warning against killing? Other examples are recorded in *Youyang Zazu*: Emperor Yang of Sui ate a clam, which contained one Buddha and two Bodhisattvas. During the reign of Emperor Wenzong of Tang, a turtle shell contained an image of Guanyin Bodhisattva. *Xu Yijian Zhi* records that Shi Hao ate a clam, which contained two Buddha images, with spiral hair and jewelry, and feet stepping on lotus flowers. *Yishi Zihai* records that Shao Chongyi cut open a clam, which contained a statue of a Bodhisattva. Jian Qu said that a villager from Shuanglin Town cut open a clam, which contained eight immortals made of pearls. *Yijian Bingzhi* records that Zheng Boying found an image of Guanyin in a clam from Chuzhou, with a wonderful and dignified appearance, including a willow branch and a clean vase. He also found a ghost judge in a crab's belly, with hair standing up, terrifying and ugly. *Jian Guji* records that a villager from Suichang County cut open a turtle, which contained a monk sitting upright, holding a pearl, with neat clothing and shoes. In a chicken egg at Tang Xun's house, there was a Bodhisattva sitting on a lotus flower. All these cases can be said to be the appearance of immortals and Buddhas, as a warning against killing.

As for what is recorded in other books, there are instances where small Buddha statues are found inside silkworm cocoons, appearing like an Avalokiteshvara in meditation. There are also cases where monkeys are found inside chicken eggs. Such phenomena, then, what can be said about them? The rise of strange and unusual events ultimately lies beyond ordinary comprehension. **Lake City** In the fourth month of the Jiaqing 25th year (1800), the stone embankment at the western gate of Gaoyou County, facing the lake, collapsed. The river commander appointed a commissioner for repairs. There was a state official named Summer Xiang, who supervised the workers as they went back and forth on the embankment. As the day was nearly over, he suddenly saw a city by the lake as if it were real, with lush forests, a small bridge spanning the broken shore, watchtowers, palisades, and barricades all prepared on the side of the bridge, and someone holding a large umbrella, moving quickly against the wind on the bridge, while under the shade of the willows, two donkeys were grazing among them. At that time, the setting sun was sinking behind the mountains, evening clouds were rising in all directions, and it was just at the location of the city gates by the lake, with a myriad of golden and green rays. The water ripples along the embankment were like fine silk, interacting with the setting sun, creating a scene that was strange and varied, beyond description.

In the city, smoke rises in thin wisps from cooking fires, towering buildings stand majestic, and pagodas rise high into the sky, their bells ringing as if in one's ears. Suddenly, the evening wind arises, and the cityscape of trees, bridges, pavilions, and towers gradually fades and recedes, vanishing in an instant, though several hours have already passed. I have heard that in the regions of Dengzhou and Laizhou in Shandong, there is the mythical "sea city," and in Qingcheng and Qiancheng (likely a misspelling of Qianfo Mountain) in Sichuan, there is the "mountain city." Now, in this place, there is even a "lake city," also a rare sight. *Ice Mountain Record* After the confiscation of Yan Song's property in Fenyi, there was a record titled *Ice Mountain Record* documenting the events. Wang Rengyu's *Remnants of the Kaiyuan and Tianbao Eras*: Yang Guozhong, with his immense power, attracted ambitious scholars to his door. Zhang Tan, a successful candidate in the imperial examinations, was renowned. Someone advised him to visit Yang Guozhong to seek honor and prominence. Zhang Tan laughed and said, "You all think that Yang Gong's power is as solid as Mount Tai. In my view, it is like an iceberg. When the bright sun rises, this mountain will mislead people." Indeed, as he predicted, people recognized Zhang's foresight. The following year, Zhang passed the examinations and became the magistrate of Huayin County. At that time, the local officials were not competent, but Zhang had administrative skills. Whenever he proposed measures, the officials always suppressed them. Zhang said with resolve, "A true man has the ambition to surpass the world, yet he is confined to a low position, like standing in a low house where one cannot look up." He then left his official robes and returned home, taking refuge in Mount Song. The *Ice Mountain Record* is based on this principle.

Mount Tai Some say that the *mowei* (empty word) stone at Mount Tai is not truly a stone, but rather contains something hidden within it, likely inscriptions related to the *fengshan* ritual and precious materials such as jade tablets and gold seals. Once, an official sent to inspect local administration disliked that the stone caused doubt among the people and ordered it to be removed. As soon as they moved its cover, sudden storms and thunder arose. The story seems almost strange, yet there is no doubt that the stone contains something. Gu Ningren, however, argues that *Shiji* and *Hanshu* only mention the erection of stones without mentioning carving. This shows that the reader's mind is meticulous. Yet, the *Sui Shu Jing Ji Zhi* records a volume titled *The Inscriptions on Stones Erected by Emperor Qin Shi Huang During His Eastern Tour*, which likely refers to the stones erected. Therefore, this statement cannot be taken as a definitive conclusion. Ethnic Customs According to *Goryeo Dojeon* (Illustrated Records of Goryeo), it is said that in some customs, men and women often bathe together in the same river. Among the southwestern ethnic groups, the practice of "jumping the moon" requires that couples first have children out of wedlock before officially marrying. This custom is considered difficult to explain using concepts of honor and shame. However, there are also elements of honor and shame within these customs: Among the Yao and Dong ethnic groups living in the mountains of southwestern China, women begin bathing in water from April until September. Men and women sometimes mix together, and if they touch each other privately, they do not avoid it; but if they touch their breasts, they angrily kill each other, believing that this is part of women's conduct, and thus consider it extremely important. This is a kind of virtue.

The Customs of Siam: When a Chinese man has an affair with his wife, the husband is pleased and takes it as a source of honor. Sometimes he invites the Chinese man to drink together, saying that his wife is beautiful, so the Chinese man loves her. This is a kind of perspective. (It is also said that men in Siam all adorn themselves with mirrors, bells, and precious stones, using gold and silver for the wealthy and copper and tin for the poor, making a clinking sound when they walk. In marriage, monks welcome and escort the couple. When the groom arrives at the bride's home, a monk takes the bride's needlework and sticks it to the groom's forehead, calling it a good omen.) The Indigenous People of Taiwan: They do not know the calendar and do not practice ancestor worship. Beyond their parents, they do not have terms for uncles, aunts, nephews, or nieces. They value daughters more than sons. Regardless of whether they have children, they often trade with each other. During the hot months, men and women sit naked facing each other. In matters of sexual desire, the older ones avoid the younger ones, such as brothers, sisters, and children, and they do not feel ashamed to avoid. This is another kind of distinction. The People of Wuhu: When they marry a beautiful wife, they give her to their older brother. The Soldiers of Luo Gui: The new bride does not bow to her in-laws and walks in naked to perform the ritual washing, calling it serving the hall. Then it is clear that they are accustomed to courtesy and yielding. The People of Western Countries: Their custom is that those who commit adultery are put to death. Only when Chinese men have affairs with foreign women do they shave their heads and give the woman to him as a wife, not allowing him to return. Once there was a woman who killed her husband, and her husband's wife reported it to the local authorities. The local magistrate was angry and gave her to the man as a wife, saying, "Making your wife also a widow." Their method of judging cases is based on something they have learned.

**The Ling Family of Shuanglin** From the epitaph of Shen, the Filial Son of Changshu: During the time of dynastic change, he once carried his mother through the wilderness. Encountering bandits who seized his provisions, his mother refused to give them up, and the bandits, enraged, intended to kill her. The filial son wept and begged to be substituted in her place, offering himself to them. When a fire broke out in the neighborhood and spread to his mother’s sleeping chamber, and her illness was at its height and could not be changed, the filial son cried out in anguish to heaven, and the wind reversed, extinguishing the fire. Later, when his mother was over eighty and fell gravely ill, the physicians all said that no known treatment could cure her. He cut off a portion of his thigh and fed it to her, but she did not recover. Then he dreamed of a deity in red robes who said: “This illness cannot be treated by the five medicines. The physician Ling is in Shuanglin.” He immediately went to bring him. When Ling arrived, he treated her with acupuncture, and she suddenly and completely recovered. This is recorded in *The Wangxi Collection*. It is said that the filial son’s extraordinary virtue was sufficient to repel bandits and move the gods. And yet, how could the Ling family’s renown for acupuncture within their household have been accidental? The descendants of the Ling family have continued this profession down to the present day. The filial son’s name was Yu, who died in the 49th year of the Kangxi reign, at the age of ninety-four. He was the ancestor of Shu, who held the title of *Hanlin bianxiu* during the reign of Emperor Yongzheng. **Appendix from *Bili Zhicun*** Ling Hanzhang was from Huzhou. During the Chenghua period, he was renowned for his miraculous skill in acupuncture, gaining fame in Wu and Zhe regions. *The Two Zhe Region Record of Virtuous Men* describes him as generous and loyal, regarding others’ illnesses as if they were his own. Whenever someone summoned him, he would rush to their aid without fail, even in the dead of night or through wind and rain. Wherever he applied his砭石 (stone needles), various ailments vanished instantly. Every morning at the hour of the day, when the gate opened, dozens or even hundreds would come seeking treatment in carts or on foot. He never accepted payment from the poor, and thus, on the day of his death, his family had no surplus wealth.

Yang Yuan, the Master of Yang Garden Yang Yuan, the Master of Yang Garden, intended to bury his parents. After divining the site, the villagers obstructed the burial, and it could not be completed. He then had the coffins temporarily placed in the village and ordered the tenant farmers to guard them. Thieves came and set fire to the village, burning down the place and affecting the two coffins. When the criminals were caught, they were beheaded and their heads were offered as sacrifices at the tomb. Yang Yuan wore coarse hemp clothing for the rest of his life as a sign of mourning. His son-in-law, You Jiesi, was young and talented, carrying a bag of books to follow the Master in study. They spoke and acted according to rules and norms, getting along very well. Yang Yuan gave his daughter to him in marriage. When his brother, Shisi, passed the imperial examinations, he became addicted to alcohol and women. You Jiesi followed his example, despite repeated warnings. Later, he bought a prostitute as a concubine. Yang Yuan's daughter, who was well-versed in the teachings of the inner chambers, used the *Book of Poetry* and *Book of History* to advise and criticize her husband. The husband found her advice unpleasant to hear and, with the concubine, plotted to poison and kill her. Yang Yuan went to mourn her, saw the state of her poisoning, and sued in court. The court stripped the husband of his scholar's cap and gown and expelled the concubine, but ultimately did not punish him for the crime of killing his wife. After the death of his son, Yang Yuan's only grandson also died young, and the family line ended. The historian said: Chen Guming, known as Chen Zǐ, at the age of twenty, served under Yao Zhe'an. The Master said: "The lowest level of foolishness cannot be changed, as seen in the case of You Jiesi disgracing Yang Yuan. And Zhou's son-in-law also disgraced Cheng An, with Yang Yuan being the matchmaker. This is also a matter that deeply pains the former teacher. But there are people in the world who cannot be educated or reformed. One killed his wife, the other became a thief. What harm did this do to the two masters?" This argument is true. However, the fate of Yang Yuan was truly cruel! I once discussed this with Meng Lu, who said: "This is what is meant by fate." Alas! Is this really true?

According to the master's biography, in the seventh year of the Chongzhen era, the year Jia Xu, when the master was twenty-four years old, he stayed at the home of Yan Shifeng. At that time, literary societies in the southeast were just beginning to flourish. The master and Yan Shifeng agreed not to rashly attend, but instead exchanged and polished both literature and conduct with a few fellow students, such as Qiu Heng. However, the master, in his interactions with the gentlemen Yan Yingsheng, Qiu Jixin, and Ling Yu'an, often took the imperial examinations as a warning. If there were invitations to teach one's children, he would refuse in succession, considering it from a utilitarian perspective. At the age of sixty, Mr. Yao Yuhu and his younger brother Lian visited the master at Zhang Peichong's study. Happening to bring dozens of volumes of *Donggao Yixuan* from Yuxi, the boatman carried them up, continuously calling out that they were very heavy. The master jokingly said, "This may not be heavy; I consider it as light as a feather." Mr. Yao then asked, "Can scholarship and the imperial examinations be pursued together without hindrance? Or must they be completely set aside before one can engage in them?" The master replied solemnly, "The *Book of Songs* says: 'The bitter wormwood and the wild leek decay, while the millet and the foxtail millet flourish.'" This shows how strict he was in self-discipline.

The Master wrote to Mr. Wu Puozhong saying, "Heaven has bestowed upon me benevolence and filial piety, and I know of the joy of not needing medicine. Reading the final record of heaven, I am moved to tears without ceasing, truly the same as the sorrow expressed in 'luxuriant are the莪.' As a son, I have reached this point, and there is nothing more I can do. I can only stand by a deep abyss and walk on thin ice, following the aspirations of Zengzi. The *Record* says, 'To fulfill one's duties until the end is difficult.' And it elaborates, 'To fulfill one's duties means not until the end of one's parents' lives, but until the end of one's own life.' Therefore, as long as a son is alive, each day is also a day to serve his parents. I hope to continue encouraging my esteemed elder brother."

Water Moon Temple In the Gate of Gen Mountain in West Lake, Hangzhou, stands the Water Moon Temple, which was once the home of the Water Moon Old Man. The old man, whose surname was Sun and given name Wen, styled himself Wenzhi and nicknamed Shuiyue. He was a scholar from Kuaiji. After the fall of the Ming dynasty to the Qing dynasty, he lived in seclusion in Hangzhou and named his residence Plum Garden. His nature was calm and peaceful, and he did not take even the smallest thing. Occasionally, he wrote short and long poems and songs. When asked about his age, he often claimed to be ninety years old. He was completely bald, and people often called him a monk. When Fan Zhongzhen, a prominent official from Shenyang, was a child, the old man once placed his hand on his head and said, "This child will establish his authority in our land, and I will still see him." When Fan Zhongzhen governed Zhejiang, his mother told him about the old man, and Fan sought him out, withholding his entourage and went to pay respects. They had several meaningful conversations. At that time, the Xixi area was known for its many tigers. The old man warned him about the danger. He said, "Tigers on the mountain can be dealt with, but tigers inside the gate should be avoided." When Fan Zhongzhen became the governor of Fujian, the old man sent him off with the advice, "When you are in a critical situation, you must have a plan." Later, Fan Zhongzhen died in the difficult situation of the Geng rebellion. People began to realize his foresight and rushed to see him. The old man disliked the attention and left, and no one knows what happened to him. The local people honored him by renaming his residence as Water Moon Temple. They worshiped a likeness of the old man as a monk and established it as a temple for incense and offerings. The *Chibei Oudan* referred to him as Water Moon Monk.

**The Commentator Says:** The *Xichao Xinyu* (New Sayings of the Prosperous Age) also recorded this event, but omitted the words "fire erupted behind the ear," and did not elaborate on his life as a remnant of the late Ming dynasty. Since matters are predestined, the elder already knew this, yet he still refused to bend his knee to the new dynasty, just as Gong Xu refused to serve the Yongle Emperor, saying he feared betraying the sorrow at the Golden Gate. Since ancient times, loyal ministers and filial sons would not change their resolve because of the times or fate! **Speaking from the Abdomen** The *Liaozhai Zhiyi* (Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio) records: There was a bandit who, after being sentenced to execution, several paces away still turned around and shouted, "What a swift blade!" This was merely an exaggerated way of praising the sharpness of the blade. I once mentioned this to others, but they all laughed at it. According to the biography of Yang Weidou in the *Ming Shi* (History of the Ming Dynasty), after the fall of the state, the scholar did not yield to execution. His head had already fallen, yet his voice came from his neck—thus, the *Liaozhai* account is not false. Again, during the Han dynasty, Jia Yong served as the governor of Yuzhang. In battle with the enemy, he lost his head, yet still carried his bow and wore his armor, riding back to camp on horseback. He asked the assembled generals, "Is it better to have a head or not to have a head?" The generals answered, "Better to have a head."
