Jia Yong then spoke from his abdomen, saying, "Even without a head, it is still good." All such cases are like a drunk man who falls from a cart but survives—his spirit remains intact. I once heard that during the Tang dynasty, Huaqing served as the military governor of Jian'nan. He was ordered to campaign against Duan Zizhang and successfully pacified him. Later, when he led an expedition, he rode out alone into battle and was surrounded. He lost his head, yet still went to the stream to wash his hands. A washer woman saw this and said, "How can you wash your hands without a head?" Upon hearing this, she collapsed. This is the evidence of a dispersed spirit.

During the early period of Emperor Xuanzong's reign, Wang Mu of Taiyuan served as a general under Lu Min. After suffering a defeat at Nanyang, he was pursued by enemy cavalry who struck him with a sword, severing his neck and breaking his bones and tendons, leaving only his throat still connected. Initially unaware that he was dead, he soon realized his condition. His head was resting on his navel, and he soon felt food leaking through his throat. He then used his hands to lift his head and reattach it to his neck. A moment later, his head fell again, and he fainted as before. After a long while, he regained consciousness. After reattaching his head, he tied his hair on both sides to keep it in place, allowing him to sit up. The horse he was riding did not leave him; as he tried to mount, his left hair tie loosened, and his head fell into his chest. That night, he regained consciousness again and reattached his head by tying his hair. He thought that the horse needed to lie down for him to mount, and the horse suddenly lay down, allowing him to get on. The horse then got up and carried him southeast. Holding his cheeks with both hands, he traveled forty li. Several dozen scattered soldiers from his command saw him and helped him to a village, where they eventually took him back to the camp. Wang Mu spent over two hundred days recovering in the city before fully healing. Around his neck, there were lumps the size of fingers, and his head was slightly tilted. This was a particularly strange case where his head fell multiple times and could still be reattached. During the early period of the Jiaqing era, the Miao rebels incited unrest in Chu and Shu. The Qing military forces went to suppress them and fought at Xianglu Ping, defeating the rebels. One rebel leader was killed by the Qing forces, and his head had already fallen, yet he still held a large axe in a defensive posture. A white vapor rose from his neck, about two zhang in diameter, and after a while, he fell. Perhaps some kind of magic was involved, as this was not something that could be compared to ordinary events.

Liu Zizhuang Liu Zizhuang, courtesy name Keyou, was from Huanggang, Hubei Province. He was exceptionally intelligent from a young age and could read several lines at a glance. His writing was unique and unrestrained, and he passed the provincial examination in the year of Gengwu during the reign of Emperor Chongzhen. After being recommended, he dreamed of a deity who told him, "You must have Zhu Zhiyi as your examiner to pass the spring examination." Upon arriving in the capital, he went out for a walk and saw several children carrying their schoolbags past his door. One child stood out particularly, and they engaged in conversation. He saw the child's book with the name Zhu Zhiyi written on it, and he was greatly surprised. He followed the child to his home. Zhu Zhiyi's father was a woodcutter, and he gave Liu several writing implements as a gift, bidding him farewell with great respect. Later, Liu suffered from the chaos caused by rebel bandits and did not attend the spring examination. In the year of Yichou during the reign of Emperor Shunzhi, Zhu Zhiyi had become an examiner, and Liu's essay was the top scorer, indeed Liu himself. This story is also found in *New Words of the Prosperous Reign*. Reading this, one understands that success and failure are fated, and their timing is also predetermined, which evokes a sense of melancholy. Xiong Boliang Previously, court essays all used the four-six style. In the year of Yichou during the reign of Emperor Shunzhi, Emperor Shunzhi personally presided over the examination and ordered that the old four-six style not be used. Liu Zizhuang's essay was well-received, and he was personally designated as the top scorer in the first rank, equal in fame to the second-place winner Xiong Boliang. Xiong served as the chief examiner in Zhejiang and produced three top scorers in a single examination: Shi Dacheng in the year of Yiwei, Yan Wosi in the year of Jiachen, and Cai Qifu in the year of Gengxu. The scholarly world honored him.

**Paintings in the Treasury** In the inner palace of the Ming dynasty, there were paintings stored in a treasury that had remained unopened for generations. During the reign of Emperor Chongzhen, the emperor wished to open the treasury and view its contents. The eunuchs argued against it, stating that the treasury had never been opened before. However, the emperor was resolute, and the eunuchs dared not defy him, so the treasury was opened. Upon inspection, there was nothing inside except for a small red box. When the box was brought to the emperor, it was inscribed with the date of the opening: "Chongzhen, year and month." The emperor found it remarkable that the box had been prearranged with the date. When he opened it, he found only three paintings inside: one depicted countless soldiers and civilians in disarray, another showed officials and commoners fleeing in panic, and the third showed a single figure with long hair and no clothes, hanging from a tree, which exactly resembled the emperor's image. The eunuchs were visibly moved, and the emperor left in a melancholy mood. **Divination with the Oracle** During the reign of Emperor Chongzhen, the palace would often summon immortals or generals to inquire about the events of the coming year, and they always responded. In the past, the summons would arrive immediately, but this time, they did not come for a long time. After a while, the spirit of Emperor Wu descended, and the oracle wrote: "The heavenly generals have all descended into the human world, and there are none left to respond to the summons." The emperor bowed twice and asked what the heavenly generals were doing after descending and if there were any who had not yet descended. The oracle wrote: "Only the Lord of Hanshouting (Guan Yu) is deeply contemplative and has not yet descended." After the oracle finished writing, it became silent, and when asked again, there was no response.

#### The Jade Carver People from the Crane Country are three inches tall and can travel eight thousand miles in a day, with a speed like flying. They are often eaten by sea cranes. The people of the Crane Country are extremely skilled and craft jade statues of themselves, forming groups of a hundred, and gathering them in the wilderness by the water. The cranes mistake these jade statues for humans and eat them, dying as a result. Later, other cranes see the real people and are afraid to eat them. In the modern world, there are many who add wings to tigers and eat people, but among them, are there not also "jade people" (skilled individuals)? It is only because the wisdom of those who consume them is not as great as that of the cranes, so they often follow one another in gluttony without realizing, and cannot help but be misled by petty people. #### Catholicism The name of the Lord is Jesus, from the Atlantic. In the Wanli era, there was a person from the Atlantic named Matteo Ricci, who traveled nine thousand miles by sea and arrived at Xiangshan Bay in Guangdong. He began to spread the Catholic faith in the Middle Kingdom. According to their teachings, Jesus was born in Judea, a region in Asia, which is also known as Byzantium, the Great Qin. This was in the second year of Yuanshou during Emperor Ai of Han's reign, 1581 years ago, and Jesus died in the ninth year of Wanli. The country has existed for over six thousand years, and the Lord was born in the land of humanity. The emperor was pleased with his distant journey and provided him with generous gifts. As a result, a Catholic church was built inside Xuanwu Gate in the capital city. The church is elaborately decorated and houses a statue of Jesus. On the right is the Virgin Mary, who looks like a young girl, holding a child, who is Jesus. Thus, the faith spread widely.

Those who wish to join the faith are given a bowl of clear water by their teacher, which confuses their minds, and they are then given fifty taels of silver, leading many to join. When they return, they are required to destroy ancestral tablets, stove gods, and door gods, and dedicate themselves exclusively to the cross-shaped wooden frame. When encountering objects shaped like a cross, they dare not be disrespectful. If a follower dies, the teacher always sends two people to recite scriptures beside the corpse, covers the body with cloth, and inspects it before leaving. Some believe it is because they steal the pupils of the eyes. Once there was a very poor man who had no means to borrow. He knew that joining their faith would earn him fifty taels of gold, so he warned his family in advance, telling them to wait until he returns with the silver and then give him a drug to make him vomit. After receiving the teachings and returning, he indeed wanted to destroy the ancestral tablets and dedicate himself to the cross. His family bound his hands and feet and gave him the drug, causing him to first vomit clear water and finally a blood clot. His family placed the blood clot in a water basin, and after a night, the blood dispersed, leaving an object that did not disperse, which took the form of a human with eyebrows and hair. Upon close inspection, it was the appearance of the teacher who had taught him.

**Boldness** In the *Biography of Wu Yingji*: The southern capital fell, and he raised an army to support Jin Sheng. After being defeated, he fled to the mountains, was captured, and died with dignity. At the same time, Wu Han Chao also raised an army and joined forces with Qiu Zude, Ma Sanheng, and other military units. They successively took Jurong, Lizhui, Gaohu, Lirui, Jing, and Taiping counties. In the following January, they attacked the southern city of Ningguo, scaling the southern wall at night. The army was defeated. The city officials investigated the person who initiated the action, and Wu Han Chao, having already left the city, thought of his mother and feared that others might be implicated. He returned and said, "I am the one who initiated the action." His gallbladder was cut open and measured at three inches. Thus, the gallbladder of Jiang Wei was said to be like a chicken egg, but this is not worth mentioning. **Xiang Wang's Horse Racing Track** In the *Stone Pillar Record*: The old mountains of Bian had Xiang Wang's horse drinking pool, horse tying stone, and horse racing track. Zhu Shaozhi's *Differences* says: Xiang Yu sought refuge in Wu and passed a large stream. There was a strange creature that ate people and livestock in the morning and evening. Xiang Yu rode on its back, holding its neck with one hand, gripping its belly with both feet, and hugging a tree with the other hand. He pulled out several large trees. At dawn, he saw that it was a horse. Its body was covered in black dragon patterns, so he named the stream after it, which is now known as Longxi. Zheng Mingxuan's poem: "Xiang Wang once rode a horse, with golden tracks on all sides. In his decline, his horse did not move, and he sang a sad song to a red face."

Wu Zhiqi According to the *Gu Yue Du Jing* (Ancient Mountain and Water Classic): Yu the Great controlled the floods and visited Tongbai three times, where he encountered a sudden and violent storm. Enraged, Yu summoned all the spirits and commanded the dragon to chase the deity. They captured the water deity of the Huai River, named Wu Zhiqi, who resembled a monkey, had a retracted forehead and a prominent nose, a green body and white head, golden eyes and white teeth, a neck as long as a hundred feet, strength surpassing that of nine elephants, and movements so swift that they were difficult to look at for long. Yu tried to control Wu Zhiqi by giving it to Tonglu, but he failed; then he tried Wushu Tian, who also failed; finally, Gengchen, armed with a spear, successfully captured it. Wu Zhiqi was restrained with a large chain and a golden bell on its nose, and relocated to the foot of Gui Mountain in Huaiyang. The *Shan Hai Jing* (Classic of Mountains and Seas) states: water deities often cause storms and rain. Yu locked Wu Zhiqi at the foot of Jun Mountain, and its name was Wuzhiqi. This is the same deity. During the Tang dynasty, a magistrate wanted to see this creature. He had criminals touch it, and it was captured. Sixty-four oxen were used, along with a pulley system, to pull the chain out. As the chain was almost fully extended, the creature leaped into the air, let out a thunderous cry, and disappeared, taking the chain, the people, and the oxen with it. My hometown magistrate, Tang Gong Shiji, who once served as a magistrate in Huaiyang, told this story to Zhou Menghou from Lize.

According to the *Shui Jing Zhu*, when Yu controlled the waters up to the Huai River, the Huai River god appeared, and its form was that of a monkey. It clawed the ground, creating water. Yu ordered Geng Chen to capture it and chain it under the Turtle Mountain. According to the *Jian Hu Ji*, when the Hongwu Emperor passed by Turtle Mountain, he ordered a strongman to lift and inspect the chain. They pulled the iron chain, filling two boats, and a thousand people managed to lift it, revealing only an old monkey with long hair covering its body. The monkey roared loudly and suddenly plunged into the water. In the *You Yang Za Zu*, during the reign of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, when he performed the ritual of sacrificing to Mount Tai, Zhang Yue was appointed as the enfeoffment envoy. Zhang Yue's son-in-law Zheng Yi was originally a ninth-rank official. According to the old custom, after the enfeoffment ceremony, officials from the Three Dukes would be promoted by one rank. However, Zheng Yi was suddenly promoted to a fifth-rank official and was also granted a red robe. A great banquet was held. The next day, Emperor Xuanzong noticed Zheng Yi's sudden rise in rank and asked him about it. Zheng Yi had no answer. Huang Fanchuo said, "This is the power of Mount Tai." Nowadays, people call their father-in-law "Mount Tai," and this usage may have originated from this event. Human-faced Sore Once upon a time, a merchant from the south had a sore on his left shoulder that looked like a human face. Initially, it caused no pain, and it drank and ate like a person. If someone dripped wine into its mouth, its face would turn red. It ate everything, and if it ate too much, the sore would swell, as if there was a stomach inside. If it didn't eat, one arm would become paralyzed. A doctor taught him to try various medicines, including minerals, stones, herbs, and plants. When given *Bai Bu* (a type of herb), the sore would frown and close its eyes. The merchant was delighted and said, "This must be the cure!" Using a small bamboo tube, they destroyed its mouth and poured the medicine in. After several days, it formed a scab and healed.

Chen Guoshan Chen Guoshan, also known as Zhao Lun, was an imperial scholar during the reign of Emperor Yongzheng, passing the imperial examination in 1730. In the early years of Emperor Qianlong's reign, he was recommended for entry into the Hanlin Academy and later appointed as the governor of the capital region. Throughout his life, he was gentle and approachable, always praising even minor merits. Those who sought his opinion on their poetry and prose received generous praise, which made others enjoy his company. His calligraphy was inspired by the *Lantingji Xu* (Preface to the Poems Composed at the Orchid Pavilion) and took a simple and distant meaning. Liang Shanzhou, a scholar and official, commented: "Among those of this dynasty who are not famous for their calligraphy but whose works will be passed down, there are only Chen Wenjian and Chen Guoshan." Burying Silkworms In the county, the Wu family raised silkworms every year. One year, due to a shortage of mulberry leaves, the silkworms could not be fed adequately, so they buried more than ten baskets of silkworms in a pit. They sent three household servants to sail to the market to buy mulberry leaves. On their return journey, a large carp suddenly jumped into the boat, and the three servants were overjoyed, bringing it back home. On their way, they passed through Zao Lin, where the local official found the small boat suspicious and the use of two oars to row quickly unusual, so he pursued and arrested them. Upon searching, they found no other items, but in the front cabin, there was a human leg. When questioned, the three servants were all confused and did not know where it came from. The local official immediately bound them and sent them to the higher-level judicial official, subjecting them to severe interrogation. He asked about the whereabouts of the body and head, and the three servants, unable to withstand the torture, falsely confessed: "We saw it buried in a vacant lot at home." The official then ordered the guards to take them to their home and dig it up, which turned out to be the same place where the silkworms were buried. However, the silkworms were nowhere to be found, only a single corpse, with a complete body but missing one leg. The evidence matched exactly, so the three servants and the head of the household were all punished. This incident is recorded in the *Wuqing Zhi* (Annals of Wuqing).

The historian says: Heaven and Earth take the love of life as virtue. Those who bury silkworms have a heart that is truly cruel. However, during the time when silkworms are abundant and mulberry leaves are expensive, nowadays, there are also those who bury their silkworms and sell their mulberry leaves. One does not hear that they immediately suffer a terrible retribution. But the Wu family alone suffered this strange disaster. It is because of their cruelty. Then their daily accumulation of evil deeds must have been even worse than burying silkworms. This is the origin of their misfortune, and it must have its own source. I once heard the elders say: Once there was a village farmer. Because the mulberry leaves were expensive, he threw away all his silkworms. But his son's wife secretly hid several baskets of silkworms. The farmer had several acres of mulberry trees, and the leaves were still there. His son, having no mulberry leaves, secretly went at night to pick them. The farmer happened to be patrolling his fields, in the darkness, mistook him for another thief, and stabbed him with a spear, killing him instantly. Afterwards, realizing it was his own son, he was filled with grief and anger, and hanged himself to death. And his wife and daughter-in-law cried until dawn, and also hanged themselves to death, wiping out the entire family. The farmer who stabbed his son did not know it was his own son. But consider that if it were someone else, they would only be a thief stealing mulberry leaves, and their crime would not warrant death. How cruel was he? Heaven used his hand to kill his son! The severity of retribution, how terrifying it is!

A Note on the Burial of Silkworms The practice of burying silkworms is already documented in Huangfu Mo's *San Shui Xiao Du*. In that account, the subject was Wang Gongzhi, a resident of Beicun village in Cijian Town, Xin'an County. When he sold his silkworms, he received three thousand coins and bought a shoulder of pork and some cakes to take home. Upon reaching Huian Gate, the gatekeeper noticed blood from the pork shoulder spilling from his bag and questioned him. Wang Gongzhi explained that he had purchased the meat and requested a search. Upon opening the bag, they found only a human arm, as if freshly severed. The arm was then sent to the governor, who in turn handed it over to the Prefect of Henan for investigation. Wang Gongzhi told the truth, and the Prefect ordered a subordinate to take him back to the village to gather testimony from the neighbors. All the neighbors testified that they knew Wang had buried the silkworms and that he had no other bad deeds. When they dug up the silkworm pit, they found a dead person wrapped in silk, missing its left arm. When the arm was attached, it fit perfectly. This was reported to the Prefect, who declared that Wang Gongzhi was not guilty of murder but that silkworms were divine creatures of heaven and earth, the foundation of silk and cloth. Therefore, destroying them was equivalent to killing someone, and he ordered Wang Gongzhi to be beaten to death in the market. This account is roughly the same as the previous one. What is recorded in the annals is likely based on hearsay.

**The Dog That Pays Off Its Debts** In a certain town, there was a man who once kept a dog. Every night, the dog would cross the water to a house in Henan and keep watch for them. One day, the man called out to the dog and scolded it with his eyes, saying, "You eat from me, yet you guard the night for others. Tomorrow, I will certainly find someone to kill you and sell you." That night, the man dreamed that the dog stood upright and barked, saying, "I once owed money to someone in Henan, so every night I go to guard their place to repay the debt. Now I only owe thirteen coins. Once the debt is paid, I will no longer cross the river. I swear to repay my master's great kindness." When morning came, the man called the dog to him and tied thirteen coins around its neck, saying, "I had a dream like this. Now I am returning the money to you, so you can avoid crossing the river again." The dog lowered its head to accept the instruction and then threw the coins into the home in Henan and returned. From then on, it never crossed the river again. Later, the man went to visit his daughter late at night and got drunk. He lost his balance and fell into a pond. The dog barked loudly, jumped into the water, bit his clothes, and dragged him to the shore. It then jumped to the house and bumped its head against the door, waking the master's wife. She followed the dog to the pond and saw the man lying stiffly unconscious. She helped him back to the house, and he did not wake up until evening. When he finally woke up, he told her what had happened. His wife said, "The dog had a dream before, swearing to repay your kindness. Today, it has not broken its word."

After several months, the house accidentally caught fire while the whole family was sound asleep. The dog returned to the bedroom, bumped its head against the door, and barked simultaneously. The husband and wife woke up in alarm and looked, and the flames were about to reach the house. They managed to extinguish it in a hurry. After the dog died, the owner buried it in a coffin. This is what Mr. Yang Zhou recorded in his *Records of Reaping What One Sows*. Ah, a dog, and yet it could remember and repay kindness so faithfully? Is it perhaps an animal in appearance but not in heart? I therefore excerpted and recorded it to warn the world of those who betray their benefactors and masters. In the *Yijian Zhi*, Xu Yuanhuiqing, a scholar from LePing, had a father who dreamed of a black-clothed guest who said, "I borrowed three hundred coins from you yesterday, and now I am returning them." Before he could ask who the person was or when the loan was made, he woke up. Usually raised more than ten ducks, on that day, they returned and saw one black duck outside the usual count. The child thought it belonged to someone else and tried to drive it away, but the duck circled around and left an egg before leaving. From then on, for a month, every day it was the same, totaling thirty eggs. And then it left, never to return. No one knew who it was, but counting its value, it was exactly three hundred coins. It seems that the one who borrowed money did not forget to repay, even among animals, this is often seen. How can humans be less than animals in this regard?

Peeling the Skin At the end of the Chongzhen era, a fortune-teller recounted: "During the Xizong reign, I once traveled to the capital. Five people were drinking together in an inn, and one of them loudly spoke of Wei Zhongxian's wickedness, predicting that he would soon fall. The other four were either silent or frightened, advising him to speak cautiously. This person said, 'Though Wei Zhongxian is powerful, he surely cannot peel my skin; what have I to fear?' At midnight, when he was sound asleep, suddenly someone pounded on the door, lit a fire to illuminate his face, and seized him. Soon after, they captured the other four and brought them in, where they saw the captured man with his hands and feet nailed to the door panels. Wei Zhongxian told the four men, 'This person said he could not be peeled, so let us try it now.' He then ordered bitumen to be poured over the man's entire body and struck it with a mallet. In no time, the entire body was peeled off, and the skin shell still resembled a person. The four men were terrified to death, and Wei Zhongxian gave each of them five taels of gold to calm their nerves, releasing them. This is recorded in *The Survivor's Record*. Alas! The cruelty of Wei Zhongxian was indeed unparalleled in history."

However, there are also things that power cannot reach. The *Erxin* records: In the month of March of the year of the Tiger, on the birthday of Wei Zhongxian, all the high-ranking officials and officials of various departments gathered together. Suddenly, a Daoist hermit appeared, wearing a headband and a loose robe, carrying a bamboo walking stick and a whisk. He knocked on the door and requested an audience. The gatekeeper scolded him, saying, "So many venerable elders and high-ranking officials have been waiting all day. They cannot be received; how dare you, a vagrant, want to see our thousand-year-old ruler?" The hermit replied, "I am a poor friend of Wei Gong. Today, I wish to speak face-to-face and wish for a thousand autumns of life." The gatekeeper dared not report this, but raised a gourd club and a halberd, pointing to the hermit's head, and scolded and chased him, saying, "You dare to speak recklessly without fear! Fortunately, it is today, the birthday. If it were another day, you would be smeared with oil and ears." The hermit knocked on the drum with his stick, and all the officials turned pale. They then pushed him inside.

The Taoist bowed deeply and spoke sternly, saying, "I have been away from you for a long time, and today we meet again here. Now you are extremely wealthy and powerful, could you possibly forget me?" Wei Zhongxian was furious and said, "You wicked Taoist dare to act so wildly, how could I associate with you?" He ordered his attendants to bind him and hand him over to the Supreme Court for strict investigation. The Taoist said, "I am a seer of the world, having observed many people, but do you not recognize that you have the appearance of a thief and the heart of a beast? I only wish to save many lives of loyal officials and righteous men." He pointed to the sky and said, "Can you deceive the emperor and the people, but the heavens? I will see you cut into pieces, and even dogs and pigs will not eat your remains. Can you kill me?" He raised his hand and leaped, breaking all the ropes, brushing the air with his sleeves, and suddenly disappeared, causing everyone to be shocked. This is similar to the dog-skin Taoist in Zhang Xianzhong's court in *Xu Yu Chou Xin Zhi*, both of whom were able to make the rebel's evil power ineffective, which is quite pleasing to see.

Note:** The practice of skinning, from ancient times, has never been heard of. Only in wild histories is it recorded: Jing Qing intended to carry out Yu Rang's plan, and Emperor Chengzu found the sword, ordering the skinning of someone and using their skin as a cover to hang at the Changan Gate. The next morning, when the emperor passed by, the rope suddenly broke, appearing as if the person had committed an offense. Thus, the skin was ordered to be stored in the treasury. However, the death of Jing Qing has many different accounts. Only Zhang Xianzhong once used this method: if the skinning was not completed and the person had already died, the executioner would be skinned instead. It seems they did not master the method. How cruel indeed! Wei Zhongxian's cruelty is truly no different from that of the bandit Xian! (According to *New Ears*, it is said that when Wei Zhongxian was executed by slicing, his body did not decompose. Upon execution, he seemed to still have a faint breath, with fresh blood spurting out, as if waiting for heavenly punishment.) **Divine Prescription** From *Seven Revisions Collection*: At the end of the Yuan Dynasty, Xu Tongpan of Houzhucun in Tongxiang had always admired Lu Dongbin. He offered daily worship and rituals. One day, he developed a severe boil on his back, near death, yet he still managed to rise and pay his respects. He happened to see a sheet of white paper under a clean water pot, on which was written a poem: "Fragments of golden dust from the grave, petals of white jade orchid flying. The ruler takes one catty, the minister four liang; mix and take to heal instantly." Thinking it was a divine prescription, he did not know what substances were meant by "golden dust" and "white jade." He summoned the immortal and asked about the use of rhubarb and angelica. The immortal replied, "Yes." After taking it, it proved effective. Later, when he used it to treat others, it was always successful.

Xu Wuzi, his son-in-law Shen, has been making a living this way until now. People come from hundreds of miles away to sell medicine without a single day off. The Shen clan is large and divided into dozens of families, but only the main branch living under the large cypress tree finds the medicine effective. Xu's son-in-law once studied under my friend Xu, the judge, and I heard that his medicine now includes pangolin, angelica sinensis root, and honeysuckle. However, with so much rhubarb, it is not advisable to use it without distinguishing between yin and yang diseases, as it might also be harmful. Despite this, people continue to come and go, and the medicine works uniquely under the cypress tree, isn't it the will of heaven? And so on. However, the Shen family, I once saw during the Jiaqing era when they were in decline. At their peak, there was a famous person named Geng Wen, who was especially skilled in surgery and was often compared to Hua Tuo. By now, the medical profession has revived, and even from a hundred miles away, people still invite him, but it is not heard that he specializes in treating people with this formula. If there is anyone today who practices medicine and is particularly famous, his name is Tai, and he is the disciple of my father-in-law, Mr. Zhang Menglu. According to the biography: At that time, the Censorial Officials, known for their bold speech, included Tong and Chen E, and the whole court feared their demeanor.

Eke, courtesy name Kezhong, was from Fanyu. During the Yongle era, he was selected through local examinations and admitted to the Imperial Academy, where he was appointed as a Censor of the Criminal Department. Whenever he presented matters to the emperor, his voice was as loud as a bell. The emperor ordered him to be starved for several days, but when he presented matters again, his voice remained unchanged. The emperor said, "This is a natural endowment." Whenever the emperor saw him, he would call him "the loud-tongued scholar." On one occasion, Eke spoke against the emperor's wishes, and the emperor ordered him to be buried up to the neck in the ground at the Gate of Heaven, with only his head exposed. After seven days, Eke was still alive, and the emperor pardoned him and restored his position. Eke had a humorous nature. When he was buried, he sighed and said to the person performing the burial, "I did not expect to die in a large jar today!" When someone asked him why, he replied, "Do you not know? The court buries people in jars to make them die quickly." The person performing the burial followed his words, and Eke was able to move his limbs and survive. It is said that when burying people, the earth is piled up to the chest, which causes suffocation and death. If only the head is exposed, one would surely be unable to bear it for even a moment, let alone seven days. **Posthumous Praise for Lu Zhongyi** Lin Lu said: When the mother of Lu Zhongyi was pregnant, she dreamed that a divine figure, surrounded by ceremonial music and flags, fell from the clouds directly into her womb, and then Lu Zhongyi was born. When he was young, he had a noble and resolute appearance and was renowned for his extensive learning in Jiangxi. After he completed his writings, people from all directions referred to his style as "the Xiling style." When he was selected for the imperial examination, he dreamed of the Grand Tutor and the Minister of Justice speaking to him. The words they spoke were mostly secret, and no one could understand them. Shen Jun Dingxin died suddenly and then revived, saying that he saw Lu Zhongyi and others assisting in the judgment of the underworld, similar to the story of Wang Xiang. Alas! Loyalty and filial piety are the highest virtues. Only if one is not ashamed before others can one be considered a god. What is there to be surprised about?

According to the records: His name was Lu Pei, courtesy name Kun Ting, and pseudonym Bu Lou, registered in Qiantang. He had six brothers, Bo Qi and Shu Jie, who were already renowned before him. From a young age, Lu Pei upheld moral integrity; if slightly offended, he would weep and swear to die. Both his mother and grandmother deeply loved and cherished him. As he grew older, his brothers' reputations became even more prominent, and together with Bo Qi and Shu Jie, they promoted learning in the southeast. Chen Dazun, a government official, once said, "I associate with the Lu family, and it is like being among Kong Rong and his peers." At the age of sixteen, he was admitted as a student. In the year Ji Mao, he passed the provincial examination and knelt before his grandmother. His mother was delighted and said, "Your father and uncle sang 'The Deer's Call' just as if it were yesterday. I am nearly eighty years old, and yet I still see you achieve fame. The state's benevolence is profound, so strive hard!" The following year, he passed the imperial examination. Lu Pei had a majestic and upright appearance. He lived a secluded life, shut himself in to study, and engaged in scholarly discussions with famous scholars to write classical prose. He made friends across the land and enjoyed guiding and supporting younger scholars. However, he was frank in criticizing others' faults, and those with evil intentions would hold their breath and quickly back away upon seeing him. Once, he traveled with Lu Jun Xiang Wu to Moli (present-day Nanjing), where they visited the temples dedicated to Fang Zhengxue and Xu Changgong. A guest gave Lu Jun Xiang Wu a bow and arrows, and as Lu Jun Xiang Wu was composing a poem, Lu Pei looked solemn and said, "Watching the land sink into chaos, I wander aimlessly without achieving anything. With your talent, you should fight on horseback and write dispatches on foot, which would at least satisfy others." They stayed at an inn, reading history daily and drinking heavily for a month. One night, his body gradually shortened to three or four inches, and after a long time, he could stretch it back.

In the year Jiashen (1644), the rebel Li Zicheng breached the imperial capital, and the protagonist wailed loudly facing north, aspiring to gain the emperor's favor. His wife urgently stopped him, saying, "You have always been a reader. Haven't you heard of the events of the Jin and Song dynasties? There is still hope." Soon after, he traveled to Jiankang, where he was appointed to the Office of the Envoy, serving as deputy to Xiong Yulin, a censor. Together, they held a ceremonial staff to pay homage at the Huai River. Xiong was known for his bold remonstrances, and during their discussions of current affairs, the protagonist was deeply moved. In the following year, Yiyou (1645), the rebel army was defeated on the riverbank. The protagonist, along with his brothers, took care of his mother in Yanguan, and he instructed his son, Fan Chao, to accompany them. The provincial capital was in chaos, so the protagonist fled to Tongwu Valley in Huangshan. Passing by the residence of his old friend Chen Tinghui, they shook hands and wept, saying, "We are about to part ways." Chen tried to stop him, but the protagonist replied, "Even if I die, it will not benefit the country; I am just fulfilling my duty." Upon returning home, his wife ordered the servants to guard him. The protagonist smiled and said, "Death cannot be revived. My mother is old, and I should seek refuge in a place like Peach Blossom Spring to live a simple life." Soon after, he closed the door and attempted to hang himself, but was saved by a guest. The next day, at dawn, he called for writing materials and put on formal attire. He bowed five times facing north and three times facing south, then handed a sock rope to his two servants, saying, "You know my intention; help me complete this." He sat on the large bed and calmly hung himself until death. On the table, he left three letters: one for his mother, one for his brothers, and one for his old friends. He was twenty-eight years old at the time.

The woman vowed to follow her husband even unto death, and as she fell from the building, it seemed as if a divine force was holding her up. She survived for ten days without food. Her mother-in-law, Lady Qiu, said to her, "This is heaven's will for you to live; disobeying heaven would be inauspicious." Thus, she did not die. Her husband's brother, Qi, and younger brother, Jie, were also deeply committed to moral principles and were likely remnants or survivors of a particular era or group. It was not yet a year since the husband's death when Chen, a government official, fought against his captors and drowned himself. Chen, a high-ranking official, took his wife and concubines with him as he drowned in a rapid river. Chen had previously offended the common people, and the "common people" here refers to those who had written to him to express their displeasure. Xiong, upon entering Fujian, was suspected by Zheng Zhilong and both he and his son drowned at sea. A traveler from Hainan reported that Yao, a person named Qiyun, cut off his own head and died. When Fang was writing to the censors, Qiyun had tried to dissuade him, but Fang did not agree, and they both ended up dying. Alas! These four individuals could be called loyal friends of the deceased. At the same time, Wang, a fellow official from the same province, also died upon hearing of the husband's death. In the Jiangdong region, the deceased was posthumously honored with the title "Zhongyi" (loyal and valiant). Dong, the Minister of Revenue, said, "Two people died together, was it because Dao Kun was not a jinshi (imperial scholar-official)?" Thus, Wang was posthumously honored with the title "Jiemin" (diligent and compassionate).

**Strange Beast** In the region of Chu, there was a scholar-official who entered the city from the mountains. Due to the problem of tigers, he was accompanied by two hunters with iron forks. As evening approached, he stopped at a small inn near a post station. Suddenly, a tiger roared and came towards them. The two hunters led the scholar-official to a tree in front of the inn and stood up to confront the tiger in battle. The tiger was killed. Then, another tiger came with two cubs. The two hunters were exhausted and died. The scholar-official was terrified, when suddenly a creature appeared, resembling a small dog, with white fur and red hair, golden eyes, and running like the wind. It charged forward and attacked the three tigers. The three tigers cowered and did not move, and all were killed. The creature ate a little of their brains, but the first to die only smelled them and did not eat. Soon, it came to the tree where the scholar-official was, and it barked loudly at him, leaping up and falling into a ravine, getting caught in the vines and unable to escape. The scholar-official was terrified and thought that waiting to die was foolish, so he climbed down the tree and took the fork to kill it. He then took the creature to the magistrate, who took its skin and used it as a lining, which repelled snow and did not get wet. The oppressive policies of government are more dangerous than tigers, and the cruel actions of officials are often driven by greed. This creature had already eaten three tigers, yet it was still greedy and did not know when to stop, leading to its own downfall. This serves as a warning. According to: This story is similar to what is recorded in the *Book of Natural History* about a barbarian who came to present a fierce beast like a dog. However, the *Book of Natural History* states that it could eat tigers, while this creature also wanted to eat people. Additionally, the *Book of Lost Zhou* mentions that a flying dog could eat tigers and leopards, but this creature does not seem to be able to fly. What kind of creature is it?

Wang Shizhen's *Lóng Shǔ Yú Wén* records: The *jiǎo duān* is said to inhabit Wawu Mountain; it does not harm humans but only preys on tigers and leopards. Mountain monks keep them to protect themselves. According to the *Zhonghua Gujin Zhi*, the people of *Qū Sǒu Guó* presented a small dog that could fly and eat tigers and leopards. This is interpreted as the *qū quǎn* being the *jiǎo duān*. I refer to the *Yishu Wang Huì Jiě*, which states that the people of *Qū Sǒu Guó* used *qū quǎn*. The *qū quǎn* is a type of small dog, or rather, a variant name for it. Initially, there was no mention of the *jiǎo duān*. The *Erya* describes the *qiū* as resembling a horse with one horn. The *lín* has a deer-like body, a cow's tail, horse's hooves; it is yellow, has round hooves, one horn, and the tip of the horn is fleshy. Therefore, the *jiǎo duān* is indeed a type of *lín*. Why is it compared to the *qū quǎn*? Furthermore, during the reign of Emperor Wu of Han, nomads from the north of Dayuan presented a creature as large as a dog, whose sound could startle people, causing chickens and dogs to run away when they heard it. It was called a fierce beast. The emperor found it strange that it was so small. When it was released in the imperial park, he wanted to let tigers and leopards eat it. When tigers saw this creature, they immediately lowered their heads to the ground. The emperor thought the tigers were lowering their heads to attack, but the creature saw the tigers and was very pleased. It licked its lips, wagged its tail, and went straight onto the tiger's head. It then urinated on the tiger's face. The tigers then closed their eyes, lowered their heads, and lay down without moving. After it finished urinating and left, the tigers dragged their tails away. The creature looked back, and the tigers closed their eyes again. I once heard my ancestors say that tigers fear small dogs. These dogs are smaller than domestic dogs. When tigers see them, they lie down and do not move. The dogs then urinate around them, and the tigers cannot move out of their spot. This is likely the same creature.

The Palace Examination Papers In the twenty-third year of the Wanli era, the Wujin County scholar, known posthumously as Wen Jie Gong, submitted his responses to the palace examination questions. While he was serving in the Ministry of Rites, he retrieved these papers himself and kept them at home, where they remain to this day. Each line of his responses contained thirty-two characters. In provincial and metropolitan examinations, there were both horizontal and vertical red lines, but in the palace examination, only vertical lines were used. The reason for this difference can be inferred from the intention behind the establishment of the system: since the length of the response essays varied, the spacing of the characters could be adjusted accordingly without any issue. However, in modern times, it has become customary to write palace examination responses with twenty-two characters per line, which has deviated from the essence of the original rules. Moreover, before the fiftieth year of the Qianlong era, co-examiners still graded based on the interpretation of the classics, and the oral examination questions on poetry were held in the second session. Now, these questions have been moved to the first session, and the examiners no longer have the names of the Five Classics. The reason for not dividing the examiners according to the Five Classics is that scholars have become familiar with all five classics. However, I have seen earlier scholars who specialized in one of the classics, and they would always have a manuscript copy of the classic they studied. These copies included detailed research on the origins and development of the texts, as well as a comprehensive understanding of the teachings, almost to the point where studying one classic made them proficient in all five. Today, however, scholars tend to neglect the study of the classics and focus only on commentaries and contemporary literary works, often setting them aside. As a result, when entering the examination hall, they are often at a loss when asked about the source of a question, completely unaware of its origin. How can one not sigh deeply at this?

Appendix In the 39th year of the Kangxi era, the Censor General Man Jin submitted a report on the accumulated problems in the imperial examination system, while Governor-General Guo Xiu submitted a report on the problems in the education system. Both reports were sent to the Nine Ministers for deliberation. Upon receiving the reports, the emperor ordered them to be shown to the provincial governors Li Guangdi and Hu Peng, as well as the Governor-Generals Zhang Penghe and Guo Xiu. Li Guangdi proposed three measures to reform the imperial examination system and four measures to reform the education system. In conclusion, he stated: Recently, officials responsible for education have often acted carelessly, leading to students neglecting classical studies and ancient wisdom, even though they cannot memorize and recite the *Four Books*. They only read a hundred or so pieces of contemporary literature, imitating others without originality, relying on luck to succeed, which is far from the state's way of cultivating and achieving its goals. Last year, the emperor issued an edict to the officials responsible for education, ordering children to enter school and using *The Elementary Text*. At that time, children's knowledge and understanding suddenly became new, and they suddenly understood ancient meanings. This is clear evidence of the effectiveness of elementary education in attracting and educating children. However, if they cannot recite the books fluently, they have not truly mastered them. Officials should be instructed to admit those who can recite the classics and elementary texts fluently and explain the *Four Books* on the day of the examination, even if their writing and reasoning are only at a basic level. If they can recite three or five classics, they should be given additional benefits to show encouragement. Additionally, children should be taught the basics of elementary education to set a good foundation for their young minds, and students and exam topics should focus on *The Classic of Filial Piety*, but often repeat the same topics. It seems appropriate to also include *The Collected Commentaries on the Four Books* and *Comprehensive Mirror to Aid in Government* to encourage scholars with broad and deep knowledge.

The memorial was submitted and then sent to the Nine Ministers for consultation, combining it with three memorials by Zhang Penghe et al. to reach a final decision. For the provincial examination, a separate official number system was implemented, with nine quotas for civilian candidates and one for official candidates. This decision was made by the emperor's intention, and Guangdi especially supported it. The topics for the examination included *Commentary on the Taiji Diagram*, *Book of Universal Principles*, *Western Inscription*, and *Correct Meaning*. Alas! Since the Ming dynasty, the habits of scholars have deteriorated, and the situation has worsened. This proposal is appended to show that the state's laws are not inherently bad, but there are good laws, but no good people to implement them, leading to accumulated problems that cannot be reversed, and the laws themselves are soon abandoned and not discussed. How can we find such virtuous men to restore it and bring it closer to the ancient standards? Also, in *Random Notes from the Cottage of Restraint* it says: When I was young, studying for the imperial examinations, there were no printed copies of exam papers. There was a bookseller who visited the homes of my friends who were preparing for the exams, copying dozens of exam papers, each paper copied onto twenty to thirty sheets. When he came to my family's school, he selected a few papers, each containing two or three essays. I remember that when Jingchuan passed the metropolitan examination as the top scholar, his papers were also printed by his disciple Cai Ying and his relative. When Xue Fangshan passed the metropolitan examination as the top scholar, his three exam papers were printed by his disciple Qian Mengwang from Donghu Academy, whom I encouraged to do so. I had not heard of any printed versions from the market. Nowadays, everywhere you look, there are printed versions from the market, which is also a sign of the decline in the quality of the times.

Yang Zichang said: The "Eighteen Room" texts, which began with the *Gouxuan Lu* of the Renchen year in the Wanli era, included marginal annotations. It was Fang Wangzhong (Shisu) who first selected the *Chengmo* (model essays). After the Jiwei year, there were four types of printed texts: *Chengmo*, which were the essays of candidates and examiners; *Fanggao*, which were the works of the ten examiners who passed the exam; *Xingjuan*, which were the works of those who passed the provincial exams; and *Shegao*, which were the collective works of students. For each exam, hundreds of copies of the examiners' essays were printed, all produced in Suzhou and Hangzhou, and bought and sold by people from Central China and the north. Across the country, people only knew that these texts could bring them fame and wealth, calling this "learning" and "talent," while neglecting all other books. In the reigns of Tianshun and Chenghua, Qiu Wenzhuan had already remarked that candidates who passed the exams knew nothing about the titles of historical books, the sequence of dynasties, or the structure of Chinese characters. The entire country only read the "Eighteen Room" texts, studying them for three or five years, and if they happened to pass the exam, ignorant children suddenly behaved like high-ranking officials, while the ways of literature and martial arts were discarded like a hat. Alas! The dominance of the eight-part essay has diminished the importance of the Six Classics, and the rise of the "Eighteen Room" texts has led to the neglect of the Twenty-One Histories. This is why the *Rizhilu* laments it!

I have examined what Wen Zhuang said, and at that time there were already Fang drafts. Now there are even more people who ignore Tian, Chong, and the early years of the state, let alone the periods before Jia and Long. This is yet another change in the world's customs. The *Tui Bei Tu*: From the *Ting Shi* (History of the Ting): During the Tang Dynasty, Li Chunfeng composed the *Tui Bei Tu*. During the chaos of the Five Dynasties, kings and feudal lords rose to power, and people had hopes, so their studies became more intense. The prophecy of opening the mouth and drawing the bow, Wu and Yue even named their sons with this, but they did not know the significance of the prophecy for the founding of the state. When the Song dynasty arose, the symbols of divine mandate were especially famous. When the founding emperor ascended the throne, an edict was issued to ban prophetic books, fearing that they would confuse the people's minds and lead to complicated legal penalties. However, the *Tui Bei Tu* had been circulating for several centuries, and there were many copies in private hands, which could no longer be collected. The officials were concerned about it. One day, Zhao Han Wang presented a case from Kaifeng, saying that the number of offenders was so great that they could not all be punished. The emperor ordered to retrieve the old copies, and all the ones that had been verified were mixed up and written in a chaotic manner. A total of one hundred copies were made, so that they could circulate alongside the existing ones. Thus, those who transmitted it were confused about the order, and did not know which was incorrect. Occasionally, some of the existing ones were not verified again and were also discarded and not kept. Is this why what is transmitted today is so varied and mixed?

During the early years of the Xuanhe era of the Song dynasty, the imperial workshop produced silk fabrics known as "peaches all over the ground." Additionally, hats made from painted silk, featuring two peach designs, were called "paired peaches," and these became widely imitated across the land. Perfumes were also referred to as "佩香" (pei xiang). When the Jurchens invaded the capital, people of all social statuses fled their homes, becoming captives of the Jurchens, which fulfilled a prophecy. How could this be found in the *Tui Bei Tu*? As for Li Zicheng, the *Li Zhou Zhi* (local history of Li Prefecture) records that according to unofficial histories, his death occurred at Luo Gong Shan in Tongcheng, while the *Ming Shi* (History of the Ming dynasty) states it happened at Jiu Gong Shan in Tongshan County. Both accounts suggest that he died at the hands of villagers. However, it is now known that Luo Gong Shan is actually located in Qianyang, and Jiu Gong Shan is in Tongshan County. The references to Tongcheng in these accounts are incorrect.

Professor Sun told me: Li Zicheng actually fled to Lishui, reaching Qinghua Post Station, followed by ten or so riders, he fled to Guniu Dam (located in what is now Anyi County), again fled on horseback, and alone fled to Jia Mountain in Shimen to become a monk. Now his tomb is still there, they say. I was surprised by this, especially went to Jia Mountain, saw a stone pagoda beside the temple, covered with a building. The pagoda's front prominently inscribed "Fengtian Yuheshang." In front of it was a stele, written by his disciple Ye Fu, recording that the monk did not know his family name. An old monk in his seventies, could still recount old stories of Jia Mountain, saying: the monk entered the temple at the beginning of Shunzhi's reign to serve in the Buddhist order, did not say where he came from, his voice sounded like that of a Westerner. A few years later, another monk came, saying he was his disciple, belonging to the Chan school, named Ye Fu, from Jiangnan. He served the monk very respectfully. The monk died in the second month of the year Jiayin during the Kangxi reign, he was about seventy years old. On his deathbed, he left a final message to Ye Fu. At that time, he was young and did not hear it. The temple still has his portrait. I was asked to look at it, it showed a high cheekbone, deep face, hawk-like eyes, and a scorpion-like nose, a fearsome appearance, the same as recorded in the *History of Ming*. Li Zicheng assumed the title of Fengtian Chanyi Dàyuánshuài, later he called himself the New Shun King. He called himself Fengtian Yuheshang, he did this to obscure his identity. And Ye Fu, as a Chan monk, served him respectfully, was he one of his former ministers or close attendants? The *History of Ming* records that Li Zicheng was killed in Jiu Gong Mountain, it also says: "Our soldiers sent someone to identify his body, but it had rotted and could not be recognized." But the old monk personally heard his coughing, and his Western accent was particularly unusual.

The Memorial to Li Zicheng's Tomb, written by Jiang Bingyu (also known as Jiang Yuzhi), refutes the error in the *History of the Ming* regarding "Tongcheng" using the *Li Prefecture Gazetteer*. Consequently, the mistake about "Luogong Mountain" does not even require debate. The references he cites are also precise. However, according to earlier histories, there is no doubt that Li Zicheng was killed by villagers. The account states that the villagers dug him up, stripped his clothes, and found a dragon robe and a golden seal, with one eye missing. The villagers were greatly surprised and suspected it to be Li Zicheng. This claim is not without basis. This old monk, who knew the beginning of the monk's entry into the temple and the exact time and date of his death, must have been able to remember his appearance. It is regrettable that at that time, Professor Sun did not have the opportunity to ask for more details.

According to the biography of He Tengjiao: When Li Jin (Li Zicheng's nephew, later granted the name Chixin) and Gao Bizheng (Li Zicheng's brother-in-law) returned to He Tengjiao in Jingzhou, He Tengjiao submitted a memorial stating, "The chief villain has been eliminated, slightly relieving the anger of the gods and people, and it is appropriate to announce thanks at the temples of heaven and earth." The Prince of Tang was greatly pleased and immediately appointed He Tengjiao as Grand Councillor of the East Pavilion and Minister of War, bestowing upon him the title of Duke Dingxing. However, he doubted whether Li Zicheng was truly dead. He Tengjiao said, "Li Zicheng may be dead, but his body and head had already decayed." He refused to claim credit, firmly declining the title of nobility, but was not allowed to do so. At that time, there were also doubts about whether Li Zicheng was actually dead, which is why this account is included in the biography. The Qing dynasty's claim that officials were sent to verify the body aligns with the report from the Prince of Yuying, who stated, "A defected soldier reported that Li Zicheng had fled to Jiugong Mountain, was trapped by the villagers, and hanged himself to death, with the body having decayed beyond recognition." If Li Zicheng was not actually dead, then who is the person who claimed to have obtained his dragon robe, golden seal, and was missing an eye?

**Xu Zhiyuan** **Shi Yanke in his *Continuation of the Shi Family Traditions* says:** My concubine, Xu Zhiyuan, courtesy name Shanhuai, was from Yangzhou. At the age of thirteen, she married my father. Previously, a northern official wished to marry her, and she wept, saying, "They are wealthy and powerful for generations, and their habits are those of the idle and luxurious. Where can I go? I wish to serve a scholar, like Chao Yun of Dongpo, which would be enough for me." My father heard this and felt compassion for her, and he married her. Four years later, she gave birth to a younger sister who died shortly after birth, and she did not conceive again. In the year Jiwu (1679), my father changed positions to become a lecturer, and my concubine wrote a poem, which included the lines, "If Heaven could understand my heart's suffering, the harsh northern winds and frost have all turned south." My stepmother, Li Yiren, ordered my eldest brother to escort her to the capital.

Another three years passed, and my late father fell ill. My elder brother returned after coughing up blood, and I also returned to the south. My stepmother burned incense and appealed to heaven, cutting flesh from her thigh to offer, and swore to the gods, saying, "The late father's virtue accumulated throughout his life. Even if he certainly does not recover, should we wait for his son to have a final farewell? Otherwise, as a great man of letters, dying by the hands of a concubine, the master's eyes will not close." She therefore cried out loudly until dawn, like this for three days and nights. On the evening of the third day, there was a white light like a ribbon, which fell from the roof, and a strange fragrance arose before the bed. The late father suddenly awakened, and from then on, he began to eat congee. This was the event on the twenty-seventh day of the third month in the year of Gui Hai. I heard the news and rushed to see him. Another seventy days passed, and the late father passed away. My stepmother mourned and made offerings every day as if he were still alive, for five years without a day's difference, and finally died of depression, and was buried together with the late father in the Snail Hill. My stepmother was skilled in poetry, and she often burned her drafts. After her death, a few poems were found, and they are included in the *Xue Yu Ji*. From *Xiao Fen Chang Za Shi*: Zhu Yuan once wrote a poem titled "Sending to the North," which says, "The wind is strong, pulling at partings, the lamp is dim, and the person has not yet slept. This body has no wings, how can it reach your side?" Yu Shan replied with a poem, saying, "Do not blame the long years of separation, endure the cold and sleep alone. My old soul is about to break, and I cannot dream to your side." He also wrote a poem in reply to Xiao Jing, saying, "We have promised to accompany each other with white hair and green mountains, but fate drives us to live without rest. Sending the letter with the embroidered words to the lotus mirror, I break my heart alone, gazing at the sorrowful face."

The poetry and prose of Mr. Mao are all gentle and sincere, reflecting his character. They are all the product of genuine emotional and intellectual depth. His scholarly pursuits and personal charm make him a timeless lover. It is no wonder that his feelings are as deep as pearls in the sea, truly worthy of comparison to Mr. Su Shi's Baoyun. **Discussion on the Biography of Mao Wenlong** Mao Wenlong seized Pi Island to restrain the current dynasty. This act was not without assistance in the strategy to counter the enemy at the time. However, since he established a command post on the island and resisted the current dynasty, he repeatedly failed in battle. His crimes of wasting military funds, violating laws, and killing surrendered enemies were well-known to court officials. Even though Cao Wenlong (Chonghuan) listed twelve specific crimes against him, they were all clearly stated. Therefore, his arrogance and coercion had a justification for execution. When the court officials debated the retention or dismissal of military leaders during the reign of Emperor Tianqi, Zhang Heming alone stated: "If Wang Huazhen leaves, Mao Wenlong will not be effective, and the Liao soldiers will disband." This shows that Mao Wenlong's arrogance and difficulty in control were evident. The biography of Cao Wenlong also states: "Although the strategic situation in Dongjiang is sufficient to restrain the enemy, Mao Wenlong lacks strategic vision and repeatedly fails. He wastes enormous amounts of military funds and is solely focused on expanding commercial activities, trading in prohibited goods. In times of peace, he deals in ginseng and cloth, and in times of crisis, he is rarely of any use." Even if it is said that his crimes did not reach the level of treason, at the meeting on the Double Islands, Cao Wenlong first proposed changes to the military system and the establishment of supervisory officials, but Mao Wenlong angrily rejected the proposal. Cao Wenlong then decided to execute him. His fate was self-inflicted.

Yuan Chonghuan's unauthorized execution was sufficient to give people a reason to criticize him. The biography describes the execution of Yu Wencheng, which differs slightly from the official history, and the writing is somewhat weaker, with language that is somewhat coarse and colloquial. The description of a close, whispered conversation and the later reference to a "return of a hundred-knife oath" seem like the work of a child putting on a village play. This is likely because the biography includes statements such as "I could not succeed, and the Emperor also punished me for executing Yu Wencheng" and "Yuan Chonghuan falsely executed Yu Wencheng, and the Emperor realized this and executed Yuan Chonghuan," leading to a simplistic and superstitious interpretation. However, it is important to note that Yuan Chonghuan's execution was primarily due to Qian Longxi's decision to frame him in the "逆案" (rebellion case). When Emperor Chongzhen executed Yuan Chonghuan, he was also influenced by internal conflicts within the Ming Dynasty. Nevertheless, this shows that Yuan Chonghuan's genuine efforts to serve the country led to his being hated by the Ming Dynasty and the eunuch faction. Therefore, his execution, as recorded in history, was seen as unjust by the people. The idea that people competed to buy and eat his flesh is absurd, given his character. Is the author truly a descendant of Yu Wencheng? The biography was intended to vent anger over Yu Wencheng's execution but ended up being a baseless and fictional account.

Xu Quan once praised the innocence of Xiong Tingbi, and he was a man of integrity and uprightness. His memorial is fully recorded in Xiong Tingbi's biography, but this memorial is not included in the *Ming Shi* (History of the Ming Dynasty). Perhaps the author fabricated the content? Nevertheless, one should take the official history as the basis. According to *Lie Huang Xiao Shi* (A Brief Record of the Late Emperor), Mao Wenlong feared the emperor's brilliance and sought a way to establish himself. He communicated with the Qing, willing to donate three million gold coins to exchange for the lands of Jin and Fu, and to report on the recovery of these territories. The agreement was already made. When Yuan Chonghuan was appointed as commander-in-chief and left the pass, the emperor summoned him to ask for strategies. Yuan Chonghuan answered that he could pacify Liaodong in five years. Upon taking office, he discovered that Mao Wenlong had already made an agreement and hastily sent a lama to the Qing, offering generous benefits, to break Mao Wenlong's agreement and align it with his own. However, the Qing highly valued oaths and insisted on not agreeing. The lama said, "Now there is only one option: to kill Mao Wenlong. This would not violate the oath for the Qing, and I could achieve success." Yuan Chonghuan then used the pretext of reviewing troops to go directly to Pidao Island. Mao Wenlong held a grand banquet. The next day, when Mao Wenlong paid a visit, Yuan Chonghuan also hosted a banquet. Halfway through the banquet, Yuan Chonghuan claimed to have a secret imperial decree and arrested and executed Mao Wenlong immediately. At the time, Mao Wenlong's camp was strict and orderly, and the crowd also dared not interfere. After the matter was settled, Yuan Chonghuan then reported to the court, which temporarily tolerated it. Later, the Qing demanded a bribe, and Yuan Chonghuan specifically requested an additional three million gold coins, claiming that in five years, all of Liaodong would be recovered, which would be a one-time solution for all. The court deliberation insisted on not agreeing, and thus allowed the Qing to invade, leading to the Zunhua incident. Therefore, Yuan Chonghuan's execution of Mao Wenlong was originally for the sake of gaining credit. However, this is not recorded in the biography, perhaps because the author did not see this explanation?

**Appendix: "The Biography of Mao Wenlong"** Mao Zhihuang composed *The Biography of Mao Taibao*, which states that Mao Wenlong was born on the fourteenth day of [a certain month] in the fourth year of the Wanli era in Songsheng Village, Qiantang. He had a handsome beard and eyebrows, with bright, piercing eyes like lightning. His personality was unrestrained and unorthodox; he did not concern himself with livelihood, but instead loved to discuss military affairs. Once, while drinking with a group of people at a building, he struck the table in a drunken state and declared, “I will not rest until I am made a marquis.” The others all laughed at him. At the age of thirty, he traveled to Yan but found no opportunity, so he went further east to the Liao region. The Liao commander took him into his entourage and appointed him as an officer in Haizhou, and he gradually rose to the rank of *Du Tong*. Later, recommended by Wang Huazhen, he was granted hundreds of blank military orders, allowing him to act as circumstances required. This was during the first year of the Tianqi era. At this time, he led his one hundred ninety-seven subordinates eastward to seize control of Pi Island. Pi Island, formerly Joseon territory, was surrounded on all sides by steep, impassable mountains, with passage for boats possible only from the western corner. After Mao Wenlong took it, the states of Jin, Fu, Hai, and Gai were all alarmed. The imperial court then appointed him as the official commander-in-chief, bestowed upon him the imperial sword, promoted him to the position of Left General-in-Chief, and further conferred upon him the title of Taibao. He was granted hereditary titles for three generations, and his son inherited the position of commander of the Jinyiwei.

Upon the public's encouragement, the official took initiative, constructing walls and towers, setting up cannons as defensive equipment, and establishing government offices and temples. He also set up schools, allowing students to participate in the provincial examinations of the northern provinces and Shandong, with some passing the exams. He established agricultural settlements, minted coins, and engaged in maritime trade for long-term planning. Facing the enemy with courage, he achieved repeated victories and used unpredictable strategies without limit. Once, he fought at Great Stone Ridge, where arrows came like rain, and he changed horses twice, both of which were shot dead. At midnight, he climbed a mountain and entered an abandoned temple, where he saw a black horse under the eaves. He mounted it and rode out, and the horse ran very fast. The enemy looked at it and all fled in fear. At dawn, he returned to the camp, and the soldiers all cheered. When he dismounted, it turned out to be a black tiger that leaped away. The people were greatly shocked and said, "General, you are a divine being!" In the winter of the Dingmao year, a high-ranking official was summoned to the capital and spoke with a close friend before parting. The friend asked, "What is the most urgent matter at present?" Coincidentally, this friend had a personal grudge against the official and hesitated before saying, "The eastern island is greatly to be feared." Initially, the official had recruited over ten thousand soldiers, costing the court thousands of gold daily, but the rations were not timely issued. The official repeatedly submitted memorials, but they were still not issued. In his final memorial, he wrote, "If the soldiers were to suddenly rebel, I would die a thousand deaths, but I could not prevent their disloyalty. How would the state fare?" The court already suspected him of coercion, and the official was just entering. At that time, Yuan Chonghuan, newly appointed as the strategic commander, was stationed in the eastern part of Liaodong, and the high-ranking official secretly ordered to plot against him.

Meng Quan Yi, the Director of Military Farming, remembering the high achievements of the public, resented the many errors in court deliberations, and therefore submitted a memorial arguing four points that cannot be understood. He said: The Guan Ning garrison spends three million silver taels and one million three hundred thousand bushels of rice every year. Now, from the second to the seventh year of Tianqi, Pi Island has totaled one million nine hundred thousand silver taels plus, and rice and beans totaling over nine hundred thousand stones. Still, it is incessantly blamed for being excessive? This is the first point that cannot be understood. The farthest reach of Guan Ning is no more than four hundred li, yet it mustered troops up to eighteen million. Over twenty islands under its jurisdiction, a thousand li of vastness, without a large number of troops, how can they be linked and respond to each other? General Wen Long mustered troops of only fifteen million, yet claimed that it was only two million eight thousand, the rest were all fictitious and embezzled. This is the second point that cannot be understood. General Wen Long's wife and children have already returned to Zhejiang for a long time, perhaps also in the sense of General Wang Jian asking for land and houses, but still worry that it is too big to control. This is the third point that cannot be understood. Since it called Pi Island a strategically important place, but relied on Wen Long, yet blocked its supplies. This is entrusting it to the enemy. Even if it called Wen Long one person not worth pitying, but Pi Island has already been lost, the mainland will certainly be in danger. Not considering this, but instead worry about Wen Long day and night. This is the fourth point that cannot be understood. The rest of the defense of Wen Long, a total of over one thousand two hundred words. And said, 'I dare to use my three sons and one grandson to guarantee that Wen Long has no ulterior motives.' Not understanding.

Yuan Chonghuan summoned Gong to meet him on Shuangdao Island by letter. Shuangdao Island is west of Pidao Island. Yuan Chonghuan said, "I have something confidential to say." Gong sailed there calmly and intended to discuss military funding matters in detail. At the time, the military considered it suspicious and asked to bring more followers. Gong replied, "As a major general responsible for the eastern front, if he does not follow the imperial decree, how dare he kill me? If there is indeed an imperial decree, having more people would not help. It would only cause suspicion and doubt. Moreover, have you not heard of Guo Fenyang attending a banquet hosted by Yu Chao'en?" After they met at an ancient temple, Yuan Chonghuan told Gong, "What I want to say to you, others should not hear." The two of them dismissed their attendants, and only Yuan Chonghuan was followed by a scholar. Yuan Chonghuan looked back and said, "He is a remarkable strategist in my camp, and he has often accompanied me." So they walked into the temple holding hands. The scholar was short but strong and had a short knife hidden in his sleeve. After entering, they sat down. Yuan Chonghuan moved closer to Gong to speak, and after a long while, he suddenly said, "I want to behead you today." Gong laughed and said, "Do not jest." Yuan Chonghuan said, "I received a secret imperial order." He took out a small piece of paper from his chest, which was a forged imperial order. Gong was suspicious, and Yuan Chonghuan said, "If I were to kill you with one stroke, I would repay you with a hundred strokes in the future." Gong immediately knelt down, wept without saying a word. The scholar then took out his knife and killed Gong. It was on the fifth day of the sixth month of the second year of Chongzhen, and he was fifty-four years old.

Chonghuan had already killed the official, and the official had a nephew named Chenglu, whom he adopted as his son. Chenglu served alongside his adoptive father on the island, holding the rank of Deputy General. Upon hearing of the changes, he abandoned his post and returned to Hangzhou. Chonghuan captured him, coerced him, and forced him to falsely confess to conspiring with his father, Wenlong, to rebel. This was an attempt to absolve himself of the crime of unauthorized execution. Chenglu took paper and a writing brush, and boldly wrote the four characters "Yue family father and son." All present were moved to sorrow and anger, and even Chonghuan was shaken. Eventually, Chenglu was executed. The soldiers on Pidao then jointly prepared the coffin for the official, carrying his coffin northeast. The coffin refused to move in the sea, and the ship instead drifted westward. The soldiers could do nothing, so they collectively bowed to it, floated it into the sea, and then proceeded northeast. Pidao was thus abandoned. This was the beginning of the loss of the left arm. Meng Quanyi then submitted another memorial to clear the official's name, but it was not accepted, and Quanyi resigned and returned home. Soon after, military documents came in thick and fast, causing great turmoil in the capital. The court knew that the official had indeed been wrongfully killed and also greatly valued his contributions, so they arrested Chonghuan and executed him publicly by slicing. As each piece of flesh fell, people competed to buy and eat it. The "hundred-blade oath" was thus fulfilled, and even high officials were implicated in the affair. The official, as a general, was strict, and his rewards and punishments were always fair. He shared the hardships and joys of his soldiers, embodying the spirit of ancient renowned generals. However, he relied on his own abilities and never bribed powerful officials. If someone offered him a thousand taels of silver, he would only accept it in exchange for an equivalent value in ginseng. This was also the reason for his downfall. Temples were built for him on both Tieshan and Pidao. Remnants of the Liaodong region, carrying their wives and children, had nowhere to go, and wept and hanged themselves at the temple.

腹语 刘子壮 熊伯龙 库中画 乩书 玉人 天主教 大胆 项王走马埒 续集卷二 无支祈 人面疮 陈句山 瘗蚕 偿债犬 剥皮 仙方 耿通 陆忠毅公传赞异兽 殿试卷 推背图 李自成 徐珠渊 毛文龙传辨 自序 余自辛卯迄癸巳，二老亲相继见背，始绝意进取。鸟已倦飞，骥甘终伏。生平知交，大半零落，而又畏见一切得意之人，俯仰四壁，惟日与幼女形影相依，盖生人之趣尽矣。乃喟然叹曰：穷矣！然身可穷，心不可穷也！余诚弃材，不足与海内诸豪俊比数矣。夫蝉蚓不知雨雪，蟪蛄不知春秋，犹能以其窍自鸣，岂樗散之余，遂并蛄蚓之不若乎？于是或酒边灯下，虫语偎阑，或冷雨幽窗，故人不至，意有所得，辄书数行，以销其块磊，而写髀肉之痛。当其思径断绝，异境忽开，窅然如孤凤之翔于千仞，俯视尘世，又何知有蝇头蜗角事哉！于是辄又自浮一白曰：惜乎！具有此笔，乃不得置身史馆与马、班为奴隶也，是亦足聊以自娱矣！
今兹春归里门，箧中携有此本。诸同人见之，咸谓可以问世，谋醵金付梓。顷来此间竹屏蒋君又力任剞劂事。蒙诸君雅意，使得免仲翔没世之感，余亦何能复拒乎？独是余老矣，追忆五十以来，以有用之居诸，供无聊之歌哭，寄托如此，其身世亦可想矣！因书数语，以志吾恨焉。道光二十五年岁次乙巳良月八日，归安朱翊清梅叔氏自题于浔溪寓舍。

穿云琴 康熙间，勾曲道士忘筌，本武昌名家子，以幼孤避乱，入道劳山。性豪逸，耽书嗜饮，善画墨竹。尤精于琴，遇良材，必重价购之，至于典质不倦。 后闻新安吴商名畏龙者，蓄琴颇富，裹粮往访。商见其携有古琴，问：“炼士亦善此乎？”对曰：“固生平所好也，但恨未遇名材耳。”即指手中所携者曰：“此宋贾相悦生堂中物，向以五百金购得之，然亦非上品。闻先生多蓄古琴，故不惮远涉，未识可赐一观否？”商与论琴理，筌为细述勾拨挑剔之法，语多神解。商一时未能尽领，请传之妙手。筌解囊，为弹《水仙操》一阕。商危坐竦听，如有山林杳冥、海涛汩没起于座中，辄为叹绝。

筌停琴，言曰：“此调自伯牙传至嵇康，名《广陵散》，所谓观涛广陵者也。康死，此调已绝，某特以意谱之耳。”商乃出其素所珍藏者十余琴，皆不足观。最后一琴，以金猫睛为徽，龙肝石为轸，背刻二字曰“穿云”，质理密栗，古色黝然，旷代物也。筌爱玩不忍释，请以所携琴易之，不许，增以五百金，亦不许，呼仆取入。筌乃起，怅然而出，谋诸阍者。阍者谢曰：“主人亦徒慕风雅耳，本无真赏。今见师赏鉴若此，岂复能动以利乎？”筌乃出，赁居一僧寺，誓不得琴不返，然卒无可为计，惟日饮。
无何，一夕对月独酌，念资用将竭，而宝琴终不可得，凄然泣下。忽闻墙阴屟响有声，一女子丰姿绰约，含笑而至，曰：“如此良夜，请为清歌侑酒，以破岑寂，可乎？”筌讶问美人何来？女曰：“勿劳穷诘，当非祸君者。”遂于怀中取黄牙拍板，唱《琴心》一折，音韵凄婉，顾盼生姿。筌连釂数觥，竟醉倒于床上。及醒，窗中斜月莹然矣，女犹坐于灯前。遽起，促之归寝，女曰：“妾亦非私奔者，自蒙青盼，觉人间尚有中郎。
继知君情深如许，故背主而来，将以此身相托。即君心中事，或者犹可借箸，不意见拒之深也。”言已，以袖揾泪。筌见其罗袂单寒，转更韵绝，乃拥之入怀，为诉流连之故。女曰：“此易事耳。”筌闻之，喜极曰：“然则今夕愿为情死。”遂拥入，共相缱绻。既而鸟语参横，女急起，曰：“吾二人岂可复留此耶？”筌辞以商琴未得，女笑语曰：“第行勿忧也。”即往墙角取一小箧，出水田衣裙各一，并冠履，易作道装，相与促装，启后扉而行。
中途入一村店沽饮，先有一道者在座，筌揖与谈，理致玄远，遂邀共饮。女避去。道人密语曰：“君相随少尼，非人也。今夜共枕时，某于门外作法，君当紧抱勿释。”如其言，果得一琴，即商所宝藏者也。大喜，持示道人，道人曰：“此杨贵妃遗琴也，传至南宋理宗，曾以殉葬，后为杨琏真伽掘得，非君不足当此物。亦见古今神物，必不终沦于俗子手中，然君亦不可复至劳山矣。”筌乍闻，恍若梦醒，遂起再拜，携琴入终南山，不返。
